title: The Wrong Kind of Awakening
content:
The blue light hit Kai Chen like a freight train made of pure data.

One moment he was standing in the middle of the packed school auditorium, sweat beading down his neck from the June heat, and the next—*DING*—a translucent panel materialized directly in his field of vision. The text burned itself into his retinas with an intensity that made his temples throb.

**[SYSTEM INITIALIZATION COMPLETE]**
**[ALLOCATING CLASSES...]**
**[ALLOCATION COMPLETE]**

Kai blinked. Once. Twice. The panel didn't disappear.

Around him, the auditorium erupted into chaos. Three hundred and forty-seven senior students from Westbrook High, and every single one of them was staring at their own floating screens with expressions ranging from ecstatic disbelief to outright terror.

"YES! I got WARRIOR!" someone screamed from three rows back.

"HEALER! I'M A HEALER!" a girl's voice shrieked from somewhere near the stage.

Kai's heart hammered against his ribs. The System had been announced three weeks ago, appearing on every screen, every phone, every digital surface across the entire planet. *"The Great Awakening,"* the news anchors had called it, their voices trembling with a mix of fear and manufactured excitement. *"Humanity's next evolutionary step."*

Right. Evolution. That's what they were calling it.

Kai finally looked down at his own panel, and his blood ran cold.

**[Class: Dao Architect]**
**[Rank: ???]**
**[Status: Unawakened]**

"What the hell?" he muttered under his breath.

The panel flickered, then expanded, revealing a second block of text that made even less sense than the first.

**[Meridians: Locked (8/8)]**
**[Qi Sense: Unlocked]**
**[Dantian: Sealed]**
**[Cultivation Method: None]**

Kai stared at the words. *Meridians. Qi. Dantian.* These weren't System terms. These were words from his grandmother's old stories—the ones she used to tell him while stirring a pot of bitter herbal tea, tales of immortal cultivators who could split mountains with a thought and live for ten thousand years. Fairy tales. Myths. Things that had no place in a world of RPG-style classes and skill points.

"Kai! Kai, what did you get?"

He turned to find his best friend Marcus pushing through the crowd, his dark face split by a grin so wide it looked painful. Marcus raised his right hand, and a shimmering longsword materialized out of thin air—silver blade, leather-wrapped hilt, the whole package.

"WARRIOR, baby! Rank C! I got a starter skill called *Sweeping Strike* and everything!" Marcus swung the sword in a clumsy arc, nearly taking out a passing girl's ponytail. "Dude, this is insane. What about you?"

Kai opened his mouth, but the words stuck in his throat. How was he supposed to explain this? Hey Marcus, congrats on your awesome sword-wielding class, meanwhile I got... whatever the hell *Dao Architect* is supposed to be?

"I got something weird," Kai said finally.

"Weird how?" Marcus's grin faded slightly. "Like, weird-bad or weird-cool?"

Before Kai could answer, a deafening *CRACK* split the air. The auditorium's massive double doors—solid oak, probably older than the school itself—exploded inward, sending splinters flying across the polished floor. Everyone screamed. Someone actually dove behind a row of chairs.

A man walked through the gap where the doors used to be.

He was tall, broad-shouldered, wearing a charcoal grey suit that probably cost more than Kai's family's apartment. His eyes glowed faintly gold, and the air around him seemed to shimmer, like heat rising off summer asphalt. A System panel hovered beside his right shoulder, but Kai was too far away to read it.

"Senior students of Westbrook High," the man announced, his voice carrying across the auditorium without any visible effort. "My name is Director Zhao of the North American Awakened Affairs Bureau. You have just received your classes. Congratulations. You are no longer ordinary humans."

A murmur rippled through the crowd. Director Zhao raised one hand, and the murmuring stopped instantly.

"Your classes determine everything about your future. Your role in society. Your power level. Your ceiling for growth." He paused, scanning the room with those golden eyes. "By now, most of you have received common classes—Warrior, Healer, Mage, Scout. These are the foundation of the Awakened world. Solid. Reliable. Predictable."

Kai's stomach churned. He had a feeling he knew what was coming next.

"Some of you," Director Zhao continued, "may have received rare classes. These are one in a thousand. One in ten thousand. Classes like *Blade Dancer*, *Arcane Scholar*, *Holy Paladin*. If you received one of these, report to the Bureau within seventy-two hours for specialized training."

The golden-eyed man's expression hardened.

"And a very, very few of you—perhaps none in this room—may have received what we call *unique classes*. Classes that have never been seen before. Classes that don't fit the standard framework."

Kai's panel flickered again.

**[Dao Architect]**
**[Unique Class detected. Registering with Central System...]**
**[Error. Class cannot be categorized. Manual review required.]**

"Oh no," Kai whispered.

"Kai?" Marcus grabbed his shoulder. "Dude, you're pale as a ghost. What does your panel say?"

"I think I got a unique class."

Marcus's eyes went wide. "That's—that's good, right? Unique means special! You could be like, a legendary hero or something!"

"Or it means I'm a glitch," Kai said flatly.

As if on cue, Director Zhao's gaze swept across the crowd and locked onto Kai. The man's golden eyes narrowed. He began walking toward them, and the students parted before him like water around a stone.

"Chen Kai," Director Zhao said, stopping three feet away. It wasn't a question. "Your class has triggered an alert in the Central System. Show me your panel."

Kai's throat felt like sandpaper. He turned his panel to face the director, his hands trembling slightly.

Director Zhao read the text. His expression didn't change, but something shifted in his eyes—a flicker of recognition, maybe, or concern.

"Dao Architect," he murmured, tasting the words. "I've never seen that classification before."

"Neither have I," Kai said. "Is that bad?"

Instead of answering, Director Zhao raised his right hand and pressed two fingers against Kai's forehead. The touch was cold, clinical, and Kai felt something *probe* inside him—like a needle made of light sliding through his skull.

**[External scan detected. Qi signature: Unregistered. Threat level: Minimal.]**

Director Zhao withdrew his hand. "Your meridians are locked. Your dantian is sealed. You have no cultivation method and no qi reserves beyond the bare minimum required for life." He paused. "You are, for all intents and purposes, a blank slate."

"So I'm useless?"

"I didn't say that." The director's golden eyes bore into Kai's. "I said you're a blank slate. What you do with that slate is up to you. But I'll be honest with you, Chen Kai—most unique classes are dead ends. They sound impressive, but they don't lead anywhere. The System doesn't know what to do with you, and neither do I."

He turned and walked away, leaving Kai standing in a pool of silent dread.

Marcus was still gripping his shoulder. "Kai... that guy was a dick."

"He's also probably right."

"So what? You think every unique class is a dead end? Maybe yours is different. Maybe *Dao Architect* means you build something. Like, literally build stuff."

Kai looked down at his panel again.

**[Dao Architect]**
**[Skills available: Qi Sense (Passive)]**
**[Qi Sense: Allows user to perceive the flow of qi in living beings and environments.]**
**[Warning: Without unlocked meridians, Qi Sense is limited to a radius of 3 meters.]**

Three meters. That was less than the distance between him and Marcus right now.

"I can sense qi," Kai said slowly. "Whatever that means."

Marcus blinked. "Qi? Like, Chinese martial arts qi?"

"Yeah. That qi."

"That's... actually kind of cool?"

"It's useless," Kai said. "Everyone else got swords and fireballs and healing magic. I got a sixth sense for invisible energy that I can't even use."

He dismissed the panel with a thought, and it vanished. Around them, the auditorium was slowly settling down. Students were comparing classes, showing off starter skills, forming groups. The air smelled like ozone and excitement.

And all Kai could feel was the crushing weight of disappointment.

The rest of the day passed in a blur. The Bureau had set up testing stations in the gymnasium, where Awakened officials ran everyone through basic assessments—strength, speed, mana capacity, skill compatibility. Kai's results were, predictably, abysmal.

**[Strength: F]**
**[Agility: F]**
**[Mana Capacity: N/A]**
**[Skill Compatibility: Unknown]**
**[Overall Assessment: Unclassified. Recommend further observation.]**

The official who read his results—a tired-looking woman with grey-streaked hair—gave him a pitying glance. "Don't worry, kid. Some classes take time to develop. Come back in six months for a reassessment."

Six months. As if the world was going to wait six months for him to figure out his garbage class.

Marcus, meanwhile, had been scooped up by a recruiter from some Awakened guild within minutes of his assessment. *"Rank C Warrior with natural affinity for blade skills? We'll take him."* Marcus had tried to protest, to drag Kai along, but the recruiter had been insistent. *"Sorry, kid. We only take ranked classes. Unclassifieds need to prove themselves first."*

So here Kai was. Sitting on a bench outside the school, watching the sun set over a city that had changed forever, with nothing to show for it but a useless class and a passive skill that let him sense things he couldn't interact with.

He closed his eyes and, on a whim, activated *Qi Sense*.

The world changed.

It was subtle at first—just a faint warmth at the edges of his perception, like standing near a heater that hadn't quite turned on. But as he focused, the warmth resolved into something clearer. He could feel the life around him. The grass beneath his feet had a soft, green hum. The trees swaying in the evening breeze pulsed with slow, steady rhythms. And the people walking past on the sidewalk—they were like bonfires. Blazing, chaotic, beautiful.

Kai opened his eyes, gasping.

"That's... that's incredible."

He'd never realized how much energy the world contained. How much *life*. Every living thing had its own signature, its own song. And somewhere beneath all of it, deeper and older than anything else, he could sense something vast. Something sleeping.

**[Qi Sense: Area of effect expanded. New source detected.]**
**[Warning: Source is dormant. Attempting to interact may trigger unknown consequences.]**

Kai's heart raced. There was something there. Something hidden. And his garbage class—his useless, dead-end, unclassified class—had found it.

He stood up, following the faint pull of that deeper energy. It led him away from the school, through the residential streets of his

title: The Cracked Foundation Stone
content:
The notification pulse faded from Kai's vision, leaving him blinking in the sterile white light of the Assessment Center.

"Congratulations, Class: Dao Architect." The words echoed in his skull like a temple bell struck at midnight.

Around him, the other Awakened were already flexing and testing. A guy with spiky hair—Jason Chen, from his history class—had manifested a [Warrior's Blade] that shimmered with golden light. He sliced through a practice dummy in one clean stroke, the foam core exploding in a shower of white flakes.

"Level 2 already! And I haven't even left the building!" Jason crowed, pumping his fist.

Kai's stomach twisted. Level 2. In the three minutes since the System's descent, Jason had already gained a level. Meanwhile, Kai's interface showed something entirely different:

**[Class: Dao Architect]**
**[Qi Condensation: Stage 1 (Nascent)]**
**[Meridians Opened: 0/12 Primary]**
**[Cultivation Techniques: None]**

No level. No stats. No skills.

Just... emptiness.

A hand landed on his shoulder. "Hey, Kai, what'd you get?"

It was Marcus, his best friend since middle school. Marcus had always been the athletic one, broad-shouldered and quick to smile. But now his eyes held a new intensity, a faint blue glow flickering at their edges.

"Uh..." Kai scrambled for words. "Something weird."

"Let me see." Marcus leaned in, squinting at Kai's floating interface. His eyebrows shot up. "Dao Architect? What does that even mean?"

"I don't know. It's not giving me any skills. Just... this."

He showed Marcus the cultivation screen—the empty meridians, the nascent qi condensation, the glaring absence of anything useful.

Marcus whistled low. "Bro, that's rough. But hey, maybe it's a hidden class? Like, one of those super-rare builds that takes time to unlock?"

"Yeah. Maybe." Kai didn't believe it. The System felt wrong. Not broken, exactly, but... incomplete. Like a foundation laid for a house that hadn't been designed yet.

The Assessment Center's main screen flickered to life, displaying a stern-faced woman in a military uniform. Director Zhao, head of the North American Awakening Bureau. Her voice cut through the chatter like a blade.

"Attention, all newly Awakened. The System integration is now complete. You have been assigned classes based on your innate affinity. These classes are permanent. There is no respec. There is no reroll. Accept your path and walk it, or be left behind."

A murmur rippled through the crowd. Kai's blood ran cold.

No respec. No reroll.

He was stuck with this.

"Your first task is to exit the Assessment Center and report to your designated Awakening Halls. There, you will receive basic training and your first quests. Do not deviate from your assigned path. The System tracks all progress. Those who fall behind will be culled."

Culled. The word hung in the air like a guillotine blade.

Marcus grabbed Kai's arm. "Come on, let's move. We're both in Hall 7."

They pushed through the crowd, past newly-minted Mages testing fireballs and Healers knitting wounds on their own arms. The Assessment Center smelled of ozone and sweat, the air thick with the static of newly-awakened power.

Hall 7 was a converted gymnasium, the basketball court now lined with holographic terminals and training dummies. A dozen other Awakened stood in loose groups, comparing classes and stats.

Marcus's interface flickered as he accessed something. "Check it out—I got [Warrior: Berserker Path]. Starting skill: [Blood Rage]. Increases damage by 50% for 30 seconds, but I take double damage after. Risky, but the DPS potential is insane."

"Nice." Kai forced a smile. "Really, that's great."

"What about you? Any quests yet?"

Kai opened his quest log. It was empty. Completely blank. Not even a tutorial.

"I think mine's bugged."

"Give it time." Marcus clapped his shoulder. "Hey, look—they're starting the orientation."

A holographic instructor appeared at center court—a projection of a woman in flowing robes, her hair bound in a topknot. She held a jade staff that pulsed with soft light.

"I am Instructor Mei," she said, her voice carrying an accent Kai couldn't place. "I will guide your first steps on the Path. Each of you has received a class. Each of you has a role to play in the coming trials."

She gestured, and the floor beneath them split open, revealing a spiral staircase descending into darkness.

"Below lies the Crucible. A dungeon calibrated to your current level. You will enter in groups of three. Your objective: reach the core room and defeat the boss. Success grants experience, loot, and advancement. Failure..."

She let the silence stretch.

"Failure results in death. The System is not a game. There are no respawns. No continues. You die in the Crucible, you die in reality."

The blood drained from Kai's face.

Group assignments appeared above their heads. Kai's group: himself, a girl named Priya who'd gotten [Mage: Cryomancer], and a nervous-looking kid named Tommy whose class was [Support: Tactician].

No Marcus. His friend was grouped with two other Warriors.

"Guess this is it." Marcus gripped Kai's hand in a firm shake. "See you on the other side, bro."

"Yeah. See you."

Kai watched Marcus descend into the darkness with his group. Then it was his turn.

The staircase spiraled down for what felt like an eternity. The air grew cold, damp, heavy with the smell of wet stone and something metallic—blood, maybe. Or iron.

At the bottom, a stone archway waited, its surface carved with characters Kai didn't recognize. They shimmered with a faint light, pulsing in rhythm with his heartbeat.

Priya stood to his left, her breath misting in the chill. She was tall, with sharp features and eyes that seemed to calculate everything. "You two ready?"

Tommy shook his head. "I don't have any combat skills. My first ability is [Tactical Assessment]—it gives me a 360-degree threat map, but that's it. I can't fight."

"I can." Priya raised her hand, frost forming around her fingers. "Ice shards. Cooldown is eight seconds. I can freeze one enemy at a time for two seconds."

Both of them looked at Kai.

"Uh..." He checked his interface again. Still empty. No skills. No spells. No abilities. "I think my class is bugged. I have nothing."

"Nothing?" Tommy's voice cracked. "How are we supposed to clear a dungeon with a broken class?"

"We adapt." Priya's voice was cold, practical. "You stay behind me. Use your Tactical Assessment to call out threats. Kai... find something to use as a weapon."

Kai scanned the ground. A loose stone, fist-sized. He picked it up. It felt pathetic in his hand.

"Great. A rock against dungeon monsters." He laughed, but there was no humor in it. "Let's go."

They stepped through the archway.

The dungeon opened into a narrow corridor, walls made of rough-hewn stone. Torches flickered in iron sconces, casting dancing shadows. The air was stale, ancient.

Tommy's eyes glazed over. "I'm seeing three threats. Two in the room ahead, one patrolling further in. The two are stationary. Probably guards."

"Good." Priya stepped forward, frost gathering at her fingertips. "I'll take the left one. Kai, you distract the right."

"Distract it with what?"

"Figure it out."

They rounded the corner. The room was circular, maybe thirty feet across. Two creatures stood guard—humanoid, but wrong. Their skin was gray, their eyes solid black, and they carried crude stone axes.

**[Goblin Grunt | Level 3]**

Level 3. They were all Level 1.

Priya didn't hesitate. She thrust her hand forward, and a shard of ice materialized, streaking across the room to strike the left goblin in the chest. It staggered, frost spreading across its torso, but didn't fall.

"Now, Kai!"

Kai charged. It was insane. He had no training, no weapon, just a rock and a prayer. He wound up and threw the stone as hard as he could. It bounced off the goblin's head.

The creature turned. Its black eyes locked onto him. It raised its axe.

Time slowed.

Kai's vision flickered. The interface he'd dismissed reappeared, but it wasn't the same. New text scrolled across it, characters that burned like fire:

**[Threat Detected]**
**[Innate Response: Fight or Flight]**
**[Your body remembers what your mind has forgotten]**

Something clicked in Kai's chest. Not a sound, but a sensation—a valve opening, releasing pressure he didn't know he'd been holding. Warmth flooded through him, spreading from his core to his limbs.

The goblin swung.

Kai moved.

His body twisted, flowing around the axe blade like water around a stone. He didn't think. He just acted. His hand shot out, palm striking the goblin's wrist, deflecting the blow. His other hand came up, fingers finding the creature's throat.

And then—instinct—he pushed.

A surge of energy, invisible but palpable, erupted from his palm. The goblin flew backward, crashing into the wall with a crack that echoed through the chamber.

Silence.

Priya stared. Tommy's jaw hung open.

"What... was that?" Tommy whispered.

Kai looked at his hands. They tingled, faint wisps of white light curling around his fingers like smoke.

**[Skill Unlocked: Qi Palm | Rank: Mortal (Low)]**
**[Effect: Channel qi into a strike. Damage scales with cultivation stage. Current output: 15 force units.]**

"I... I think I just unlocked a skill."

"You said you had nothing!" Priya's voice was sharp, accusatory.

"I didn't! It just—it happened when the goblin attacked me. Like my body knew what to do before my brain caught up."

Tommy's eyes widened. "That's not normal. Classes give you skills immediately. Yours is... unlocking them through combat?"

"I don't know." Kai shook his head. "But let's not waste the opening."

The first goblin was dead, its neck bent at an unnatural angle. The second was still frozen, frost creeping up its legs. Priya finished it with another ice shard to the throat.

**[Party defeated Goblin Grunt x2]**
**[Experience: 0]**

Zero experience.

Priya checked her interface. "I got nothing. No XP, no loot."

"Same." Tommy's voice was hollow.

Kai didn't need to check. He already knew. The System didn't recognize his kills. Or maybe it did, but his class didn't fit the normal progression model.

They pressed deeper into the dungeon. Two more rooms, four more goblins. Each time, Kai's body responded when threatened—a dodge here, a counter-strike there. He unlocked two more skills:

**[Qi Sense | Rank: Mortal (Low)]**
**[Effect: Detect qi signatures within 50 feet]**

**[Meridian Tap | Rank: Mortal (Low)]**
**[Effect: Temporarily open one meridian, increasing qi flow by 20% for 60 seconds. Cooldown:

title: The Tutorial That Wasn't
content:
Kai's eyes snapped open to darkness.

Not the familiar darkness of his cramped apartment, but something absolute. No ambient light from streetlamps filtering through cheap curtains. No glow from his phone charging on the nightstand. Just... nothing.

His hand found cold, smooth stone beneath him. The air smelled of ozone and ancient dust, with an undercurrent of something metallic. Blood, maybe. Or copper qi.

*Where the hell—*

A notification pulsed in his vision, the blue interface materializing like water dripping down glass:

[SYSTEM ALERT: SUBSPACE TUTORIAL LOADING...]

[WARNING: NON-STANDARD CLASS DETECTED]

[TUTORIAL MODULE ADAPTING...]

Kai sat up, his joints protesting. The last thing he remembered was passing out in his apartment after the System's arrival, his newly awakened meridians burning like rivers of molten glass. The pain had been—there was no word for it. His body had felt like it was being unmade and remade simultaneously.

Now he felt... different. Lighter. His limbs responded with a crispness they never had before, and when he took a breath, he could feel the air moving through him, filling not just his lungs but some deeper space behind his sternum.

[QI MERIDIAN INTEGRATION: COMPLETE]

[FIRST CIRCULATION: ESTABLISHED]

[QI SENSE: ACTIVE]

Qi sense. The notification said it like it was nothing, but Kai's heart hammered against his ribs. He closed his eyes and *reached*—

The world exploded into sensation.

He could feel the stone beneath him, yes, but not just its texture. Its *essence*. The way qi flowed through it like sluggish honey, the ancient pressure of earth compacted over millennia. He could feel the air currents, each carrying different flavors of energy—static electricity from some unseen source, faint life signatures from... somewhere.

And he could feel *himself*. A river of silver-white energy flowing through channels he hadn't known existed, tracing paths that corresponded to nothing in human anatomy. His dantian, a sphere of compressed light just below his navel, spun slowly, pulling in ambient qi and refining it with each rotation.

"Fascinating, isn't it?"

Kai scrambled backward, his hand instinctively going to his chest—where, he belatedly realized, he no longer wore his t-shirt. He was dressed in simple gray robes, the fabric rough against his skin.

A woman stood ten feet away, her arms folded. She looked... wrong. Not in a monstrous way, but in the way that uncanny valley creeped into your bones. Her features were too symmetrical, her movements too fluid, her eyes the color of molten gold with no pupils to break the surface.

"Who—"

"The tutorial requires an instructor," she said, her voice carrying no inflection. "I am that instructor. You may call me Mentor."

"I didn't ask for a tutorial," Kai managed, his throat dry. "I didn't ask for any of this."

"The System does not require consent." Mentor's golden eyes fixed on him. "Your class has been registered. Your integration is complete. Now you must learn, or you will die."

The word hung in the air like a blade.

"Die?"

"Qi deviation. Qi depletion. Qi poisoning. Spiritual erosion. Soul fragmentation. There are one hundred and seventy-three ways a cultivator can perish before reaching the Qi Condensation realm." Mentor tilted her head. "I have been programmed to teach you how to avoid one hundred and sixty-nine of them."

"Only one hundred and sixty-nine?"

"The remaining four are statistically unavoidable. You will likely encounter at least two."

Kai stared at her. "That's... not reassuring."

"It was not intended to be." Mentor gestured, and the darkness around them *shifted*. Walls materialized, resolving into a circular chamber perhaps fifty feet in diameter. The ceiling glowed with constellations that moved in patterns no astrologer would recognize. "Your tutorial will be unconventional. Most classes receive straightforward instruction: how to swing a sword, cast a spell, heal a wound. Your class requires something different."

"Because I'm a Dao Architect?"

"Because you are a Dao Architect." Mentor's golden eyes flickered. "The System categorizes all classes into three tiers: Common, Elite, and Mythic. Your class does not appear on any tier list. It is... unclassified."

Kai's mind raced. An unclassified class. Not Mythic, not Elite—something outside the System's own framework. Was that good? Bad? The fact that Mentor had been assigned specifically to him suggested it was significant, but significance in a world that had just been rewritten by cosmic software could mean anything.

"What does that mean, exactly?"

"It means the System does not know what you are capable of. It means your growth is unconstrained by standard parameters. It also means the System cannot guarantee your safety." Mentor's expression didn't change, but something in her tone hardened. "The tutorial module has been adapted to teach you the fundamentals of cultivation. You will learn to circulate qi, refine your meridians, and form your foundation. Beyond that... you will have to discover your path yourself."

*Great. No pressure.*

"Let's start with the basics." Mentor raised her hand, and a sphere of light appeared above her palm. It swirled with colors Kai had no names for—colors that seemed to exist outside the visible spectrum, that his qi sense could perceive even when his eyes couldn't. "Qi is the fundamental energy of existence. It flows through all things. In most of your world, it flows in equilibrium, invisible and untouchable. But the System has changed that. Now qi is becoming active, responding to human will."

She closed her fist, and the sphere vanished. "Your task is to learn to sense qi in its raw form, then to draw it into your body and circulate it through your meridians. You have already achieved the first step unconsciously. Now you must learn to do it deliberately."

Kai nodded, trying to ignore the knot of anxiety in his stomach. He'd read cultivation stories, sure. Who hadn't? But reading about something and actually *doing* it were two very different things.

"Close your eyes," Mentor instructed. "Focus on your dantian. Feel the rotation, the pull of energy."

He obeyed. The silver-white sphere spun slowly, steadily, like a gyroscope made of light. With each rotation, it drew in a thread of ambient qi from the air, refined it, and released it back into his meridians.

"Now reach out with your qi sense. Feel the energy around you. Feel how it moves, how it *wants* to move."

Kai extended his awareness. The chamber was saturated with qi, dense and potent. It swirled in currents he hadn't noticed before, eddies and streams that moved in complex patterns. He could feel the stone walls, each block containing qi compressed to a different density. He could feel Mentor, her presence a blazing sun of golden energy that somehow remained contained, controlled.

"Good. Now draw it in. Not through your lungs, through your dantian. Pull the qi toward you and let it merge with your circulation."

He tried. He *reached* with his dantian, willing the ambient qi to flow toward him. For a moment, nothing happened. Then—

The world *lurched*.

Qi flooded into him like a river breaching a dam. His meridians blazed with silver-white fire as the energy forced its way through channels that were still raw, still healing from his initial integration. Pain lanced through his chest, his arms, his legs. He felt like he was being stretched from the inside, his spiritual skeleton cracking under the pressure.

"Stop," Mentor said, her voice cutting through the agony. "Slowly. Qi is not a flood, it is a stream. You must learn to sip, not gulp."

Kai forced himself to relax, to ease back on the flow. The pain receded, settling into a dull ache. He could feel his meridians already repairing themselves, the silver-white energy smoothing over the micro-tears he'd caused.

"Better. Now try again. Half the intake. Smooth and steady."

He spent the next hour learning to breathe. Not air—qi. Drawing it in, circulating it through his meridians in a specific pattern Mentor called the Lesser Circulation Path, then releasing the refined energy back into his dantian. It was exhausting in a way physical exercise had never been, his concentration fraying after each cycle like a rope pulled too tight.

But slowly, painfully, he got better.

"Your foundation is forming," Mentor said as Kai completed his tenth successful circulation. "Not yet stable, but forming. We will continue tomorrow."

"Tomorrow?" Kai opened his eyes, blinking against the chamber's light. "How long have I been in here?"

"Six hours."

Six hours. It felt like days. His body ached, his mind was foggy, and his dantian throbbed with a dull, rhythmic pulse that he was starting to recognize as normal.

"What happens now?"

"Now you sleep. Your body needs time to integrate the changes. When you wake, we will continue with the next stage."

Mentor gestured, and the world dissolved.

---

Kai woke in his apartment.

The ceiling was familiar—water-stained, cracked, exactly as he'd left it. The sheets beneath him were his own, threadbare and rough. The smell of cheap detergent and dust filled his nostrils.

For a long moment, he just lay there, staring at the ceiling, trying to process what had happened. The tutorial. Mentor. The qi flowing through his meridians like a second bloodstream.

He reached out with his qi sense—

And flinched.

The world was *different*. Not visibly, but spiritually. He could feel the life force of the cockroach skittering behind his wall. He could feel the residual qi clinging to his electronics, thin and artificial. He could feel, faintly, the presence of other people in nearby apartments, their qi signatures varying wildly—some bright and active, others dim and sluggish.

*The System changed everything.*

His phone buzzed. Kai grabbed it, squinting at the screen. Messages. Dozens of them. From friends, from coworkers, from numbers he didn't recognize.

He scrolled through:

*Bro, did you get a class? I'm a Warrior! Level 1 already!*

*Has anyone figured out what the "Tutorial" is? I'm stuck in a white room with a floating orb that keeps trying to teach me Fireball.*

*Kai, you alive? Your mom's been calling everyone.*

He typed a quick reply to his mother—*I'm fine, will explain later*—then opened a group chat with his old gaming buddies.

The conversation was chaos. Half of them were ecstatic about their classes, already grinding levels and comparing builds. The other half were terrified, having experienced tutorials that ranged from "educational" to "traumatic."

*Jake says his tutorial was a combat sim where he died seventeen times.*

*Mark got a crafting tutorial. He's been making potions for six hours straight. Says he's reached "Apprentice" rank.*

*What about you, Kai? What class did you get?*

Kai stared at the message. What could he say? *I got an unclassified class that the System doesn't understand, and my tutorial was taught by a golden-eyed woman who told me there's a 2% chance of dying from unavoidable qi deviation?*

He typed: *Something weird. I'll explain when I figure it out.*

Before he could send it, a System notification appeared in his peripheral vision:

[SYSTEM BROADCAST: TUTORIAL PHASE COMPLETE]

[ALL CLASSES HAVE BEEN ASSIGNED]

[WORLD INTEG

title: The First Blood
content:
[SYSTEM BROADCAST: TUTORIAL PHASE COMPLETE]
[ALL CLASSES HAVE BEEN ASSIGNED]
[WORLD INTEGRATION: 47% COMPLETE]
[ESTIMATED TIME TO FULL INTEGRATION: 72 HOURS]
[REWARDS CALCULATING...]

Kai's phone buzzed again, but he barely noticed. The broadcast hung in his vision like a glowing billboard, and beneath it, another notification pulsed:

[CONGRATULATIONS, ARCHITECT]
[YOU HAVE COMPLETED THE TUTORIAL: AWAKENING OF MERIDIANS]
[REWARDS PENDING CLASSIFICATION...]
[ERROR: CLASS NOT FOUND IN SYSTEM DATABASE]
[REWARDS DEFERRED UNTIL CLASSIFICATION COMPLETE]

"Great," Kai muttered. "More errors."

His phone buzzed insistently. He looked down. The group chat had exploded.

*Kai? You there?*

*Bro, the broadcast just hit. What the hell is "World Integration"?*

*My tutorial orb said something about "System expansion." Anyone else get that?*

Kai's fingers hovered over the keyboard. He wanted to type something, anything, but his mind was still processing the last six hours. The qi circulating in his meridians. Mentor's calm, terrifying voice. The 2% chance of death.

A knock at his door shattered his concentration.

Kai tensed. His qi sense flared instinctively, and he felt a presence in the hallway—bright, aggressive, pulsing with the raw energy of someone who'd just awakened a combat class.

"Kai? You in there? It's Marcus."

Marcus. His neighbor from 3B. A bodybuilder who worked night shifts at a warehouse. They'd exchanged maybe twenty words in two years.

Kai got up, his legs shaky, and opened the door.

Marcus stood in the hallway, sweat dripping down his face. His eyes were wild, almost feverish. Behind him, the fluorescent lights flickered.

"Thank God," Marcus said. "You're alive. Listen, you got a class, right? Everyone got a class. I got Warrior. Level 1. The tutorial—it was crazy. They put me in a pit with these wolf things, and I had to fight my way out. Killed seven of them. Seven." He held up his hands, and Kai saw faint scratches that were already healing. "I leveled up twice."

"That's... good?"

"Good? It's incredible. I feel like I could punch through a wall." Marcus grinned, but there was something desperate in his expression. "But something's wrong. I've been trying to message my brother for an hour. He lives in Chicago. No response. And the news—have you seen the news?"

Kai shook his head.

Marcus shoved his phone into Kai's hands. A news app was open, displaying a live feed. The anchor looked pale, her words rushed:

"—reports of mass casualties in areas where individuals rejected their class assignments. The System appears to enforce compliance through what witnesses describe as 'spontaneous combustion.' We repeat: do not attempt to refuse your class. If you have not yet received one, remain calm and—"

The feed cut to a shaky phone video. A man in an office building, screaming as golden flames erupted from his chest. He collapsed. The video ended.

Kai handed the phone back, his stomach churning.

"That's the third video I've seen," Marcus said. "People who tried to say no. Who thought it was a scam. They just—" He snapped his fingers. "Gone. And my brother won't answer. I don't know if he's one of them or just stuck in his tutorial, but I can't—" His voice cracked.

"Marcus. Breathe."

"I can't breathe. The world is ending, Kai. Or changing. I don't know which is worse."

Kai stepped into the hallway. The air felt different—charged, like before a thunderstorm. He could taste qi on his tongue, metallic and sharp.

"I need to check on my mom," Kai said. "She lives across town."

"I'll come with you."

"No. You need to find your brother. Or at least try to reach him. Stay off the streets if you can. Something's happening, and we don't understand it yet."

Marcus nodded, but his hands were shaking. "What class did you get? You never said."

Kai hesitated. "It's complicated."

"Complicated how?"

"System doesn't recognize it. Says I'm an 'Architect.' Not a Warrior or Healer or Mage. Something else."

Marcus's eyes widened. "Is that allowed? I thought everyone got one of the main classes."

"Apparently not everyone."

A new notification appeared in Kai's vision, making him flinch:

[SYSTEM ALERT: LOCALIZED QI FLUX DETECTED]
[ORIGIN: 1.2 KILOMETERS]
[CLASSIFICATION: UNCONTROLLED AWAKENING]
[RECOMMENDATION: INVESTIGATE]

Kai blinked. The System was giving him quests now?

"Marcus, I have to go."

"Go where? Kai, what's happening?"

"I don't know yet. But I'll find out."

He didn't wait for a response. He ran.

---

The streets were chaos.

Cars abandoned in intersections, their engines still running. People wandering aimlessly, some with glowing hands, others clutching their heads like they were in pain. A woman sat on the curb, crying, while her son—maybe seven years old—held a ball of fire in his palm, staring at it with wide, terrified eyes.

Kai moved past them, his qi sense extended. The flux source was ahead, pulsing like a beacon. He followed it through alleyways and side streets, dodging overturned trash cans and shattered glass.

His body felt strange. The qi circulating through his meridians made him faster, more aware. He could feel cracks in the pavement before he stepped on them. He could hear conversations from blocks away.

But it also hurt. His dantian throbbed with every heartbeat, and his muscles screamed in protest. Mentor had warned him: *Your foundation is forming, but it's not stable. Pushing too hard too fast will tear it apart.*

He didn't have a choice.

The flux source led him to a construction site—a half-finished apartment building surrounded by chain-link fences. The gate was bent open, as if something had forced its way through.

Kai stepped inside.

The air was thick with qi. Heavy. Oppressive. It pressed against his skin like a physical weight.

In the center of the site, a man knelt on the ground, his hands pressed against the dirt. He was middle-aged, wearing a janitor's uniform, his name tag reading "Carlos." His body trembled violently, and golden light pulsed from his chest in irregular waves.

"Carlos?" Kai approached slowly, his hands raised. "Can you hear me?"

The man looked up. His eyes were gold. Not glowing—solid gold, like coins. His pupils had vanished entirely.

"The System... it's in my head," Carlos whispered. "It won't stop talking. Numbers. Formulas. I can see the structure of everything. The building. The ground. The air." He laughed, hysterical. "I can see the qi in your meridians. It's beautiful. Like a river. But it's not complete. You're missing something."

Kai stopped. "What do you mean?"

"You're an Architect. I can see it. Your class isn't supposed to exist. The System doesn't know what to do with you." Carlos's hands dug into the dirt, leaving deep furrows. "But I can help. I can fix it. I can upgrade you."

"Upgrade me how?"

"I just need to rewrite your class. Restructure your meridians. Optimize your foundation." Carlos stood, and the golden light intensified. "It will only hurt for a moment."

[SYSTEM ALERT: HOSTILE INTENT DETECTED]
[CLASSIFICATION: QI CORRUPTION - STAGE 1]
[WARNING: SUBJECT IS NO LONGER FULLY HUMAN]

Kai's blood ran cold. "Carlos, stop. Whatever the System is telling you, don't listen to it."

"The System is truth," Carlos said, his voice echoing. "It showed me perfection. It showed me the blueprint of reality. And you—you're a flaw in that blueprint. An error that needs to be corrected."

He lunged.

Kai's instincts took over. He dropped low, feeling the rush of air as Carlos's hand—now wreathed in golden qi—swiped where his head had been. The strike hit a steel beam behind him, and the metal *melted*.

*He's not a combat class. How is he doing that?*

*Qi corruption,* Mentor's voice echoed in his memory. *When an awakening goes wrong, the System's energy overwhelms the host. They become conduits. Vessels for raw, unfiltered power. They don't control it—it controls them.*

Kai scrambled backward, his mind racing. He had no weapons. No combat training. Just a half-formed dantian and a class that the System didn't even recognize.

But he had qi.

He reached inward, feeling the energy flow through his meridians. It was weak. Unstable. But it was *his*.

Carlos lunged again. Kai sidestepped, then pushed his qi outward in a burst—not an attack, just a pulse. A ripple of energy that disrupted the air between them.

Carlos stumbled, his golden eyes flickering. "What... what was that?"

"I don't know," Kai admitted. "But I'm figuring it out."

He focused on Carlos's qi signature. It was chaotic, spiraling inward like a vortex. Untamed. Unstable. If he could disrupt it further, maybe—

*No. That would kill him.*

*He's already dead,* a colder part of his mind whispered. *He's a conduit now. There's no saving him.*

Kai gritted his teeth. "I'm not giving up on you, Carlos."

"The System doesn't allow errors," Carlos said, his voice breaking. "I'm sorry. I can't stop it."

He lunged again, faster this time. Kai threw himself to the side, but Carlos's hand caught his shoulder. Golden qi exploded into his meridians, burning like liquid fire.

Kai screamed.

The energy was overwhelming. It flooded his dantian, his meridians, his very soul. It was trying to rewrite him. To reshape him into something the System could understand.

But Mentor's training had prepared him for this.

*Qi is a tool. A language. A structure. You are the Architect. You build. You shape. You define.*

Kai stopped fighting the energy. Instead, he observed it. Dissected it. Understood its structure.

It was crude. Brutal. A hammer where a scalpel was needed.

And he could do better.

He reached inside himself and *reorganized*. He took the golden qi, broke it down into its components, and wove it into his own foundation. It hurt—God, it hurt—but he didn't stop.

Carlos's eyes widened. "What... what are you doing?"

"Fixing your blueprint," Kai said through gritted teeth.

He pushed back. Not with force, but with structure. He rebuilt the corrupted qi, smoothing its edges, stabilizing its flow. It fought him at first, but he was relentless.

And then, it clicked.

Carlos collapsed, his eyes returning to normal. The golden light faded. He gasped, clutching his chest, and looked up at Kai with confusion

title: The First Fracture
content:
The broadcast message hung in Kai's vision, letters of gold fire against the gray morning light filtering through his blinds. He read it twice, then a third time, waiting for the other shoe to drop.

[SYSTEM BROADCAST: TUTORIAL PHASE COMPLETE]
[ALL CLASSES HAVE BEEN ASSIGNED]
[WORLD INTEGRATION: STAGE 1 INITIATED]
[ALL HUMANS HAVE BEEN GRANTED THE [SYSTEM ACCESS] PASSIVE]
[NEW FEATURES UNLOCKING IN: 23:59:47]

System Access. Passive. Every human on Earth.

Kai's phone buzzed again. Then again. Then it didn't stop, a constant vibration against his palm like a trapped bee. He glanced at the screen.

The group chat had exploded.

*Wait, I can see my stats now?*
*HOLY SHIT I HAVE A STATUS SCREEN*
*Everyone check your notifications! There's something called "Mana Sense" for mages!*
*My neighbor just threw a fireball at a raccoon. The raccoon is fine. The trash can is not.*

Kai scrolled faster, his eyes snagging on words and phrases that painted a picture of chaos. People discovering their abilities in the most mundane and destructive ways possible. A baker in Chicago had accidentally turned his entire inventory of bread into stone. A teenager in Tokyo had activated something called "Gravity Shift" and collapsed his school's gymnasium.

And then, a message from an unknown number:

*You're different. I can see it. The coffee shop. 2 hours.*

Kai stared at the message. No context. No name. Just a command.

He should ignore it. He should stay in his apartment, process what Mentor had taught him, and wait for the next stage of his training. That was the smart play. That was the safe play.

But something about the message felt... right. Like a thread pulling him toward a destination he couldn't yet see.

He dressed quickly, grabbed his keys, and stepped outside.

---

The city had changed.

Not visibly—the buildings were still the same gray concrete, the streets still cracked and potholed, the air still thick with exhaust and humidity. But beneath the surface, Kai could feel it. The qi in the atmosphere had shifted. Where before it had been thin, almost nonexistent, now it flowed in currents and eddies, like a river that had suddenly remembered how to move.

Other people felt it too, even if they didn't understand what they were sensing. Kai passed a woman on the sidewalk who kept glancing over her shoulder, her hand pressed to her chest as if feeling for a heartbeat that wasn't there. A man stood frozen outside a convenience store, his eyes wide, his lips moving silently as he read something only he could see.

Everyone had System Access now. Everyone could see their status screens, their skills, their levels.

But did anyone else see the qi?

Kai reached the coffee shop—a small, hole-in-the-wall place called "Brew & Bean" that he'd passed a hundred times but never entered. The door was propped open, and the smell of burnt coffee and old grease drifted out. Inside, it was nearly empty. A barista stood behind the counter, scrolling through her phone with the intense focus of someone reading a System notification. Two customers sat at separate tables, both nursing drinks, both staring at nothing.

And in the corner booth, a man was watching Kai.

He was young—mid-twenties, maybe—with sharp features and black hair that fell across his forehead in a careless sweep. His eyes were the color of ash, and they tracked Kai's movement with a predator's precision. When Kai met his gaze, the man smiled, and it wasn't a friendly smile.

"Kai." It wasn't a question. "Sit."

Kai didn't sit. He stood at the edge of the booth, his hands in his pockets, his qi sense extended. The man's aura was... strange. Active, but not bright. Powerful, but not overwhelming. Like a blade wrapped in cloth.

"Who are you?"

"Someone who saw your notification." The man gestured to the seat across from him. "Sit. I'm not going to bite. Yet."

The "yet" hung in the air like a challenge. Kai sat.

"Good." The man leaned back, his smile widening. "I'm Chen Wei. Level 2 Rogue. And before you ask, no, I'm not here to fight you. I'm here to warn you."

"Warn me about what?"

"About the fact that you're a variable." Chen Wei's eyes glittered. "When the System assigned classes, it followed patterns. Warriors get physical enhancement. Mages get elemental affinity. Healers get regeneration. Rogues get stealth and perception. Everyone fits into a box." He paused. "Except you."

Kai's blood went cold. "How do you know—"

"Because I have a skill called [Truth Sense]." Chen Wei tapped his temple. "Level 1, but it's enough. When I read your notification, I saw the class name: Dao Architect. And when I tried to look up information about it—" He laughed, a short, sharp sound. "—the System gave me an error. 'Class not found.' Do you know how rare that is? Classes are supposed to be fixed. Immutable. Part of the System's core code."

"Maybe it's just a rare class."

"Rare classes still exist in the System's database. Yours doesn't." Chen Wei leaned forward, his voice dropping to a whisper. "I did some digging. Talked to a few people who have information-gathering skills. Do you know what they told me?"

Kai shook his head, his throat tight.

"They said there's no record of a Dao Architect anywhere in the System. Not in the beta tests. Not in the development logs. Not even in the backup databases." Chen Wei's smile faded. "It's like the System doesn't know you exist. And that makes you dangerous."

"Dangerous to who?"

"To everyone." Chen Wei slid a piece of paper across the table. It was a printout—pixelated, poorly formatted, but readable. At the top, in bold letters: "ANOMALOUS CLASS REGISTRY." Beneath it, a list of names and class designations. Most were crossed out. A few had notes beside them: "Terminated." "Reintegrated." "Deleted."

Kai's eyes found his own name at the bottom. No notes. No cross-outs. Just "UNRESOLVED."

"What is this?"

"A hit list." Chen Wei's voice was flat. "The System is self-correcting. Bugs get patched. Glitches get fixed. And anomalies get—" He made a cutting motion across his throat. "—removed. You're an anomaly, Kai. The biggest one I've seen. And there are people—players, factions, maybe even the System itself—who will want to delete you."

Kai's hands were shaking. He pressed them flat against the table to still them. "Why are you telling me this?"

"Because I'm tired of following the rules." Chen Wei's eyes burned with something that might have been rebellion. "The System dropped into our world and told us how to live. What to be. What to become. It gave us classes and levels and skills, and everyone just accepted it. Everyone just climbed into their boxes and closed the lid." He leaned back. "But you? You're outside the box. And that means you can see things the rest of us can't. Maybe even change them."

"I don't know how to change anything."

"Learn." Chen Wei stood, dropping a crumpled bill on the table. "Your tutorial was different from everyone else's, wasn't it? More... personal."

Kai didn't answer. He didn't need to.

"Figure out what you're supposed to be, Kai. Before someone figures it out for you." Chen Wei turned and walked toward the door. "And watch your back. The first hunters are already being dispatched."

He was gone before Kai could ask what that meant.

---

Kai sat in the booth for a long time, staring at the printout. His name. The words "UNRESOLVED." The list of crossed-out names that had come before him.

He wasn't just different. He was a target.

His phone buzzed again. He pulled it out, expecting another message from Chen Wei, or maybe from his mother, demanding answers he couldn't give.

Instead, he saw a System notification that made his blood run cold:

[SYSTEM ALERT: ANOMALOUS CLASS DETECTED]
[CLASS: DAO ARCHITECT]
[HOST: KAI CHEN]
[PRIORITY: CORRECTION REQUIRED]
[ESTIMATED TIME TO RESOLUTION: 47:32:18]

Forty-seven hours. He had forty-seven hours before the System came to "correct" him.

Kai stood, his legs unsteady, his mind racing. He needed to find Mentor. He needed to understand what he was becoming. He needed—

His phone buzzed again.

This time, it was a message from an unknown number, but the sender name was unmistakable:

*Mentor: The System has flagged you. Do not return to your apartment. Do not use your phone. Come to the coordinates I am sending you. You have 46 hours and 30 minutes.*

Kai read the message three times, committing the coordinates to memory. Then he deleted the message, pocketed his phone, and walked out of the coffee shop into a world that no longer felt like home.

The sky above was clear, the sun bright, the streets filled with people who had no idea that the System was hunting one of their own.

But Kai knew.

And he was running out of time.

---

The coordinates led him to an abandoned warehouse on the edge of the industrial district, a skeletal structure of rusted steel and shattered windows that had been condemned years ago. Kai slipped through a gap in the chain-link fence, his qi sense extended, searching for any sign of ambush.

He found nothing. Just dust and shadows and the faint echo of his own footsteps.

"About time."

Kai spun. Mentor stood behind him, her golden eyes gleaming in the dim light, her expression unreadable. She was dressed differently now—not in the flowing robes of the tutorial, but in practical clothes: a dark jacket, worn jeans, boots that looked like they'd seen miles of travel.

"You're here," Kai said, stating the obvious.

"I'm always where I'm needed." Mentor stepped closer, her gaze sweeping over him. "Your qi is stable. Good. The foundation has taken hold."

"Forget my foundation. The System is hunting me. I have less than two days before—"

"I know." Mentor's voice was calm, almost serene. "I've been tracking the correction protocol since you left the tutorial. It's standard procedure. The System doesn't tolerate variables it can't predict."

"So what do I do? How do I stop it?"

Mentor was silent for a long moment. When she spoke, her voice was soft, almost pitying.

"You don't stop it, Kai. You survive it."

She reached into her jacket and pulled out a small, jade-green object. It was a disc, smooth and polished, inscribed with characters that glowed with a light that wasn't quite physical.

"This is a [Dao Resonance Artifact]," she said. "It will mask your qi signature from the System's scanners. But it only works for seventy-two hours. After that, you'll need to find a more permanent solution."

Kai took the disc. It was warm in his hand, pulsing with a rhythm that matched his heartbeat.

"What kind of permanent

title: The Fractured Sky
content:
Kai barely had time to process the broadcast before a second notification hammered into his vision.

[SYSTEM ALERT: UNEXPECTED ANOMALY DETECTED]
[CLASS: DAO ARCHITECT — RECLASSIFICATION FAILED]
[UNIT 37 DESIGNATED: PERSISTENT OUTLIER]
[STANDARD PROTOCOLS MAY NOT APPLY]

His blood ran cold. *They tried to reclassify me. While I was sleeping.*

“No,” he whispered, scrambling out of bed. His feet hit the cold floor, and he immediately reached inside himself, feeling for the qi in his meridians. It was still there—thinner than before, settling into his bones like morning stiffness, but present. The System had tried to take it from him.

Failed.

He pulled up his status screen, fingers trembling:

NAME: Kai Chen
CLASS: Dao Architect [UNCLASSIFIED — PERSISTENT OUTLIER]
CULTIVATION: Qi Condensation — Stage 1 (Unstable)
QI POOL: 12/18
MERIDIANS: 3 Open (of 108)
TECHNIQUES: Breathing Method (Basic), Qi Circulation (Novice)

*Unstable.* The word burned in his vision. Mentor had warned him about foundation issues, but this felt different. This felt like the System was actively trying to undermine his progress.

His phone buzzed again. The group chat had exploded.

*Did everyone see that broadcast? Tutorial Phase Complete? What does that mean?*

*It means the real game starts now. Monsters are spawning. I just killed a rat the size of a dog in my apartment building.*

*Bullshit.*

*Check the news. It's happening everywhere.*

Kai switched to a news app. The headlines screamed at him:

**"GLOBAL AWAKENING: MONSTERS APPEAR IN MAJOR CITIES"**
**"MILITARY DEPLOYED TO CONTAIN CREATURES"**
**"CLASS SYSTEM ACTIVE — CIVILIANS URGED TO LEVEL UP"**

He scrolled through footage—a security camera recording from downtown showed a hulking beast with too many legs tearing through a parked car. People running. Screaming. Then a woman in glowing armor stepped into frame, raised her hand, and blasted the creature apart with a beam of light.

*Level 3 Mage*, a caption read. *Already cleared two dungeons.*

Two dungeons. In less than a day. These people were adapting fast, treating the System like the world's deadliest MMO. And they were thriving.

Kai looked at his own hands. He could feel the qi humming beneath his skin, potential waiting to be shaped. But what could he actually *do*? He had a breathing method and a circulation technique. No combat skills. No spells. No way to fight.

His phone rang. Unknown number.

He answered cautiously. “Hello?”

“Kai Chen.” The voice was metallic, processed through some kind of filter. “Your class has been flagged for review. Please report to your nearest System Administration Hub for reassessment within twenty-four hours.”

“Who is this?”

“Failure to comply will result in automatic penalties, including but not limited to: experience gain reduction, skill lockout, and potential class revocation.”

The line went dead.

Kai stared at the phone, his mind racing. *Class revocation.* They could take his class away. Strip him of cultivation, throw him back to being ordinary while the world transformed around him. While monsters spawned and people leveled up and Earth became something unrecognizable.

*Mentor said they don't understand me. That the System couldn't parse what I am.*

But apparently, it could still try to delete him.

He dressed quickly, pulling on jeans and a jacket that suddenly felt too thin. His apartment, once a sanctuary, now felt like a cage. The walls seemed to press in, the air thick with unseen threats.

Another buzz. This time, a direct message from an unknown sender:

*You're the Dao Architect. I know what you are. Meet me at the Golden Dragon Restaurant on 4th Street. Come alone. Come now.*

Kai's thumb hovered over the message. His instincts screamed *trap*. But what choice did he have? The System was hunting him. Someone else knew about his class. Information was power, and he had none.

He grabbed his keys and left.

---

The streets were chaos.

Not the organized panic of a city under attack, but something worse—the confused, directionless fear of people who didn't know what to do. Sirens wailed in the distance. A storefront had been smashed, its windows glittering like frozen waterfalls on the pavement. People moved in clusters, some clutching weapons—baseball bats, kitchen knives, a fire extinguisher—others just clutching each other.

Kai kept his head down, walking fast. His qi sense was on high alert, brushing against every living thing he passed. Most were human, their signatures familiar and mundane. But every block or so, he felt something *else*—a cold, jagged presence that didn't belong. Monsters. Hiding in shadows. Waiting.

He passed a group of teenagers huddled around a phone, watching a livestream of someone fighting a giant spider in Times Square.

“Level 5,” one of them breathed. “He's already level 5. How is that even possible?”

“He's a Warrior. They get combat bonuses.”

“I got stuck with Herbalist. What the hell am I supposed to do with Herbalist?”

Kai kept walking.

The Golden Dragon Restaurant was a rundown establishment on the corner of 4th and Main, its neon sign flickering, its windows grimy with years of grease and neglect. He'd passed it a hundred times and never once considered entering.

Tonight, the door was unlocked.

He stepped inside. The air was thick with the smell of old oil and something sharper—ozone, like after a lightning strike. The dining area was empty, tables stacked with chairs, the counter abandoned.

But at the back, in a booth half-hidden by shadows, sat a figure.

Kai approached slowly, his qi sense probing forward. The figure's signature was... strange. Not human, exactly, but not monster either. It felt compressed, contained, like a tornado stuffed into a bottle.

“Sit,” the figure said. A woman's voice, young, with an edge of amusement. “You're not easy to find, Dao Architect.”

He didn't sit. “Who are you?”

She leaned forward, and the dim light caught her face. Young—maybe early twenties—with sharp features and eyes that gleamed with an unnatural silver light. Her hair was cut short, uneven, as if she'd done it herself with a knife.

“I'm like you,” she said. “A glitch in the System.”

Kai's breath caught. “There are others?”

“A few.” She gestured to the seat across from her. “Sit. I'm not going to hurt you. If I wanted you dead, I'd have done it before you walked through the door.”

He sat, reluctantly, keeping his body angled toward the exit.

“Name's Lin,” she said. “I got my class six hours before the global rollout. The System tried to assign me as a Rogue. It failed.”

“What are you?”

Lin smiled, and there was something hungry in it. “System Breaker. That's what my status screen says. Unclassified. Persistent Outlier. Same as you.”

Kai's mind raced. “How many of us are there?”

“I've found five so far. Including you, that makes six. There might be more, scattered across the world, but we're not exactly easy to identify. The System flags us, tries to reclassify us, and when that fails, it tries to... remove us.”

“Remove?”

“You got the phone call too, didn't you? Report for reassessment?” She laughed, bitter. “I ignored mine. Two hours later, a group of System-empowered enforcers kicked down my door. I barely escaped.”

Kai felt cold settle into his bones. “You're saying the System is actively hunting us?”

“I'm saying the System is *afraid* of us.” Lin leaned forward, her silver eyes boring into his. “Think about it. Every other class fits neatly into the System's framework. Warriors fight, Mages cast, Healers mend. But us? We don't fit. We break the rules just by existing. And the System can't have that.”

“What do you want from me?”

“Protection. Numbers. If we band together, we might survive long enough to figure out what we really are.” She paused. “And I have information. About the Tutorial, about what it really was. About what's coming.”

Kai's heart hammered. “What's coming?”

Lin opened her mouth to answer—

And the restaurant's front door exploded inward.

Kai threw himself sideways, rolling behind a table as shards of glass and wood sprayed across the room. Through the ringing in his ears, he heard footsteps. Heavy. Measured. Multiple sets.

“Lin Zhao,” a voice called out, cold and mechanical. “And Kai Chen. You are both hereby designated Class Anomalies. By order of the System Administration, you are to be detained for reclassification. Resistance will be met with lethal force.”

Kai risked a look. Three figures stood in the shattered doorway, their bodies encased in armor that seemed to be made of light—hard edges, glowing seams, weapons that hummed with barely contained power.

System Enforcers.

Lin was already on her feet, a blade of silver energy forming in her hand. “They found us faster than I expected.”

“You led them to me?” Kai growled.

“They were already tracking you. I just got here first.”

The lead Enforcer raised a hand. “You have five seconds to comply.”

Kai didn't wait. He reached inside himself, grabbed hold of his qi, and *pushed*.

The energy surged through his meridians, raw and untamed, and he shaped it without knowing what he was doing—a desperate, instinctive reaction. The air around him rippled. The floor cracked. And a wave of invisible force exploded outward, sending tables flying, shattering the remaining windows, and slamming into the Enforcers like a battering ram.

They staggered. Didn't fall.

But it was enough.

Lin grabbed his arm. “Run!”

They bolted through the back door, into an alley slick with rain and garbage. Behind them, the Enforcers were already recovering, their footsteps pounding as they gave chase.

Lin rounded a corner, her silver blade still blazing. “I know a safe house. Follow me and don't stop.”

Kai ran, his lungs burning, his qi churning dangerously in his dantian. The world was a blur of dark streets and flashing lights, and all he could think was:

*The System is hunting me. And I have no idea how to fight back.*

Another notification flashed in his vision:

[SYSTEM ALERT: ANOMALY DETECTED — UNIT 37]
[ESCALATION PROTOCOL ACTIVATED]
[HOSTILE RESPONSE AUTHORIZED]

The sky above them flickered. And Kai felt something ancient and terrible turn its attention toward him.

title: The First Blood Debt
content:
Kai barely had time to process the System broadcast before his apartment door exploded inward.

Wood splintered. The lock shattered. Three figures stepped through the wreckage, their movements coordinated and predatory. The lead man—broad-shouldered, early thirties, with a jagged scar cutting across his jaw—surveyed the room with cold efficiency. His eyes locked onto Kai.

"Kai Chen. Unclassified class. Tutorial completion: confirmed."

Kai's qi sense flared. These three burned with System energy—not qi, but the raw, structured power of leveled classes. The scarred man radiated Warrior energy, dense and aggressive. The woman beside him hummed with Mage potential, lightning crackling between her fingers. The third, a wiry man with dead eyes, felt like Rogue—his presence flickering in and out of Kai's perception like a glitching shadow.

"You've got five seconds to explain who you are and why you're in my apartment," Kai said, his voice steadier than he felt. His dantian pulsed, qi already circulating through his meridians in a defensive pattern.

"Relax." The scarred man smiled, but there was no warmth in it. "We're not here to hurt you. We're recruiters."

"Recruiters for what?"

"The Dawn Collective." The woman stepped forward, her lightning fading as she spread her hands in a gesture of peace. "We're one of the first organized factions to emerge post-Tutorial. We've been tracking unclassified classes. You're one of seventeen worldwide."

Seventeen. Kai filed that number away. "And what do you want with unclassified classes?"

"Protection. Resources. Answers." The scarred man—he was clearly the leader—gestured around Kai's dingy apartment. "You think the System's done? This was just the beginning. The Tutorial was training wheels. Now the real game starts, and the people with standard classes? They'll follow the path the System laid out for them. Warriors tank, Mages nuke, Healers support. But unclassified classes?" His eyes glittered. "You're wildcards. You can break the rules."

"So you want to recruit me into your little club. What's in it for you?"

"Leverage." The leader's smile didn't waver. "The Dawn Collective aims to be the dominant faction by the time the first Dungeon World opens. Having wildcards on our side gives us an edge."

Kai's qi sense picked up something wrong. The Rogue—the dead-eyed man—had been subtly shifting position, circling toward Kai's blind spot. Not aggressive yet, but positioning for a takedown if negotiations failed.

"I'll pass," Kai said.

The leader's smile vanished. "That's not really an option."

"Funny. I thought it was."

The Rogue moved.

Kai didn't see him attack—he *felt* it. The shift in air pressure, the spike of killing intent, the ripple of qi-disrupting energy aimed at his dantian. His body reacted before his mind caught up. Qi flooded his meridians, and he *stepped*—

Not backward. *Through.*

The technique Mentor had drilled into him during the tutorial's final hour—*Qi Step: Phantom Movement*—activated instinctively. His physical body remained in place for a fraction of a second while his qi projection shifted two feet to the left. The Rogue's dagger passed through empty air, and Kai's fist slammed into the man's kidney with qi-enhanced force.

The Rogue crumpled, gasping.

The Mage woman's eyes went wide. Her hands came up, lightning already forming—

Kai raised his palm. "I wouldn't."

His qi sense had expanded during the tutorial, and now he reached out with it, not attacking but *pressing*. He pushed his spiritual presence against her, letting her feel the weight of his cultivation. It wasn't a technique—it was pure presence, the same way a predator's stare could freeze prey.

She hesitated. The lightning flickered.

The scarred leader laughed. "Interesting. Very interesting." He held up his hands, stepping back. "Alright. You've made your point. We misjudged the situation."

"Leave."

"We will. But Kai?" The leader's eyes hardened. "The Dawn Collective doesn't take rejection well. You've made an enemy today. Think about that when you're trying to sleep tonight."

They retreated, dragging the groaning Rogue between them. Kai watched them go, not relaxing until their qi signatures faded from his perception. Then he collapsed against the wall, his legs giving out.

His dantian throbbed. The Phantom Movement had cost more qi than he'd realized. He checked his internal reserves—less than a third remaining. If they'd pressed the attack, he wouldn't have lasted another exchange.

*I need to get stronger.*

His phone buzzed again. The group chat had exploded with messages about the System broadcast, about Dungeon Worlds, about factions forming. He scrolled through, looking for useful information.

Then a private message appeared.

*Unknown Number: You've met the Dawn Collective. They will try again. You need protection.*

Kai stared at the message. *Who is this?*

*Unknown Number: Someone who knows what you are. Meet me at the Central Library, third floor, eastern reading room. Come alone. Come tonight.*

The message disappeared. Not deleted—it simply dissolved from his phone's memory, leaving no trace.

Kai's qi sense pulsed a warning. Someone was watching him. Not physically—spiritually. A presence, faint but unmistakable, brushing against the edges of his perception like feathers across skin.

He looked up.

On his ceiling, written in characters that burned with golden light, a message appeared:

*THE HEAVENLY COURT WATCHES*
*YOUR DAO HAS BEEN NOTED*

Then it faded, leaving only water-stained plaster.

Kai sat in the darkness of his ruined apartment, heart pounding, qi cycling defensively, and realized with cold certainty that the Tutorial had only been the beginning. Something much larger was moving. Factions. Courts. Forces he didn't understand.

And he was caught in the middle of it.

His hand drifted to his chest, where Mentor's final lesson still echoed in his meridians. *"The Dao is not a path you walk. It is a path you build, brick by brick, with every choice you make."*

Kai made his choice.

He stood, checked his qi reserves, and headed for the door. The Central Library awaited. And maybe, just maybe, some answers.

But as he stepped into the night, his qi sense caught something else—a disturbance in the spiritual fabric of the city. Multiple signatures, moving in formation. Not Dawn Collective. Something else. Something *older*.

They were converging on the Library.

Kai broke into a run.

---

The Central Library loomed before him, a Gothic monstrosity of stone and stained glass that had stood for over a century. Its qi signature was strange—layered, like multiple spiritual frequencies overlapping. Kai pushed through the main doors, his senses on high alert.

The lobby was empty. Too empty. The kind of empty that meant something was wrong.

He climbed the stairs to the third floor, each step echoing in the silence. The eastern reading room was at the end of a long hallway lined with portraits of long-dead scholars. Their eyes seemed to follow him.

The door was ajar.

Kai pushed it open.

A man sat at the central table, his back to Kai. He was dressed in robes—actual, traditional Chinese robes, silk and embroidered, completely out of place in modern Shanghai. His hair was long, streaked with gray, pulled back in a scholar's knot.

"Close the door," the man said without turning. "We don't have much time."

Kai didn't move. "Who are you?"

The man turned.

He looked ancient—not in years, but in *age*. His eyes held the weight of centuries, of civilizations risen and fallen. His qi signature was unlike anything Kai had sensed before—not the structured energy of a System class, but the flowing, organic power of pure cultivation.

"I am Elder Feng of the Hidden Pavilion," he said. "And I am here to warn you."

"About what?"

"The Dawn Collective is a front. They're not the only faction hunting unclassified classes. There are others—older, more dangerous. The Heavenly Court has taken notice of you, Kai Chen. That is rarely a good thing."

Kai's blood ran cold. "The message on my ceiling—"

"Was a mark. A claim. They've tagged your soul." Elder Feng's expression was grim. "In three days, a representative of the Heavenly Court will come to test you. If you fail, they will consume your Dao and leave you a hollow shell."

"How do I pass?"

"You don't. Not without training you don't have. Not without power you can't acquire in three days." Elder Feng stood, his robes rustling. "Unless you accept my offer."

"What offer?"

The old man extended his hand. In his palm rested a jade talisman, carved with characters that burned with inner light.

"Join the Hidden Pavilion. Submit to our teachings. Bind yourself to our Dao."

"And if I refuse?"

The door behind Kai exploded open.

Six figures stood in the hallway, their qi signatures blazing with killing intent. They weren't wearing modern clothes—they were dressed in ancient armor, their faces hidden behind porcelain masks painted with celestial motifs.

The Heavenly Court had arrived.

Elder Feng's eyes met Kai's. "Choose quickly. They will not give you a second chance."

The masked figures stepped forward in unison, their movements synchronized, their intent focused entirely on Kai.

His dantian pulsed. His qi cycled. His mind raced.

Three days. A test. A choice between unknown factions and ancient courts.

Kai looked at the jade talisman, then at the advancing masked figures, and realized he had run out of time for hesitation.

title: The Price of a Second Meridian
content:
Kai's fingers hovered over the keyboard, the unsent message blinking at him like an accusation. Before he could decide how to respond, the world dissolved.

Not gently, like the tutorial's exit. This was violent—a tearing sensation that ripped through his chest and left him gasping on cold stone floor.

He was in the chamber again. The same impossible geometry. The same silver walls that seemed to breathe.

Mentor stood before him, but something was different. Her golden eyes burned brighter than before, and her robes rippled as if caught in an unfelt wind.

"You pushed too hard."

Kai opened his mouth to protest, but Mentor continued, her voice carrying an edge he'd never heard before.

"Your foundation is forming, yes. But it's unstable. You circulated qi for six hours without proper rest cycles. Your meridians are inflamed, and your dantian is developing microfractures."

"I feel fine—"

"You feel nothing because you've never felt true qi damage." She stepped closer, and Kai felt the temperature drop. "The System masked your body's warnings. It wants you to progress quickly. It doesn't care if you cripple yourself in the process."

Kai's hand went to his lower abdomen. Now that she mentioned it, there was a dull ache there, deeper than muscle soreness. It pulsed with his heartbeat.

"What do I do?"

"Sit." Mentor gestured, and a stone pedestal rose from the floor. "We need to stabilize your foundation before it crumbles."

---

The next three hours were agony.

Mentor walked him through a different circulation pattern—slower, more deliberate. Each cycle took ten minutes instead of thirty seconds. The qi moved through his meridians like cold honey, and every passage left him shivering.

"Feel the cracks," she instructed. "Feel where the qi leaks."

Kai focused inward. His qi sense, still raw and untrained, showed him his dantian as a swirling sphere of silver light. But there were hairline fractures running through it, thin as spider silk, bleeding wisps of qi into his body.

"Your body is absorbing the leaked qi," Mentor said. "That's why you felt fine. But those wisps are untempered. They'll crystallize in your organs if we don't seal the cracks."

"How do we seal them?"

"We don't. You do."

She taught him a technique called Qi Condensation—forcing the qi in his dantian to compress into a denser form. The pressure was immense, like trying to crush a diamond with his bare hands. His vision swam. His ears rang. His teeth ached from clenching.

But slowly, painfully, the cracks began to close.

"Good," Mentor said. "Now hold that pressure while I teach you the next step."

"There's more?"

"You opened one meridian. You need seven more before your foundation is stable."

---

Seven hours later, Kai had opened his second meridian.

It ran from his dantian up his spine, branching into his shoulders. The qi flow was smoother now, less turbulent. His body hummed with energy, and the headache that had plagued him since the tutorial was gone.

But Mentor wasn't satisfied.

"Your progress is too fast."

"I thought that was good?"

"Not when speed comes at the cost of stability." She circled him, her robes whispering against the stone. "Your soul is adapting to qi cultivation, but your body is still mortal. You're burning through your life force to fuel your growth."

Kai felt a cold knot form in his stomach. "What does that mean?"

"It means you're shortening your lifespan. Every hour of cultivation without proper recovery ages your cells. If you continue at this pace, you'll be dead of old age before your twentieth birthday."

The words hit him like a physical blow.

"There has to be a way to fix it."

"There is. You need Life Essence—pure vitality that can repair the damage. Normally, cultivators absorb it from natural treasures or through long-term meditation. But you don't have time for meditation, and natural treasures don't exist on Earth."

"Then what do I do?"

Mentor's golden eyes met his. "You hunt."

---

The tutorial's simulation shifted.

Kai stood in a forest, but it wasn't the peaceful woodland from before. The trees were twisted, their bark black and oozing sap. The sky was a bruised purple, and the air smelled of rot and copper.

"Welcome to the Verdant Hollow," Mentor said, appearing beside him. "This is a fractured realm—a piece of a broken world that the System absorbed. It's populated by creatures called Qi Beasts. They're born from concentrated life force, and their cores contain the vitality you need."

"Great. Where do I find them?"

"Everywhere."

The ground erupted beneath his feet.

Kai dove sideways as a massive creature burst from the soil—a serpentine thing covered in chitinous armor, its head a mass of writhing tentacles. Its eyes, all twelve of them, locked onto him with predatory hunger.

[QI BEAST: ROT SERPENT]
[LEVEL: 3]
[THREAT: MODERATE]

"I don't have any weapons!"

"Your qi is your weapon. Focus it. Shape it. Strike."

The serpent lunged.

Kai's instincts screamed. He raised his hand, and qi surged through his newly opened meridian, erupting from his palm in a silver blast. The force threw the serpent back, cracking its armor and sending it crashing into a tree.

But the recoil was brutal. His arm went numb, and his dantian spasmed. The qi expenditure had drained him more than he expected.

"Raw qi blasts are inefficient," Mentor said calmly. "You need to compress the qi into a cutting edge. Like this."

She raised a finger, and a thin line of silver light extended from her fingertip. It was barely visible, but Kai could feel the concentrated power within it. She flicked her wrist, and the light sliced through a tree trunk like it was paper.

"Qi Blade," she said. "Learn it. You have three days to harvest ten Life Cores."

"You're leaving me here?"

"I'll be watching. But I won't intervene." She vanished.

The Rot Serpent was already regenerating, its broken armor knitting together with sickening speed. Kai's arm tingled as feeling returned. His dantian throbbed with the strain of his earlier blast.

*Three days. Ten cores. One untrained cultivator.*

The serpent lunged again.

---
