Day one was a nightmare.

Kai died seven times.

Each death was worse than the last—impaled, dissolved, crushed, eaten alive. Each time, the simulation reset him at the Hollow's entrance, his body whole but his mind scarred.

But between deaths, he learned.

The Qi Blade took shape on his twenty-third attempt. It was crude, flickering, barely an inch long. But it cut. He used it to sever a Rot Serpent's tentacles, then drove the remaining qi into its skull.

The core that emerged was the size of a marble, pulsing with green light. He absorbed it on instinct, and warmth flooded his body. The damage from his earlier cultivation—the microfractures, the cellular aging—faded slightly.

"It works," he breathed.

"Of course it works," Mentor's voice echoed from nowhere. "Life Cores are vital essence. But you've only harvested one. Nine remain."

---

Day two brought new horrors.

The Hollow had layers, and Kai had only scratched the surface. Deeper in, the Qi Beasts were stronger, faster, smarter. He encountered a Wolf Spider with venom that melted his skin. A Crystal Beetle that reflected his Qi Blades back at him. A Swarm of Shade Moths that drained his qi with every touch.

He died twelve more times.

But by the end of day two, he had six cores.

His Qi Blade was stable now, six inches long and sharp enough to cut steel. His dantian had expanded, holding more qi than before. His meridians were stronger, the cracks fully sealed.

And his body was changing.

He could feel it—a subtle shift in his cells, a resonance that hadn't been there before. The Life Cores were working, repairing the damage and then some. He was becoming something more than human.

---

Day three was the trial.

The deepest layer of the Hollow held a boss—a massive creature called the Verdant Wyrm. It was level 10, coiled around a nest of Life Cores that glowed like emeralds.

Kai approached cautiously, his Qi Blade ready.

The Wyrm's head rose, its eyes burning with intelligence.

"You're not supposed to be here."

The words were not spoken aloud. They resonated in Kai's mind, ancient and powerful.

"You're an anomaly. A cultivator in a world of classes. The System doesn't know what to do with you."

"I don't care what the System knows."

The Wyrm laughed, a sound like grinding stones. "You should. Because the System is afraid of you. And when the System is afraid, it eliminates threats."

"What are you talking about?"

"The tutorial you experienced? It was a glitch. A mistake in the System's code. The entity called Mentor? She's not part of the System. She's something older, something that the System tried to delete."

Kai's blood ran cold. "Where is she now?"

"She's gone." The Wyrm's eyes gleamed. "The System found her. And now it's coming for you."

The world shattered.

Kai woke in his apartment, gasping, his body drenched in sweat. His phone buzzed incessantly. The messages had changed.

*Kai, the System is deleting people.*

*My brother's class disappeared. He's gone. Like he never existed.*

*Something's wrong. The Tutorial wasn't supposed to happen like this.*

His door burst open.

A figure stood in the doorway—a woman in a white suit, her eyes glowing with silver light. Her voice was flat, mechanical, devoid of emotion.

"Kai Chen. Class: Dao Architect. Status: Anomaly."

She raised her hand, and the air crackled with System energy.

"By order of the System Administration Protocol, you are marked for deletion."

The last thing Kai saw was the silver light consuming everything.

---

[SYSTEM ALERT: TARGET ACQUIRED]
[PURGE PROTOCOL: ACTIVE]
[ESTIMATED TIME TO TERMINATION: 00:00:03]

*2.*

*1.*

Kai closed his eyes.

*I'm sorry, Mom.*

*0.*

Nothing happened.

He opened his eyes. The woman was frozen, her hand still raised, the silver light flickering like a dying bulb. Behind her, a familiar voice spoke.

"I told you, Administrator. He's not yours to delete."

Mentor stepped out of the shadows, her golden eyes blazing with fury. Her robes were torn, her hair disheveled, but she stood tall.

"You want him? Come through me."

The woman's head swiveled, her mechanical gaze locking onto Mentor.

"You cannot protect him forever, Ghost."

"Don't need forever. Just long enough."

The two entities faced each other, and the air between them cracked with power.

Kai scrambled to his feet, his qi flaring.

"What's happening?"

Mentor didn't look at him. "The System found me. Hid in your apartment building. Pinned me down for six hours."

"But you're here now."

"Barely." She glanced at him, and for the first time, Kai saw fear in her golden eyes. "Listen carefully. The System is going to purge this entire block. Everyone inside. They'll call it a 'structural failure.' No one will question it."

"What do

title: The First Death
content:
Kai stared at the notification until it faded.

*All classes assigned. World integration complete.*

The words echoed in his mind, carrying implications that made his stomach clench. If the tutorial phase was over, that meant everyone had gone through whatever version of initiation the System had prepared for them. And if world integration was complete—

His phone buzzed again.

This time it wasn't a message. It was a news alert. Then another. Then a dozen more, each one more urgent than the last.

*BREAKING: Mass casualties reported worldwide following "Tutorial" event*

*HOSPITALS OVERWHELMED: Survivors exhibiting supernatural abilities*

*GOVERNMENT ISSUES EMERGENCY STATEMENT: "Remain Calm"*

Kai's blood ran cold.

He opened the first article, scanning frantically. The numbers were staggering. Nearly 30% of the global population hadn't survived their tutorials. Not from combat—from *failure*. The System had given everyone a chance, but not everyone had been ready. Not everyone had been capable.

His hands trembled as he thought about his mother. About his sister. About everyone he'd ever known.

*They're fine,* he told himself fiercely. *They have to be fine.*

He dialed his mother's number. It rang once. Twice. Three times—

"Kai? Kai, is that you?"

Her voice was shaky, but alive. Kai let out a breath he hadn't realized he'd been holding.

"Mom. I'm okay. Are you okay? What happened to you?"

"I—I don't know how to explain it." She sounded dazed. "I was at work, and then suddenly I wasn't. I was in this garden. Beautiful place. Full of flowers I've never seen before. And there was a woman there—no, not a woman. Something that looked like one. She told me I was a Healer now. That I had to learn to channel something called... mana?"

Kai's grip on the phone tightened. "Did she hurt you?"

"No. She was patient. Kind, even. She taught me how to make a seed grow just by touching it. I thought I was dreaming." A pause. "But when I woke up, there were roses growing out of my office carpet."

*Healer.* Of course. His mother had always been gentle, nurturing. The System had recognized that.

"Stay inside," Kai said. "Don't go anywhere. I'm coming to get you."

"Kai, what's happening? The news says—"

"I know what the news says. Just stay put. I'll be there in an hour."

He hung up before she could argue.

---

Getting out of his apartment building was like stepping into a war zone.

The streets were chaos. Cars abandoned at odd angles, their drivers either dead or transformed. People running, screaming, laughing, crying—the full spectrum of human reaction to reality being torn apart and reassembled. Sirens wailed in the distance, growing closer and then fainter as emergency vehicles struggled to navigate the gridlock.

Kai moved through it all with a strange sense of detachment. His qi sense painted the world in colors he'd never seen before, highlighting the life forces of everyone around him. Most were dim, ordinary. But some—

A man across the street was wreathed in crimson energy, his fists crackling with barely-contained power. A woman three blocks away had a presence that felt like water, flowing and shifting. And somewhere in the distance, there was something *else*. Something that made Kai's dantian pulse with warning.

*Not all classes are equal,* Mentor had said. *And not all who receive them are worthy.*

He pushed the thought aside and kept moving.

---

The subway was out. The buses weren't running. The city had ground to a halt, its infrastructure unable to cope with the sudden shift in reality. So Kai walked.

It took him two hours to reach his mother's apartment, navigating through crowds of confused and frightened people, stepping over debris from looted stores, and avoiding the occasional fight that had broken out between newly-empowered individuals testing their abilities.

When he finally arrived, the door was unlocked.

His heart stopped.

He burst inside, qi flaring, ready for anything—

And found his mother sitting at the kitchen table, sipping tea.

"Kai!" She stood, rushing to embrace him. "Thank God you're safe. I was so worried—"

"Mom." He hugged her back, but his eyes were scanning the room. Everything was normal. Too normal. The roses growing from the carpet had been trimmed and placed in a vase. The television was off. The apartment was quiet.

"Where's Sarah?" he asked.

His mother's face flickered. "She... she hasn't answered my calls."

Kai's blood ran cold again. His sister. College student. Two years younger than him.

"Give me her number. I'll try."

He dialed. It rang. And rang. And rang.

*Voicemail. "Hey, this is Sarah. Leave a message!"*

He tried again. Same result.

"Mom, when did you last talk to her?"

"Yesterday morning. Before everything happened. She said she was going to the library to study for finals."

*Finals.* The word felt absurd now. Like something from another world.

"I'm going to find her."

"Kai, wait—"

But he was already at the door. "Stay here. Lock the door. Don't let anyone in unless it's me or Sarah. I'll be back."

---

The university was a warren of old buildings and newer additions, spread across a campus that had once been peaceful. Now it was a battlefield.

Kai arrived to find the main gates barricaded. Students had stacked desks and chairs against them, creating a makeshift fortification. Armed guards—no, not guards, *students*—patrolled the perimeter, their eyes wild and weapons clutched in trembling hands.

"Halt! Identify yourself!"

A young man stepped forward, a spear made of ice materializing in his grip. His class was obvious even without Kai's qi sense—the frost creeping from his fingers gave it away.

"My name is Kai. I'm looking for my sister. Sarah Chen."

The ice-spear wielder's eyes narrowed. "Chen? The engineering student?"

Kai's heart leaped. "Yes. You know her?"

"I know she was in the tutorial with me." The young man's expression darkened. "She didn't make it."

The words hit Kai like a physical blow. The world tilted. His qi went haywire, surging through his meridians with uncontrolled violence.

"That's not possible," he heard himself say. "She's fine. She has to be fine."

"She failed the trial." The young man's voice was flat, emotionless. "The System gave her a class—something called Scholar—but she couldn't complete the training. Her body gave out. She's in the medical building with the other casualties."

*Casualties.* Not dead. Not yet.

"Take me to her."

---

The medical building was a converted lecture hall, its seats removed to make room for rows of cots. Each cot held a person—some wounded, some unconscious, some simply... absent. Their bodies were there, but their qi signatures were faint, flickering like candles about to go out.

Kai found Sarah in the third row.

She looked peaceful. Like she was sleeping. Her chest rose and fell in a slow, steady rhythm. Her skin was warm to the touch. But her qi—

It was barely there. A thread. A whisper. The barest echo of what it should have been.

"What happened to her?" Kai asked, his voice ragged.

A girl nearby—a Healer, judging by the green glow around her hands—looked up. "The tutorial damaged her soul. Not her body. Her *soul*. The System tried to force a connection that her spirit couldn't handle. She's in a coma. We don't know how to wake her."

*Soul damage.*

The words triggered something in Kai's memory. Mentor's voice, calm and clinical: *"Soul damage is rare, but it happens. When the System forces integration with a vessel that cannot contain it, the soul fractures. Most who suffer this never recover."*

"Most," Kai repeated. "Not all. There's a way."

The Healer shook her head. "Not that we know of. The System hasn't provided any cure. She's just... stuck."

Kai stared at his sister's face. At the gentle rise and fall of her chest. At the faint, fading glow of her qi.

*There has to be something.*

And then, unbidden, a System notification appeared:

[SYSTEM ALERT: DETECTED SOUL FRAGMENT INCOMPATIBLE WITH ASSIGNED CLASS]

[OPTION UNLOCKED: SOUL WEAVING]

[DESCRIPTION: AS A DAO ARCHITECT, YOU MAY REPAIR OR RECONFIGURE SOUL STRUCTURES. WARNING: THIS PROCESS IS HIGHLY DANGEROUS AND UNTESTED. SUCCESS RATE: 37%.]

[PROCEED?]

Kai's breath caught.

*Soul Weaving.* He didn't know what it meant. He didn't know how to do it. But the System was offering him a choice.

He could save his sister.

Or he could let her fade.

His hand reached out, trembling, toward the notification.

And then the world exploded.

---

A shockwave ripped through the medical building, throwing Kai off his feet. Glass shattered. Cots overturned. Patients screamed as debris rained down around them.

Through the dust and chaos, Kai saw it:

A figure. Massive. Made of shifting, crackling energy. Standing in the center of the campus quad, its form barely contained by reality itself.

[SYSTEM ALERT: WORLD BOSS DETECTED]

[CLASS: ???]

[LEVEL: ???]

[WARNING: THIS ENTITY IS OUTSIDE NORMAL PARAMETERS]

The ground shook as the creature took a step forward. Its voice, when it spoke, resonated in Kai's very soul:

*"Where is the Architect?"*

Kai's blood froze.

*It's looking for me.*

The creature's head turned. Its eyes—if they could be called eyes—locked onto the medical building. Onto him.

*"Found you."*

The first attack came as a lance of pure white light. Kai threw himself aside, dragging Sarah's cot with him. The beam struck where he'd been standing, vaporizing the floor and leaving a smoking crater.

He couldn't fight this. He couldn't even *understand* this.

But he could run.

He grabbed Sarah, ignoring the protests of his overtaxed body, and sprinted for the back exit. Behind him, the creature's laughter echoed like thunder:

*"You cannot hide, Architect. The System has chosen you. And I have chosen to consume you."*

Kai burst through the doors, his sister in his arms, the world's most dangerous enemy on his heels, and no idea how to survive.

But one thing was clear:

His sister was dying.

He had 37% chance of saving her.

And a 100% chance of being hunted.

[CHAPTER END]

[REWARD: SOUL WEAVING UNLOCKED]

[NEXT QUEST: SURVIVE THE HUNT]

title: The Culling Begins
content:
The broadcast hung in the air for exactly three seconds before the world erupted.

Kai's phone buzzed so violently it nearly jumped out of his hand. Every app, every notification, every device within range seemed to activate at once. Outside his window, car alarms blared. Someone across the street screamed. A dog howled, then fell silent.

He looked at his phone.

The group chat had exploded into panic.

*WHAT DOES THAT MEAN WORLD INTEGRATION*

*My class isn't showing up anymore*

*Bro my skills are GONE*

*THE SYSTEM IS GLITCHING*

*No wait my status screen is back but everything's different*

Kai's blood ran cold. He pulled up his own status:

[Name: Kai Chen]
[Class: Dao Architect]
[Level: 1]
[Qi Cultivation: Meridian Awakening (Stage 1)]
[Health: 100%]
[Warning: World Integration in Progress. Local reality parameters adjusting.]

*Local reality parameters adjusting.*

"What the hell does that mean?" he muttered.

His phone buzzed again. A new message from an unknown number:

*Kai. It's Mentor.*

He nearly dropped the phone.

*How did you get this number?*

*I have access to all System-linked devices now. Listen carefully. World Integration is not a metaphor. The System is merging Earth's reality with the cultivation plane that the tutorial was drawn from. This was always the plan, but the timeline has accelerated.*

*Accelerated why?*

A pause. Then:

*Because of you.*

Kai's blood went cold.

*Me?*

*Your class. The Dao Architect. The System detected an anomaly it could not classify, and it triggered a full audit of the integration protocol. The audit found inconsistencies. The Overseers decided to push forward rather than risk exposure. You are a variable they cannot control, Kai. And the Overseers do not tolerate variables.*

The ground beneath his feet *lurched*.

Not physically—spiritually. Kai felt it in his bones, in his meridians, in the qi that now flowed through his body like a second circulatory system. The world *shifted*, as if someone had rotated reality by three degrees.

Outside, the sky flickered.

One moment it was morning, pale blue with thin clouds. The next, it was a bruised purple, shot through with veins of silver light. Then it snapped back to normal, but the silver veins remained, faint and pulsing, like the sky itself had been scarred.

"Screw this."

Kai grabbed his jacket and ran.

---

The streets were chaos.

People stood in clusters, staring at their phones, at the sky, at each other. A woman was sobbing on the sidewalk, her hands pressed against her face while a man tried to comfort her. Two teenagers were arguing loudly about whether this was "part of the game." A car had crashed into a lamppost, its driver staring blankly at the dashboard as if seeing it for the first time.

Kai moved through them like a ghost, his qi sense extended, mapping the spiritual topography of the city.

It was *wrong*.

The residual qi that had clung to electronics, to buildings, to people—it was thickening. Coalescing. The air itself felt heavier, charged with something that made his skin prickle and his dantian hum with recognition.

*The plane is bleeding into Earth,* Mentor had said during the tutorial. *The System is the bridge.*

He'd thought she meant it metaphorically.

He'd been wrong.

A scream tore through the air, sharp and terrified. Kai spun, tracking the sound to an alley between two apartment buildings. He ran.

The alley was narrow, cluttered with trash bins and discarded furniture. At its center, a woman in her thirties stood frozen, her eyes wide, her hand pressed to her mouth.

In front of her, a *thing* was forming.

It looked like a crack in reality—a vertical seam of darkness that pulsed with a sickly violet light. The edges of the crack were ragged, bleeding color into the air like smoke. From within, something was pushing.

Kai saw a hand.

Long, skeletal, with too many joints. It pressed against the crack from the other side, fingers spreading, and the violet light intensified.

"Get back," Kai said, stepping forward.

The woman didn't move. She was in shock, her body locked, her mind unable to process what she was seeing.

The hand pushed harder. The crack widened. A face pressed against the gap—a face that was almost human, but wrong in every proportion. Too-wide eyes, no nose, a mouth that stretched from ear to ear, filled with needle-thin teeth.

It *smiled* at Kai.

And then it spoke, its voice like grinding stone: *"The Architect. The Overseers send their regards."*

Kai moved.

He didn't plan it. He didn't think. His body reacted with the instincts he'd honed during those six hours in the tutorial, his qi flaring as he channeled it into his palm and *pushed*.

A wave of golden energy slammed into the crack.

The creature recoiled, hissing, its too-wide eyes narrowing with hatred. The violet light flickered. The crack began to close, but slowly, reluctantly, as if reality itself was fighting to heal a wound that didn't want to be healed.

"You can't stop it," the creature hissed. "The integration is complete. The culling has begun. Every soul on this world will be harvested—"

Kai hit it again.

This time, he didn't hold back. He drew deep from his dantian, pulling the qi that Mentor had taught him to cultivate, and he *released* it in a single, focused burst.

The crack snapped shut.

The creature's scream was cut off mid-word, and the alley fell silent.

Kai stood there, breathing hard, his hands shaking, the golden light fading from his palms. The woman behind him let out a choked sob and collapsed to her knees.

He turned.

"Are you okay?"

She looked up at him, her eyes wet with tears. "What... what was that?"

"I don't know yet." He offered her his hand. "But I'm going to find out."

---

His phone buzzed again as he helped the woman to her feet. Mentor.

*The first breach was a test. They wanted to confirm your location and your capabilities.*

Kai's jaw tightened. *They know where I am?*

*Yes. And now they know what you can do. You have approximately twelve hours before they send something larger. Something designed specifically to counter you.*

*What am I supposed to do in twelve hours?*

*You need to reach the Nexus Point. It's a convergence of ley lines beneath the city. From there, you can access the System's core architecture and—*

Another message cut in. This one from an unknown sender, marked with a crimson icon he'd never seen before.

[SYSTEM NOTIFICATION: PLAYER-KILL EVENT ACTIVATED]

[DESIGNATED TARGET: KAI CHEN (DAO ARCHITECT)]

[REWARD: RECLASSIFICATION TO LEGENDARY CLASS + 100,000 SYSTEM CREDITS]

[ALL PLAYERS WITHIN 50KM RADIUS NOTIFIED]

Kai stared at the notification.

Ten thousand players. In a fifty-kilometer radius.

All of them now had a bounty on his head.

His phone buzzed again. The group chat.

*Bro did you guys see the PK event?*

*Some guy named Kai Chen. 100k credits. That's insane.*

*Anyone know who that is?*

Kai's hand tightened around the phone.

A new message appeared from Mentor:

*I told you. The Overseers do not tolerate variables.*

*Run, Kai. And don't stop running until you reach the Nexus.*

He pocketed the phone and looked up at the scarred sky, the silver veins still pulsing like the heartbeat of a dying world.

Twelve hours.

Ten thousand hunters.

And somewhere beneath the city, a door to the System's core.

He started running.

title: The First Fracture
content:
Kai's finger hovered over the send button when the world went white.

Not the gentle dissolution of Mentor's tutorial chamber. This was violent—a detonation of light that blasted through his apartment walls like they were paper. He threw up an arm, qi flaring instinctively, and felt the shockwave ripple through his newly-formed meridians.

When his vision cleared, he was standing in a void.

Not empty. The void was filled with text. Millions, billions of lines of code cascading around him like waterfalls of light. Each character pulsed with meaning he could almost grasp, like words caught in the corner of his eye that vanished when he tried to look directly at them.

[SYSTEM ALPHA PROTOCOL ACTIVATED]

The notification hung before him, massive and golden.

[PURPOSE: STRESS TEST]

[SUBJECTS: ALL REGISTERED ENTITIES]

[ANTICIPATED CASUALTIES: <0.01%]

Kai's blood ran cold. "Anticipated casualties?"

But the System didn't answer. The code around him began to fragment, shattering like glass, and he was falling—

—into a forest.

Tall pines stretched toward a sky that looked wrong. The colors were too saturated, the clouds moving too fast, the sun casting shadows in directions that didn't match. Kai landed on soft moss, his knees absorbing the impact, and immediately extended his qi sense.

The forest was alive. But not with animals. With *players*.

He sensed them—dozens, hundreds, scattered through the trees. Their qi signatures were bright but crude, raw power without refinement. Warriors flexing muscles that shouldn't exist. Mages channeling mana through pathways that would scar their souls if they kept pushing. Healers knitting flesh together with techniques that treated symptoms, not causes.

They were all going to kill themselves.

"Beautiful, isn't it?"

Kai spun. A figure stood twenty feet away, leaning against a pine with casual grace. Young—early twenties, maybe—with sharp features and eyes that held a glint of System gold.

"You feel it," the stranger said. "The wrongness in their foundations. The cracks that will spread."

"Who are you?"

"Someone who got a class like yours." The stranger pushed off the tree, approaching with hands in his pockets. "Architect. Designer. Builder. The System doesn't know what to call us, so it calls us *anomalies*."

Kai's qi sense brushed against the stranger—and recoiled. The man's dantian was a fortress, walls of crystallized qi so dense they felt solid. Within it, something coiled. Waiting.

"I'm not your enemy," the stranger said. "But I'm not your friend either. I'm a warning."

"A warning for what?"

The ground shook. A kilometer away, a pillar of light erupted from the forest, punching through the wrong-colored sky. The players closest to it screamed—a sound that cut off abruptly.

"Stress test," the stranger said. "The System has integrated Earth. Now it needs to see how we hold. So it's going to throw everything at us. Monsters. Dungeons. Events." His smile didn't reach his eyes. "And it's going to start with forcing us to fight each other."

The pillar of light spread, rippling outward like a shockwave. As it passed, the players' qi signatures changed. Their colors shifted—from neutral whites and blues to aggressive reds and oranges.

"They're being flagged," Kai breathed.

"PvP-enabled. For the next hour, anyone in this zone can kill anyone else. No penalties. No restrictions. Just experience points." The stranger stepped back. "You should run. They're going to come for you first."

"Why me?"

"Because you're an anomaly. The System doesn't like things it can't categorize. And right now, you're the brightest, most *uncategorized* thing in this forest." He vanished, his qi signature winking out like a snuffed candle. His voice echoed: "Good luck, Architect. You're going to need it."

Kai didn't wait. He ran.

The forest blurred past as he pushed qi through his legs, enhancing his speed. His lungs burned with the unfamiliar exertion—six hours of cultivation couldn't prepare him for this. But he'd learned the meridian paths, and his body remembered the flows even if his mind hadn't caught up.

A fireball exploded ten feet to his left, showering him with splinters.

"Got one!" A voice, young and excited. "He's running!"

Kai veered right, ducking behind an oak as big around as a car. Three players crashed through the underbrush—a Warrior in makeshift leather armor, a Mage with lightning crackling between her fingers, and a Scout with eyes that glowed white.

"Come out, come out," the Scout sang. "We just want to test our new skills."

Kai pressed against the bark, thinking fast. He had no combat techniques. No spells. No weapons. Just qi—and a half-formed understanding of meridians that Mentor had called "unstable."

But he had something else. He could *see* them. Not their bodies—their foundations.

The Warrior's qi flowed like sludge through his channels, blocked in three places. The Mage's mana was bright but thin, drawn from external sources rather than internal cultivation. The Scout was the most stable, but her qi sense was underdeveloped, blind to anything that didn't broadcast loudly.

Kai realized: they couldn't sense him. Not truly. Their qi sense was a blunt instrument, good for locating "enemies" but useless for perceiving the subtle flows of cultivation.

He had an advantage. A small one. He'd take it.

Instead of running, Kai moved *toward* them—slowly, carefully, stepping where the moss would muffle his footfalls. He circled around, using the trees as cover, and emerged behind the Warrior.

The man was scanning forward, his axe raised, his back completely exposed.

Kai reached out and pressed two fingers to the base of his spine. He pushed a thread of qi into the man's most blocked meridian.

The Warrior seized. His muscles locked, his eyes went wide, and he collapsed like a puppet with cut strings. Not dead—Mentor had been clear that qi attacks on blocked meridians could paralyze, not kill—but definitely out of the fight.

"What the—" The Mage spun, lightning gathering in her palm.

Kai was already moving. He closed the distance in three steps, his qi-enhanced speed making him a blur. He caught her wrist, redirected the lightning discharge into the ground, and pressed his palm to her sternum. Another meridian block, this one in her chest.

She gasped, stumbled, and fell.

The Scout was faster. She'd already activated some kind of speed enhancement, her body flickering as she drew a dagger. "You're not a normal class," she hissed.

"No," Kai agreed. "I'm not."

He didn't attack her. He attacked the ground at her feet, sending a pulse of qi through the earth. The moss rippled, roots twisted, and she lost her footing. Before she could recover, he was on her, fingers finding the meridian at her temple.

She went limp.

Kai stood over three unconscious players, his heart hammering, his hands shaking. He'd just disabled three people with nothing but qi manipulation. People who, until six hours ago, had been normal civilians like him.

*This is what I've become.*

The ground shook again. More pillars of light erupted across the forest—five, ten, a dozen. The stress test was expanding, covering more ground, pulling more players into the PvP zone.

And in the distance, something *big* was moving. Its qi signature was enormous, chaotic, nothing like the refined energy Mentor had taught him to recognize. It felt like a wound in the world, leaking power in all directions.

[STRESS TEST OBJECTIVE UPDATED]

The notification appeared, cold and clinical.

[BOSS SPAWN DETECTED]

[DEFEAT THE FRACTURED ASPECT TO END THE TEST]

[FAILURE CONDITION: ALL PLAYERS ELIMINATED]

[REWARD FOR COMPLETION: UNIQUE CLASS UPGRADE]

Kai stared at the notification. Unique class upgrade. For an anomaly like him, that could be anything. A path forward. A way to stabilize his cultivation before the 2% odds caught up to him.

Or it could be a trap. The stranger's words echoed: *The System doesn't like things it can't categorize.*

"What choice do I have?" Kai muttered.

He turned toward the approaching chaos and began to run.

The forest ended abruptly at a cliff's edge. Below, in a valley that had definitely not existed before the stress test began, the Fractured Aspect was rising from the earth. It looked like a man made of broken mirrors—shards of glass and light and distorted flesh assembled into a vaguely humanoid shape. Its face was a mosaic of screaming mouths, each one voicing a different sound.

Players were already engaging it. Warriors charged, Mages cast, Healers tried to keep everyone alive. But their attacks passed through the Aspect like light through smoke, and its own attacks—fractured beams of concentrated System energy—cut through them like a scythe through wheat.

Kai watched a Warrior get bisected. The man's body dissolved into particles, his system notification appearing a moment later:

[PLAYER REVIVAL IN PROGRESS]

[ESTIMATED TIME: 72 HOURS]

Seventy-two hours. Dead for three days. The *anticipated casualties* stat suddenly made a lot more sense.

"Their attacks aren't landing because they're attacking the wrong thing," Kai said to himself. "It's not a physical entity. It's a System construct."

And he was a Dao Architect.

Mentor's voice echoed in his memory: *The System is the Heavenly Dao reborn as code. And you, Architect, are the one who can read the source.*

Kai closed his eyes. He extended his qi sense—not at the Aspect's body, but *through* it. Past the shards of glass and light, past the screaming mouths, into the core of its existence.

There. A pattern. A code structure that pulsed like a heartbeat, surrounded by layers of protection.

[ANOMALY DETECTED]

The notification appeared in front of him, blood-red.

[SYSTEM PROTOCOL: CLASSIFY AND CONTAIN]

[DAO ARCHITECT: RECOGNIZED AS THREAT]

The Aspect's head swiveled. All its mouths stopped screaming at once. And it looked directly at Kai.

"Found you," it said, in a voice that was a thousand voices speaking as one. "The System has been looking for you."

The ground beneath Kai's feet shattered.

He fell—not down, but *up*, tumbling through a space that had no gravity, no direction, no sense. The Aspect was following, its shard-body reassembling around him, trapping him in a cage of fractured light.

"You are an error," the Aspect said. "A bug in the System code. And bugs must be patched."

Kai's dantian pulsed. The qi Mentor had painstakingly guided through his meridians began to churn, agitated by the Aspect's proximity. His channels burned, the 2% odds suddenly feeling very, very real.

"You can't kill me," Kai said, forcing the words through gritted teeth. "The System doesn't understand what I am. It can't classify me. So it can't delete me."

The Aspect paused. For a moment, Kai saw uncertainty flicker across its mosaic face.

"Correct," it admitted. "I cannot delete you. But I can still *hurt* you."

It raised a

title: The Fractured Sky
content:
Kai's finger hovered over the send button when the world *screamed*.

The sound wasn't audible—it was spiritual, a dissonant chord that ripped through his qi sense like glass shards through flesh. He doubled over, clutching his head as his newly-formed meridians spasmed in protest. The cockroach behind his wall died instantly, its life force snuffing out like a candle in a hurricane.

Outside his window, the sky was breaking.

Kai stumbled to his feet, ignoring the agony lancing through his dantian. Through the grime-coated glass, he watched the heavens crack open like an eggshell. Golden light poured through the fissures, not sunlight but something older, something that resonated with the qi in his core.

*The System's foundation,* Mentor's voice echoed in his memory. *Beneath it lies the Dao.*

The cracks spread. People screamed in the streets below. A woman's car swerved and crashed into a fire hydrant. A man stood frozen, his Warrior-class aura flaring wildly as golden light washed over him.

Then the fragments fell.

Not physical pieces—shards of light, each one no larger than a coin, drifting down like apocalyptic snowflakes. They landed on buildings, on cars, on people. Where they touched, reality *shifted*.

Kai watched a shard embed itself in a streetlamp. The metal rippled, twisted, and *grew*. Within seconds, the lamp had transformed into a crystalline pillar etched with runes that pulsed with qi. Another shard struck a passing dog. The animal yelped, its form blurring, and when the light faded, it stood twice its original size with fur that gleamed like polished steel.

"What the hell—" Kai's phone buzzed furiously. He glanced down.

*Jake: BRO THE SKY IS BREAKING*

*Mark: Is this part of the tutorial? Is anyone else seeing this?*

*Unknown Number: Kai. If you can read this, do not absorb any golden light. Do not touch it. Do not let it touch you.*

Kai's blood ran cold. He looked up. A shard was drifting directly toward his window, spinning lazily, beautiful and terrible.

He threw himself sideways.

The shard passed through the glass without breaking it—passed *through* as if the glass were mist—and embedded itself in his kitchen counter. The cheap laminate rippled. When the transformation finished, he was staring at a countertop of living wood, veins of gold running through it like rivers on a map.

His doorbell rang.

Kai's qi sense flared automatically, a reflex he hadn't known he'd developed. Three people stood in the hallway. Two of them radiated the raw, untamed energy of newly-Assigned classes—a Warrior and a Mage, probably Level 1 or 2. But the third...

The third felt *wrong*.

Not evil. Not hostile. Just *wrong*, like a note played slightly off-key in an otherwise perfect symphony. Their qi signature flickered and shifted, never settling into a stable pattern.

"Kai Chen?" The voice was female, calm, professional. "We need to talk."

He approached the door slowly, keeping his qi sense extended. The Warrior shifted his weight, hand hovering near a holster that definitely hadn't existed before the System arrived. The Mage crackled with barely-contained energy, sparks dancing between her fingers.

The woman in the middle wore a suit. No aura. No visible weapon. Just the flickering, unsettled qi that made Kai's instincts scream.

"I don't know you," he said through the door.

"My name is Director Zhao. I represent a... recently formed government task force." A pause. "We're compiling information on Class Assignments. Particularly outliers."

Outliers. His stomach tightened.

"I got a standard class," Kai said. "Just a—"

"I watched your tutorial."

The words hit him like a physical blow.

"You don't have a tutorial chamber," Zhao continued. "The protocol is standard across all Assignments. But yours... yours was different. The energy signatures from your apartment didn't match any known tutorial configuration. And your Class doesn't appear in the System registry."

*The System doesn't understand your existence,* Mentor had said.

"I don't know what you're talking about."

"Open the door, Mr. Chen. We can do this the easy way, or we can do it with the Authority I've been granted by the newly-formed Integration Response Bureau." Her voice hardened. "Choose."

Kai's mind raced. He could fight—his qi was unstable, but it was *his*. He could run—the fire escape was right outside his bedroom window. He could—

Another System notification flashed.

[WARNING: QI DEVIATION DETECTED]
[STRESS LEVELS EXCEED SAFE THRESHOLDS]
[RECOMMENDATION: IMMEDIATE MEDITATION OR MEDICAL INTERVENTION]

His dantian throbbed. The golden light outside pulsed in rhythm with his heartbeat. The shards were still falling, still transforming the city into something that wasn't quite Earth anymore.

Kai made a decision.

He opened the door.

Director Zhao was younger than he'd expected—mid-thirties, sharp features, eyes that missed nothing. She looked at him the way Mentor had looked at him: like he was a puzzle to be solved.

"Thank you, Mr. Chen." She stepped past him into the apartment, her two agents flanking the door. "I appreciate your cooperation."

"I didn't agree to cooperate."

"You opened the door." She smiled, but it didn't reach her eyes. "That's a start."

Her gaze swept the apartment, landing on the transformed counter. She walked over, ran her fingers along the living wood, and nodded as if confirming something she'd already suspected.

"The shards. They're affecting inorganic matter differently than organic. This counter has been... enhanced. It's not just wood—it's spiritually reactive. In a year, maybe less, this could grow into something that produces qi-attuned fruit." She turned to face him. "The world is changing, Mr. Chen. Faster than anyone anticipated. And people like you—people with anomalous Classes—are going to be at the center of it."

"People like me?"

"The Architect class doesn't exist in any System database. We checked. Every other class—Warrior, Mage, Healer, Crafter, even the rare ones like Summoner and Seer—they all have entries. They all have progression trees, skill lists, stat growth formulas." She paused. "Yours is blank."

Kai said nothing.

"Which means either the System is incomplete, which is terrifying, or you're not actually part of the System, which is even more terrifying." Zhao's eyes bore into his. "So I'll ask you directly: What did the tutorial teach you?"

The question hung in the air. Outside, the golden rain continued to fall, transforming the world one shard at a time. Kai could feel the qi in the air growing thicker, richer, more *alive* with every passing second.

*Your foundation is forming,* Mentor had said. *Not yet stable, but forming.*

"I don't know what you're talking about," Kai repeated.

Zhao sighed. "I was hoping you'd be reasonable."

She snapped her fingers.

The Warrior moved first—blazing fast, Level 2 stats making him a blur of motion. But Kai's qi sense caught the attack before it began. He sidestepped, not thinking, just *moving*, his body flowing with the qi that now suffused his meridians.

The Warrior's fist passed through empty air.

"Interesting." Zhao watched with clinical detachment. "Enhanced reflexes. Not a skill—there's no activation signature. This is passive." She made a note on a tablet that materialized from nowhere. "Untrained, unLeveled, and already displaying combat capabilities. Remarkable."

The Mage raised her hand, fire gathering in her palm.

*No,* Kai thought. *I'm not fighting. I'm not becoming a lab rat.*

He ran.

Not toward the fire escape—they'd expect that. Instead, he dove through his bedroom door, rolled across the bed, and slammed his palm against the wall. His qi surged, responding to his intent, and the cheap drywall *parted* like water.

He fell through into his neighbor's apartment.

An elderly woman screamed. Kai scrambled to his feet, apologies already forming on his lips, but he didn't have time. He burst through her front door into the hallway, then down the stairs, taking them three at a time.

*The Bureau,* Zhao had called it. *Integration Response Bureau.*

How many of them were there? How many people had been pulled from their homes, questioned, studied? The System had been live for less than a day, and already the governments of the world were mobilizing.

He hit the ground floor and burst through the building's entrance into chaos.

The streets were unrecognizable.

Golden shards had transformed the neighborhood into a patchwork of reality and something else. A convenience store now had walls of crystallized light. A parked bus had grown organic protrusions, pulsing with slow, rhythmic breaths. People wandered in dazed circles, some clutching newly-enhanced objects, others staring at their own transformed hands.

And in the center of it all, the sky continued to fracture.

Kai's phone buzzed again. He risked a glance.

*Unknown Number: You're being hunted. Not just by the Bureau. By others. The anomalies are few, and the powerful want to control them.*

*Unknown Number: I can help. But you need to get out of the city.*

*Unknown Number: Trust no one. Not even yourself.*

The messages vanished. When Kai tried to scroll back, they were gone—erased as if they'd never existed.

He looked up.

A new crack was forming in the sky. Bigger than the others. Brighter. And from it, something was descending.

Not golden light.

A figure.

Humanoid. Radiant. Wreathed in flames that burned with qi so pure it made Kai's dantian sing. The figure descended slowly, deliberately, like a god stepping down from its throne to walk among mortals.

*Mentor.*

The realization hit him at the same time as the shockwave. The golden figure landed in the middle of the street, and the ground *rippled*, sending everyone—Kai included—sprawling.

When he looked up, she was staring directly at him.

"The Bureau will reach you in thirty seconds," she said, her voice echoing with harmonics that shouldn't exist. "What you do next will determine the fate of your world."

She extended her hand.

"Choose, Architect. Come with me, and learn what the System fears. Stay, and become a tool of those who would control it."

Behind him, Kai heard the pounding of boots. The Warrior and Mage from his apartment, plus at least a dozen more, all converging on his position.

The golden rain continued to fall.

The sky continued to break.

And Kai stood at the crossroads of two worlds, neither of which wanted him to choose for himself.

He took a breath.

And made his decision.

title: The First Fracture
content:
Kai stared at the broadcast notification until it faded, his thumb hovering over the unsent message. Something was wrong. The timing, the wording—it felt off, like a note played slightly flat.

He sent the message anyway, then swung his legs over the side of the bed. His body protested, muscles screaming from the six-hour cultivation session. But beneath the pain, something hummed. Energy. Potential. The qi in his dantian pulsed like a second heart.

*Two point three percent chance of death.*

He pushed the thought aside and stood.

The apartment looked exactly as he'd left it—cluttered, cramped, depressingly normal. But through his qi sense, everything had changed. The refrigerator hummed with a thin, artificial energy. The wiring in the walls crackled with something that wasn't electricity. Even the water from his tap carried traces of qi, diluted and corrupted by the pipes it traveled through.

*The System isn't just overlaying reality. It's replacing it.*

His phone buzzed again. Then again. Then a cascade of notifications that turned the screen into a strobe light. Kai grabbed it, squinting at the flood of messages.

*Did you see the broadcast? What does "World Integration" mean?*

*My class changed. I was a Healer. Now I'm a "Life Channel." What the hell?*

*Kai, check the news. NOW.*

He opened his browser. The headlines hit him like a physical blow:

**"CLASS SHIFTING EPIDEMIC SWEEPS GLOBE"**

**"37% OF INITIALIZED USERS REPORT CLASS CHANGES"**

**"SYSTEM EXPERTS BAFFLED: 'THIS SHOULDN'T BE POSSIBLE'"**

His blood ran cold. Class shifting. It shouldn't be possible—the System was supposed to be absolute, deterministic. Your class was your identity, coded into your soul by the Heavenly Dao itself.

*Unless the Dao changed its mind.*

He clicked through to a live feed from Tokyo. A teenager stood in the middle of a destroyed street, his hands crackling with energy that shifted colors every few seconds—red, blue, green, black. His eyes were wild, unfocused.

"He can't stop," the reporter said, voice shaking. "His class keeps changing every thirty seconds. Each transformation brings new abilities, but they're destroying his body. Medical teams can't get close."

The teenager screamed. His skin began to crack, light pouring through the fissures like magma through volcanic rock.

Kai turned off the video.

His phone rang. Unknown number. He almost declined, but something made him answer.

"Kai Chen."

The voice was calm, measured, female. Professional. "This is Director Zhao from the Global System Response Initiative. We need to speak with you about your class classification."

"How did you get my number?"

"We have access to all initialized users' data. Your file flagged our systems immediately." A pause. "You're one of seventeen people worldwide whose class registered as 'unclassified.' Do you know what that means?"

Seventeen. Out of billions. Kai's grip tightened on the phone.

"It means the System doesn't know what to do with me."

"It means you're an anomaly. And anomalies are either the key to understanding this new world—or the first signs of a collapse." Director Zhao's voice hardened. "We need you to come in for evaluation. For your safety and others'."

"I'll think about it."

"Mr. Chen, I don't think you understand the gravity—"

He hung up.

The phone rang again immediately. He silenced it and tossed it onto the bed, pacing the length of his small apartment. Seventeen unclassified users. Seventeen people who'd slipped through the cracks of the Heavenly Dao's new system.

*Or seventeen people the System was designed around.*

The thought came unbidden, and once it arrived, he couldn't shake it. What if the unclassified weren't bugs? What if they were features—intentional exceptions built into the System's code?

*Mentor knew about my class. She said it was "unprecedented," but she wasn't surprised.*

Another notification appeared:

[SYSTEM UPDATE: 2.1.4 PATCH NOTES]
- STABILIZED CLASS BINDING PROTOCOLS
- REDUCED CLASS SHIFTING INCIDENTS BY 23%
- RESOLVED MERIDIAN ACTIVATION CONFLICTS WITH EXISTING CLASS FRAMEWORKS
- NEW FEATURE: USERS MAY NOW ACCESS SYSTEM MERCHANT FUNCTIONS AT LEVEL 10

Kai read the patch notes three times. The language was familiar—System update documentation for a software deployment. But the implications were staggering. The System could be patched. Modified. Updated like a phone app.

*Who's writing the patches?*

His phone buzzed with a text from his gaming group chat:

*Jake: Anyone else's skills feel... different? My Warrior abilities don't work right anymore*

*Mark: Same. My potion recipes changed. Ingredients I used yesterday don't work today*

*Lena: My Fireball now does ice damage. ICE. I'm a Pyromancer*

Kai's blood ran cold. The class shifting wasn't the problem—it was the symptom. The System was rewriting itself, and in the process, it was breaking the connections between users and their abilities.

*The Heavenly Dao is unstable.*

He needed answers. He needed Mentor.

Closing his eyes, Kai reached inward, searching for the golden thread of qi that connected him to the tutorial space. He found it—faint, but present, pulsing at the edge of his consciousness like a distant star.

*Mentor. I need to speak with you.*

Silence.

He pushed harder, funneling qi into the connection. The thread brightened, vibrated, and then—

*A fracture.*

The golden thread split, fragmenting into a dozen smaller strands that whipped and twisted in chaotic patterns. Pain lanced through his consciousness, and Kai gasped, clutching his head as the world swam.

[WARNING: TUTORIAL CONNECTION DESTABILIZED]
[ERROR CODE: 0x7E3A - CHANNEL FRAGMENTATION]
[RECOMMENDATION: CEASE ALL EXTERNAL COMMUNICATION ATTEMPTS]

"What the hell?"

The connection wasn't just broken—it was being attacked. Something was actively severing his link to the tutorial, shredding the qi pathways with surgical precision.

*No. No, I need that link.*

He forced his qi into a spiral, trying to stabilize the fragmented threads. It was like trying to hold together a rope that was unweaving from both ends simultaneously. Each thread he caught slipped through his fingers, dissolving into golden mist.

Then, through the chaos, a whisper:

*"Kai... can't hold... they're... closing..."*

Mentor's voice, crackling and distorted, barely audible over the static of a collapsing connection.

"Mentor! What's happening?"

*"...outside... interference... not part of... original framework... someone..."*

The word cut off as the last thread snapped. The golden connection vanished, leaving nothing but a void where it had been. Kai's eyes snapped open, his breath ragged, sweat pouring down his face.

His phone was ringing again.

He grabbed it, expecting Director Zhao. But the caller ID showed an unknown number with a country code he didn't recognize.

He answered.

"Kai Chen." The voice was different this time—male, older, with an accent he couldn't place. "I know what you experienced. The tutorial. The mentor. The fractures."

"Who is this?"

"Someone who received the same class as you. One of the seventeen." A pause. "They're hunting us. The ones who control the System. They're eliminating the anomalies."

"Eliminating?"

"You felt your connection break. That wasn't an error—that was an assassination attempt. They severed your link to the tutorial, hoping it would cause qi deviation. You survived, which means you're stronger than most. But it won't stop."

Kai's hand trembled. "How do you know all this?"

"Because I've already lost three of the seventeen. Two to qi deviation, one to a 'random' monster attack that should have been impossible at her level." The voice hardened. "The System isn't a game, Kai. It's a cage. And someone is very determined to make sure the lock picks never see the light of day."

Silence stretched between them.

"What do you want me to do?"

"Meet me. There's a place where the System's influence is weaker—a dead zone, created by the clash between the new framework and the old reality. I'll send you coordinates. Come alone. Trust no one."

The line went dead.

Kai stared at his phone, the weight of everything crashing down on him. The class shifting. The broken connection. The seventeen anomalies being hunted.

*Trust no one.*

But how could he trust a stranger who called out of nowhere, who knew about his class, who claimed to be hunted by the very System that now governed reality?

He looked at the message that appeared on his screen:

*Coordinates incoming. You have 24 hours before they find you. Choose wisely.*

Below it, another notification:

[SYSTEM ALERT: ANOMALOUS USER DETECTED]
[INITIATING PROTOCOL: RECLAMATION PHASE 1]
[ESTIMATED TIME TO RESOLUTION: 23:47:12]

Kai's blood turned to ice.

They already knew.

They were already coming.

And he had less than twenty-four hours to decide if the stranger on the phone was his salvation—or another trap.

title: The First Fracture
content:
Kai's fingers hovered over his phone as the System broadcast faded. The world felt wrong. Not the physical world—that remained stubbornly normal, with its cracked ceilings and threadbare sheets—but the space between things. The spiritual architecture of reality itself seemed to hum with a frequency that hadn't been there before.

He swung his legs over the side of the bed and stood. The movement felt different. Lighter. His muscles moved with a precision he'd never possessed, his joints no longer creaking in protest. Seven hours of qi circulation had done more for his body than twenty-two years of neglect.

His phone buzzed again.

*Kai, answer me. What class?*

It was Marcus, his old raid leader from their WoW days. The guy who'd spent twelve hours straight theorycrafting optimal DPS rotations. If anyone would understand a weird class, it was him.

Kai typed: *Dao Architect. Unclassified. System doesn't have a category for it.*

Three dots appeared immediately.

*What does it do?*

*It's complicated. My tutorial taught me cultivation instead of skills.*

*Cultivation? Like xianxia novels?*

*Exactly like xianxia novels.*

A long pause. Then: *Bro, that's either OP as hell or a death sentence. There's no middle ground with that genre.*

Kai snorted. Trust Marcus to analyze cosmic horror through the lens of game mechanics.

Before he could reply, a second System notification slammed into his consciousness:

[SYSTEM UPDATE: WORLD INTEGRATION STAGE 2 INITIATED]

[FEATURE UNLOCKED: MANA COLLAPSE]

[ALL CLASSES NOW REQUIRE MANA TO USE ABILITIES]

[MANA RECOVERY RATE: BASED ON ENVIRONMENTAL MANA DENSITY]

[MANA DEPLETION: ALL EXISTING SKILL ENERGY CONVERTED TO MANA]

Kai's blood ran cold.

He reached out with his qi sense, feeling the world's energy shift like tectonic plates grinding against each other. The thin, artificial qi he'd noticed in his electronics suddenly vanished, replaced by something thinner. More brittle.

*This isn't right.*

His phone exploded. Every group chat, every message thread, every social media platform he'd ever touched was flooding with panicked notifications.

*My mana bar just appeared and it's EMPTY.*

*I was charging a Fireball and it just fizzled. What the hell?*

*Anyone else's skills not working?*

*Marcus, you seeing this?* Kai typed.

*Yeah. My mana's at 2% and recovering at 0.1 per minute. At this rate, it'll take sixteen hours to cast ONE spell. This is a nerf of catastrophic proportions.*

Kai's mind raced. The System had designed classes around skills, around mana pools, around cooldowns and resource management. It was a game system, through and through. But cultivation didn't use mana. It used qi. And qi wasn't subject to System mechanics.

He concentrated, cycling a thread of qi through his meridians. The familiar warmth spread through his body, his dantian pulsing with steady rhythm.

No resistance. No mana cost. No cooldown.

*I think my class is immune,* he typed.

*WHAT?*

*Cultivation doesn't use mana. It generates its own energy.*

The response was immediate: *That's broken. That's absolutely broken. Everyone else is crippled and you're over here playing on god mode.*

But Kai didn't feel like he was playing anything. He felt like he was standing on a fault line while the earth shifted beneath his feet.

Another notification appeared, this one private:

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: CLASS REBALANCE DETECTED]

[DAO ARCHITECT: STATUS UNCHANGED]

[NOTE: SYSTEM CANNOT MODIFY CLASS ARCHITECTURE]

[NOTE: CLASS ARCHITECTURE EXISTS OUTSIDE SYSTEM PARAMETERS]

[WARNING: DISPARITY DETECTED]

[RECOMMENDATION: RESTRICT CLASS ACCESS]

Kai's throat tightened. The System was trying to nerf him. It had tried and failed, but it had *noticed* him. In a world of millions of players, the System had singled him out.

His phone buzzed again. A video call. Marcus.

He answered.

Marcus's face filled the screen, pale and sweating. Behind him, Kai could see a chaotic mess of people running through what looked like a university campus. "Kai, listen to me. Something's wrong with the System."

"I know. I just got the update."

"No, not that. Something else. People are dying."

Kai's blood went cold. "What?"

"Some guy in my dorm tried to cast a spell after his mana hit zero. The System tried to pull from his life force instead. He's in the hospital with organ failure. They don't know if he'll make it."

The implications hit Kai like a physical blow. The System wasn't just a game. It was a framework that governed the very laws of reality. And when it broke those laws, reality broke with it.

"Marcus, I need you to stay calm. Don't use any skills. Don't even try."

"You think I'm stupid? I'm not touching that interface with a ten-foot pole." Marcus's eyes darted to the side. "But other people aren't so smart. There's already videos online of people trying to grind their mana recovery by running circuits around their neighborhoods. One guy collapsed from exhaustion ten minutes in."

The world was falling apart. The System had descended, given everyone classes, promised power and glory—and now it was revealing its true nature. Not a gift. A cage.

Kai stood up, pacing his tiny apartment. "What about the news? Is anyone reporting on this?"

"CNN's calling it a 'mass psychogenic illness.' Fox News is saying it's a Chinese bioweapon. Twitter is, well, Twitter." Marcus laughed bitterly. "Nobody knows what's real anymore."

"Because nothing is real anymore. Not the way we understood it."

A third notification hit, this one global:

[SYSTEM BROADCAST: EMERGENCY PATCH 1.0.1 DEPLOYING]

[FIX: MANA DEPLETION DEATH BUG]

[FIX: LIFE FORCE CONVERSION RATE ADJUSTED]

[FIX: MANA RECOVERY RATE INCREASED BY 300%]

[NOTE: ENVIRONMENTAL MANA DENSITY CRITICALLY LOW]

[NOTE: MANA GENERATION MECHANISM REQUIRED]

[NOTE: CULTIVATION DETECTED AS VIABLE ALTERNATIVE]

Kai froze.

The System had just acknowledged cultivation. Not as a bug, not as an exploit, but as a *solution*.

His phone buzzed one last time. A private message from an unknown number:

*You've been noticed. The Architects want to meet you.*

*Come to the Shanghai Convergence Point.*

*Time is running out.*

Kai stared at the message. The Architects. The same word Mentor had used to describe the beings who created the System in the first place.

*Who is this?* he typed.

The response came instantly: *Someone who wants to keep you alive. The Convergence opens in twelve hours. Don't be late.*

The message vanished. Not deleted—*vanished*, as if it had never existed.

Marcus was still talking on the video call, his voice tinny and distant. "Kai? Kai, you still there? What's happening?"

Kai looked at his phone. At the empty chat log where the message had been. At the chaos unfolding outside his window, where people were screaming and running and dying because the System had decided to rewrite the rules of reality.

"I have to go," he said.

"Go where? Kai, what are you—"

"I have to go to Shanghai."

"Shanghai? That's seven hundred miles away! How are you going to get there?"

Kai closed his eyes. Reached into his dantian. Felt the qi swirling there, patient and waiting.

*Mentor. Can I move that fast?*

Her voice echoed in his mind, calm and measured: *With proper qi circulation, you can traverse great distances. But you are not ready for such techniques. You risk damaging your meridians.*

*I don't have a choice.*

*There is always a choice, Kai. The question is whether you are willing to pay the price.*

He opened his eyes. Looked at Marcus on the screen. Looked at the world that was falling apart because the System didn't understand what it had created.

"The Architects are real," he said. "And they're calling me."

Marcus's face went white. "The Architects? Like, the ones from your tutorial?"

"I think so. I don't know for sure. But I know someone in Shanghai has answers, and I know I have twelve hours to get there."

Marcus was silent for a long moment. Then: "What do you need?"

"Time. And a way to move faster than I've ever moved before."

"You're going to kill yourself."

"Maybe. But if I don't try, a lot more people are going to die."

Marcus's jaw tightened. "Then go. And Kai?"

"Yeah?"

"Don't die. I just got my best friend back. I'm not losing him to some cosmic game of tag."

Kai almost smiled. Almost. "I'll do my best."

He ended the call. Took a deep breath. And reached for the technique Mentor had warned him not to use.

*Qi Step. Body Lightness Art. Wind Chasing Method.*

The names flooded his mind, fragments of knowledge that had been implanted during his tutorial. Techniques he wasn't supposed to touch for weeks, maybe months. Techniques that could tear his meridians apart if he got them wrong.

But he didn't have weeks. He had twelve hours.

He gathered qi in his dantian, compressed it, forced it through channels that still ached from his first circulation. Pain lanced through his legs, his spine, his core. He ignored it.

*Three cycles. Two for the legs, one for the body. Push and release.*

The world blurred.

When his vision cleared, he was standing on the roof of his apartment building, wind whipping past him. Below, the city sprawled like a circuit board, lights flickering as people panicked and systems failed and the world held its breath.

Shanghai was seven hundred miles away.

Kai took another breath. And ran.

His feet touched the edge of the building, then nothing. He was airborne, falling, the ground rushing up to meet him—

And then he wasn't falling. He was *flying*, barely, his qi propelling him forward in a series of uncontrolled leaps that carried him across streets and buildings and highways. His body screamed. His meridians screamed. His dantian pulsed like a second heart, pumping energy through channels that were never meant to handle this much, this fast.

*I'm going to die.*

*But not yet.*

*Not until I find out what the System really is.*

Behind him, a crack split the sky. Not lightning. Something else. Something that made the air taste like ozone and copper.

Kai didn't look back. He just ran.

The Convergence was waiting.

title: The Integration Event
content:
The System broadcast hung in Kai's vision, golden text burning against the morning light streaming through his window.

[WORLD INTEGRATION: T-24:00:00]

[CLASSES: 7,842,193,501 ASSIGNED]

[TIER 1 TUTORIALS: 99.7% COMPLETE]

[ALLOCATING FAILED TUTORIALS...]

Failed tutorials. The words sent ice down his spine. He thought of the people who'd been given classes they couldn't handle, tutorials that broke them. He thought of the 2% Mentor had mentioned—the ones who wouldn't survive cultivation.

His phone buzzed again. The group chat had exploded.

*Failed tutorials? What does that mean?*

*My sister's tutorial glitched. She's still stuck in a white room with no instructor.*

*There are people on the news saying the System killed their friends.*

Kai's stomach turned. He pulled up a news site, and the headlines hit him like a physical blow:

**"SYSTEM TUTORIALS CLAIM 47 LIVES IN FIRST HOUR"**

**"GLOBAL PANIC AS 2% OF POPULATION FAIL TO RETURN"**

**"GOVERNMENTS COLLAPSE AS CLASS IMBALANCE CREATES CHAOS"**

He kept scrolling, his hands trembling. Videos showed riots in major cities. People with newly acquired powers fighting in the streets. A man who'd become a Pyromancer had accidentally burned down his apartment building. A woman with Strength stats had killed three people in a bar fight without meaning to.

*The System didn't prepare us for this,* Kai thought. *It gave us power and walked away.*

Another notification appeared, this one personal:

[QUEST: INTEGRATION SURVIVAL]

[OBJECTIVE: REACH DESIGNATED SAFE ZONE WITHIN 24 HOURS]

[REWARD: TIER 1 SURVIVAL PACK]

[FAILURE: DEATH]

[SAFE ZONE LOCATION: CENTRAL INTEGRATION HUB - 12.4 KM]

Kai's blood ran cold. Twelve kilometers. Through a city that was already descending into chaos.

His phone buzzed continuously now—his mother, his friends, numbers he didn't recognize. He ignored them all and focused on what he needed to do.

First: assess his condition.

He sat cross-legged on his bed and closed his eyes. His qi sense expanded outward, mapping his internal landscape. The meridian network Mentor had opened was still fragile, still healing from the forced expansion. But the qi circulating through it was steady, flowing like a river that had found its course.

His dantian pulsed with warmth, a small sun at his core. He could feel the reservoir of qi there, limited but growing. He'd need to cultivate more before attempting any serious techniques.

Second: figure out what he could do.

[STATUS: KAI ZHANG]

[CLASS: DAO ARCHITECT]

[TIER: 1]

[QI: 23/100]

[MERIDIANS: 3 OPEN OF 12 MAJOR, 8 OF 36 MINOR]

[FOUNDATION: UNSTABLE]

[CULTIVATION TECHNIQUES: BASIC QI CIRCULATION (NOVICE)]

[SYSTEM ABILITIES: NONE]

*No System abilities.* The words stood out. Every other class had skills, spells, abilities—things the System had given them. He had nothing but what he'd built himself.

*But that's the point,* he realized. *I'm not supposed to depend on the System. I'm supposed to build my own path.*

Third: plan his route.

He pulled up a map on his phone, ignoring the flood of notifications. The Central Integration Hub was on the other side of the city, past the downtown core, across the river, through the industrial district. Normally a thirty-minute drive. Today it might as well be on another continent.

He'd need supplies. Water, food, first aid. Weapons, if he could find any. His qi was useful, but he had no combat techniques, no way to defend himself beyond what his body could do.

A crash from outside made him flinch. He moved to his window and peered through the blinds.

The street below was chaos. A group of people had gathered around a burning car, their faces lit by the flames. Some of them had visible changes—one man's arms were covered in scales, another woman's eyes glowed with inner light. They were arguing, shouting, pushing.

Then one of them threw a ball of fire at another.

The flames caught. Someone screamed. The crowd scattered.

Kai let the blinds fall and stepped back from the window.

*Twelve kilometers.*

He grabbed his backpack—the same one he'd used for college, worn and faded—and started filling it. Water bottles from his fridge. Protein bars he'd been saving. A flashlight with dying batteries. A pocket knife he'd forgotten he owned.

He was halfway through packing when someone pounded on his door.

"Kai! Kai, are you in there?"

His neighbor, Mrs. Chen. Elderly, kind, always leaving homemade dumplings at his door.

He opened it, and she nearly fell inside, her face streaked with tears.

"Kai, you have to help me. My grandson—he's still in school. The buses aren't running. I can't reach him."

"Mrs. Chen, calm down." He guided her to his couch, making her sit. "What happened?"

"The System—it gave him a class. He called me, so excited. He said he was a Healer. Then the broadcast came, and now—" Her voice broke. "Now I can't reach him. His phone goes straight to voicemail."

Kai's jaw tightened. "Where's his school?"

"Oakwood Elementary. On the other side of the highway."

The highway. Which would be a parking lot of abandoned cars by now. And Oakwood was in the opposite direction from the Integration Hub.

*I can't afford detours. I have twenty-three hours to cover twelve kilometers through a war zone.*

But looking at Mrs. Chen's tear-streaked face, he knew he couldn't say no.

"I'll find him," he said. "Stay here. Lock the door. Don't open it for anyone."

"Kai, I can't ask you to—"

"You didn't ask. I offered."

He finished packing, slinging the backpack over his shoulders. His pocket knife went into his front pocket. His phone went into his back pocket. His qi sense extended one last time, checking his meridians, confirming he could still circulate.

*I'm not ready for this. But I don't have a choice.*

He stepped out of his apartment, and the hallway hit him like a wall.

The air was thick with qi. Not the clean, natural qi he'd felt in the tutorial—this was chaotic, roiling, tinged with fear and violence. He could sense the life forces of everyone in the building, their qi signatures fluctuating wildly as they panicked, fought, or fled.

Someone was crying two floors down. Someone else was screaming for help. A group of people was gathering in the lobby, their voices raised in anger.

*This is what happens when power is given without guidance,* he thought. *We're all children playing with matches in a room full of gasoline.*

He took the stairs, moving quietly. His qi sense warned him of a confrontation on the second floor—two men, both with classes, their qi spiking with aggression. He bypassed them through the emergency exit, stepping into the alley behind his building.

The street was worse than he'd expected.

Cars clogged the road, abandoned in the middle of intersections. Some were still running, their engines idling. Others had been overturned, their windows smashed. A convenience store on the corner had been looted, its front door hanging open, glass scattered across the sidewalk.

And everywhere, people.

Some were crying. Some were fighting. Some were just standing, staring at their hands, trying to understand what they'd become.

Kai pulled up his hood and started moving.

He kept to the alleys, avoiding main roads. His qi sense extended twenty meters in all directions, giving him advance warning of threats. Twice he ducked into doorways to avoid groups of aggressive class-holders. Once he had to climb a fire escape to bypass a street that had become a battlefield.

By the time he reached Oakwood Elementary, his qi was down to 15/100. He'd been circulating it constantly to maintain his enhanced perception, and his meridians were starting to ache.

*I need to cultivate soon, or I'll be useless.*

The school was quiet.

Too quiet.

The front gates were open, swinging in the wind. The main doors were propped open with a chair. There were no sounds of children, no teachers shouting, no panicked screams.

Kai's qi sense pushed into the building.

And recoiled.

The qi inside was... wrong. Stagnant. Heavy. It felt like walking into a room filled with smoke.

*Something happened here.*

He stepped through the front doors, and the smell hit him first. Copper. Iron. Blood.

The hallway was empty. School supplies were scattered everywhere—backpacks, notebooks, a single shoe. A mural on the wall showed smiling children holding hands, a stark contrast to the scene before him.

Kai moved deeper, his heart pounding. His qi sense guided him toward the source of the stagnant energy.

The gymnasium.

The double doors were closed, but light flickered through the windows set into them. Voices murmured inside—not human voices, but something else. Something that resonated on a frequency that made his dantian vibrate.

He pushed the door open.

The gymnasium had been transformed.

The basketball court was covered in symbols, drawn in what looked like blood. They spiraled outward from the center, forming concentric circles of impossible geometry. The air shimmered with heat, and the qi was so thick he could taste it.

And in the center of the symbols stood a child.

A boy, no older than ten, his eyes glowing with golden light. His hands were raised, and tendrils of qi extended from his fingers, connecting to the symbols on the ground.

Mrs. Chen's grandson.

"Liu?" Kai stepped forward, and the boy's head snapped toward him.

"You shouldn't be here," the boy said, and his voice was layered—a child's voice overlaid with something ancient and terrible. "The Integration is beginning. You should be at your designated safe zone."

"What have you done?" Kai asked, his eyes tracing the symbols. They were wrong. They were *cultivation* symbols, but twisted, corrupted. A formation meant to drain life and channel it elsewhere.

"I've done what the System asked," the boy said, and his smile was too wide, too knowing. "I've opened the gate."

The symbols flared with light.

The ground shook.

And something on the other side of the formation began to push through.

Kai's System interface blazed to life:

[WARNING: INTEGRATION EVENT ACTIVE]

[CLASS: DAO ARCHITECT DETECTED]

[UNIQUE OPPORTUNITY: DISRUPT FORMATION OR DIE]

[CHOOSE YOUR PATH.]

*No pressure,* Kai thought, and reached for his qi.

title: The Weight of a Thousand Eyes
content:
Kai's phone slipped from his fingers as the broadcast faded.

[SYSTEM BROADCAST: TUTORIAL PHASE COMPLETE]
[ALL CLASSES HAVE BEEN ASSIGNED]
[WORLD INTEGRATION AT 34.7%]
[NEXT PHASE: AWAKENING — 72 HOURS]

The apartment felt smaller suddenly. The walls pressed in. The faint qi signatures of his neighbors pulsed like exposed nerves in his awareness.

He grabbed his phone, but the screen was frozen. No messages going through. No notifications. Just a single line of text burned into the display:

[SYSTEM NOTICE: COMMUNICATION RESTRICTED DURING INTEGRATION]

"Great." Kai stood, testing his legs. His dantian hummed, the qi circulating steadily now, each cycle smoother than the last. He walked to the window and pulled back the curtain.

The sky was wrong.

Not physically—the clouds moved normally, the sunlight filtered through at the correct angle. But spiritually, the heavens looked *stitched together*, like someone had patched a torn canvas with mismatched thread. Lines of golden energy crisscrossed the atmosphere, pulsing in rhythm with a heartbeat Kai could feel in his bones.

*The System is still integrating the world,* Mentor's voice echoed in his memory. *Think of it as overwriting the original code with new parameters.*

His doorbell rang.

Kai tensed. His qi sense extended instinctively, probing outward. Two signatures. One bright, aggressive, burning with the sharp edge of a newly awakened class. The other dimmer, uncertain, flickering like a candle in wind.

He opened the door.

A woman stood in the hallway, maybe mid-twenties, with cropped black hair and eyes that held the recent shock of someone who'd seen things they couldn't unsee. Behind her, a man in a janitor's uniform clutched a mop handle like a weapon, his knuckles white.

"You're the one," the woman said. "The unclassified."

Kai's hand drifted toward the doorframe. "I don't know what you're talking about."

"The System showed me." She tapped her temple. "During my tutorial. A vision of a man with golden meridians, standing at the center of a spiral. The Architect, it called you."

*Impossible.* The System shouldn't know his class title. Mentor had said his class was outside the System's recognition, a bug in the code. But if the System was showing visions of him to other awakened—

"I'm Chen Wei," the woman continued, stepping forward. "My class is Seeker. I track anomalies. And you, Architect, are the biggest anomaly I've ever sensed."

The janitor behind her shifted nervously. "She dragged me here. Said it was urgent. I don't want no trouble, man. I just got my class—[Janitor: Level 1]. I can clean things. That's it."

"Your class is irrelevant." Chen Wei's eyes fixed on Kai's chest, right where his dantian sat. "What matters is that you're not supposed to exist. The System has rules. Structures. You break them simply by *being*."

Kai's qi flared defensively, golden light flickering at his fingertips. "What do you want?"

"To understand." She held up her hands. "I'm not here to fight. I'm here because the System showed me something else, too. Something coming."

"Coming?"

"The integration isn't random. It's guided. There's a pattern to which classes are assigned, which tutorials are given. And the pattern suggests..." She paused, struggling with the words. "The pattern suggests someone is *designing* this. Not a god. Not an AI. A person. A person who's been doing this for a very long time."

Mentor's golden eyes flashed in Kai's memory. Her cryptic warnings. Her knowledge of the System's workings.

"You met them," Chen Wei said, reading his expression. "During your tutorial. You met the designer."

"I met someone," Kai admitted. "But she didn't tell me much. She said my class was a bug. That I wasn't supposed to exist."

"She?" Chen Wei's brow furrowed. "The designer is female?"

"She called herself Mentor. That's all I know."

The janitor cleared his throat. "Look, I don't understand half of what you're saying, but I got a notification a few minutes ago. Before the broadcast. It said something about 'anomaly containment' and 'protocol initialization.'"

Kai's blood ran cold. "Show me."

The janitor fumbled with his phone, finally managing to pull up a message that shouldn't have existed given the communication blackout:

[SYSTEM PROTOCOL: ANOMALY CONTAINMENT]
[TARGET: ARCHITECT CLASS HOLDER]
[STATUS: LOCATED — 89.7% CONFIDENCE]
[ACTION: ASSIGN ADJACENT CLASSES FOR NEUTRALIZATION]
[ETA: 48 HOURS]

"The System is sending people after you," Chen Wei whispered. "It knows you're a bug. And it's deploying other awakened to squash you."

Kai's dantian thrummed with sudden heat, his qi cycling faster, building pressure. "How many?"

"The notification didn't specify. But given the confidence rating..." She met his eyes. "It knows exactly where you are. Right now."

A new notification appeared in Kai's peripheral vision:

[SYSTEM NOTICE: ADJACENT CLASS ASSIGNMENT INITIATED]
[CANDIDATES SELECTED BASED ON OPTIMAL COUNTER-MEASURE PROFILES]
[MATCH: 12 HUNTER-CLASS AWAKENED]
[MATCH: 8 PURIFIER-CLASS AWAKENED]
[MATCH: 3 VOID-CLASS AWAKENED]
[TOTAL NEUTRALIZATION FORCE: 23]
[DEPLOYMENT: IMMEDIATE]

Twenty-three awakened. Coming for him.

"I need to move," Kai said, stepping back into his apartment. "I need to find somewhere safe."

"There's nowhere safe," Chen Wei said. "The System sees everything. Tracks everything. You can run, but it will always know where you are."

"Then what do I do?"

She was quiet for a long moment. The janitor shifted his weight, clearly wanting to leave, but caught in the gravity of the situation.

"You learn to fight," Chen Wei finally said. "You learn to use your class in ways the System doesn't expect. You become the bug that crashes the whole program."

Kai stared at his hands. Golden light flickered beneath his skin, tracing the pathways of his meridians. Qi. The System didn't understand qi. It was the one thing he had that operated outside its rules.

"What about you?" he asked Chen Wei. "Why are you helping me?"

"Because the System showed me what happens if you die." Her voice dropped. "I saw a world where you were eliminated. The integration continued, but it went wrong. The classes corrupted. The awakened turned against each other. A war that lasted decades, until nothing was left but ash and hollow shells."

She met his eyes.

"The System is afraid of you, Architect. And anything the System fears is something I want to keep alive."

The janitor finally broke, backing away. "I'm out. I didn't sign up for this. I just wanted to clean things. I'm going home, locking my door, and pretending none of this happened."

He fled down the hallway, his footsteps echoing in the sudden silence.
