Kai looked at Chen Wei. "Can you fight?"

"Seeker class. No combat skills. But I can track, I can analyze, and I can find weak points." She smiled thinly. "I'll be your eyes. You'll need them."

Another notification pulsed:

[SYSTEM ALERT: NEUTRALIZATION FORCE DEPLOYED]
[ETA TO TARGET: 46 HOURS, 23 MINUTES]
[RECOMMENDATION: SURRENDER VOLUNTARILY FOR OPTIMAL OUTCOME]

"Forty-six hours," Kai breathed. "That's all I have."

"Then we'd better get to work." Chen Wei stepped into his apartment, her eyes scanning the cluttered space. "First, we need to understand exactly what your class can do. The System doesn't know. That's our advantage."

Kai closed the door, locking it out of habit more than necessity. A lock wouldn't stop twenty-three awakened hunters.

But maybe, just maybe, a bug in the system could.

His dantian pulsed, and for a moment, he felt it—the faintest thread of connection to something vast and unknowable. The Dao. The source code beneath reality's surface.

*I need more power,* he thought. *I need to understand what I am.*

As if in answer, his class interface flickered, revealing a new option that hadn't been there before:

[CLASS: DAO ARCHITECT]
[SUBCLASS UNLOCK: ???]
[REQUIREMENT: SURVIVE FIRST NEUTRALIZATION WAVE]
[REWARD: ACCESS TO DAO COMPILATION]

A subclass. Something the System hadn't mentioned. Something that existed entirely outside its framework.

Kai smiled grimly. "Alright. Let's see how deep this rabbit hole goes."

Outside, the stitched sky pulsed with golden light, and somewhere in the city, twenty-three awakened hunters received their targets, their eyes glowing with System-granted purpose.

The hunt had begun.

title: The Integration Protocol
content:
Kai's finger hovered over the send button when the world went white.

Not the gentle dissolution of the tutorial chamber. Not the gradual fade of meditation. This was violent—a detonation of light that scoured away reality like a sandblaster stripping paint. For one terrifying second, Kai existed nowhere, suspended in infinite brightness.

Then the System notification slammed into his consciousness, so forceful it left a physical ache behind his eyes.

[SYSTEM BROADCAST: TUTORIAL PHASE COMPLETE]
[ALL CLASSES HAVE BEEN ASSIGNED]
[WORLD INTEGRATION PROTOCOL INITIATING...]
[ESTIMATED TIME TO COMPLETION: 72 HOURS]
[ALL HUMANITY WILL BE TRANSFERRED TO THE PRIME REALM]

The notification hung in his vision, pulsing with an urgency that made his heart race. Prime Realm? Transfer? What the hell did that mean?

His phone buzzed again. Then again. Then all at once, a cascade of vibrations that chattered against his nightstand like machine-gun fire. Kai grabbed it, squinting at the screen.

The group chat had exploded.

*WHAT THE FUCK IS A PRIME REALM*
*My apartment is flickering. Literally flickering. Like a bad video game texture.*
*Can confirm. I can see through my walls. There's a forest outside. THERE WAS NEVER A FOREST OUTSIDE.*
*Everyone calm down. The System probably has a plan.*
*OH GREAT THE SYSTEM HAS A PLAN THAT'S SO REASSURING*

Kai's blood ran cold. Forest?

He crossed to his window in three strides and threw open the blinds.

His apartment faced a brick wall. Had always faced a brick wall, the neighboring building so close he could almost touch it. That wall was gone now. In its place stretched an endless expanse of ancient trees, their trunks so massive that five men couldn't circle them, their branches weaving together into a canopy that blotted out the sky. The air that drifted through his window smelled of moss and damp earth and something else—something sharp and electric, like ozone before a storm.

Below, the street was chaos.

People stood on the sidewalk, still in their pajamas, staring at the impossible forest that had replaced their city. Some were crying. Some were laughing hysterically. A few had already drawn weapons—swords that materialized from nothing, staves that crackled with raw magic—and were eyeing the treeline with predatory focus.

*Level 1 Warriors already looking for fights*, Kai thought. *They're going to get themselves killed.*

His phone buzzed one last time, a private message from an unknown number.

*Kai. You need to get to the convergence point. East side of the city, old industrial district. Come alone. —Mentor*

Kai stared at the message. How did she have his number? She was a construct of the tutorial, a piece of the System given form. She shouldn't even exist outside that white room.

But then again, nothing about his situation made sense anymore.

He grabbed his jacket—a thin windbreaker that suddenly felt laughably inadequate—and headed for the door.

---

The streets were a nightmare.

Kai had lived in this city his whole life. He knew every alley, every shortcut, every cracked sidewalk and broken streetlight. But the city he knew was dying, overwritten by something vast and ancient. Buildings flickered between existence and non-existence, their walls transparent one moment and solid the next. Trees erupted through asphalt, their roots cracking foundations. And everywhere, everywhere, the air hummed with qi.

Not the thin, artificial qi of Earth's polluted spiritual environment. This was pure, primal energy, so dense it made Kai's newly awakened meridians sing. Every breath felt like he was drinking liquid fire, his dantian spinning faster and faster, trying to process the influx.

He rounded a corner and stopped dead.

A man stood in the middle of the street, arms spread wide, head tilted back. His eyes glowed white, and qi poured off him in visible waves, distorting the air like heat haze. Around him, the ground had begun to change—grass sprouting through cracks in the concrete, flowers blooming in impossible colors, the very pavement shifting into rich, dark soil.

"A Druid," Kai breathed. "He's already leveling."

The man's head snapped toward him. His eyes—white, pupil-less, terrifying—fixed on Kai with an intensity that made his skin crawl.

"You can see it," the Druid said, his voice layered, echoing. "You can see the qi."

Kai didn't answer. He just watched, cataloging the man's movements, his posture, the way the qi coiled around him like living serpents. Mentor had taught him to read spiritual signatures, and this one was... unstable. Powerful, but raw. Untrained.

"You're one of us," the Druid continued, taking a step forward. "A cultivator. Not one of those Warrior brutes or Mage cowards. A real cultivator."

"I don't know what you're talking about," Kai said carefully. "I just woke up like everyone else."

The Druid laughed, and the sound was wrong—too many notes, too much harmony, like a choir singing through a single throat. "Liar. I can taste the cultivation on you. You've already started. Your foundation is forming. You're ahead of the curve."

He took another step, and Kai's hand drifted toward his pocket. He had no weapon, no combat training, nothing but a freshly awakened dantian and a dozen hours of meditation. But he'd be damned if he went down without a fight.

"I'm not your enemy," Kai said. "I'm just trying to figure out what's happening."

"We're all trying to figure out what's happening," the Druid replied. "But some of us are figuring it out faster than others. And the ones who figure it out fastest will be the ones who survive."

He raised his hand, and the ground between them erupted. Vines—thick, thorn-covered, moving with predatory intent—shot upward, forming a wall that cut off Kai's retreat.

"You're going to help me," the Druid said, his white eyes gleaming. "You're going to teach me what you know. And then maybe, just maybe, I'll let you live."

Kai's mind raced. Mentor's lessons flashed through his head—the principles of qi circulation, the pathways of energy, the way intent shaped spiritual force. He didn't know any combat techniques. He didn't have any offensive abilities. But he understood, on a fundamental level, how qi worked.

And the Druid's qi was unstable.

Very unstable.

"Your foundation is cracking," Kai said, keeping his voice calm. "You absorbed too much too fast. Your meridians are inflamed. If you keep forcing qi through them, you'll rupture something."

The Druid's smile faltered. "You're lying."

"I'm really not. Look at your own spiritual signature. There's a knot in your third meridian, just below the heart. Every time you circulate, you're putting pressure on it. Another hour of this, and you'll either pass out or die."

The Druid's hand twitched. The vines wavered.

"How do you know that?"

"Because I can see it. And because I almost did the same thing." Kai spread his hands, showing he was unarmed. "I'm not your enemy. But I'm also not your teacher. If you want to survive this, you need to learn patience. The System gave us power, but it didn't give us wisdom. That we have to earn ourselves."

For a long moment, the two of them stood there, the air crackling with tension. The Druid's eyes flickered, the white receding slightly, revealing a hint of the terrified human beneath.

Then the vines withered.

"Fine," the Druid said, his voice dropping back to normal. "But this isn't over. There are others like us. We're going to find each other. And when we do, we're going to decide who leads."

He turned and walked into the forest, his form dissolving into shadows.

Kai let out a breath he didn't know he'd been holding. His hands were shaking. His dantian was throbbing. And somewhere in the distance, he could hear screams.

He checked his phone. The message from Mentor was still there, the coordinates glowing faintly.

*East side. Old industrial district. Come alone.*

He started running.

---

The industrial district was unrecognizable.

The factories and warehouses that had dominated this part of the city were gone, replaced by structures that looked like they'd been grown rather than built. Spires of crystal and bone rose from the earth, their surfaces covered in flowing script that Kai couldn't read but somehow understood. The air was thick with qi, so dense it formed visible currents that swirled through the streets like mist.

And at the center of it all stood Mentor.

She looked different outside the tutorial chamber. More real, somehow. Her golden eyes held the same ancient wisdom, but now they were set in a face that looked almost human, almost vulnerable. Her robes were gone, replaced by simple clothing—pants, a jacket, practical boots—that made her look like she belonged in this world.

"You came," she said, and there was relief in her voice. "Good. We don't have much time."

"What's happening?" Kai demanded, skidding to a stop. "The System just—everything is—the tutorial wasn't supposed to—"

"The tutorial was never supposed to be the end," Mentor interrupted. "It was always the beginning. The System was designed to integrate Earth into the Prime Realm, but the integration was supposed to be gradual. Controlled. Something went wrong."

"Something went wrong? What do you mean something went wrong?"

Mentor's expression was grim. "I mean the integration protocol was triggered prematurely. By someone—or something—inside the System itself. The Prime Realm is flooding into Earth at an accelerated rate. In seventy-two hours, the transformation will be complete. And most of humanity won't survive the transition."

Kai felt the blood drain from his face. "Most? How many?"

"The estimate from the original protocol was 60% casualties. With the acceleration..." She paused, her golden eyes flickering with something that might have been grief. "Closer to 90%."

Ninety percent.

Six billion people.

"Unless," Mentor continued, "we can fix it."

"Fix it? How the hell are we supposed to fix it? I've had my class for twelve hours. I barely know how to circulate qi without passing out."

"You're not just a cultivator, Kai. You're a Dao Architect. The System doesn't understand your class because your class was never meant to exist within the System's framework. You're an anomaly. And anomalies have the power to break rules."

She reached into her jacket and pulled out a small crystal, pulsating with inner light. The moment Kai touched it, his vision exploded with data—lines of code, spiritual patterns, the very fabric of reality laid bare before him.

"The System is the Heavenly Dao reborn as code," Mentor said. "And code can be rewritten. But only by someone who understands both the spiritual and the digital. Someone who can see the Dao in the numbers."

Kai stared at the crystal, feeling its weight in his hand. Somewhere in the distance, he heard another scream, followed by the crash of collapsing buildings.

"There's a central hub," Mentor continued. "The heart of the integration protocol. It's located in what used to be the city center. If we can reach it, you can access the System's core and slow down the integration."

"We?"

Mentor's smile was sad. "I can guide you. I can teach you. But I can't

title: The Architect's First Fracture
content:
The broadcast faded, leaving a ringing silence in its wake. Kai's phone buzzed again—more messages, more chaos—but he couldn't look away from the notification still lingering in his vision.

[WARNING: MERIDIAN INSTABILITY DETECTED]

His dantian pulsed, sharp and wrong. The qi he'd carefully cultivated during the tutorial churned like a caged beast, pressing against the walls of his meridians with increasing violence.

"What the—"

He doubled over, clutching his stomach. The world swam. His apartment's familiar walls warped and twisted, shadows deepening into pools of absolute darkness. The cockroach behind his wall scrabbled frantically, as if sensing something far more dangerous than itself.

Then, as suddenly as it started, the pain stopped.

Kai gasped, straightening slowly. Sweat dripped down his forehead. His hands trembled.

*That was new.*

[WARNING: QI DEVIATION RISK INCREASED TO 7%]

*Seven percent?* It had been two percent during the tutorial. What had changed? He hadn't done anything—hadn't cultivated, hadn't fought, hadn't even left his bed.

His phone buzzed again. He ignored it, focusing inward, reaching for his qi sense—

The world exploded into color.

Not metaphorically. Literally. The air around him blazed with ribbons of light—crimson, azure, gold—all tangled together in a chaotic web that stretched beyond his apartment walls, beyond the building, beyond the city itself. He could see the qi of thousands of people, each thread pulsing with their emotions, their health, their *potential*.

And at the center of it all, like a dark star, something was *watching*.

Kai recoiled, slamming his qi sense shut. His heart hammered against his ribs.

"What was that?"

No answer. The apartment was silent except for the hum of his refrigerator and the distant traffic from the street below. But he knew—*knew*—he'd seen something he wasn't supposed to.

His phone buzzed one last time. A private message from an unknown number:

*You saw it too.*

Kai's blood ran cold.

He typed back: *Who is this?*

The reply came instantly: *Someone who needs to talk. Public library, downtown. One hour. Come alone.*

*Why should I trust you?*

*Because I know what you are. Dao Architect. And I know why the System doesn't recognize your class.*

Kai stared at the message. His thumb hovered over the keyboard, ready to type a dozen different questions, a dozen different refusals.

Instead, he wrote: *How do I know this isn't a trap?*

*You don't. But if I wanted you dead, I wouldn't warn you about the fracture.*

The fracture.

He looked at the System notification still lingering in his vision. The warning about meridian instability. The increased qi deviation risk.

*What fracture?*

He typed the question, but the message bounced. The number was already disconnected.

---

The library was a three-story building of faded brick and cracked concrete, nestled between a pawn shop and a laundromat. It had survived the System's integration with minimal damage—a few cracked windows, some displaced bookshelves—but the real changes weren't visible to the naked eye.

Kai felt them.

The qi here was *old*. Not stagnant, but ancient, layered like sediment. He could taste centuries of knowledge in the air, feel the weight of countless minds that had passed through these walls. It was comforting and unsettling in equal measure.

He found the stranger on the second floor, in a corner section labeled "Local History." She was young—maybe early twenties—with sharp features and hair pulled back in a tight ponytail. Her clothes were ordinary: jeans, a hoodie, sneakers. But her qi was anything but.

It burned like a blue flame, hot and bright, with a density that made Kai's own cultivation feel like a candle next to a bonfire.

"You came," she said, not looking up from the book she was reading. "I wasn't sure you would."

"You knew my class. My real class. How?"

She closed the book and turned to face him. Her eyes were pale gray, almost silver, and they seemed to see right through him.

"Because I have the same one."

Kai's breath caught. "You're a Dao Architect?"

"No." She shook her head slowly. "I'm a *former* Dao Architect. Or I was, before I broke."

"Broke?"

She lifted her left sleeve. Her arm was a patchwork of scar tissue, the skin twisted and discolored, with faint lines of black running through it like veins of corruption.

"Qi deviation," she said, her voice flat. "Stage three. I survived, but my cultivation was destroyed. The System reclassified me as a 'Failed Experiment' and slapped a generic Mage class on me as a placeholder. I've been stuck at Level 1 ever since."

Kai stared at the scars. The black lines pulsed faintly, as if alive.

"Why are you telling me this?"

"Because you're going to face the same fate if you keep following the tutorial's path." She leaned forward, her silver eyes boring into his. "The woman who taught you—Mentor, you called her—she's not what she seems. None of them are."

"Them?"

"The Architects. The ones who built the System." She paused, letting the words sink in. "They didn't create it to help humanity. They created it to harvest us."

The library's fluorescent lights flickered. Somewhere in the stacks, a book fell from its shelf with a thud.

"Harvest us for what?" Kai asked, his voice barely above a whisper.

"Power. Lifespan. Potential." She gestured vaguely at the air around them. "The qi you're cultivating? It's not yours. It's borrowed. Every time you cycle it through your meridians, you leave a trace of yourself behind. A signature. A key."

"A key to what?"

"The System's core. The Heavenly Dao's true form." She smiled, but there was no warmth in it. "You're not an architect, Kai. You're a brick in someone else's wall."

Kai's mind raced. The tutorial. Mentor's careful instructions. The way she'd emphasized *foundation* over *power*, *stability* over *speed*. It all made sense now—a terrible, horrifying sense.

"The 2% deviation risk," he said slowly. "It wasn't accurate."

"It never is." She stood, tucking the book under her arm. "The real risk is closer to 20% for the first month. After that, it compounds. By the time you reach Foundation Establishment, you'll have a 50% chance of spontaneous combustion every time you cultivate."

"Then why are you warning me? If I'm destined to fail anyway..."

"Because you have something I didn't." She reached into her pocket and pulled out a small, obsidian shard, no larger than her thumb. It pulsed with a dark, hungry light. "A choice."

Kai took the shard. It was warm to the touch, almost alive.

"What is this?"

"A fragment of the original System. Before it was corrupted. Before the Architects twisted it into a harvesting mechanism." She met his eyes. "If you can learn to read it—to understand its true code—you might be able to rewrite your own cultivation path. Break free from their design."

"And if I can't?"

"Then you'll end up like me." She pulled her sleeve back down, hiding the scars. "Or worse."

The library's lights flickered again, longer this time. The shadows seemed to deepen, stretching toward them like grasping hands.

"They're coming," the woman said, her voice suddenly urgent. "They know I've been in contact with you. You need to leave. Now."

"Who's coming?"

"The Architects. Or rather, their enforcers." She grabbed his arm, pulling him toward the emergency exit. "Don't trust anyone from the tutorial. Don't trust Mentor. And whatever you do—"

The door exploded inward.

A figure stood in the doorway, silhouetted against the harsh afternoon light. Tall. Broad-shouldered. Wearing what looked like a business suit, but with lines of glowing code running across its surface like veins.

"Subject Alpha-7," the figure said, its voice a flat, synthetic monotone. "You are in violation of Directive 3. Surrender the fragment, and your sentence will be reduced to permanent cultivation suppression."

The woman—Alpha-7—pushed Kai behind her. "Run," she hissed. "Don't stop until you're out of the city. Find the others."

"What others?"

But she was already moving, blue fire erupting from her hands as she charged the enforcer. The impact sent shockwaves through the library, toppling bookshelves and shattering windows.

Kai ran.

He burst through the emergency exit, into the alley behind the library. His lungs burned. His dantian throbbed in protest. The obsidian shard in his pocket pulsed like a second heartbeat, guiding him forward with an insistent, rhythmic pull.

Behind him, he heard the sounds of battle—crashes, screams, the crackle of qi being unleashed. Then silence.

Then footsteps.

He didn't look back. He just ran, the shard growing warmer and warmer against his thigh, until it was almost too hot to bear.

And then a voice, cold and familiar, echoed in his mind:

*"You cannot escape your design, Architect. You were built for this."*

Kai stumbled, nearly falling. The shard burned, searing through his pocket, through his skin, fusing with his flesh—

[SYSTEM NOTIFICATION: NEW ITEM ACQUIRED]

[UNKNOWN FRAGMENT - LEVEL: ???]

[WARNING: FRAGMENT INTEGRATION IN PROGRESS]

[ESTIMATED TIME TO COMPLETION: 00:00:47]

*No.*

Kai tried to throw the shard away, but it was already part of him, the black lines spreading up his arm like the scars on Alpha-7's flesh.

[INTEGRATION COMPLETE]

[ABILITIES UNLOCKED: SYSTEM OVERRIDE (PARTIAL)]

[NEW TITLE ACQUIRED: THE FIRST FRACTURE]

The world went white.

And when Kai opened his eyes again, he was no longer in the alley.

He was standing in a vast, empty space—a white void stretching infinitely in all directions. And in front of him, seated on a throne of pure light, was a figure he recognized.

Mentor.

"Hello, Kai," she said, her golden eyes gleaming. "I see you've found one of my little experiments."

"Experiment?" His voice cracked. "You—you knew. About the harvesting. About the Architects."

"Of course I knew." She smiled, serene and terrible. "I designed it."

The void trembled. The shard in his arm pulsed, screaming warnings he couldn't understand.

"But you're wrong about one thing," Mentor continued, rising from her throne. "We're not harvesting humanity."

She stepped closer, and the void darkened around her, swallowing the light.

"We're ascending it. And you, Kai, are going to help us—whether you want to or not."

[SYSTEM NOTIFICATION: TUTORIAL PHASE 2 INITIALIZED]

[LOCATION: THE ARCHITECT'S THRESHOLD]

[OBJECTIVE: SURVIVE]

[WARNING: THIS IS NOT A DRILL]

title: The Price of Awakening
content:
Kai's phone slipped from his fingers as the broadcast seared itself into his consciousness.

[WORLD INTEGRATION BEGINNING IN 7 DAYS]
[FIRST WAVE: MONSTER SPAWNS TO COMMENCE AT ALL REGISTERED DUNGEON LOCATIONS]
[SYSTEM REWARDS: ACTIVE FOR ALL PARTICIPANTS]
[SYSTEM PENALTIES: ACTIVE FOR ALL NON-PARTICIPANTS]

The message hung in the air for three heartbeats before fading, but its afterimage burned behind Kai's eyes. Seven days. One week until monsters started appearing in the real world.

His phone buzzed again. Then again. Then it didn't stop.

*Kai, did you see that?*

*What the hell is a dungeon location?*

*My apartment complex. The broadcast says my apartment complex is a registered dungeon location.*

Kai's blood ran cold. He reached out with his qi sense, expanding his awareness beyond his own walls, beyond the neighboring apartments, out into the street—

And felt it. A thin, membranous barrier, like soap film stretched across reality itself, coating the foundations of every building within a three-block radius. Below ground, deeper than any basement should go, something pulsed. Something hungry.

*No. No, no, no.*

He grabbed his phone, fingers moving before his mind caught up.

*Everyone stay calm. Don't go into basements. Don't go underground. I'll explain when I—*

The message failed to send. Error code: 734. Network congestion.

Kai swore, throwing himself out of bed. His body screamed in protest—six hours of cultivation had left him sore in ways he didn't have words for—but he pushed through it. He had to get to his mother. Had to get to his friends. Had to—

A System notification appeared, personal this time.

[NEW QUEST AVAILABLE: FOUNDATION OF DAWN]
[OBJECTIVE: STABILIZE YOUR MERIDIAN NETWORK]
[REQUIREMENTS: COMPLETE 100 SUCCESSFUL QI CIRCULATIONS WITHIN 12 HOURS]
[REWARD: MERIDIAN STABILIZATION, +1 QI RESERVOIR CAPACITY]
[FAILURE PENALTY: MERIDIAN COLLAPSE, CLASS DEGRADATION]

Kai stared at the quest. His dantian throbbed in warning. He'd barely managed ten circulations in six hours. One hundred in twelve? That wasn't just ambitious—it was insane.

But the penalty. *Meridian collapse. Class degradation.*

He didn't know what that meant, exactly, but he could guess. His class was already unstable, already something the System didn't understand. If it degraded further, if his meridians collapsed...

He might not survive.

"You're awake."

Kai spun. Mentor stood in his apartment doorway, golden eyes gleaming in the dim morning light. She looked exactly as she had in the tutorial chamber—ageless, composed, utterly alien in his cramped living space.

"How did you get in here?"

"The System granted me limited access to your reality. I am what you might call a... liaison." She tilted her head, studying him. "You received the quest."

"Was that you? Did you—"

"No. The System generated it based on your current instability. Your foundation is still too weak. If you attempt to integrate with the world when the first wave arrives, you will shatter."

Kai's jaw tightened. "I have seven days. I need to find my mother. I need to—"

"Your mother is safe. For now." Mentor raised a hand, and a holographic map materialized in the air between them. Kai's apartment building glowed at its center, surrounded by a web of red lines—the membranous barriers he'd sensed earlier. "These are dungeon seeds. They have been planted throughout your city, throughout the world, in locations chosen by the System."

"Planted? By who? The System is just—"

"The System is not *just* anything." Mentor's voice carried an edge Kai hadn't heard before. "It is the Heavenly Dao reborn as code, yes. But code must be written. Parameters must be set. Someone—or something—designed this System. And that entity has been waiting for a world like yours."

Kai's mind raced. "You're saying the System was created deliberately? By a person?"

"By a being beyond your current comprehension. The Architect of this System is not bound by your reality's laws. It observes. It tests. And when it finds a specimen of interest, it integrates."

"Specimen." The word tasted like ash. "You're talking about humanity. We're specimens."

"Humanity is the test subject. You, specifically, are the variable." Mentor's gaze bore into him. "Your class—Dao Architect—was not meant to exist. It was a glitch, a deviation from the System's intended parameters. But the Architect noticed. It became *curious*."

Kai felt the floor drop out from under him. "I'm a bug. A glitch. And the System is going to—"

"Study you. Pressure you. Break you, if necessary, to understand what you are." Mentor's expression softened, almost imperceptibly. "That is why you must grow stronger. Not just to survive the monster waves, but to survive the Architect's interest."

The holographic map shifted, zooming in on a point three blocks away. Kai's old high school. A red marker pulsed over its gymnasium.

[REGISTERED DUNGEON LOCATION #7342: WESTBROOK HIGH SCHOOL]
[ESTIMATED DUNGEON DEPTH: 3 FLOORS]
[ESTIMATED MONSTER LEVEL: 1-5]
[RECOMMENDED PARTY SIZE: 3-5]

"Your first trial," Mentor said. "The dungeon beneath your school will be one of the first to activate. It is small, manageable. Perfect for a beginner."

"I'm not a beginner. I'm—"

"You have cultivated for six hours. You have completed ten qi circulations. Against a level 3 monster, you would last approximately four seconds." Mentor's words were clinical, brutal. "You need the Foundation of Dawn quest. You need to stabilize your meridians. And you need to enter that dungeon before the first wave."

"Why? If it's going to activate anyway—"

"Because dungeons that activate naturally spawn monsters without limit. Dungeons that are cleared by a party before activation become permanent resources. Safe zones. Training grounds." Mentor's golden eyes gleamed. "Clear that dungeon before the first wave, and you claim it. You, your mother, your friends—you have a sanctuary."

Kai's hands clenched. "And if I don't?"

"Then when the monsters come, they will come through an uncontrolled gate. Your school will become a slaughterhouse. And the monsters will spread."

Silence stretched between them. Outside, Kai could hear the city waking up—cars honking, people shouting, the distant wail of sirens. The world was changing, and most people had no idea how fast.

"One hundred circulations in twelve hours," Kai said slowly. "I barely managed ten in six."

"Your technique was inefficient. You were learning by instinct." Mentor reached into the air and pulled out a scroll, its surface covered in flowing characters that seemed to shift as Kai watched. "This is the Meridian Breathing Method. It will increase your circulation speed by a factor of three. But it comes at a cost."

"What cost?"

"Each circulation will drain more of your vital energy. You will need to eat. A lot. And you will need to sleep precisely four hours after you finish, or your body will begin consuming itself."

Kai took the scroll. It felt warm in his hands, almost alive. "Why are you helping me? If the Architect wants to study me, shouldn't you be letting me fail?"

Mentor was silent for a long moment. When she spoke, her voice was barely a whisper.

"I was once like you. A deviation. A glitch in a previous world's integration. The Architect studied me, broke me, rebuilt me into what I am now." She met his eyes, and for the first time, Kai saw something human in them. Pain. Regret. "I did not have a mentor. I will not let you suffer the same fate."

Before Kai could respond, she faded, dissolving into golden particles that scattered like dust in sunlight.

He stood alone in his apartment, scroll in hand, the weight of everything pressing down on him.

*One hundred circulations. Twelve hours.*

He unrolled the scroll, reading the first line of instructions. The characters glowed, searing themselves into his memory. He sat down, crossed his legs, and began.

---

Eleven hours later, Kai collapsed.

His body was wracked with tremors, sweat soaking through his clothes, his dantian burning like a furnace. But he'd done it. One hundred and three circulations. Three extra, just to be sure.

[QUEST COMPLETE: FOUNDATION OF DAWN]
[REWARD: MERIDIAN STABILIZATION, +1 QI RESERVOIR CAPACITY]
[NEW ABILITY UNLOCKED: QI SIGHT (STAGE 1)]

The notification flickered, and for a moment, Kai saw the world as it truly was—threads of energy connecting everything, the dungeon seeds pulsing like hearts beneath the earth, the System's code running like rivers through the air.

And at the center of it all, watching him, a presence so vast it made Mentor seem like a candle flame before a sun.

The Architect.

*Interesting.*

The word echoed in his skull, ancient and amused. *You grow faster than expected. But speed is not enough. Stability is not enough. You must be tested.*

The presence receded, but its parting words lingered.

*Seven days. Prove yourself worthy of my attention. Or be discarded.*

Kai gasped, the vision fading, leaving him trembling on his apartment floor. His phone buzzed. A text from his mother.

*Sweetie, are you okay? The news is saying something about monsters. I'm scared.*

He typed back with shaking hands.

*I'm coming to get you. Stay inside. Lock the doors. Don't let anyone in.*

He grabbed his jacket, his keys, the scroll still warm in his pocket. Outside, the sun was setting, painting the city in shades of orange and red. Somewhere in the distance, a scream echoed—high, terrified, abruptly cut off.

The first wave wasn't in seven days.

It had already begun.

title: The Price of Power
content:
Kai's finger hovered over the send button when the world lurched.

The notification split into three, then five, then a dozen cascading windows that flooded his vision:

[SYSTEM BROADCAST: TUTORIAL PHASE COMPLETE]
[ALL CLASSES HAVE BEEN ASSIGNED]
[WORLD INTEGRATION STAGE 2 INITIATING]
[MANA DENSITY INCREASING: 0.01% → 0.1%]
[DUNGEONS GENERATING: 47 ACTIVE SITES DETECTED]
[MONSTER SPAWNS ACTIVATED: URBAN ZONES MARKED]
[CLASS EVOLUTION UNLOCKED AT LEVEL 10]
[FIRST CLEAR BONUS: ACTIVE FOR NEXT 72 HOURS]

His phone exploded with notifications. The group chat went silent for exactly three seconds, then erupted.

*Holy shit dungeons*
*There's a spawn point in my kitchen. A FUCKING SLIME IN MY KITCHEN*
*47 sites? That's not a lot right? RIGHT?*

Kai swung his legs over the bed, ignoring the protest from his qi-sore muscles. The cockroach he'd sensed earlier was dead—he could feel its life force wink out, replaced by something else. Something colder. Hungrier.

*Scritch. Scritch. Scritch.*

The sound came from inside his wall.

He reached out with his qi sense, and his blood ran cold. A shape moved through the drywall, roughly the size of a cat but with too many legs. Its qi signature was faint but *wrong*—a discordant note in the symphony of the world, like a single instrument playing off-key.

"Mentor," he whispered. "What's happening?"

Silence. Then, her voice in his mind, tinged with something he hadn't heard before: concern.

*The System accelerates. Your world adapts faster than projected. The veil between planes thins before schedule.*

"You knew this would happen?"

*I knew it could. There are variables even I cannot calculate. One of them is you.*

Another crash from the wall, closer now. The thing was moving toward his apartment door. Toward the hallway. Toward the rest of the building.

Kai grabbed his jacket—the same one he'd worn to the tutorial, still smelling faintly of ozone—and stepped into the hallway.

The building was chaos.

Mrs. Chen from 3B was screaming at a floating mass of shadows that had manifested outside her door. Mr. Rodriguez, the retired marine from 2A, had somehow acquired a sword—literally materialized from light—and was hacking at a creature that looked like a giant centipede made of rusted metal and teeth. The air hummed with ambient mana, thick enough now that Kai could taste it on his tongue, metallic and sharp.

And everywhere, *everywhere*, people were staring at holographic screens, swiping through interfaces, comparing stats.

"Level 2 already! How are you level 2?"

"I found a mana crystal in my sock drawer. My SOCK DRAWER."

"Does anyone have a healing skill? Mrs. Chen got scratched!"

Kai pushed through the crowd, using his qi sense to navigate. The chaos was disorienting, but beneath it, he could feel patterns—flows of mana that moved like currents in a river. Some areas were dead zones, where the energy pooled and stagnated. Others were rapids, where it churned and twisted.

And one spot, three blocks away, was a whirlpool.

He stopped at the building's entrance, staring toward the disturbance. The mana there wasn't just dense—it was *alive*. It pulsed with a rhythm that matched his heartbeat, as if it recognized him.

*The dungeon,* Mentor said. *This city's primary convergence point. It calls to those with potential.*

"Should I go?"

*You should run. You are not ready.*

"Then why are you telling me about it?"

A pause. When Mentor spoke again, her voice was softer. *Because running is not in your nature. I have observed you for six hours of cultivation and twenty-three years of memory. You have never once chosen the easy path.*

She wasn't wrong.

Kai took a step toward the dungeon. Then another. His body screamed in protest—the qi in his meridians still unstable, his foundation barely formed—but something deeper pulled him forward. The same instinct that had driven him to complete the tutorial despite the 2% death rate. The same stubborn refusal to let the world change without him changing with it.

His phone buzzed again. He glanced at it.

The group chat had shifted from panic to organization. Someone had created a map of spawn points. Someone else had compiled a list of classes and their apparent roles. A third person was coordinating a response team.

*Warriors front line, Healers behind, Mages in back. Basic formation. Who's in?*

Kai typed a single response: *I'm in. But I'm not a Warrior.*

*Then what are you?*

He thought about it. The Architect. The class that the System didn't understand. The one that built foundations instead of breaking bones.

*Support,* he typed. *I'll explain when I get there.*

He ran.

---

The dungeon entrance was a crack in reality.

It hung in the air above an abandoned parking lot, shimmering like heat haze but cold to the touch. The edges were jagged, as if something had torn through the fabric of the world and left the wound unhealed. Mana bled from it in visible waves, distorting the light around it.

A crowd had gathered. Some were gawking, phones out, recording for their social media feeds. Others stood with weapons drawn—improvised bats, kitchen knives, a few actual swords that had materialized from class summons. Their eyes were wild, hungry.

"First clear bonus," someone muttered. "Whoever beats it first gets extra rewards."

"Levels. Items. Maybe even rare skills."

"The government's gonna try to lock this down. We gotta move now."

Kai pushed to the front of the crowd, and several people turned to stare. He was unremarkable—average height, average build, wearing a jacket that had seen better decades—but something about him made them step back.

It was his qi. He could feel it now, seeping from his pores like heat from a furnace. His cultivation had changed him in ways he was only beginning to understand. His presence registered on a level that normal people couldn't process, triggering an instinctive wariness.

"Who's this?" a man demanded. He was tall, broad-shouldered, with a sword that glowed faintly blue. A Warrior, likely Level 2 or 3. "You a Healer? We need Healers."

"Support," Kai said.

"We don't need support. We need people who can fight."

"You'll need me."

The Warrior scoffed, but before he could respond, the dungeon entrance *pulsed*. The crack widened, and a wave of mana washed over them—thick, suffocating, carrying the scent of ozone and decay.

From within, a voice emerged. Not a human voice. Something older, deeper, resonating with the weight of ages.

*One may enter. One may claim the prize. One may prove worthy.*

The crowd went silent.

"One?" someone whispered. "Just one?"

*The dungeon tests the individual, not the group. Send your champion, or flee. The choice is yours.*

The Warrior turned to Kai, a cruel smile spreading across his face. "Looks like you're out, support boy. This requires real combat potential."

He stepped forward, and the crowd parted for him. He was confident, arrogant, radiating the easy certainty of someone who had always been stronger than those around him.

Kai watched him approach the dungeon entrance. Watched him reach out to touch the shimmering crack.

And then he reached out with his qi sense.

What he found made his blood freeze.

The dungeon wasn't testing combat. It wasn't testing strength or speed or skill. The mana pattern was wrong—asymmetrical, unbalanced, structured like a cage rather than a challenge. It was a trap. A feeding mechanism designed to draw in the powerful and consume them.

"Wait," Kai said.

The Warrior didn't stop.

"Wait! It's a trap!"

The Warrior glanced back, amused. "Scared, support boy? Stay here. I'll bring back the loot."

He stepped through the crack.

For a moment, nothing happened. The shimmer held, the mana pulsed, the crowd watched with bated breath.

Then the screaming started.

It wasn't a human scream. It was the sound of a soul being unmade, torn apart and reassembled into something new. The crack in reality *shuddered*, and a hand emerged—not the Warrior's hand, but something else. Something covered in scales, tipped with claws, dripping with black ichor.

The hand grabbed the edge of the crack and pulled.

The dungeon was not a dungeon. It was a doorway. And something on the other side was trying to open it from within.

The crowd scattered. Kai stood frozen, his mind racing. The System had generated dungeons, yes—but what if some of them weren't what they seemed? What if the integration wasn't just bringing monsters into their world, but opening channels for something worse?

*Mentor,* he thought. *What do I do?*

Her response was immediate, and terrifying.

*The Architect builds, Kai. But sometimes, the Architect must also unbuild. The structure before you is flawed. Can you see it?*

He reached out with his qi sense, forcing calm through the panic. The crack in reality was a pattern—a weave of mana and intention, tangled and corrupted. But beneath the corruption, there was structure. A framework. A design.

He could see the seams.

And he knew, with a certainty that defied logic, that he could pull them apart.

"Stay back," he said to the few people who hadn't fled. "All of you. Get to cover."

He stepped forward, hands raised, and began to trace patterns in the air with his qi. The movements were unfamiliar, but his body remembered them from the tutorial—muscle memory implanted by Mentor, waiting to be awakened.

The crack hissed. The hand withdrew, replaced by an eye—massive, pupil-less, burning with cold fire.

*You,* it said. *We sensed you. The anomaly. The variable.*

"Who are you?" Kai demanded.

*We are what comes after. We are the end of your world's isolation. And you... you are the key.*

The crack widened. Mana exploded outward, throwing Kai backward. He hit the ground hard, his vision swimming, his dantian screaming in protest.

But he didn't stop tracing.

The patterns were working. He could feel them, threads of pure order weaving through the chaos, tightening, pulling, *restructuring*. The dungeon's framework was crumbling, the trap unraveling, the doorway closing.

*No!* The voice shrieked. *You cannot—this is not your world to protect!*

"It's not yours to take," Kai growled.

He pulled.

The crack snapped shut.

The mana vanished. The eye disappeared. The parking lot was silent, empty, untouched—as if nothing had happened.

Kai lay on the concrete, gasping, his meridians burning with overuse. His vision flickered, and a System notification appeared:

[ARCHITECT SKILL UNLOCKED: STRUCTURAL ANALYSIS]
[ARCHITECT SKILL UNLOCKED: PATTERN WEAVING]
[WARNING: QI DEVIATION RISK INCREASED TO 5%]

He didn't have time to process it. His phone buzzed again. The group chat.

*Kai, where

title: The First Casualties
content:
Kai's thumb hovered over the send button as the broadcast continued scrolling across his vision like a ticker tape from hell:

[WORLD INTEGRATION PHASE 2 INITIATING IN 24 HOURS]
[ALL SYSTEM FUNCTIONS NOW ACTIVE: LEVELING, SKILLS, ATTRIBUTES]
[MONSTER SPAWNING WILL BEGIN AT DAWN]
[CLASS RESTRICTIONS: NONE]
[REWARDS FOR SYSTEM COMPREHENSION ACTIVE]

Monster spawning.

The words hit him like a physical blow. He sat up so fast his vision swam, the qi in his meridians sloshing unpleasantly. His phone buzzed again, but he ignored it, staring at the golden text that still lingered in the corner of his eye.

*Monsters. Actual monsters.*

He'd read enough web novels to know what that meant. Dungeons. Raids. Experience points earned through blood and terror. The System wasn't just a game overlay—it was a catalyst for extinction.

His phone buzzed continuously now. He picked it up.

The group chat had exploded.

*Jake: MONSTERS?? LIKE WITH TEETH AND CLAWS?*
*Mark: I just got a quest. [Kill 5 Goblins. Reward: 50 XP, Bronze Dagger]. This is real.*
*Sarah: My tutorial told me to "prepare for the Culling." What the hell does that mean?*
*Unknown Number: The arrays are forming. I can see them from my window. You need to get out of the city.*

Kai's blood went cold.

*Arrays.*

He scrambled to his feet, ignoring the protest from his muscles, and stumbled to his apartment window. The street below looked normal—cars honking, people walking, a vendor selling skewers on the corner. But when he reached out with his qi sense, he felt it.

Pressure.

Like invisible pillars of force pressing down on the city from above. They were subtle, barely perceptible, like the static charge before a thunderstorm. But they were *there*, arranged in a pattern that made his newly awakened instincts scream.

*That's a formation,* Mentor's voice echoed in his memory. *The System uses them to contain and direct energy flows.*

"Contain what?" Kai whispered.

His phone rang. He answered without looking.

"Kai." His mother's voice, tight with controlled fear. "Tell me you're somewhere safe."

"I'm in my apartment, Mom. Where are you?"

"Home. Your father's here too. We—" She paused, and he heard muffled conversation in the background. "The news is saying there were attacks. In Tokyo. And São Paulo. People died."

"Attacks?"

"Monsters. They just... appeared. In the middle of streets. Shopping malls. The military is scrambling, but they don't know what they're dealing with."

Kai's grip tightened on the phone. "Mom, listen to me. Don't go outside. Barricade the doors. If you see anything—anything that looks like it came out of a fantasy game—stay away from it."

"Kai, what happened to you? In your tutorial?"

He wanted to tell her. He wanted to explain about Mentor, about qi, about the dantian that now pulsed with foreign energy in his stomach. But the words wouldn't come. How do you tell your mother that you've become something the System itself doesn't understand?

"I got a class," he said finally. "A strange one. But I think it might be useful."

"Useful how?"

"I don't know yet. But I'm going to figure it out."

---

He spent the next hour cycling through his apartment like a caged animal, testing the limits of his new abilities.

The qi sense was the easiest to work with. It had become second nature, like a sixth sense that had always been there but only now awakened. He could feel the life forces of everyone in his building—the elderly woman three floors down, the couple arguing two doors over, the teenager playing video games in the basement.

*Thirty-seven people,* he counted. *Thirty-seven souls, each with their own qi signature.*

But there was something else. Faint, but growing. Like distant drums beating in rhythm. He focused on it, and the sensation sharpened—qi signatures that didn't feel human. Jagged. Hungry. Wrong.

*The monsters are coming. They're already spawning.*

He checked his phone again. The news was a waterfall of chaos. Videos of creatures materializing in broad daylight—wolf-like things with too many eyes, humanoid figures made of shadow, birds that left trails of frost in their wake. Death tolls climbed by the minute.

And everywhere, people were leveling.

Warriors were charging into battle with newly acquired swords. Mages were hurling fireballs from rooftops. Healers were dragging the wounded from wreckage, their hands glowing with golden light. The world was adapting, its people evolving into weapons.

But Kai had seen behind the curtain. He'd seen Mentor's golden eyes, heard her warning about the 2% chance of death from qi deviation. He knew that the System wasn't a game—it was a test. And the pass/fail rate was measured in corpses.

His phone buzzed again. A private message from an unknown number.

*You're Kai Chen? The one with the unclassified class?*

He stared at the message. Who would know about his class? The only people who should have that information were the System itself and—

*Mentor.*

He typed back: *Who is this?*

The reply came instantly: *Meet me at the Central Library. 3rd floor, east reading room. Come alone. I know about the Architect class.*

Kai's heart hammered. This could be a trap. Someone could have hacked the System, or worse, someone could be hunting unclassified class holders. But if there was another person who understood what he was going through—

*I need answers.*

He grabbed his jacket and headed for the door.

---

The streets were a warzone of normalcy and madness.

People walked their dogs, bought coffee, argued on phones—while in the distance, plumes of smoke rose from where the first monster attacks had occurred. Sirens wailed, a constant backdrop of urgency that most civilians seemed determined to ignore.

Kai moved through the crowd with his qi sense extended, mapping every life force within a block radius. He felt the Warriors, their qi bright and aggressive, already training in hidden spots. He felt the Mages, their energy flowing in controlled streams as they practiced their spells. He felt the Healers, their warmth like gentle flames in the cold city.

And he felt the monsters.

They were few, for now. Scattered. But their signatures were unmistakable—qi that was corrupted, twisted, hungry. He passed an alley and felt one lurking in the shadows, watching. It didn't attack. Not yet. But it was waiting.

*They're scouting,* he realized. *Learning our patterns.*

The Central Library loomed ahead, a Gothic revival structure that had survived three centuries of urban development. Kai climbed the steps, his senses on high alert. The building's qi was old, layered with decades of human presence, but there was something else mixed in—a faint trace of something that didn't belong.

He found the east reading room on the third floor. It was empty, save for a single figure sitting at a table near the window.

A woman. Early twenties, with short-cropped hair and sharp eyes that tracked him the moment he entered. She wore a simple hoodie and jeans, but Kai's qi sense told him everything he needed to know—she was a Warrior, Level 5, with a specialization he couldn't quite identify.

"You're Kai," she said. It wasn't a question.

"And you are?"

"Lin Mei. I'm a Seeker."

"Seeker? That's not a standard class."

"Neither is Architect." She gestured to the chair across from her. "Sit. We don't have much time."

Kai remained standing. "How do you know about my class?"

"Because I know about all the unclassified classes. There are twelve of us in this city. Maybe more worldwide." She leaned forward, her eyes intense. "The System gave everyone a class from its database. Warriors, Mages, Healers, Crafters, Scouts—all pre-defined templates. But some of us got something else. Something the System didn't create."

"What do you mean?"

"The System is a framework. It assigns classes based on potential, but it can only assign what it understands." Lin Mei pulled out her phone and showed him a screen filled with code. "I've been reverse-engineering the System's architecture. It's like a massive program, layers upon layers of rules and permissions. But there are gaps. Holes in the code where the framework doesn't apply."

"And those holes are the unclassified classes?"

"Yes. The System doesn't recognize us because we don't fit its categories. We're outliers. Anomalies. And that makes us valuable." She paused. "And dangerous."

Kai finally sat down. "Dangerous to whom?"

"To the System itself. And to whoever—or whatever—created it."

The words hung in the air between them. Outside, another siren wailed past, and Kai felt the distant pulse of a monster spawn, closer than before.

"Why are you telling me this?"

"Because we need to organize. The unclassified classes are scattered, isolated. We don't have tutorials that teach us how to level. We don't have skill trees or predefined paths. We have to figure everything out ourselves, and the System is actively working against us." She pulled up a document on her phone. "In the last hour, three unclassified class holders have died. One from qi deviation, two from monster attacks that specifically targeted them."

"Targeted?"

"The monsters can sense us. We don't register as normal class holders, so we show up as threats. The System is flagging us for elimination."

Kai's blood ran cold. He thought about Mentor's warning, about the 2% chance of death that had seemed abstract at the time. It wasn't abstract. It was a statistical probability, and the odds were getting worse.

"We need to find the others," Lin Mei continued. "There's a safehouse in the industrial district. Someone who's been studying the System since before the integration. They might have answers."

"Who?"

"I don't know their name. But they've been posting encrypted messages on the dark web, talking about the System's core code. They claim to have found something—something about the true purpose of the integration."

Kai's instincts screamed at him to be cautious. This could still be a trap. Lin Mei could be working for something else, someone else. But the alternative was facing this alone, and he'd already seen how that ended.

"I'll come," he said. "But I need to check on my parents first."

"Fine. But hurry. The safehouse contact said they'd only wait until midnight." Lin Mei stood, her movements fluid with the grace of her Warrior class. "And Kai? Be careful. The unclassified classes aren't the only ones hunting anomalies."

She walked past him and disappeared down the stairwell, leaving Kai alone with the dying light of the afternoon sun streaming through the library windows.

He pulled out his phone and called his mother again. No answer.

He called his father. No answer.

He tried the home line. Busy signal.

His qi sense flared, and he felt it—a sudden spike of corrupted energy, less than a mile away. In the direction of his parents' apartment.

Kai ran.

title: Chapter 5
content:
CHAPTER 5: The First Blood in the City

Kai's thumb hovered over the send button when the world went silent.

Not literally. The refrigerator still hummed. Traffic still rumbled outside his window. But something deeper had shifted—a pressure that pressed against his consciousness like a thumb on a bruise.

The System notification pulsed:

[SYSTEM BROADCAST: TUTORIAL PHASE COMPLETE]
[ALL CLASSES HAVE BEEN ASSIGNED]
[WORLD INTEGRATION INITIATING]
[WARNING: UNAUTHORIZED MODIFICATIONS DETECTED IN CLASS ARCHITECTURE]
[INVESTIGATION PROTOCOL ENGAGED]

Kai's blood turned cold.

*Unauthorized modifications. Investigation protocol.*

He was still holding his phone when a second message came through, this one private:

[SYSTEM ALERT: CLASS "DAO ARCHITECT" FLAGGED FOR REVIEW]
[STATUS: UNDER OBSERVATION]
[CONTINUED USE MAY TRIGGER MANUAL INTERVENTION]

"Shit."

He threw off the covers and stood too fast, his newly awakened meridians protesting with a sharp throb. His dantian pulsed in rhythm with his heartbeat, the qi inside it sloshing like liquid mercury in a glass bowl.

*Mentor said the System would notice eventually. But not this fast.*

His phone buzzed again. The group chat had exploded:

*DID EVERYONE GET THAT?*
*Yeah, what the hell does "Investigation Protocol" mean?*
*My tutorial guide said it's normal. Something about balancing classes.*
*That's not what mine said.*

Kai scrolled faster:

*Mine said RUN.*

Then:

*Anyone else's class feel WRONG?*

A new message appeared from an unknown number:

*Kai. It's Jake. Call me. Now.*

Jake had his number from the old gaming days, but they'd never actually called. Texting was their language. For him to demand a call meant something was very, very wrong.

Kai hit dial.

"Finally." Jake's voice was tight, clipped. "Where are you?"

"Apartment. What's going on?"

"Look outside your window."

Kai crossed to the window and pulled back the curtain. His apartment faced an alley, but beyond that, he could see the main street. People were moving—but not normally. They walked with purpose, heads swiveling, eyes scanning.

"See them?" Jake asked.

"Who are they?"

"Players. First-wave class assignees. The ones who got combat classes in their tutorials." Jake's voice dropped. "They're hunting."

"Hunting what?"

"Anything that gives XP. Roaches. Rats. Stray dogs." A pause. "Each other."

The bottom dropped out of Kai's stomach. "What?"

"Word spread fast. Killing gives XP. Not just monsters—anything. Someone in my tutorial tested it. He killed another trainee. Leveled up twice in a minute."

Kai's mind raced. In the game-like System that everyone else experienced, death was temporary. Respawn at checkpoints. But Mentor had told him the truth: this wasn't a game. When you died in the System, you stayed dead.

"Jake, listen to me—"

"No, you listen. I know you got something different. I saw your class in the friend list. It's greyed out. Unclassified. People are gonna notice."

"Who else knows?"

"Just me for now. But the friend list updates every hour. Once people check theirs, they're gonna see that blank spot where your class should be."

Kai pressed his palm against the cold glass. His qi sense extended automatically, brushing against the life forces outside. Most were dim—normal humans, still adjusting. But a few burned bright. Combat classes. Players who'd already started grinding.

"Jake, what class did you get?"

"...Shadow Stalker. Assassin-type. My tutorial was kill-or-be-killed." A bitter laugh. "Guess the System knows what I'm good at."

"Don't go out there. Stay inside. Lock your door."

"Kai, I'm not the one who needs protection. I can handle myself. But you—you're a walking question mark. And in a System that hates unknowns, that makes you a target."

The call went dead.

Kai stared at his phone, then at the window, then at his own reflection—pale, tired, eyes holding a faint luminescence that hadn't been there yesterday.

*What do I do?*

He needed information. Mentor had given him the basics of cultivation, but she'd said nothing about how to navigate a world where everyone else was playing a game while he was building a path.

His phone buzzed again. This time, it was a System notification:

[NEW FEATURE UNLOCKED: LOCAL CHAT]
[RANGE: 1KM]
[ALL PLAYERS IN RANGE HAVE BEEN ADDED]

A chat window opened in his vision, text scrolling faster than he could read:

*Anyone near 5th and Main? There's a group forming. We're clearing the subway station.*
*XP is good. Mobs spawn every 15 minutes.*
*Watch out for the rats. They hit hard.*
*Anyone seen a Mage? Need buffs.*
*I'm at the park. Found a boss spawn.*

Kai's finger twitched. He could join them. Pretend he was a normal player. Grind levels, get stronger, figure out his class later.

But he knew, with a certainty that came from his brief time with Mentor, that wouldn't work. His path was different. If he tried to play their game, he'd lose.

Another notification:

[SYSTEM BROADCAST: FIRST BLOOD EVENT ACTIVE]
[KILL 10 ENEMIES WITHIN 24 HOURS]
[REWARD: RARE SKILL ORB]
[BONUS REWARD FOR FIRST 1000 COMPLETIONS]

The chat exploded:

*FIRST BLOOD EVENT?*
*Rare skill orb? That's huge!*
*Time to hunt.*

Kai closed the window. His hands were shaking.

He needed to cultivate. Needed to stabilize his foundation. But how could he meditate when the world outside was turning into a bloodbath?

His doorbell rang.

Kai froze. He extended his qi sense carefully, feeling for the presence at his door. One person. Male. Qi signature was... strange. Not like the bright flames of the combat players outside, not like the dim pulse of normal humans. This one was cold. Controlled. Deliberate.

"Kai?" The voice was muffled through the door. "It's Chen. From the tutorial? My apartment's two floors up."

Chen. The quiet guy who'd barely spoken during the entire white room experience. Kai remembered his class: [Null]. Same as the others.

But his qi felt nothing like a normal class.

"I know you're there. Open up."

Every instinct screamed at Kai to refuse. But his qi sense picked up something else—a faint thread of recognition. Chen's qi signature resonated with his own. Not the same, but similar. Like two instruments tuned to the same frequency.

Kai opened the door.

Chen stood in the hallway, dressed in sweatpants and a hoodie. He looked exactly as he had in the tutorial—unremarkable, forgettable, the kind of face you'd pass on the street and never remember.

But his eyes held the same faint luminescence as Kai's.

"You felt it too," Chen said. It wasn't a question.

"Felt what?"

"The investigation. The flag." Chen glanced down the hallway, then back at Kai. "We need to talk. There are others like us."

"Others?"

"People who got different classes. Unclassified. Greyed out." Chen's voice dropped. "I've found four so far. We're meeting in an hour. Basement of the old library on 3rd."

Kai studied him. His qi sense didn't detect lies, but it didn't detect truth either. Chen's signature was too controlled for that.

"Why should I trust you?"

"Because the System just flagged you." Chen pulled up his sleeve, revealing a mark on his forearm—a glowing symbol that Kai didn't recognize. "And because I've already been flagged twice. I know what comes next."

"What comes next?"

Chen's expression didn't change, but something flickered in his eyes. Fear.

"Deletion."

The word hung in the air between them. The refrigerator hummed. Traffic rumbled. Somewhere in the distance, a scream cut through the city noise.

Kai made his decision.

"One hour. I'll be there."

Chen nodded and turned to leave, then paused. "One more thing. Don't use your qi sense on the others when you meet them. Some of them... they're not what they seem."

He disappeared down the stairwell before Kai could ask what that meant.

Kai closed the door and leaned against it, his heart pounding. His phone buzzed again—more messages, more chaos, more players rushing to kill for XP.

But he ignored them. Instead, he closed his eyes and reached into his dantian, feeling the qi swirl inside him. He'd only completed ten successful circulations. His foundation was fragile, barely formed.

*I need more time.*

But time was the one thing the System wasn't giving him.

He opened his eyes and grabbed his jacket. The library was six blocks away. Through streets that were quickly becoming war zones.

As he stepped out of his apartment, a final notification appeared:

[SYSTEM ALERT: SECONDARY INVESTIGATION INITIATED]
[TARGET: CLASS "DAO ARCHITECT"]
[ESTIMATED TIME TO RESOLUTION: 23 HOURS, 47 MINUTES]

Twenty-three hours. Less than a day before the System decided whether to delete him.

Kai walked into the chaos of his transformed world, knowing that the clock was already ticking.

title: Chapter 6
content:
CHAPTER 6: The First Quest

Kai's phone buzzed again before he could send his message. Then again. And again. The group chat exploded with notifications as everyone reacted to the System broadcast.

*World integration? What does that mean?*

*I just got a quest! [Kill 5 Goblins - Reward: 50 XP, 10 Credits]*

*Goblins? Where?!*

Kai's blood ran cold. He scrambled out of bed, ignoring the protest from his sore muscles, and stumbled to his apartment window. Outside, the street looked normal. Cars passed. People walked their dogs. A delivery drone hummed overhead.

But something was wrong.

The qi in the air had changed. It was thicker, denser, more active. Like the atmosphere before a thunderstorm, charged and waiting. And beneath that charge, Kai sensed something else—presences that hadn't been there before. Alien. Hostile.

His phone screen flickered, and a new notification appeared, this one personal:

[QUEST AVAILABLE: TUTORIAL COMPLETION]
[Objective: Survive the Arrival]
[Time Limit: 24 Hours]
[Reward: Variable based on performance]
[Penalty for Failure: None]
[Note: This is a tutorial quest. Failure simply means the System will reassign you to a more suitable path.]

Kai read the note three times. *Reassign you to a more suitable path.* The words were carefully neutral, but the implication was clear. Fail, and you get a different class. One that might be worse. Or one that might not exist at all.

He thought of Mentor's warning. *The System doesn't recognize your class.*

If he failed this quest, what would the System reassign him to? Would it even be able to?

Another buzz. His friend Mark had sent a video to the group chat. Kai opened it.

The footage was shaky, filmed on a phone held by trembling hands. Mark's apartment—Kai recognized the messy gaming setup in the background. But the focus was on the window. Or rather, what was trying to get through it.

A creature pressed against the glass. Humanoid, but wrong. Its skin was a mottled green, its face a nightmare of sharp teeth and yellow eyes. It clawed at the pane, leaving scratches that sparked with each strike.

"Bro, what do I do?!" Mark's voice cracked from the video. "It's been out there for five minutes. My door's barricaded but—"

The glass shattered.

The video ended.

Kai's hands moved before his mind caught up. He called Mark's number. It rang. Once. Twice. Three times.

Voicemail.

"Mark, pick up. Pick up, man."

He called again. Nothing.

The group chat had gone silent. Then, slowly, messages started trickling in:

*Something's at my door.*

*Anyone else hearing howling?*

*I think there's a dungeon forming in the park. I can see the entrance from my window. It's... glowing.*

Kai forced himself to breathe. Panic wouldn't help. He needed information. He needed to understand what was happening.

He closed his eyes and extended his qi sense, pushing it further than he had before. The world unfolded in his mind—a web of life forces, qi signatures, and something else. Rifts. Tiny tears in the fabric of reality, scattered across the city like wounds. Through them, he could feel the presences. Monsters. Dozens of them. Hundreds.

And they were getting closer.

His phone buzzed again. This time, it wasn't a message. It was a call. Unknown number.

He answered. "Hello?"

"Kai Chen." The voice was familiar—calm, measured, and utterly inhuman. Mentor. "You have awakened. Good. You are ahead of schedule."

"Ahead of schedule? There are monsters breaking into people's homes! My friend—"

"Is likely dead."

The words hit like a punch to the gut.

"Mortality is the first lesson of cultivation," Mentor continued, her tone unchanged. "Attachment is a weakness. Those who cannot protect themselves will fall. The System does not discriminate. It simply observes."

"I don't accept that." Kai's voice came out harder than he expected. "Mark is alive. I can feel it. His qi signature—it's still there. Faint, but there."

A pause. "You can sense individual qi signatures from this distance?"

"I don't know. I just... can."

"Interesting." Another pause, longer this time. "Your foundation is expanding faster than I anticipated. The Dao Architect class is adapting to you. Or perhaps you are adapting to it. Either way, this changes things."

"What do you mean?"

"The System's tutorial phase was designed to acclimate the population to their new reality. Those who succeed will grow. Those who fail will be recycled. But your path is different. The Dao Architect does not participate in the System's hierarchy. It builds its own."

Kai stared at the phone, processing her words. "You're saying I'm not supposed to follow the quests?"

"I am saying that the quests are designed for classes the System understands. Yours is not one of them. If you follow the System's path, you will be forced into a mold that does not fit. You will cripple your potential."

"Then what do I do?"

"Your first task is simple. Find the rifts. Close them. Not through the System's interface, but through your own understanding of qi. You have the tools. Now you must learn to use them."

The line went dead.

Kai lowered the phone, his mind racing. Close the rifts. Through qi. He had no idea how to do that. His cultivation was barely stable. He'd only completed ten successful qi circulations. And now he was supposed to—

A crash from outside interrupted his thoughts.

He rushed to the window. In the street below, a car had flipped over, its hood crumpled. A creature stood on top of it—larger than the one in Mark's video, more bestial. It had four arms, each ending in claws that glinted with an unnatural light. Its head swiveled, scanning the street, and Kai felt its gaze pass over his building.

*It knows I'm here.*

He stepped back from the window, his heart pounding. His dantian pulsed, the qi inside it reacting to his fear, surging and ebbing like a restless tide. He could feel the creature's presence now—a dark, twisted qi signature that felt wrong in a way he couldn't articulate. Like a stain on reality.

*Close the rifts.*

How?

He focused, reaching out with his qi sense, tracing the creature's signature back to its source. It led him to a shimmering tear in the air, hovering above the street. The rift. It pulsed with a malevolent energy, and as he watched, another creature stepped through it.

Two. Now two.

The first creature—four arms, bestial—sniffed the air. Then it turned, its gaze locking onto Kai's window.

*It saw me.*

Kai moved. He grabbed his backpack, stuffed a few essentials inside—water, a charger, a granola bar—and slung it over his shoulder. His hands were shaking, but his mind was clear. He couldn't fight these things. Not yet. He needed time. He needed to understand.

But understanding wouldn't come from hiding in his apartment.

He opened his door and stepped into the hallway. The lights were flickering, casting shadows that seemed to move on their own. The air was thick with qi, heavy and oppressive. He could hear sounds from the other apartments—screaming, crashing, the occasional burst of System-enhanced combat.

A woman ran past him, her eyes wild. "They're everywhere! They came through the—"

She didn't finish. A shadow detached itself from the wall, a creature of pure darkness, and wrapped around her. She vanished without a sound.

Kai's qi sense flared. The shadow creature turned toward him.

*Move.*

He ran. Down the stairs, three at a time, his body moving with a speed and precision he'd never possessed before. The qi in his dantian surged, feeding his muscles, sharpening his reflexes. He burst through the lobby doors and into the street.

Chaos.

Cars abandoned. Windows shattered. Bodies lying motionless on the pavement. And everywhere, the rifts. Dozens of them, scattered across the cityscape, each one a tear in the fabric of reality. Through them, he could feel the presences. More creatures. Waiting.

But he could also feel something else. A pattern.

The rifts weren't random. They formed a network, a web of energy that connected each tear to the others. And at the center of that web, somewhere deep in the city, was a source. A focal point. A rift that was larger than all the others.

*Close the rifts.*

Kai understood now. He couldn't close them one by one. That would take too long, and he didn't have the strength. But if he could reach the central rift, if he could understand its structure, maybe he could disrupt the entire network.

His phone buzzed. Another message from the group chat:

*Anyone still alive?*

He typed a quick reply: *Meet at the old warehouse on 5th street. Stay low. Don't fight unless you have to.*

Then he started running.

The city unfolded around him, a nightmare of qi and violence. He dodged a creature that looked like a giant spider made of crystal. He leaped over a rift that opened at his feet, its void-like pull nearly dragging him in. He felt his qi depleting, his stamina fading, but he pushed on.

Because Mentor was right. He couldn't follow the System's path. He had to build his own.

And that started with closing the rifts.

He reached the central rift an hour later. It stood in the middle of a collapsed building, a tear in space that shimmered with an almost beautiful light. The qi emanating from it was overwhelming, a pressure that made his knees buckle.

But as he studied it, he saw something else. The rift wasn't just a wound. It was a structure. A framework of energy, held together by a pattern. A pattern he recognized.

It was the same pattern Mentor had shown him in the tutorial. The foundation of the Dao.

*Oh no.*

The rift was a cultivation technique. A twisted, corrupted version of the same foundation he was building in his own dantian.

And it was growing.

Kai reached out with his qi sense, trying to understand the pattern, trying to find a way to unravel it. But as his qi touched the rift, something happened.

The rift responded.

It pulsed, and a voice echoed in his mind. Not Mentor's. Something older. Something vast.

*Architect. You have come.*

The ground beneath him cracked. The sky above him darkened. And the rift began to expand.

title: The First Blood
content:
Kai's phone slipped from his fingers as the System notification blazed in his vision, golden characters burning against the morning light streaming through his window.

[SYSTEM BROADCAST: TUTORIAL PHASE COMPLETE]
[ALL CLASSES HAVE BEEN ASSIGNED]
[WORLD INTEGRATION: 72 HOURS REMAINING]

His heart hammered. Three days. The System had given them three days before... what? He didn't know. Nobody knew.

The phone buzzed again, vibrating across his cheap wooden floor. He snatched it up.

The group chat had exploded.

*72 hours until what?*

*My tutorial orb said something about "stable integration window." Anyone else get that?*

*Stable? What happens if we're not integrated?*

Kai scrolled faster, but no one had answers. Just fear and confusion, the same emotions clawing at his chest.

Then a private message popped up. From his mother.

*Kai, something's wrong with your sister.*

Ice flooded his veins.

He dialed before he could think. Two rings. Three. On the fourth, his mother's voice crackled through, high and strained.

"Kai, thank God. She won't wake up. The ambulance came, but they said her vitals are normal. They said—they said it might be System-related. They said there's nothing they can do."

"I'm coming." He was already grabbing his jacket, shoving his feet into shoes. "Don't move her. Don't let anyone touch her. I'll be there in twenty minutes."

"Twenty minutes? The traffic—"

"I'll run."

He hung up and ran.

---

The streets of downtown had transformed in the hours he'd spent in the tutorial.

People gathered in clusters, some laughing, some crying. A teenager in a hoodie held a ball of fire in his palm, his face split by a manic grin. Two men argued loudly outside a coffee shop, one gesturing with a sword that gleamed with System-forged light—real metal, real edge, real danger.

Kai dodged around them, his breath coming hard. His muscles screamed from the six-hour cultivation session, but the qi in his meridians pulsed with each heartbeat, lending him speed he shouldn't have had. He leaped over a stalled car, landed lightly, and kept going.

He felt them before he saw them.

Three blocks from his mother's apartment, a cluster of qi signatures flared ahead—bright and aggressive, like bonfires in a dark forest. Fighters. Warriors. Their auras clashed with something else, something cold and sharp.

Kai rounded the corner and stopped.

A crowd had formed in the street, a loose ring of onlookers watching the spectacle. In the center, a young man in a leather jacket faced off against a creature that shouldn't exist.

It stood seven feet tall, its body a twisted mockery of human form—skin the color of bruised meat, arms too long and jointed wrong, a face with no mouth and six eyes arranged in a vertical line down its skull.

A monster. A real, actual monster.

The leather-jacket guy was a Warrior, Level 3 according to the System tag floating above his head. He held a crude iron sword, its edge already chipped from the fight. The monster had scored a gash across his arm, and blood dripped steadily onto the asphalt.

"Someone call the police!" a woman screamed.

"Police can't do anything," a man shouted back. "This is System shit!"

The monster lunged. The Warrior swung, his sword biting into the creature's shoulder. Black ichor sprayed, but the monster didn't slow. Its too-long arm whipped around, claws raking across the Warrior's chest, sending him crashing to the ground.

Kai's feet moved before his brain caught up.

He wasn't a fighter. He had no combat skills, no weapons, no idea what he was doing. But the qi in his meridians responded to his intent, flowing faster, hotter, gathering in his palms.

*What am I supposed to do with this?*

Mentor's voice echoed in his memory: *Qi is intent made manifest. Form follows thought.*

The monster turned toward him, all six eyes locking onto his position. Its mouthless face contorted, and a sound came from it—not a roar, but a vibration, a frequency that made his teeth ache and his vision blur.

Kai raised his hands.

He didn't think about techniques or combat forms. He thought about pressure, about the feeling of qi building in his dantian, about the way Mentor had shown him to compress and release.

He pushed.

A wave of invisible force erupted from his palms, slamming into the monster's chest. The creature staggered, its clawed feet scraping against the asphalt. Onlookers gasped. Someone screamed.

The monster recovered in an instant, its attention now fully fixed on Kai. It charged.

*Move.*

His body obeyed, flowing sideways with a grace he'd never possessed. The monster's claws whistled past his ear, close enough to ruffle his hair. He pivoted, one hand reaching out, fingers brushing the creature's arm.

And he saw.

Not with his eyes, but with his qi sense. The monster's internal structure unfolded before him like a diagram—dense energy at its core, a knot of corrupted qi that pulsed like a second heart. Threads of that energy ran through its body, powering its movements, connecting its limbs to that central mass.

*Break that, and it falls.*

He didn't know how he knew. He just knew.

The monster spun, claws arcing toward his throat. Kai ducked, sidestepped, and drove his fingers into the creature's torso—not with physical force, but with a spike of qi, sharp and focused, aimed directly at that corrupted core.

The qi struck.

The monster froze.

For one eternal second, their eyes met—those six dead orbs staring into his. Then the creature's body convulsed, its limbs jerking spasmodically. The corrupted core unraveled, its energy dissolving into wisps of black smoke that rose from the monster's skin.

The creature collapsed.

Silence.

Then the crowd erupted. Cheers. Screams. Questions shouted from every direction. Someone grabbed his arm, someone else was filming on their phone, and the fallen Warrior was being helped to his feet, staring at Kai with a mixture of awe and disbelief.

Kai pulled away, his heart pounding so hard he could barely hear.

He had just killed a monster.

With his bare hands.

With qi.

*What am I becoming?*

He pushed through the crowd, ignoring the hands reaching for him, the voices calling after him. He had to get to his sister. He had to—

Another System notification appeared, this one personal.

[SYSTEM ALERT: COMBAT ACHIEVEMENT UNLOCKED]
