"I didn't," Kai said quietly.

"You're lying."

"I'm not."

The man's fists crackled with renewed energy. "The System doesn't make mistakes. If you're hiding something—"

A new notification appeared, cutting him off.

[SYSTEM BROADCAST: DUNGEON ENTRANCE LOCATED]
[LOCATION: NAMSAN TOWER, SEOUL]
[CLEAR REQUIREMENT: DEFEAT BOSS MONSTER]
[REWARDS: CLASS EVOLUTION, RANK UP, SYSTEM CREDITS]
[TIME LIMIT: 72 HOURS]

The crowd's attention shifted instantly. Dungeons. They'd heard about them in the broadcasts, read about them on forums. Places of danger and reward, where classes could evolve and power could be seized.

But Kai's blood ran colder still.

Because beneath the notification, written in text so small it was almost invisible, he saw something else. A message meant for him alone.

[NOTE: DUNGEON CLEAR REQUIRED FOR ANOMALOUS CLASS VALIDATION]
[FAILURE TO CLEAR: CLASS REVOCATION, SYSTEM REMOVAL, SOUL ERASURE]

*Soul erasure.*

Not death. *Erasure.* As if he'd never existed.

The electric-fisted man grabbed his shoulder, pulling him upright. "You're coming with us," he said. "If you really killed that thing alone, you're useful. And we need everyone useful for the dungeon."

Kai looked at the man's face, read the barely-concealed threat in his eyes. He looked at the crowd, at their hope and fear and desperation.

Then he looked at the notification still hanging in his vision, counting down the hours until his soul would be unmade.

"Fine," he said. "Lead the way."

But as he followed them out of the stairwell, stepping over Mr. Park's cooling body, he reached out with his qi sense—and felt it.

The dungeon at Namsan Tower wasn't just a dungeon.

It was a *beacon*. Pulses of energy radiating outward, calling to something beyond the veil of reality. And beneath that calling, buried deep in the dungeon's core, was a presence that made his dantian tremble with

title: The Fracture Beneath
content:
Kai's thumb hovered over the send button when the second notification hit.

[SYSTEM BROADCAST: WORLD INTEGRATION PHASE 1 INITIATED]
[MONSTER SPAWN ZONES: ACTIVE]
[CLASS SKILLS: UNLOCKED]
[MANA / QI SYSTEMS: ONLINE]

The air changed.

It wasn't visible—not at first. But Kai felt it like a pressure shift before a storm. The qi in his apartment, thin and artificial moments ago, thickened. It seeped through the walls, through the floorboards, through his own skin like humidity that carried weight.

His phone screen flickered. The group chat went silent.

Then came the screams.

Kai was on his feet before he consciously decided to move. His body responded with a speed that wasn't entirely his—muscles moving on instinct, qi flooding his legs as he crossed the apartment in three strides and threw open the front door.

The hallway was chaos.

Mrs. Chen from 3B was pressed against her door, clutching a kitchen knife in trembling hands. A creature stood before her—humanoid but wrong, its skin the color of bruised meat, its joints bending in directions that made Kai's hindbrain scream *predator*. It had no face. Just a smooth, featureless head that tilted as it tracked her movements.

"Kai—" Mrs. Chen's voice cracked.

The creature lunged.

Kai moved without thinking. His hand shot out, palm open, and he *pushed* with his qi. The technique was crude—Mentor would have called it "flailing"—but the energy burst from his dantian, through his arm, and slammed into the creature's chest.

It staggered.

That was all. It didn't fall. It didn't die. It just took a single step back, its featureless head swiveling toward Kai with predatory interest.

[SYSTEM NOTIFICATION: HOSTILE ENTITY DETECTED]
[BLANKSPAWN - LEVEL 3]
[THREAT LEVEL: MODERATE]

Level 3. Kai was level 0.

The creature's form rippled. Where its chest had absorbed his qi strike, the skin *flowed* like liquid, reforming, adapting. It had no face, but Kai felt its attention like a weight—heavy, hungry, patient.

"Mrs. Chen," Kai said, keeping his voice steady. "Get back into your apartment. Lock the door. Do not open it for anything."

"But—"

"*Now.*"

She scrambled inside. The lock clicked.

The Blankspawn's head tilted further, almost curious. Then it moved.

Fast.

Kai barely got his arms up in time. The creature's fist—if it could be called a fist, more like a malformed club of flesh—caught him in the forearms and sent him sliding backward. His heels scraped against the hallway carpet. Pain lanced through his defensive meridians, and he felt one of them *strain*, threatening to tear.

*Idiot,* he cursed himself. *You've had six hours of training. You can't fight like this.*

But he didn't have a choice.

The Blankspawn came again. This time Kai didn't block. He ducked, letting the strike pass over his head, and channeled qi into his palm. Mentor had shown him how to compress energy into a single point—a technique for acupuncture, not combat. But principles were principles.

He pressed his palm against the creature's torso and *released*.

The qi exploded outward in a concentrated burst. Flesh tore. Black ichor sprayed across the hallway wall. The Blankspawn let out a sound—not a scream, but a vibration, like a tuning fork struck against stone—and collapsed.

Kai stood over it, breathing hard. His hand trembled. His dantian felt hollow, drained by the technique.

[SYSTEM NOTIFICATION: BLANKSPAWN DEFEATED]
[EXPERIENCE: 50]
[CLASS EXPERIENCE: 50]

*Class experience. For my Dao Architect class.*

He didn't have time to process. More screams echoed from outside. From the street. From the apartment building across the way.

Kai ran.

---

The streets of his neighborhood had become a warzone.

Not a dramatic one—not yet. The monsters were sparse, sporadic, appearing in alleys and doorways like they'd always been there, waiting for the right moment. People fought back with whatever they had. A man in a bathrobe cast a Fireball that went wide, scorching a parked car. A woman with a sword—where had she gotten a sword?—hacked at a Blankspawn while her child hid behind a dumpster.

Kai moved through it all, conserving his qi, only intervening when someone was about to die. He couldn't save everyone. The thought sat in his chest like a stone.

*This is what Mentor meant. The integration. The chaos.*

A group of three Blankspawns had cornered a teenager against a convenience store wall. The kid was crying, holding a baseball bat that he clearly didn't know how to use. The creatures circled him like predators testing prey.

Kai didn't have enough qi for another burst technique. But he had something else.

He closed his eyes. Reached inward. His dantian was nearly empty, but his meridians still held traces—the residue of his cultivation, the foundation Mentor had helped him build. He gathered it, coaxed it, shaped it into a single thread.

Then he pushed it outward.

The qi thread lanced through the air, invisible, intangible. It struck the nearest Blankspawn and *bonded*—not attacking, but connecting. For a split second, Kai felt what the creature felt. Hunger. Purpose. The cold, mechanical drive of a being that had no soul, only programming.

He twisted the thread.

The Blankspawn convulsed. Its form *rejected* the foreign energy, and Kai felt his qi interface with something deeper, something that looked almost like—

*System code.*

The creature shattered. Not exploded, not dissolved—shattered, like glass hit with a hammer. Its pieces scattered across the pavement, already turning to dust.

[SYSTEM NOTIFICATION: BLANKSPAWN DEFEATED]
[EXPERIENCE: 50]
[CLASS EXPERIENCE: 100]
[SKILL DISCOVERED: QI WEAVING - LVL 1]
[DESCRIPTION: Manipulate ambient and internal qi to interface with System constructs. Effectiveness scales with cultivation base.]

The other two Blankspawns turned toward him.

Kai raised his hands, ready to try again—

And they stopped.

Not attacking. Not retreating. Just... stopping. Their featureless heads lifted in unison, facing the sky. A tremor ran through their bodies, synchronized, like puppets yanked by a single string.

Then they collapsed. Both of them, at the same time, crumbling into dust.

The teenager stared, mouth open. "What... what did you do?"

Kai didn't answer. He was staring at the notification that had just appeared in his vision.

[SYSTEM BROADCAST: ANOMALOUS CLASS DETECTED]
[CLASS: DAO ARCHITECT]
[STATUS: UNRECOGNIZED]
[INITIATING CLASS PROTOCOL: ANALYSIS]

"What?" Kai whispered.

The air around him *shifted*. Not the pressure change from before—something deeper. Something that made the hairs on his arms stand up and his dantian pulse like a second heart.

A window appeared before him. Not a System notification—a proper interface, translucent, glowing with script that looked half like Chinese characters and half like code.

[DAO ARCHITECT CLASS ANALYSIS: INCOMPLETE]
[SOURCE: UNKNOWN]
[FUNCTION: UNABLE TO CLASSIFY]
[RECOMMENDATION: QUARANTINE]

*Quarantine?*

[SYSTEM DIRECTIVE: CLASS "DAO ARCHITECT" HAS BEEN FLAGGED]
[ALL SYSTEM FUNCTIONS RELATED TO THIS CLASS WILL BE RESTRICTED UNTIL FURTHER ANALYSIS]

Kai's vision dimmed. His dantian, which had been pulsing with the familiar rhythm of qi, went *quiet*. The connection he'd felt—to the System, to the energy around him, to the very fabric of cultivation—snapped shut like a door slamming in his face.

"No." He reached inward, tried to circulate qi. Nothing. His meridians were empty. His dantian was dark. "No, no, no—"

The interface flickered.
[CLASS STATUS: LOCKED]

[TO UNLOCK: COMPLETE SYSTEM DESIGNATED TRIAL]
[TRIAL ACCESS: PENDING]
[TIME REMAINING: 72 HOURS]

[NOTE: FAILURE TO COMPLETE TRIAL WILL RESULT IN PERMANENT CLASS REMOVAL]

The teenager was still staring at him. "Hey, man, are you okay? What's happening?"

Kai couldn't answer. He couldn't breathe. His cultivation—barely six hours old, fragile, the only thing that made sense in this insane new world—had been taken from him.

And somewhere, far away, he felt Mentor's presence flicker. A whisper of golden warmth, fading.

*Kai.*

Her voice, in his mind.

*They found you. They found me. The System is awake, and it does not like what we are.*

*Find me, before they—*

The connection severed.

Kai fell to his knees in the street, surrounded by dust and silence and the weight of a countdown he didn't understand.

[SYSTEM NOTIFICATION: 71:59:48 REMAINING]

title: The Fractured Sky
content:
Kai didn't sleep.

He lay in bed, staring at the water-stained ceiling, his qi sense expanding like ripples in a pond. The cockroach behind the wall had stopped moving. The neighbors' qi signatures pulsed in steady rhythms—sleeping, dreaming, unaware that their world had fundamentally changed.

But it wasn't their world that concerned him.

It was the sky.

Through his window, through the haze of city lights and pollution, Kai could feel something pressing down on the atmosphere. A weight. A presence. Like a giant hand hovering inches above the planet, waiting to close.

*The System isn't done integrating.*

He sat up, swung his legs over the edge of the bed, and checked his phone. The group chat had exploded:

*Anyone else feel that? Like something's watching?*

*It's probably just the System. Chill.*

*No, man. I'm a Mage. I can FEEL the mana in the air. It's... building. Like before a storm.*

*Kai, you there? You usually have something smart to say about this stuff.*

Kai's fingers hovered over the keyboard. He wanted to type: *The System is the Heavenly Dao. It's not a game. It's not a tool. It's the fundamental law of reality being rewritten in real-time, and we're all living inside a compiler test.*

Instead, he wrote: *Stay inside. Don't use your abilities unless you have to. Something's coming.*

He sent the message, then immediately regretted it. Panic would spread. People would do stupid things. But the alternative—pretending everything was normal—felt like a lie.

His phone buzzed again. Private message from an unknown number.

*You feel it too.*

Kai's blood went cold. He stared at the message, then typed back: *Who is this?*

The reply came instantly: *Someone else who got a weird class. Meet me at the 24-hour diner on 3rd and Main. 3 AM. Come alone.*

Kai checked the time: 2:47 AM. Thirteen minutes.

He could ignore it. Block the number. Go back to cultivating and pretend the message never came. But the person knew about "weird classes." That meant they weren't a Warrior or a Mage. They were something else. Someone who might have answers.

He pulled on a hoodie, grabbed his keys, and slipped out of his apartment.

---

The streets were empty. Wrongly empty. At 3 AM in the city, there should have been homeless people, late-night joggers, the occasional car speeding through a yellow light. But the sidewalks were bare. The roads were silent. Even the wind seemed to hold its breath.

Kai walked quickly, hands shoved in his pockets, qi circulating in a slow, defensive pattern. Mentor's training had stuck. Every step was deliberate. Every breath measured. He could feel the ambient qi thickening as he moved, like wading through invisible water.

The diner was a relic from another era—chrome trim, neon sign flickering "OPEN," a single fluorescent tube lighting the interior. Through the window, Kai saw a single figure sitting in a booth near the back.

He pushed open the door. A bell jingled.

The figure looked up. Young man, early twenties, with hollow cheeks and eyes that seemed too old for his face. He wore a threadbare jacket and had the nervous energy of someone who hadn't slept in days.

"You're Kai," the man said. It wasn't a question.

"And you are?"

"Leo. I got your number from a mutual friend. You used to play Void Raiders with my cousin, Mark."

Kai slid into the booth across from him. "Mark's a potion brewer now. He told you about me?"

"Mark told me you always found the glitches. The exploits. The stuff that wasn't supposed to be there." Leo leaned forward, lowering his voice. "I need someone who can see what others can't."

"What class did you get?"

Leo's hands trembled as he reached into his jacket. He pulled out a crumpled piece of paper and slid it across the table. It was covered in symbols—not English, not any language Kai recognized. But as he stared, the symbols shifted, rearranging themselves into words:

[CLASS: SEER]
[DESCRIPTION: You see what others cannot. Fragments of truth. Echoes of futures. Shadows of the dead.]
[WARNING: This class has no level cap. This class has no skill tree. This class has no end condition.]

Kai's blood ran cold. "No end condition?"

"Exactly." Leo's voice cracked. "Everyone else got tutorials. Instructions. A path to follow. I got... visions. Images of things that haven't happened yet. And one of them was about you."

Kai's hand shot out and grabbed Leo's wrist. "What did you see?"

"A sky cracking open. Light pouring down. People burning, not from fire, but from the inside. Their qi turning against them." Leo's eyes glazed over, unfocusing. "And you. Standing in the center of it all. Your hands raised. Your meridians glowing like a second skeleton. You weren't fighting. You were... building."

"Building what?"

"The framework. The scaffold. The thing that holds reality together when the old system breaks."

Kai released his grip. His mind raced. Mentor had said the System was unstable. That it didn't understand his class. That the Heavenly Dao was being reborn as code, and code could have bugs.

*What if the bug is me?*

"Leo, when is this supposed to happen?"

"I don't know. Soon. The visions don't come with timestamps." Leo's face twisted in agony. "But there's something else. Something darker. I saw other Seers. Dozens of them. All having the same vision. And in every single one, there's a choice."

"What choice?"

"Whether to save the world, or let it burn and start over."

The fluorescent light above them flickered. The diner's temperature dropped. Kai felt it before he saw it—a ripple in the qi around them, like a stone thrown into still water.

"Someone's coming," he whispered.

Leo's eyes went wide. "They found me. They've been hunting me for days."

"Who?"

"The ones who want the old world to end. They call themselves the Reset Protocol."

The diner's front door exploded inward.

Kai didn't think. He moved. His body reacted before his mind caught up, qi surging through his meridians in a pattern Mentor had drilled into him. He grabbed Leo and dove behind the counter as a wave of black energy ripped through the space where they'd been sitting.

The booths disintegrated. The floorboards splintered. The air itself seemed to tear.

Kai peeked over the counter. Three figures stood in the doorway, silhouetted against the streetlights. They wore black, featureless masks—no eyes, no mouths, just smooth obsidian surfaces that seemed to absorb light.

The one in the center raised a hand. Black energy coiled around their fingers like living smoke.

"You cannot hide, Seer," the figure said, voice distorted, layered, like multiple people speaking at once. "Your visions belong to the Protocol. Surrender, and your death will be painless."

Leo whimpered. "They're not human. I've seen their auras. They're... corrupted. The System warped them."

Kai's mind raced. He had no weapons. No combat skills. Only a single cultivation technique and a class that the System itself didn't understand.

But Mentor had taught him one thing: qi was energy. Energy could be directed. Shaped. Redirected.

*I'm a Dao Architect. I build. I structure. I create frameworks.*

*So let's build a weapon.*

Kai closed his eyes and reached deep into his dantian. The qi reservoir pulsed, warm and alive. He didn't know if this would work. He didn't know if he'd survive.

But Leo was right about one thing: a choice was coming.

And Kai chose to fight.

He opened his eyes, stood up, and faced the masked figures. His hands traced patterns in the air, qi flowing from his fingertips, forming a lattice of golden light that hung between him and the attackers.

"I don't know who you are," Kai said, his voice steady despite the terror screaming in his chest. "But you're not taking him."

The central figure tilted its head. A crack appeared in the mask—a hairline fracture that spread like a spiderweb.

"Interesting," the voice said. "A class with no designation. A soul with no anchor. You are the anomaly we've been searching for."

"We?"

The mask shattered.

Beneath it was not a face.

It was a void. A swirling vortex of black energy that pulled at the air, at the light, at Kai's very qi. The other two figures removed their masks, revealing the same emptiness.

"We are the System's garbage collection," the void-mouthed figure said. "We delete errors. We patch exploits. We correct anomalies."

"And I'm the anomaly."

"You are the greatest bug in the code." The void expanded, reaching toward Kai with tendrils of shadow. "And you will be fixed."

Kai's golden lattice flared. The tendrils struck it and recoiled, hissing like water on hot metal. But he could feel the strain—his qi was draining fast, the pattern unstable, his foundation still too weak to sustain a real fight.

*Mentor, if you can hear me, I could really use some advice right now.*

Silence.

*Of course. The one time I need a cryptic tutorial, and she's not here.*

Leo grabbed his arm. "Kai, I can see it. The future. It's splitting. Dozens of branches. Hundreds. Most of them end with us dead."

"Which ones don't?"

Leo's eyes glazed, then snapped back into focus. "The one where you trust your instincts. The one where you stop thinking like a gamer and start thinking like a cultivator."

Kai stared at the void-creatures, at the collapsing lattice, at the terrified Seer beside him. And in that moment, something clicked.

He wasn't a Warrior. He wasn't a Mage. He wasn't bound by the System's rules because the System didn't make him.

*I was awake before the System. I knew qi before it was code. I am not a bug.*

*I am the original source.*

Kai dropped his defensive lattice. The void-tendrils surged forward—

And stopped an inch from his face.

"Impossible," the void-mouth hissed.

Kai smiled. "You're right. I am."

He raised his hand, not to attack, but to *redefine*. His meridians blazed, qi pouring out in a flood, and the golden light didn't just form a lattice—it formed a *structure*. A framework that wrapped around the void-tendrils, reshaping them, repurposing them, turning the System's own energy against itself.

The void-creatures screamed.

It was a sound like breaking glass, like corrupted data, like a program crashing in real-time. Their bodies flickered, destabilized, began to dissolve.

Kai poured more qi into the framework, his vision swimming, his dantian burning. He was pushing too hard, too fast. His meridians were cracking. Blood trickled from his nose.

But the void-creatures were crumbling.

In seconds, they were gone. Nothing left but wisps of black smoke and the lingering echo of their screams.

Kai collapsed to his knees, gasping. Leo rushed to his side, helping him stay upright.

"You did it," Leo whispered. "You actually did it."

Kai coughed, tasted blood. "That was

title: Chapter 18
content:
CHAPTER 18: The Dead Man's Signal

Kai's phone buzzed again before he could send the message.

This time, it wasn't a text. It was a call. From a number he didn't recognize.

He hesitated, thumb hovering over the decline button. But something made him answer—a prickling sensation at the base of his skull, like a warning bell ringing just at the edge of his awareness.

"Hello?"

"You need to come to the docks. Warehouse 7. Alone."

The voice was distorted, like someone speaking through a broken speaker. But beneath the crackle and static, Kai caught something familiar—a cadence, a rhythm, a ghost of memory.

"Who is this?"

"Your tutorial wasn't the only one that went sideways, Kai. There are others like you. We need to talk before they find us."

The line went dead.

Kai stared at the phone, his qi sense flaring outward instinctively. He could feel the life signatures in his building—Mrs. Chen two floors down, her qi dim and steady. The college kid next door, his energy spiking with what felt like panic. Normal people. Normal System-awakened people.

But the voice on the phone—

*Others like you.*

He should ignore it. It was obviously a trap. Anyone with half a brain could figure out that the guy who got an unclassified class was worth targeting. The System broadcast had made sure of that.

But what if it wasn't?

What if there really were others who got broken tutorials? Others whose classes didn't fit the neat categories of Warrior, Healer, Mage? Others who'd met a mentor with golden eyes and warnings about qi deviation?

Kai grabbed his jacket and headed for the door.

---

The docks were a graveyard of rusting containers and abandoned machinery. Twenty years ago, this had been the city's economic lifeline. Now it was where drug deals happened and bodies washed up.

Warehouse 7 sat at the far end, its corrugated metal roof sagging like a tired spine. The door was ajar, a sliver of darkness visible beyond.

Kai stopped fifty feet away, extending his qi sense.

Three life signatures inside. One was strong—not powerful, but concentrated, like a flame burning with steady fuel. The other two were weaker, flickering with anxiety.

No ambush. No hidden killers.

He pushed the door open.

The warehouse was empty except for three figures huddled around a portable lantern. Two of them were young—a woman with short-cropped hair and a man who couldn't have been older than nineteen. The third was older, late forties, with graying temples and eyes that held the weight of things seen and not spoken of.

The older man stepped forward. "You came. Good."

"You called. Now explain."

The woman laughed, a bitter sound. "Direct. I like that. I'm Lin. That's Chen." She gestured at the younger man. "And this is Sergeant Zhao. Retired military. He's the one who found us."

Zhao extended his hand. Kai shook it, noting the calluses, the faint tremor in the grip.

"I was in the tutorial with you," Zhao said.

Kai's blood went cold. "Impossible. The tutorial was individual. Mentor said—"

"Mentor lied." Zhao's voice was flat. "Or didn't know. Either way, I was there. In the same white room. The same golden-eyed woman. The same warnings about qi deviation and the Heavenly Dao."

"But I didn't see you. There was no one else—"

"The room wasn't empty. It was partitioned. Like being in a maze with invisible walls. I could hear you, sometimes. Muffled, like you were underwater. But I could hear you talking to her."

Lin's eyes widened. "You mean we were all in the same tutorial? At the same time?"

"Not all of us." Zhao pulled out his phone, showing them a message thread. "There are more. Twelve that I've confirmed. All with unclassified classes. All with the same mentor. All told the same lies."

Kai read the messages, his stomach sinking with each line.

*Class: Soul Binder. Tutorial: 8 hours. Mentor said my soul is incompatible with the System.*

*Class: Reality Stitcher. Tutorial: 12 hours. Mentor said I'm a glitch in the world's code.*

*Class: Void Walker. Tutorial: 6 hours. Mentor said I shouldn't exist.*

"I'm a Dao Architect," Kai said slowly. "What did she tell you?"

"Soldier. That's my class." Zhao's jaw tightened. "She said the System doesn't recognize military hierarchies. That I'm a relic of a dead world's fighting style. That my class is obsolete."

"Obsolete," Chen muttered. "My class is Pathfinder. She said I'm looking for roads that don't exist anymore. That the world has moved on without me."

Lin crossed her arms. "I'm a Harmonist. She said I'm trying to tune an instrument that's already broken."

The four of them stood in silence, the lantern casting long shadows across the warehouse floor. Outside, the wind howled through the cracks in the walls.

"Why are you telling me this?" Kai asked finally.

"Because you're the only one who escaped," Zhao said. "The only one she didn't warn about the others."

"What do you mean?"

"She told me there were twelve of us. Twelve misfits. Twelve broken classes. But she also said one of us was different. One of us was a key."

"A key to what?"

"She didn't say. But she said we'd know him when we found him. That he'd have already started changing the world without realizing it." Zhao's eyes locked onto Kai's. "You've been cultivating, haven't you? Not just the basic qi circulation—real cultivation."

Kai didn't answer. He didn't need to.

"The rest of us can't cultivate," Lin said, her voice cracking. "We tried. The qi won't flow. It's like our meridians are blocked by something—the System, maybe, or our classes themselves. We're stuck at the first step."

"But you're not." Chen's voice was bitter. "You're already ahead of us. You're already powerful."

"I'm not powerful," Kai said. "I can barely circulate qi without passing out."

"It doesn't matter." Zhao stepped closer. "What matters is that you can do what we can't. And there's something coming that's going to require it."

The ground shook.

A deep, resonant thrumming, like a giant's heartbeat, pulsed through the warehouse floor. Dust rained from the ceiling. The lantern flickered.

"What was that?" Lin's hand went to her side, where a knife hung from her belt.

Zhao's face went pale. "They found us."

"Who?"

Before he could answer, the warehouse door exploded inward.

Not from an explosion—from a *hand*. A human hand, wrapped in glowing blue energy, that had punched through the metal like it was paper.

The hand withdrew, and a figure stepped through the gap.

He was tall, broad-shouldered, wearing what looked like military fatigues. But his eyes glowed with the same blue energy as his hand, and his skin was covered in glowing runes that pulsed in rhythm with his heartbeat.

"Sergeant Zhao," the figure said, his voice echoing with an unnatural resonance. "You've been flagged for harboring system anomalies. Surrender the unclassified individuals, and your sentence will be reduced."

"Sentence?" Kai stepped forward. "You can't just—"

"Kai, don't—" Zhao grabbed his arm.

But it was too late.

The figure's eyes snapped to Kai, and the runes on his skin flared bright. "Dao Architect. Confirmed. Priority level: Omega. Target acquired."

He raised his hand, and the blue energy condensed into a sphere of crackling power.

Kai didn't think. He moved.

His qi sense flared, and he felt the energy gathering in the figure's palm—dense, violent, hungry. He threw himself sideways as the sphere discharged, tearing through the space where he'd been standing and carving a trench in the concrete floor.

"Run!" Zhao grabbed Lin and Chen, dragging them toward a side door. "Kai, get out of here!"

But Kai couldn't run. Not yet.

Because as he scrambled to his feet, he saw something that made his blood freeze.

The figure's chest was glowing. Not with runes—with a symbol. A symbol that Kai recognized.

It was the same symbol that had appeared on his status screen.

The symbol for *unclassified*.

"You're one of us," Kai breathed. "You're another unclassified."

The figure's expression didn't change. "I was. Until I was fixed."

"Fixed?"

"The System doesn't tolerate anomalies. I was broken. Now I'm whole." He raised his hand again, the energy gathering once more. "And it's my job to make sure no one else stays broken."

The sphere of blue light grew, crackling with power that made Kai's qi scream in warning.

And in that moment, Kai realized the truth:

The tutorial wasn't the beginning of his journey.

It was the starting line of a race he didn't know he was running.

And the other competitors weren't allies.

They were hunters.

---

SYSTEM NOTIFICATION:
[WARNING: SYSTEM ANOMALY DETECTED]
[CLASS: DAO ARCHITECT - STATUS: UNSTABLE]
[RANK: UNKNOWN]
[THREAT LEVEL: OMEGA]

[PRIORITY DIRECTIVE: NEUTRALIZE]

[EXECUTION PROTOCOL ACTIVATED]
[DO NOT RESIST]

title: The Weight of Heaven's Gaze
content:
Kai's phone slipped from his fingers as the System broadcast seared itself into his vision.

[ALL CLASSES HAVE BEEN ASSIGNED]

[WORLD INTEGRATION: PHASE TWO INITIATING]

[WARNING: UNBALANCED CLASS DISTRIBUTION DETECTED]

[REBALANCING PROTOCOL ACTIVE]

The notification pulsed three times, then faded. But the words remained burned into Kai's retina, shimmering like afterimages of lightning.

*Rebalancing. That doesn't sound good.*

His phone buzzed again. Then again. Then it didn't stop buzzing—a continuous vibration that rattled against his nightstand like an angry insect. He grabbed it.

The group chat had exploded.

*Jake: DID EVERYONE GET THAT*

*Mark: Rebalancing? What rebalancing?*

*Sarah: My screen is glitching. System's showing me my class rank. I'm C-tier. What does that mean?*

*Jake: I'm B-tier. Fuck.*

*Mark: Apprentice Potioneer. Unranked. I don't have a tier.*

Kai's blood ran cold. *Unranked.*

He opened his own System interface, heart hammering against his ribs. The familiar screen materialized:

[CLASS: Dao Architect]

[RANK: UNCLASSIFIED]

[STATUS: OUTSIDE STANDARD HIERARCHY]

*Outside standard hierarchy.* The words felt like a target painted on his back.

His phone buzzed with a private message from an unknown number.

*You're the one who hasn't been assigned a tier, aren't you? I can tell. Your qi signature is... unusual. We should talk. - L.*

Kai's thumb hovered over the message. *Who the hell is L?*

Before he could reply, his apartment door exploded inward.

Kai rolled off the bed, instinct screaming, qi flaring through his meridians as he tucked into a crouch behind the mattress. Splinters of wood rained across the floor. Dust choked the air.

Three figures stood in his doorway, silhouetted against the hallway light. They wore matching black uniforms—sleek, form-fitting, lined with glowing blue circuitry. *System-issued gear. Official.*

"Kai Chen," the lead figure said. Her voice was cold, clinical. "You are hereby summoned for mandatory Class Evaluation by the World Integration Authority. Compliance is required. Resistance will be met with force."

*World Integration Authority? Since when did that exist?*

Kai's mind raced. The System had only been active for—what, a day? Less? And already there was an *authority*? Either someone had been preparing for this long before the broadcast, or the System had created them instantly.

Neither option was comforting.

"I don't know what you're talking about," Kai said, keeping his voice steady. "I just woke up."

The lead figure stepped forward. Her eyes glowed faintly blue—a class ability, Kai realized. *Some kind of detection skill.* She scanned the room, her gaze passing over him, the walls, the shattered door.

Then she stopped. Her eyes locked onto his chest.

*She can see my qi.*

"Your cultivation level is already at Meridian Awakening," she said, and there was something like surprise in her voice. "Impossible. The tutorial only ended six hours ago."

"Maybe I'm a fast learner."

"Fast learners don't advance to Meridian Awakening in six hours. They don't advance at all. The System's growth curves are logarithmic. It takes days, sometimes weeks, to reach your level." She tilted her head, studying him like a specimen. "What class did you receive?"

Kai said nothing.

The two figures behind her shifted, hands moving to their belts. Weapons materialized—not guns, but short batons that hummed with electrical energy. *System-crafted weapons. Probably stun settings. Probably lethal settings.*

"Your class," the lead figure repeated. "State it."

*If I tell them the truth, I'm a lab rat. If I lie, they'll know the moment they scan me.*

Kai made a choice.

"Dao Architect."

Silence. The lead figure's brow furrowed. She consulted something on her wrist—a small holographic display that flickered with data streams.

"That class doesn't exist in the System registry."

"I know."

"How did you obtain it?"

"I don't know."

Her eyes narrowed. "You're lying."

"I'm not." Kai rose slowly, hands open and visible. "I don't know why I got it. I don't know what it means. I spent six hours in a tutorial learning how to circulate qi while a woman with golden eyes told me I had a 2% chance of dying from qi deviation."

The lead figure's expression flickered—the first crack in her professional mask. "A woman with golden eyes? Describe her."

"Tall. Pale hair. Wore robes that looked ancient. She called herself 'Mentor.' She taught me cultivation like it was the most natural thing in the world."

The two figures behind her exchanged glances. The lead figure's hand drifted to her communicator.

"Stand by," she said into it. "We have a Code Black. Repeat, Code Black. Subject claims interaction with an unregistered tutorial entity."

A burst of static. Then a voice, distorted and urgent: *Confirm. Extract immediately. Priority Alpha designation.*

*Priority Alpha. That's not good.*

The lead figure's hand moved to her weapon. "Kai Chen, you are now classified as Priority Asset Alpha. You will come with us willingly, or we will be forced to tranquilize you and transport you by force. Your cooperation will be noted."

*Asset. Not person. Asset.*

Kai's hands curled into fists. The qi in his meridians pulsed, responding to his agitation. He could feel it—the power thrumming just beneath his skin, waiting to be used. But he didn't know how to use it. Not yet. Six hours of cultivation hadn't taught him combat. Hadn't taught him anything except how to survive his own energy.

*I need time. I need to figure out what I am before they decide what I am.*

"Fine," he said, letting his shoulders sag. "I'll come quietly."

The lead figure nodded, some of the tension leaving her posture. "Smart choice."

Kai took a step forward.

Then he *moved*.

Not with speed—he wasn't fast enough for that. But with qi. He pushed it through his legs, through his feet, and *released* it into the floor. The wooden planks splintered beneath him, sending a shockwave through the apartment. The two figures behind the leader stumbled, caught off guard. The leader herself staggered, arms pinwheeling for balance.

Kai didn't run for the door. That's what they'd expect. Instead, he dove sideways, crashing through his apartment window. Glass shattered around him as he tumbled into open air.

*Third floor. This is going to hurt.*

He hit the ground wrong. His ankle twisted. Pain exploded up his leg, white-hot and blinding. He bit down on a scream and forced himself to his feet, limping toward the alley between his building and the next.

Behind him, shouts. Footsteps. The crackle of electricity.

*They're faster than me. Stronger than me. Better equipped.*

*But they don't know what I am.*

Kai ducked into the alley, pressing his back against the damp brick wall. His breath came in ragged gasps. His ankle screamed with every heartbeat. The qi in his dantian churned, unstable, threatening to spiral out of control.

*Mentor said stability was key. That deviation could kill me.*

*Better to risk death than let them take me.*

He closed his eyes and *listened*.

Not with his ears. With his qi sense. The world unfolded around him—the life forces of rats in the walls, the residual qi clinging to garbage and concrete, the bright, sharp signatures of the three figures pursuing him. They were spreading out, coordinating, cutting off escape routes.

*They're professionals. They've done this before.*

*But they've never hunted a Dao Architect.*

Kai reached into his dantian, found the thread of qi that connected him to the world, and *pulled*.

The effect was immediate. The ambient qi in the alley surged, responding to his will like water flowing toward a drain. It wasn't controlled. It wasn't refined. But it was *something*.

He pushed the gathered qi into the ground beneath his feet.

The earth *cracked*.

A fissure shot through the alley, splitting concrete and asphalt, racing toward the street. The lead figure's shout turned into a cry of alarm as the ground gave way beneath her. She fell, cursing, her weapon clattering against stone.

Kai didn't wait to see more. He ran.

His ankle screamed. His dantian burned. The qi he'd just expelled left him feeling hollow, drained, like a battery running on empty. But he kept moving, limping through back alleys, jumping fences, squeezing through gaps that barely fit his shoulders.

He emerged onto a main street.

And stopped.

The city had changed.

People were everywhere—not just walking, but *fighting*. In the middle of intersections. On rooftops. In parks. Groups of newly-classed citizens clashed with each other, testing their abilities, establishing hierarchies. Some had formed gangs. Others had formed militias. A few simply stood in the streets, weeping, overwhelmed by the sudden violence of a world that had turned into a game.

*The System didn't just give people powers. It gave them permission.*

A man with metal skin—some kind of Tank-class—slammed a woman through a car window. She rose, bleeding from a dozen cuts, and blasted him with a bolt of lightning that sent him crashing into a fire hydrant.

No one intervened. No police. No emergency services. Just chaos.

*This is what Phase Two looks like.*

Kai's phone buzzed again. He pulled it out, expecting another message from the Authority, or from L.

Instead, he saw a notification from Mentor.

[SYSTEM MESSAGE: PRIORITY DIRECTIVE]

[STUDENT KAI CHEN]

[YOUR QI SIGNATURE HAS BEEN MARKED. THE HEAVENLY DAO DETECTS ANOMALY.]

[WARNING: HIGHER-ORDER ENTITIES HAVE TAKEN INTEREST IN YOUR EXISTENCE.]

[RECOMMENDATION: SEEK SHELTER. AVOID DETECTION. DO NOT TRUST THE AUTHORITY.]

[ADDITIONAL RECOMMENDATION: FIND THE OTHER UNCLASSIFIED.]

*Other unclassified? There are more of me?*

Before he could process the message, a shadow fell over him.

Kai looked up.

A woman stood on the rooftop above him, silhouetted against the morning sun. She was tall, lean, wrapped in dark clothing that seemed to drink the light. Her eyes—he could see them even from this distance—glowed faintly gold.

*Another one like Mentor?*

She smiled. It was not a kind smile.

"Found you," she said, her voice carrying across the distance with unnatural clarity. "The Architect. The anomaly. The one who broke the System's rules before it even finished writing them."

Kai's qi sense flared. The woman's signature was *massive*—a sun compressed into human form, radiating power that made his dantian ache just to perceive.

*She's not like Mentor. She's something else entirely.*

"Who are you?" Kai asked, his voice steady despite the fear crawling up his spine.

The woman's smile widened. "I'm the

title: The Architect's First Array
content:
Kai's phone buzzed again before he could process the broadcast.

Then again.

And again.

The notifications cascaded like a waterfall—System messages, friend requests, guild invitations, all flooding his interface simultaneously. He dismissed them with a swipe, but more appeared instantly, stacking on top of each other until his vision blurred with text.

[SYSTEM BROADCAST: CLASS DISTRIBUTION COMPLETE]
[COMBAT CLASSES: 47.3% | SUPPORT: 28.9% | CRAFTING: 18.2% | UNKNOWN: 5.6%]

Five point six percent.

That was how many people had received unclassified classes. Kai wondered how many of them were Dao Architects. How many were sitting in their apartments right now, trying to figure out what they'd become.

His front door exploded inward.

Kai's qi sense flared before his eyes could adjust. Three figures stood in his doorway, their qi signatures blazing with the raw, untamed energy of newly awakened classes. They wore athletic gear—tracksuits, sneakers, the kind of outfits people threw on when they wanted to move fast.

The leader was a man in his late twenties, broad-shouldered, with a System-issued shortsword already drawn. His qi burned bright orange, aggressive and hungry.

"Kai Chen," he said, not a question. "You're coming with us."

Kai rose slowly from his bed. His body still ached from the tutorial, his meridians raw and sensitive. But the qi inside him stirred, responding to the threat with something that felt almost like anticipation.

"Who's asking?"

"First Awakened Collective." The man's eyes scanned the room, dismissive. "You've been flagged as an unclassified. The Director wants to meet every single one of you before the integration phase begins."

"Flagged by who?"

"The System, obviously." The man stepped forward, his sword held loose and confident. "Don't make this difficult. You're not a combat class. You won't win."

Kai's hand moved before he made a conscious decision to move it. His fingers traced a pattern in the air—one of the basic techniques Mentor had shown him during the tutorial, the one she'd called *Gathering Array: Minor.*

Qi responded.

It flowed from his dantian, through his meridians, out through his fingertips. The energy coalesced into a glowing web of lines, half-visible, pulsing with soft white light. The array hung in the air for exactly two seconds before dissolving.

Nothing happened.

The man laughed. "What was that? Some kind of light show?"

Kai felt it before he saw it. The qi in the room had shifted. It was denser now, more concentrated, pressing against his skin like the pressure before a storm. The Gathering Array hadn't failed—it had worked exactly as intended. It just wasn't a combat technique.

"Last chance," the man said.

Kai looked past him, through the shattered door, into the hallway beyond. More qi signatures. At least six more people, their classes ranging from Warrior to something that felt cold and sharp, like ice cracking under pressure.

The First Awakened Collective had come prepared.

"Why now?" Kai asked. "The tutorial just ended. How are you already organized?"

The man's smile was thin. "Some of us saw the System coming. We've been preparing for months. Stockpiling resources, studying the patterns, building connections." He tapped his temple. "The Director predicted this exact scenario. Unclassified classes appearing, confused and vulnerable. Easy pickings."

"Pickings for what?"

"Recruitment. Control. The integration phase is coming, and only the organized will survive. You can join us willingly, or—" he gestured with the sword, "—we can make you."

Kai's second array was already forming.

This one was different. Mentor had taught him seven basic arrays during the tutorial, but she'd also shown him something else—a technique she'd called *Qi Weaving,* the foundation of all Dao Architect abilities. It wasn't an array. It was a method of manipulating qi directly, without the mediation of predefined patterns.

He'd spent six hours practicing it. His success rate was roughly thirty percent.

The array in his hands was a distraction.

The Weaver was the real attack.

"Last chance," Kai said, throwing the man's words back at him.

The man's eyes narrowed. "Fine. Subdue him."

The two people behind him moved simultaneously—a woman with a spear, a man with gauntlets that glowed with System-enhanced light. They were fast, faster than normal humans, their movements sharpened by class abilities.

Kai's Weaver snapped into place.

The qi in the room *twisted.*

It folded in on itself, compressing into a single point between Kai's palms. The pressure became unbearable—not physically, but spiritually. The two attackers stumbled, their qi signatures fluctuating wildly as the ambient energy around them destabilized.

The Weaver wasn't meant for combat. Mentor had called it a "foundational technique," a way to understand the nature of qi by manipulating it directly. But Kai had realized something during those six hours of practice:

Qi was energy. And energy, when forced to change states, could be destructive.

He released the compressed qi in a single burst.

The shockwave wasn't visible. It didn't break windows or knock over furniture. But it hit the two attackers like a physical wall, sending them crashing into the hallway. Their qi signatures dimmed, flickered, stabilized at half their previous intensity.

The man with the sword stared.

"What the hell was that?"

Kai's hands were shaking. The Weaver had drained him more than he'd expected—his dantian felt hollow, his meridians screaming in protest. But he forced himself to stand straight, to meet the man's eyes.

"I'm not easy pickings."

The man's grip on his sword tightened. For a moment, Kai thought he'd attack. But then something behind his eyes shifted—calculation, reassessment.

"Interesting." The man sheathed his sword. "The Director will want to hear about this. You've got something unique."

"This isn't a negotiation."

"It never was." The man stepped back, into the hallway. "You'll come eventually. They always do. When the integration phase starts, and the monsters come, and the safe zones collapse—you'll come looking for answers. And we'll have them."

He turned and walked away, his people following, dragging the two unconscious attackers with them.

Kai stood in the ruins of his apartment door, breathing hard, his qi sense tracking the Collective's signatures until they faded into the distance.

Then he collapsed.

---

The darkness lasted only a moment.

Kai opened his eyes to find himself in the white chamber again. Mentor stood before him, her golden eyes sharp with something that might have been concern.

"You overextended."

"I know."

"Your meridians are damaged. Minor, but damage nonetheless. You pushed beyond your limits."

"I had no choice."

Mentor studied him for a long moment. Then, unexpectedly, she nodded. "Acceptable. The Weaver was well-executed. Your instincts are developing."

Kai pushed himself up. The chamber's ambient qi was already seeping into his body, soothing the raw edges of his meridians. "What was that? The First Awakened Collective?"

"A faction." Mentor's voice was flat. "They are not unique. Across the world, groups are forming. The strong seek to control the weak. The organized seek to absorb the isolated."

"And the unclassified?"

"Are a target." Mentor's golden eyes flickered. "The Director you mentioned—I detected a familiar signature in his qi. One I have encountered before."

Kai's blood ran cold. "What do you mean?"

"In the previous cycles. The iterations before this one. There have been others who understood the System's true nature. Others who learned to manipulate its code beyond what was intended."

"Other Dao Architects?"

"No." Mentor's voice was quiet. "Other Architects. But not of the Dao. Of something else entirely. Something the System was designed to contain."

The chamber grew colder.

"Your training must accelerate," Mentor said. "The integration phase begins in three days. You must be ready."

"Ready for what?"

Mentor's golden eyes met his.

"For the monsters that will emerge. For the factions that will rise. For the Architect who has already begun to rewrite the System's code from within."

She raised her hand, and the world dissolved.

---

Kai woke on his apartment floor.

His phone was ringing. Unknown number. He ignored it, staring at the ceiling, trying to process what Mentor had told him.

Another Architect. Not of the Dao—of something else.

Someone who had been manipulating the System across multiple cycles.

Someone who had already started to change the rules.

His phone buzzed again. Then again. A cascade of messages, all from different numbers, all saying the same thing:

*The First Awakened Collective is recruiting.*

*Join or be left behind.*

*The integration is coming.*

Kai closed his eyes and reached into his dantian. The qi was still there, depleted but present, pulsing with the rhythm of his breathing.

He had three days.

Three days to learn enough arrays to survive.

Three days to figure out who the other Architect was.

Three days before the world changed forever.

Deep in the back of his mind, he felt something stir. A presence, ancient and vast, pressing against the edges of his consciousness like a predator testing a cage.

*Find me,* it seemed to whisper. *Before he does.*

Kai opened his eyes.

The countdown had begun.

title: The First Day of Infinity
content:
Kai's thumb hovered over the send button as the System broadcast scrolled across his vision like celestial text carved into glass.

[SYSTEM BROADCAST: TUTORIAL PHASE COMPLETE]
[ALL CLASSES HAVE BEEN ASSIGNED]
[WORLD INTEGRATION: 47.3% COMPLETE]
[ESTIMATED TIME TO FULL INTEGRATION: 72 HOURS]

He blinked, and the text faded. The percentage was climbing. When he'd first seen the notification yesterday, it had been at 23%. Now it had nearly doubled.

*Seventy-two hours.* That was three days. Three days until whatever "full integration" meant.

His phone buzzed again. The group chat had exploded.

*Jake: DID EVERYONE JUST SEE THAT?*
*Mark: 47%? It was 31% when I checked this morning*
*Jake: My tutorial orb said something about "world stabilization" before it booted me*
*Unknown Number: Anyone else's class feel wrong?*
*Jake: Wrong how?*
*Unknown Number: Like it doesn't fit. Like the System shoved me into a box that's too small*

Kai's fingers moved before he could stop them. *What class did you get?*

The reply came instantly. *Rogue. But I've never stolen anything in my life. Never even wanted to. Why would the System give me a thief's class?*

Before Kai could respond, the chat flooded with similar complaints. A girl who wanted to fight got Healer. A man who'd spent years studying martial arts got Mage. Someone else got a class called "Merchant" when they'd never shown interest in business.

*Jake: The System doesn't care what you want. It assigns based on something else.*

*Mark: Based on what?*

No one had an answer.

Kai set the phone down and stood, testing his body. The dull ache in his dantian had settled into something almost comfortable, like a muscle that knew it had been worked. He moved to his small kitchen, poured a glass of water, and drank.

The water tasted different.

Not bad. Just... more. He could feel the trace minerals, the subtle energy that all living things carried. It was like someone had turned up the contrast on his senses.

He checked the clock. 7:43 AM. The tutorial had lasted six hours, but he'd been unconscious afterward for... he checked his phone's history. Twelve hours. He'd slept through the entire night.

Outside, the city was waking up. Sirens wailed in the distance—more than usual. Car horns blared. Somewhere, someone was screaming.

Kai moved to his window and looked down at the street. It was chaos. People were running, some in armor that hadn't existed yesterday, others clutching glowing weapons or trailing wisps of elemental energy. A man in full plate mail was trying to calm a crowd while a woman with visible lightning crackling between her fingers argued with a police officer.

*The System turned everyone into something they weren't ready to be.*

His phone rang. Unknown number.

He answered. "Hello?"

"Kai." Mentor's voice. "You're awake."

"How did you get my number?"

"I have access to the System's communication protocols. More importantly, you need to move."

"What? Why?"

"The full integration is accelerating. The System's initial schedule was optimistic—it underestimated the strain of merging two realities. The last 53% will complete within the next twelve hours, not seventy-two."

Kai's blood went cold. "Twelve hours?"

"Less, possibly. The integration isn't gentle. As it progresses, more System features will manifest without warning. Monsters. Dungeons. Quests. The world will adapt, but the adaptation will be violent."

"What am I supposed to do?"

"First, survive. Second, find others who received unusual classes. They will be targets."

"Targets for what?"

Mentor paused. "The System assigns classes based on potential, not desire. Those who received standard classes—Warrior, Mage, Healer—they fit neatly into the System's architecture. But those who received non-standard classes, like yourself, represent variables. The System doesn't like variables. Other entities within the System may attempt to eliminate them."

"Entities? What entities?"

"I cannot explain further without violating my own constraints. But I can tell you this—your class, Dao Architect, is not supposed to exist. The fact that it does means someone or something deliberately broke the rules to create it. That has consequences."

The line went dead.

Kai stood frozen, the phone still pressed to his ear. *Not supposed to exist.* The words echoed in his mind. He thought of Mentor's golden eyes, her cryptic warnings, her concern about qi deviation. She'd known. She'd known from the start that his class was wrong, that he was a mistake in the System's perfect code.

Another explosion of notifications flooded his vision:

[SYSTEM ALERT: NEW FEATURE UNLOCKED]
[WORLD MAP: ENABLED]
[QUEST SYSTEM: PARTIALLY ACTIVE]
[ACHIEVEMENT SYSTEM: ACTIVE]

A translucent map appeared in front of him, showing his apartment building in wireframe blue. Little dots moved around—other people, their classes displayed in tiny text above their heads. He could see the man in plate mail three blocks away, labeled [WARRIOR - LVL 3]. The woman with lightning, [MAGE - LVL 2].

And then he saw something else.

A cluster of red dots, half a mile to the east. They weren't moving like people. They were moving like predators, zigzagging through alleys, hunting.

[SYSTEM NOTIFICATION: DUNGEON BREACH DETECTED]
[LOCATION: EASTSIDE INDUSTRIAL DISTRICT]
[THREAT LEVEL: MODERATE]
[RECOMMENDED PARTY SIZE: 4-6]

Dungeons. Already.

Kai grabbed his jacket and headed for the door. He didn't know what he was going to do, but staying in his apartment while red dots closed the distance wasn't an option.

The hallway was empty, but he could hear commotion from downstairs. Shouting. Crying. Someone was trying to calm people down, their voice cracking with stress.

He took the stairs two at a time, his body moving with a fluidity that felt foreign. The qi in his meridians responded to his intent, flowing faster as he exerted himself. His dantian pulsed.

*I'm faster than I was yesterday.*

He hit the ground floor and pushed through the building's front door into a world that had gone insane.

Cars were abandoned in the middle of the street, doors open, engines still running. A group of teenagers had formed a circle, taking turns casting spells at a trash can. One of them, a boy with spiky hair, managed to produce a wisp of flame that fizzled after three seconds. The others cheered.

An old woman sat on the curb, crying, her hands wrapped around a crystal staff that glowed with soft blue light. She looked at Kai as he passed, her eyes wide and lost. "I don't know what this is," she whispered. "I'm seventy-two. I sell flowers. Why did it give me this?"

Kai wanted to stop. Wanted to help. But the red dots were moving, and Mentor's warning echoed in his ears.

*Find others who received unusual classes. They will be targets.*

He ran.

The city had changed in ways both subtle and obvious. Streetlights flickered with an energy that wasn't electricity. The air smelled different—ozone and something metallic, like blood and copper. Shadows moved where they shouldn't, stretching in directions that defied the morning sun.

He reached the edge of the industrial district and stopped.

The dungeon breach was visible even without the map. A rift in reality, maybe thirty feet across, hung suspended between two abandoned warehouses. Its edges shimmered like heat haze, and inside it, darkness that seemed to drink the light.

Red dots were spilling out of it.

They took shape as they emerged—humanoid, but wrong. Their skin was too pale, their joints bent at angles that shouldn't work. Their eyes glowed with the same sickly green as the rift.

[SYSTEM NOTIFICATION: MONSTERS DETECTED]
[VISCERA SHAMBLERS - LVL 3]
[WEAKNESS: LIGHT/FIRE]

A party of four players was already engaging them. A Warrior with a rusted sword, a Mage throwing firebolts that hit more often than not, an Archer whose shots seemed magnetically attracted to weak points, and a Healer keeping them alive with bursts of golden light.

They were winning. Barely.

But more monsters were coming through the rift. And the red dots on Kai's map were multiplying.

*Not my fight,* he told himself. *I need to find the others. Need to figure out what's happening.*

But then he saw the Healer stumble. The Mage was out of position, too focused on dealing damage to notice the three shamblers flanking their left. The Archer was reloading. The Warrior was engaged with two in front.

The Healer went down.

Kai moved before he thought.

His body crossed the distance in seconds, qi surging through his legs. He didn't have a weapon. He didn't have combat skills. But he had something the monsters didn't expect—a class they'd never encountered.

He reached out with his qi sense and *pushed.*

The Dao Architect class reacted instantly. The qi in his meridians flowed into his palm, dense and sharp, and he slammed it into the nearest shambler's chest. The creature exploded, not with blood, but with sound—a shriek that echoed off the warehouse walls as its body unraveled into wisps of dark energy.

The other monsters turned toward him.

The players stared.

"What the hell was that?" the Warrior shouted, his sword still raised.

"Get your Healer up," Kai snapped. "More are coming."

The Healer was already scrambling to her feet, golden light knitting the wound in her side. She looked at Kai with wide eyes. "What class are you? I've never seen that skill before."

"Long story," Kai said. "Focus on the fight."

They formed up, the four players falling into a rhythm that suggested they'd trained together for years, not hours. Kai fought beside them, using his qi sense to predict the monsters' movements, to find the weak points that the System had identified. Each shambler he killed dissolved into dark energy, and with each kill, he felt something shift inside him.

[SYSTEM NOTIFICATION: SKILL UNLOCKED]
[QI STRIKE - LVL 1]
[PASSIVE: INCREASE QI EFFICIENCY BY 5%]

[SYSTEM NOTIFICATION: CLASS PROGRESSION]
[DAO ARCHITECT - LVL 1 → LVL 2]
[NEW SKILL SLOT UNLOCKED]

He'd leveled. In less than a minute of combat, he'd leveled.

The rift began to shrink, the flood of monsters slowing to a trickle. The players let out a collective breath, their weapons lowering.

"Thanks, man," the Warrior said, clapping Kai on the shoulder. "We would've been toast."

"Don't mention it." Kai was already backing away. "I need to go."

"Wait." The Healer stepped forward. "What's your class? Really. I've been scanning everyone I meet, and you don't show up in my identification skill. You show up as... nothing. Like the System doesn't know what to call you."

The other players exchanged glances.

Kai looked at the Healer, at her earnest

title: The First Dungeon
content:
Kai's phone buzzed again before he could process the System broadcast. Then again. And again. The notifications cascaded like a waterfall, each one more urgent than the last.

He looked at the screen.

[SYSTEM BROADCAST: FIRST DUNGEONS ACTIVATING IN 24 HOURS]
[ALL AWAKENED MUST ENTER THEIR ASSIGNED DUNGEON]
[FAILURE TO PARTICIPATE = FORFEITURE OF CLASS]

The chat exploded.

*WHAT THE HELL IS A DUNGEON*

*I can't even cast Fireball consistently what do you mean DUNGEON*

*Anyone else's tutorial mentor just appear in their room? Mine's standing in my kitchen.*

Kai's blood ran cold. He turned slowly, half-expecting Mentor to materialize behind him. The apartment was empty. But something had shifted in the air—a pressure, like the moment before a storm.

His phone buzzed again. A private message from an unknown number.

*You felt it too. The Architects are gathering. - L.*

Kai stared at the message. The Architects? Was that what Mentor had called him? A Dao Architect? He typed back:

*Who are you?*

Three dots appeared. Then vanished. Then appeared again.

*North side of Central Park. Under the clock tower. One hour. Come alone.*

Kai's instincts screamed trap. Every horror movie he'd ever watched, every thriller novel he'd read—this was how people died. But another part of him, the part that had spent six hours learning to circulate qi through meridians that shouldn't exist, whispered that normal rules no longer applied.

He dressed quickly. Jeans, hoodie, running shoes. Nothing that would stand out. Before leaving, he reached into his dantian, feeling the thin stream of qi that now flowed through his body. It was barely a trickle compared to what Mentor had shown him, but it was *his*.

The streets were chaos.

People stood in clusters, phones out, faces a mix of excitement and terror. A woman in a business suit was crying on the curb. Two teenagers were sparring in an alley, one throwing clumsy fireballs that fizzled before reaching target. A man had manifested a sword made of light and was posing for photos.

Kai kept his head down and walked.

Central Park was worse. The usual joggers and dog-walkers had been replaced by a nervous crowd. Some wore armor that had materialized from nowhere. Others clutched weapons that hummed with energy. A few had visible injuries—burns, cuts, one woman with a hand that looked frostbitten.

The clock tower stood at the park's north entrance, its face frozen at 11:47. Beneath it, a figure waited.

She was young—maybe early twenties—with sharp features and hair cropped so short it was almost a buzz cut. She wore military-style cargo pants and a tank top that revealed arms covered in intricate tattoo-like patterns that shifted as Kai watched.

"You came," she said. No greeting. No introduction.

"You sent the message."

"I did." She studied him with an intensity that made Kai feel like he was being x-rayed. "You're the one. The unclassified."

"How do you know about that?"

"Because I'm unclassified too." She held up her arm, and the tattoos pulsed. "Class: Architect. Specialization: Structure. The System tried to give me 'Mage' but it didn't stick. Something about my soul composition being 'incompatible with standard frameworks.'"

Kai's heart hammered. "Mentor said there were others."

"Mentor?" She raised an eyebrow. "Your tutorial guide. Mine called herself 'Instructor.' Tall, dark hair, talked like she'd been alive for ten thousand years?"

"Golden eyes?"

"Yeah." A flicker of recognition crossed her face. "They're not human. The Architects, I mean. Or whatever they are. They're something the System created to teach us."

"Why are you telling me this?"

"Because in twenty-three hours, we're going into a dungeon that the System designed to kill us, and I don't think standard classes are going to make it out alive." She stepped closer, lowering her voice. "I've been testing my abilities. The Architect class doesn't work like the others. I can't just 'level up' by killing monsters. I have to *build*. Structures. Frameworks. Foundations. It's like I'm coding reality."

Kai's breath caught. "You can manipulate the System?"

"Not manipulate. Construct. There's a difference." She looked around, then pulled him behind the clock tower, out of sight. "Watch."

She pressed her palm against the brick wall. The tattoos on her arm flared, and lines of light spread from her hand like cracks in glass. The wall rippled—and a doorway formed, opening into a space that shouldn't exist.

Kai peered inside. It was a room. Small, maybe ten feet square, with walls that glowed with the same light as Mentor's tutorial chamber.

"Took me three hours to make that," she said. "It's stable for about thirty minutes before the structure collapses. But it's a start."

"That's incredible."

"It's not enough." She let the doorway dissolve, the wall returning to normal. "The dungeon notification mentioned 'tiers.' Tier 1 dungeons for standard classes. But Architect dungeons? They're different. The System flagged mine as 'Variable Difficulty.' That means it scales to challenge me specifically."

Kai's stomach dropped. "Mine too."

"Then you understand." She met his eyes. "We need to work together. Share what we learn. The Architects told us we're rare—maybe one in ten million. That means there aren't many of us. And if the dungeons are designed to kill anything that doesn't fit the System's template..."

"We're targets."

"Exactly."

Kai thought about Mentor. About the way she'd spoken of the System with something like reverence. About the 2% chance of death from qi deviation. About the way his own body was still adjusting to the flow of energy inside him.

"I'm in," he said. "But I need to know more. What did your Instructor tell you about the Architects?"

"Not much. Just that they're 'guides' and that our path is 'unwritten.'" She made air quotes. "Vague and ominous. Real helpful."

"Mine said something similar. She also said the System was 'incomplete' and that our classes were 'anomalies.'"

"Anomalies." The woman—Kai realized he still didn't know her name—let out a bitter laugh. "Great. We're bugs in the cosmic code."

"I'm Kai, by the way."

"Lena." She extended her hand. He shook it. Her grip was firm, calloused. Soldier's hands. "We've got twenty-three hours. I've been mapping the dungeon spawn patterns from the global chat. There's a concentration forming near the harbor. That's probably where our entrance will be."

"How do you know that?"

"Because I've been awake for eighteen hours straight, cross-referencing System notifications, global broadcasts, and the random nightmares that people are posting about having." She pulled out her phone, showing him a spreadsheet that would make a data analyst weep. "Patterns, Kai. The System runs on patterns. We just have to learn to read them."

Kai studied the spreadsheet. Columns of data—time stamps, locations, class distributions, monster spawn rates. It was meticulous. Paranoid. Brilliant.

"You're preparing for war."

"I'm preparing for survival." She pocketed the phone. "The first dungeon wave is going to kill a lot of people. Standard classes will lose maybe ten percent on the first run. Architects? I'm estimating seventy percent mortality, minimum."

"That's a guess."

"It's an educated guess based on the fact that our tutorial mentors spent more time warning us about death than teaching us how to survive." She started walking, gesturing for him to follow. "Come on. I've set up a safe house near the harbor. We can pool our knowledge, train, and figure out how to not die."

Kai fell into step beside her. The city moved around them—chaotic, frightened, electric with the energy of a billion people suddenly granted power they didn't understand. Sirens wailed in the distance. Somewhere, glass shattered. A fight, probably. Arguments breaking out over resources, territory, the sudden lawlessness of a world without rules.

"What's your specialization?" Lena asked as they walked.

"Dao Architect. The system called it. I'm cultivating qi instead of mana."

"Qi. Like cultivation stories?"

"Exactly like that. Meridians, dantian, circulation." He reached out with his qi sense, feeling the energy in the air around them. "I can sense other people's qi signatures. Yours is... bright. Dense. Structured."

"You can sense that?"

"I'm still figuring out how it works. But yeah. Six hours of tutorial and I can feel the life force of a cockroach behind a wall."

Lena stopped walking. "That's not a standard Architect ability."

"It's not?"

"Mine is purely structural. I can build, modify, reinforce. But sensing? That's different." Her eyes narrowed. "What else can you do?"

Kai hesitated. He hadn't tested anything beyond basic circulation. But the thought had been nagging at him—a possibility that felt both impossible and inevitable.

"I think I can modify things," he said slowly. "Not build like you. But change. Adjust. Improve."

"Prove it."

They were standing near a park bench. An old woman sat on one end, feeding pigeons. On the other end, a broken slat jutted up at an angle, a hazard waiting to happen.

Kai focused. He reached out with his qi, felt the wood's structure—its grain, its age, the stress points where it had cracked. He imagined it whole. Smooth. Strong.

The qi flowed from his dantian, through his meridians, out through his palm. It was like breathing underwater. Like trying to thread a needle with a rope.

The wood shivered.

And then it reformed. The crack sealed. The splinters smoothed. The slat straightened, seamless, as if it had never been broken.

Lena stared. "That's not structure manipulation. That's *restoration*."

"Is that rare?"

"I don't know if it's possible." She grabbed his arm, pulling him forward. "We need to move faster. There's something I need to show you."

They hurried through the streets, past crowds and chaos, until they reached an abandoned warehouse near the harbor. The door was reinforced with steel plates that hummed with Lena's energy.

She pressed her hand to it. The locks clicked open.

Inside, the warehouse had been transformed. Walls of light formed rooms. A table floated in the center, covered in maps and notes. And in the corner, bound in chains of golden energy, was a creature Kai had never seen before.

It was humanoid. Mostly. Its skin was rough, like bark, and its eyes glowed with the same light as the System notifications. It snarled as they entered, straining against the chains.

"First spawn," Lena said. "It appeared in my kitchen an hour after my tutorial ended. Killed my neighbor before I figured out how to trap it."

Kai's blood ran cold. "What is it?"

"A scout. From the dungeon." She walked toward it, unafraid. "I've been interrogating it all morning. It doesn't speak English, but it understands intent. Watch."

She reached out and touched the creature's forehead. The golden chains flared, and the creature convulsed. Images flooded Kai's mind—a vast

title: Chapter 6
content:
CHAPTER 6: The First Quest

Kai stared at the System broadcast, his thumb hovering over the unsent message. Around him, the apartment felt suddenly smaller, the walls pressing in as if the world itself was contracting.

*World Integration*, the notification had said. Not *Game Integration*. Not *System Launch*. Integration.

He'd spent his whole life playing games that used similar language—world events, server merges, content patches. But this wasn't a game. The weight in his dantian, the qi flowing through meridians that shouldn't exist, the memory of Mentor's golden eyes—all of it screamed that this was something else entirely.

His phone buzzed again. Then again. Then a cascade of notifications that turned into a continuous hum against his nightstand.

Kai picked it up.

The group chat had exploded.

*Did everyone see that?*

*WORLD INTEGRATION? What does that mean?*

*My tutorial guide said something about "phase two" but wouldn't explain*

*Guys. Look outside.*

Kai's blood ran cold. He crossed to his window in three quick strides and pulled back the curtain.

The sky was wrong.

Not the color—it was still blue, still dotted with the same wispy clouds that had been there yesterday. But the light. The light filtered through something that hadn't been there before. A hexagonal grid, faint and shimmering, stretched across the heavens like a spiderweb made of glass. It pulsed with a soft, pearlescent glow, barely visible against the sun's brightness, but unmistakably there.

*The System's architecture*, Kai realized. *We're seeing the framework.*

His phone buzzed again. A direct message from an unknown number.

*Kai Chen. 127 Maple Street, Apartment 3B. Class: Unassigned. Status: Anomalous.*

He nearly dropped the phone.

*Who is this?*

The reply came instantly. *Your tutorial was incomplete. Report to Sector 7 Processing Center within 2 hours for mandatory reassessment. Failure to comply will result in penalties.*

A map appeared in the message, a blinking red dot marking a location three miles away. The words *SECTOR 7 OFFICIAL: SYSTEM ADMINISTRATION* appeared beneath it.

Kai's mind raced. *System Administration?* Since when did the System have administrators? Since when did it have *sectors*?

He typed back: *What penalties?*

No reply.

He waited thirty seconds. A minute. Nothing.

Outside, a siren wailed in the distance. Then another, closer. Car horns blared. Someone was screaming—not in pain, but in what sounded like pure, unfiltered panic.

Kai pulled on his shoes and grabbed his jacket. Whatever was happening, staying in his apartment felt like a trap.

---

The streets were chaos.

People milled about in confused clusters, some staring at their phones, others pointing at the sky. A woman in her bathrobe was crying on the curb, clutching a cat carrier. Two kids were chasing each other with what looked like actual swords—no, not swords. *Summoned* weapons, glowing faintly blue at the edges.

A Warrior class, probably. Level 1, if the System worked like the tutorials had suggested.

Kai kept his head down and moved through the crowd, heading toward the Sector 7 location. He didn't trust the message. He didn't trust any of this. But he needed information, and the only lead he had was that blinking red dot.

"You feel it too, right?"

The voice came from his left. A woman fell into step beside him, her movements casual but deliberate. She was maybe thirty, with sharp features and eyes that seemed to take in everything at once. No jacket, just a simple t-shirt that read *I SURVIVED THE TUTORIAL* in bold letters.

"Feel what?" Kai asked, not breaking stride.

"The grid." She pointed at the sky. "It's not just visual. It's *vibrating*. Like a tuning fork pressed against the world's skull."

Kai stopped walking. Turned to face her fully. "Who are you?"

"Name's Lin. Former software engineer, current... well, I don't have a class yet. The System couldn't figure me out."

Something cold settled in Kai's stomach. "You too?"

"Scanner said I was 'insufficiently defined.' Whatever that means." She grinned, but there was no humor in it. "They sent me a message. Told me to report to Sector 7 for reassessment."

"Same."

"Figured. They're rounding up all the anomalies. I've seen three others heading this way." She jerked her chin down the street. "There's another one. Guy in the blue hoodie."

Kai followed her gaze. A young man, maybe early twenties, was walking with his hands in his pockets, his head angled down. But he moved with a strange precision, each step measured, deliberate. Like he was counting his footfalls.

"Should we talk to him?" Kai asked.

"Already tried. He's not... he's not all there. Said his tutorial lasted three weeks. Three *weeks*, Kai. He came out speaking a language nobody recognizes."

" How do you know my name?"

Lin's grin widened. "I'm a software engineer, remember? I scraped the sector's database before they locked it down. You're listed as Priority Alpha Anomaly. Whatever that means, it's not good."

Kai's phone buzzed again. A reminder: *1 hour 45 minutes remaining.*

"We should keep moving," he said.

"Yeah. We should."

---

The Sector 7 Processing Center was a repurposed government building, all gray concrete and narrow windows. But someone—or something—had modified it. The walls were covered in glowing blue runes that pulsed in rhythm with the grid overhead. The doors had been replaced with shimmering barriers that reminded Kai uncomfortably of the portal that had taken him to his tutorial.

A line stretched out the front entrance. Dozens of people, all with the same dazed, anxious look. Some clutched phones. Others stared at the runes as if hoping to decode them.

"This is wrong," Lin murmured. "This isn't System protocol. The System is supposed to be automated. Decentralized. There shouldn't be *buildings*."

"Maybe the tutorial was just the beginning," Kai said. "Maybe the System needs administrators to handle the things it can't process."

"Like us."

"Like us."

They joined the line. The minutes crawled by. The sun climbed higher, and the grid in the sky grew more visible, its hexagonal patterns casting faint shadows on the ground.

A man in a suit emerged from the building, clipboard in hand. He looked ordinary—too ordinary, compared to the rune-covered walls and shimmering barriers. But when he spoke, his voice carried unnaturally, cutting through the ambient noise like a blade.

"Anomalies will be processed in order of arrival. You will be assigned a case worker who will evaluate your tutorial experience and determine your proper class designation. Do not resist. Do not argue. Compliance will be rewarded. Non-compliance will be... noted."

The last word hung in the air, heavy with implication.

Kai's turn came forty minutes later. The suit waved him through the barrier, and he stepped into a hallway that shouldn't have fit inside the building. It stretched on for what looked like miles, lined with doors that flickered in and out of existence.

"Room 7-C," the suit said, pointing. "Your case worker is waiting."

Kai walked. The hallway warped around him, the doors shifting, the walls breathing. He'd seen this kind of spatial manipulation before—in his tutorial. Mentor had reshaped the training chamber with a thought.

*They have access to System-level abilities*, he realized. *They're not just administrators. They're users.*

He found Room 7-C and pushed open the door.

The room was small, barely large enough for a desk and two chairs. Behind the desk sat a woman who looked like she'd stepped out of a corporate training video—neat blazer, professional smile, eyes that were just slightly too bright.

"Kai Chen." She gestured to the chair. "Please, sit."

He sat. "You're my case worker?"

"I am." She folded her hands on the desk. "My designation is Case Worker 7-C. You can call me... well, you can call me whatever you like. Names are flexible in this new world."

"What do you want?"
