"Straight to business. Good." She pulled a tablet from nowhere, its surface glowing with the same blue light as the runes. "Your tutorial was unusual. The System flagged it as an 'unexpected configuration.' Your class was registered as 'Dao Architect,' which doesn't appear in any of our records."

"Meaning?"

"Meaning you're a variable we can't account for. Variables are dangerous. They introduce instability. And instability, in a System designed to bring order to chaos, is unacceptable."

Kai's hands clenched under the desk. "So what happens now?"

She smiled. It didn't reach her eyes. "We have two options. Option one: you submit to a full System recalibration. We reset your class, reassign you something standard—something we can track and predict. You'll be a Warrior, a Mage, a Healer. Something that fits."

"And option two?"

"Option two is more... experimental. We leave your class as is, but we attach a monitoring protocol. You'll be tracked, analyzed, studied. Your growth will be documented. Your abilities will be cataloged. You'll be a resource."

Kai stared at her. "You mean a lab rat."

"I mean a valuable asset." She leaned forward. "The System is still young. It's learning. And sometimes, learning requires samples. You could contribute to the foundation of a new world order."

"And if I refuse both?"

Her smile vanished. "Then you become a liability. And liabilities are... dealt with."

The air in the room grew heavy. The runes on the walls pulsed faster. Kai felt his dantian thrum, his qi responding to the sudden tension.

"What about the others?" he asked. "The other anomalies. What happens to them?"

"That's not your concern."

"It is if we're all going to be 'dealt with.'"

The case worker's eyes narrowed. "You're not in a position to negotiate, Mr. Chen."

"I'm not negotiating." Kai stood up. "I'm leaving."

"I wouldn't recommend—"

The door slammed open behind him. Lin stood in the doorway, her eyes wild, her hands crackling with static electricity.

"Kai, we need to go. Now."

"What—"

"The grid is collapsing. Something's attacking the processing center. I don't know what, but it's eating through the runes like paper."

A tremor shook the building. The runes on the walls flickered, died, then flared back to life—wrong, their patterns twisted, their light now a sickly purple.

The case worker's professional mask cracked. For the first time, she looked afraid.

"That's not possible," she whispered. "That's not *supposed* to happen."

Kai didn't wait to hear more. He grabbed Lin's arm and ran.

Behind them, the purple light spread, devouring the runes, devouring the walls, devouring reality itself. And in the distance, rising above the chaos, Kai heard a voice he recognized.

Mentor's voice.

"You've found them, then. Good. Bring me the Architect."

A pause. Then, colder than ice:

"Kill the rest."

title: Chapter 7
content:
CHAPTER 7: The First Blood

Kai's finger hovered over the send button when the world *lurched*.

Not physically. The apartment remained solid beneath him, the water stain on the ceiling still tracing its familiar map of decay. But something shifted in the spiritual layer, a tectonic plate of reality grinding against its neighbor. His qi sense screamed a warning a split second before the notification appeared:

[SYSTEM BROADCAST: TUTORIAL PHASE COMPLETE]
[ALL CLASSES HAVE BEEN ASSIGNED]
[WORLD INTEGRATION COMMENCING: PHASE 1]
[WARNING: MONSTER SPAWN ACTIVATION IN 3...2...1...]

The countdown hit zero.

Kai threw himself off the bed as his window exploded inward. Glass sprayed across the room, glittering like frozen rain in the morning light. Something landed where his head had been, a mass of chitin and muscle that crushed his mattress flat.

He rolled, came up in a crouch, and got his first real look at a monster.

It looked like a mantis shrimp crossed with a nightmare. Four feet of segmented armor, sickly green, with six legs and two massive claws that clicked together with the sound of shearing metal. Its face was a cluster of black eyes, each one swiveling independently, tracking his movement with predatory precision.

His phone shattered beneath one of its claws. The screen flickered once, showing the unsent message, before going dark.

*Of course,* Kai thought, rage and fear mixing in his chest. *Of course the monsters spawn the moment I wake up.*

The creature lunged.

Kai's body moved before his mind caught up. Six hours of meditation, of cycling qi through unfamiliar meridians, had rewired something fundamental in his nervous system. He sidestepped the claw strike, felt the wind of its passage ruffle his shirt, and brought his palm up in a motion that felt both foreign and instinctive.

Qi gathered in his dantian. Flowed through his arm. Coalesced in his palm.

[SKILL UNLOCKED: Qi Palm (Novice)]
[EFFECT: Convert qi into kinetic force. Damage scales with qi density.]

He struck.

The impact was like slapping concrete. His palm connected with the monster's carapace, and a shockwave rippled outward, cracking the wall behind it. The creature skittered sideways, claws flailing, one of its black eyes leaking a viscous fluid.

But it didn't die.

It recovered faster than he expected, pivoting on its six legs and launching another attack. This time Kai wasn't fast enough. A claw caught him across the chest, tearing through his shirt and leaving three bloody furrows across his ribs.

The pain was electric. White-hot. He gasped, stumbling backward, and his qi circulation stuttered.

[WARNING: QI DEVIATION RISK INCREASED TO 3.2%]
[SUGGESTED ACTION: STABILIZE BREATHING BEFORE NEXT ATTACK]

Right. Breathing. Mentor had drilled that into him. *Qi follows intent, but it rides the breath. If you panic, your qi panics too.*

The monster was circling, its remaining eyes tracking his blood with obvious hunger. It wasn't rushing now. It knew he was wounded. It was savoring the kill.

Kai forced himself to inhale. Slow. Deep. Felt the qi settle back into its proper channels, the chaotic eddies smoothing out as his focus returned.

He couldn't outfight this thing. His only combat skill was Novice rank, and the monster's carapace had barely cracked from his strongest strike. He needed—

His eyes caught the kitchen. Specifically, the knife block on the counter.

*Or I need to be smarter.*

The monster lunged again. Kai dove sideways, not toward the kitchen but away from it, drawing the creature's attention. He grabbed a lamp from his nightstand and threw it. The monster's claws snapped it out of the air, distracted for a single second.

That second was all he needed.

He sprinted for the kitchen, vaulted over the counter, and grabbed the largest knife—a butcher's blade he'd used exactly once to cut a frozen pizza. It was dull, heavy, and completely inadequate for fighting a monster.

But it was steel. And steel, in the right hands, could do a lot of damage.

The monster crashed through the counter behind him, wood splintering, dishes exploding. Kai spun, knife raised, and saw it coming—claws extended, mouth open, revealing rows of teeth that glistened with something that was definitely not water.

He didn't think. He just moved.

[SKILL EVOLUTION DETECTED: Qi Palm → Qi Blade (Novice)]
[EFFECT: Channel qi through edged weapons. Increases cutting power by 300%.]

The qi flooded into the knife, and the blade *glowed*. Not with light, but with a strange inner luminescence, as if the metal itself had become translucent. The edge seemed to sharpen itself, the cheap steel taking on an almost surgical gleam.

Kai brought the knife up in an arc.

The monster's claw came down.

They met.

For a heartbeat, time froze. Kai could see every detail—the chitin splintering at the point of impact, the qi沿着the blade's edge like liquid fire, the look of shock in the creature's multiple eyes.

Then the claw separated from the arm.

Black ichor sprayed across the kitchen. The monster screamed, a high-pitched shriek that rattled the windows and made Kai's ears ring. It recoiled, staggering on its remaining legs, one claw dangling uselessly from a severed stump.

Kai didn't give it time to recover.

He pressed forward, knife flashing. A cut to the leg. Another to the thorax. The creature was slowing now, its movements growing sluggish as ichor pooled beneath it. He aimed for the cluster of eyes, and the blade sank deep into the central orb.

The monster went rigid. Twitched once. Collapsed.

[COMBAT COMPLETE]
[ENEMY DEFEATED: Rift Imp (Level 3)]
[XP GAINED: 45]
[SKILL PROGRESS: Qi Blade (Novice) 12% → 23%]

Kai stood over the corpse, breathing hard, the knife still glowing faintly in his hand. His chest burned where the claws had cut him. His dantian throbbed with the strain of channeling qi through an unfamiliar technique. But he was alive.

He looked at the knife.

*Qi Blade.*

That was new. The System had evolved his skill mid-combat, adapting to his needs. It wasn't just assigning abilities—it was learning from him, responding to his intent.

*What else can it do?*

The thought was interrupted by screams from outside.

He ran to the broken window, ignoring the glass crunching beneath his bare feet. The street below was chaos. People were running, screaming, as monsters of various shapes and sizes emerged from thin air. A giant ant was dragging a man into a sewer grate. A floating orb of purple energy was chasing a group of children. In the distance, he saw a woman in a flowing robe—a Mage, probably—launching fireballs at a creature that looked like a walking tree.

The Tutorial was over.

The real game had begun.

Kai's phone was dead, crushed beneath the Rift Imp's claw. But his laptop was still intact, lying on the floor where he'd dropped it yesterday. He grabbed it, flipped it open, and waited for it to boot.

The screen flickered to life.

His fingers flew across the keyboard, pulling up news sites, forums, social media. The internet was still working—barely. The headlines were a mix of terrified and triumphant:

*"MONSTERS SPAWN IN 47 MAJOR CITIES—GOVERNMENT DECLARES STATE OF EMERGENCY"*
*"SYSTEM GUIDE: HOW TO LEVEL YOUR CLASS IN THE FIRST 24 HOURS"*
*"MYTHIC CLASS DISCOVERED IN JAPAN—PLAYER CLAIMS 'SUN WUKONG' INHERITANCE"*
*"CASUALTY COUNT RISING: 12,000 DEAD IN FIRST HOUR"*

Twelve thousand. In one hour.

Kai's stomach turned. He thought of his mother, her qi signature faint but steady when he'd sensed it earlier. He sent a prayer to whatever gods still existed that she was safe.

A new message popped up. His gaming group chat, somehow still active:

*Anyone else fighting monsters right now?*
*Yeah, I'm barricaded in my apartment. Level 2 Warrior. Killed three rat-things.*
*Mark's dead. Crafting tutorial didn't teach him combat. A spider got him.*

Mark. The potion maker. The guy who'd reached Apprentice rank in six hours.

Dead.

Kai stared at the message, the reality of the situation crashing down on him. This wasn't a game. There were no respawns, no second chances. People were dying, and the System didn't care. It had given them classes and skills, but it had also given them monsters and death.

*"You have been chosen to build something new."*

Mentor's words echoed in his mind. She'd known this was coming. She'd prepared him for it, in her own cryptic way. But she hadn't warned him about the scale of it. The sheer, brutal chaos of the world collapsing overnight.

His laptop pinged again.

[SYSTEM BROADCAST: WORLD INTEGRATION PHASE 1 COMPLETE]
[REGIONAL DUNGEONS UNLOCKING IN 24 HOURS]
[PLAYER RANKINGS NOW ACTIVE]
[REWARD: TOP 1000 PLAYERS BY LEVEL WILL RECEIVE UNIQUE CLASS EVOLUTIONS]

Rankings. Of course. The System was turning humanity into a competition, pitting them against each other for rewards and recognition. It was the oldest trick in the book—divide and conquer.

But Kai had no interest in rankings. He had something more important to do.

He opened a new browser tab and typed a single search query:

*"Dao Architect class"*

The results were empty. Not a single mention. Not on forums, not on news sites, not in the System's official documentation. It was as if his class didn't exist.

*Or as if it's not supposed to exist yet.*

He thought of Mentor, of the golden-eyed woman who'd taught him to cycle qi. She'd called him an "Architect"—someone who builds, not someone who fights. But she'd also taught him to fight, to survive, to adapt.

She was preparing him for something bigger.

The apartment shook. Another explosion, closer this time. Kai looked out the window and saw a plume of smoke rising from a building two blocks away. More screams. More chaos.

He grabbed his knife, still stained with black ichor, and made a decision.

He couldn't save everyone. But he could save himself. He could get stronger, learn more, understand the System's secrets before it was too late. He could become the Architect Mentor had trained him to be.

And maybe, just maybe, he could build something that would change everything.

He stepped out of his apartment, into the hallway, and nearly collided with a woman running past. She was crying, her clothes torn, a deep gash on her arm. She looked at him, eyes wild with fear.

"Get back inside!" she screamed. "There's more of them! They're coming from the roof!"

Kai looked up.

The ceiling was cracking.

And through the cracks, he could hear them—a chittering, skittering sound, like a thousand claws scraping against concrete.

The monsters were already here.

[QUEST TR

title: The Assessment That Changed Everything
content:
Kai stared at the broadcast notification until it faded, his thumb hovering over the send button. He deleted the message and typed something else: *I'm alive. We'll talk later.*

He tossed the phone aside and sat up, swinging his legs over the edge of the bed. His body felt strange—lighter, yet denser. Like he'd traded fat for iron while sleeping.

The cockroach behind his wall stopped moving.

Kai froze. He could still sense it, a tiny flicker of life force, but it wasn't skittering anymore. It was *waiting*. Watching him.

*No. That's impossible. Cockroaches don't watch people.*

But the feeling persisted. A prickling awareness at the back of his neck, like being observed by something that shouldn't be intelligent enough to observe.

He pushed the thought aside and stood. The floor creaked under his weight—same as always—but his footsteps felt different. More deliberate. More controlled.

The apartment looked exactly as he'd left it: dirty dishes in the sink, clothes draped over the chair, trash piled in the corner. But now he could *see* it differently. The layer of grime on the windows wasn't just dirt—it was stagnation. The half-eaten takeout containers weren't just trash—they were wasted potential.

*Mentor's words are getting into my head.*

He walked to the kitchen and poured himself a glass of water. As he drank, he extended his qi sense outward, testing its range. The neighbor above him—a middle-aged man named Chen—was pacing. His qi signature fluctuated with anxiety. The couple next door were arguing, their qi clashing and recoiling like opposing magnets. The building's qi network was tangled, inefficient, riddled with blockages.

*They can't feel this,* Kai realized. *They don't know what they're breathing.*

Another notification appeared:

[SYSTEM ALERT: ALL CITIZENS WILL REPORT TO THEIR DESIGNATED ASSESSMENT CENTERS WITHIN 24 HOURS. FAILURE TO COMPLY WILL RESULT IN PENALTY.]

[LOCATION: DISTRICT 7 MUNICIPAL HALL, 3RD FLOOR, ROOM 312]

[TIME: 8:00 AM]

[PENALTY FOR NON-COMPLIANCE: CLASS REVOCATION]

Kai's blood ran cold. Class revocation. The System could *take away* what it had given?

He grabbed his phone and scrolled through the group chat. The broadcast was everywhere. People were panicking.

*Class revocation? What does that even mean?*

*It means you go back to being baseline human while everyone else levels up.*

*That's basically death.*

*My friend in District 3 says their assessment is mandatory combat trials. They're ranking people by combat effectiveness.*

*District 7 hasn't released details yet. Anyone know what's waiting for us?*

Kai's stomach tightened. He had a class that the System didn't recognize. A cultivation path that existed outside the normal framework. What would an assessment even measure? And what would happen when they found out he didn't fit?

He spent the rest of the night trying to stabilize his foundation. Mentor's instructions echoed in his mind—the breathing patterns, the circulation cycles, the delicate balance of yin and yang. But without the tutorial chamber's purified qi, progress was agonizingly slow. The ambient qi of the city was thin and polluted, laced with the static of electronics and the stale residue of human emotion.

By dawn, he'd managed three complete cycles. His dantian felt fuller, more settled, but far from stable.

The walk to District 7 Municipal Hall was surreal. The streets were crowded with people moving in the same direction, their faces a mix of excitement and fear. Some had already begun to change—a woman with faint green runes glowing on her arms, a man whose steps left faint impressions in the concrete, a teenager whose eyes flickered with residual lightning.

*They're all adapting,* Kai thought. *Growing into their classes. Becoming something new.*

He was adapting too. But toward what?

The Municipal Hall was a squat concrete building that had once housed the local tax office. Now it was surrounded by barricades and System-generated checkpoints. People funneled through in lines, each person stopping at a glowing blue terminal that scanned them and directed them to specific rooms.

Kai joined the line, keeping his head down. The person ahead of him—a heavyset man in a stained polo shirt—was sweating profusely. "I didn't sign up for this," he muttered. "I just wanted to play games. I didn't want to *fight*."

"Nobody signed up for anything," Kai said quietly. "It happened."

"Yeah, but—" The man turned, and his eyes widened. "Wait. I know you. You're the guy who streamed that speedrun of Eternal Abyss. You're Kai Chen."

Kai stiffened. "That was a long time ago."

"Man, you were *good*. You think that matters? For the assessment?"

"No," Kai said. "I don't think it matters at all."

The line moved forward. When Kai reached the terminal, a flat synthetic voice said: "Name: Kai Chen. Age: 22. Class: Dao Architect. Classification: Unregistered."

*Unregistered.* Of course.

The terminal paused, as if processing something unexpected. Then: "Proceed to Room 312. Second door on the left."

Kai walked through the checkpoint, his heart pounding. The hallway was lined with doors, each one marked with a number. He could hear sounds from behind them—the crackle of energy, the thud of impacts, the muffled cries of exertion.

Room 312 was at the end of the hall. The door was metal, smooth, without a handle. It slid open as he approached, revealing a small white room with a single figure standing in the center.

She was tall, with sharp features and short-cropped silver hair. Her eyes were the same unnatural gold as Mentor's, but colder. More clinical. She wore a fitted black uniform with no insignia, and her posture radiated authority.

"Kai Chen," she said. "I am Assessor Seven. You have been flagged for special evaluation."

"Flagged? Why?"

"Your class does not match any known classification. The System's records show your tutorial was..." She paused, as if searching for the right word. "...anomalous. You spent six hours in a cultivation chamber, which is not a standard tutorial environment. You emerged with a partially formed foundation in an energy system that is not recognized by the System's framework."

Kai's mouth went dry. "You can see all that?"

"The System records everything." Assessor Seven stepped closer. "You are not a Warrior. Not a Mage. Not a Healer. Not any of the thirty-seven recognized class archetypes. You are something else entirely."

"What does that mean for the assessment?"

"It means I must determine if you are a threat."

Before Kai could respond, the room shifted. The walls dissolved into a featureless white void. The floor disappeared, leaving him standing on nothing.

"Your assessment will be combat-based," Assessor Seven said, her voice echoing from everywhere and nowhere. "Survive for five minutes. That is all."

A figure materialized across from him. It was humanoid, featureless, made of shimmering gray light. Its movements were jerky, unnatural, like a puppet with tangled strings.

*It's a construct,* Kai realized. *System-generated.*

The construct lunged.

Kai's body moved before his mind caught up. He sidestepped, the construct's fist grazing his shoulder. The impact sent a jolt through his qi network, rattling his foundation.

*I can feel its qi.*

The construct was a mass of chaotic energy—unstable, inefficient, but powerful. It attacked again, faster this time. Kai ducked under a wild swing and rolled, coming up in a low stance.

*Mentor said qi isn't for combat. But that was before.*

He reached into his dantian and pulled. A thin stream of qi flowed through his meridians, gathering in his palm. He didn't know what he was doing—he was improvising, relying on instinct and the fragments of cultivation theory Mentor had drilled into him.

The construct charged. Kai thrust his palm forward, releasing the qi in a burst.

It wasn't a fireball. It wasn't a lightning bolt. It was just *force*—a shockwave of raw energy that slammed into the construct and sent it stumbling backward. Cracks spread across its form.

Assessor Seven's voice came through, sharp and curious: "What was that?"

Kai didn't answer. He was already recovering, pulling more qi, cycling it through his meridians. His dantian throbbed in protest, but he ignored it.

The construct reformed and attacked again. Kai met it head-on, palm strikes and qi bursts and desperate improvisations. Each exchange cost him. His foundation wobbled. His meridians ached. But he kept fighting.

*Two minutes. Three. Four.*

The construct dissolved. The white void collapsed. He was standing in Room 312 again, drenched in sweat, breathing hard.

Assessor Seven was watching him with an expression he couldn't read. "You passed," she said. "Barely. But you passed."

Kai leaned forward, hands on his knees. "What now?"

"Now you are classified. The System will assign you to a development path based on your performance. Given the nature of your class, I have recommended you for the Special Projects Division."

"What's that?"

"Exactly what it sounds like. Projects that require... specialized handling." She turned toward the door. "You will receive your assignment within 48 hours. Until then, report to the holding area with the other assessees."

"The holding area?"

"Follow the hall. You'll see it."

Kai walked out of Room 312, his legs unsteady. The hallway stretched longer than he remembered, lined with doors that hadn't been there before. He followed them until he reached a large open room filled with people.

Hundreds of people. All sitting on the floor, huddled in groups, staring at a massive screen mounted on the far wall.

The screen displayed a single line of text:

[WELCOME TO THE SYSTEM. CLASSES ARE NOW LOCKED. ASSESSMENT RESULTS WILL DETERMINE YOUR ROLE IN THE NEW WORLD.]

Below it, a countdown timer ticked:

[47:52:31]

Kai found an empty spot against the wall and sat down. Someone nearby was crying. Someone else was laughing hysterically. A woman was muttering to herself, reciting what sounded like a prayer.

*This is the new world,* Kai thought. *And I don't know the rules.*

His phone buzzed. He pulled it out and saw a message from an unknown number:

*You've been flagged. They know about the cultivation. Be careful who you trust.*

Kai stared at the message, his blood turning cold. He typed back: *Who is this?*

The reply came instantly:

*Someone who was in the tutorial before you. The one who didn't make it.*

*Meet me at the old library on 7th Street. Come alone. Come tonight.*

*Before they decide what to do with you.*

The screen flickered. The countdown timer changed:

[47:48:12]

Kai pocketed his phone and stood up. He didn't know who this person was, or how they knew about his tutorial. But one thing was clear:

The System wasn't just assigning classes.

It was building something. And some people weren't meant to survive the construction.

title: The Weight of Heaven's Eyes
content:
Kai's apartment had become a prison.

Three weeks since the tutorial ended. Three weeks since the world changed. And three weeks since he'd discovered that his class was something the System itself couldn't categorize.

He sat cross-legged on his threadbare carpet, knees aching against the thin padding, cycling qi through his meridians. The morning light filtered through his blinds, casting stripes across the water-stained ceiling. Each breath drew qi into his dantian, each exhale pushed it through his channels in a continuous loop.

*Twenty-three circulations today. Yesterday was twenty. The day before, eighteen.*

Progress. Slow, grinding, agonizing progress.

His phone lay face-down beside him, buzzing every few minutes with notifications he'd learned to ignore. The group chats had devolved into chaos. People were forming parties, hunting "spawn points" that had appeared in parks and abandoned buildings, grinding levels like their lives depended on it.

Because their lives *did* depend on it.

The System had announced experience points for killing monsters. It had announced level requirements for equipment. It had announced that in thirty days, the first "Dungeon Integration" would occur, and anyone below Level 5 would be barred from entry.

*Level 5. I'm still Level 1.*

Kai opened his status screen, the translucent blue text hovering before his eyes:

---

**Name:** Kai Chen
**Class:** Dao Architect (Unclassified)
**Level:** 1
**Cultivation:** Qi Condensation (Stage 1 - Foundation)
**Meridians:** 8/12 (Primary Channels)
**Dantian Capacity:** 3/100 (Qi Units)
**Techniques:** Basic Qi Circulation (Novice - 23%)

**System Notes:**
- Class progression unknown
- Skill tree unavailable
- No experience gain detected from combat
- Alternative advancement method detected: CULTIVATION

---

*Unclassified. Unknown. Unavailable.*

The System had no framework for what he was doing. No quests, no achievements, no convenient level-up notifications. Just a dantian that slowly filled with qi and a body that grew stronger with each circulation.

His phone buzzed again. Kai grabbed it, expecting another frantic message from Jake about the "Goblin Camp" that had spawned in the old warehouse district.

Instead, he found a text from an unknown number:

*You're the one. The unclassified. Meet me at the Sunrise Café, downtown. 2 PM. Come alone. — L.*

Kai's blood ran cold.

He read the message three times, searching for any hint of who sent it. The number was unlisted, the text stripped of any metadata. But the word "unclassified" hit like a punch to the gut.

*No one knows. I never told anyone.*

He'd been careful. He'd deflected questions from friends, lied to his mother, avoided the System forums where people posted their class details. As far as anyone knew, he was a low-tier crafter who'd gotten a useless "Architect" class.

But someone knew.

---

The Sunrise Café was a hole-in-the-wall establishment tucked between a laundromat and a pawn shop, its neon sign flickering with the erratic pulse of a dying star. Kai arrived fifteen minutes early, scanning the interior through the grimy windows before entering.

The place was empty except for a single figure in the back corner.

She was young—maybe early twenties—with sharp features and hair cropped so short it was almost a buzz cut. Her eyes were the color of old copper, catching the light in ways that made Kai's qi sense prickle with warning. She wore a simple black jacket, unzipped to reveal a t-shirt with a faded band logo.

When she saw him, she smiled. It didn't reach her eyes.

"Kai Chen. Sit."

He didn't sit. "Who are you?"

"Lena." She gestured to the chair across from her. "I'm not going to bite. Though I could, if I wanted. My class gives me some interesting options."

"What class?"

"Blood Weaver." She said it with the casual confidence of someone who'd already won the argument. "Niche, but effective. I can manipulate hemoglobin. Yours, your iron levels, anyone's within about thirty feet. Makes for interesting conversations."

Kai's hand twitched toward the door. "Why am I here?"

"Because you're the only unclassified in the city. Probably the country. Maybe the world." Lena leaned forward, her copper eyes boring into his. "I've been monitoring System broadcasts since the tutorial ended. Cross-referencing class distributions, anomalies, outliers. You're the biggest anomaly I've found."

"What do you want?"

"Partnership."

The word hung in the air between them, heavy with implication.

Kai finally sat, keeping the table between them. "Explain."

Lena reached into her jacket and pulled out a tablet, swiping through screens until she found what she wanted. She turned it to face him.

It was a map. A map of the city, overlaid with colored dots—red, blue, green, yellow—each clustered in specific zones.

"These are class distributions," she said. "Red for combat classes. Blue for support. Green for crafting. Yellow for utility. See the pattern?"

Kai studied the map. The dots clustered around specific locations—parks, schools, hospitals, government buildings. But there were gaps. Empty zones where no one had received classes.

"The System didn't assign classes evenly," Lena continued. "It prioritized certain locations. Certain *people*. The distribution isn't random—it's algorithmic, based on some criteria we haven't figured out yet."

"And I'm the anomaly because I wasn't part of the algorithm."

"Exactly." Lena's smile sharpened. "You weren't assigned a class. You received something *outside* the System's framework. That makes you valuable. Or dangerous. Depending on who's asking."

Kai's mind raced. "Who else knows?"

"About you? Just me. About the anomaly in general? More people than I'd like. There are groups forming—factions, they're calling themselves—who want to study unclassified individuals. Some want to help. Some want to... *harvest* them."

"Harvest?"

"Classes are transferable now. Did you know that?" Lena's voice dropped, the casual tone replaced by something darker. "If you kill someone with a rare class, there's a chance—a small chance—that their class crystal drops. You can absorb it. Gain their abilities."

Kai's stomach turned. "That's—"

"That's the System's hidden mechanic. The one it didn't announce." Lena's copper eyes gleamed. "The tutorial was just the beginning. The real game hasn't started yet."

---

They talked for another hour.

Lena explained what she'd discovered: the emerging factions, the hidden mechanics, the power structures forming in the shadows of the System's integration. She spoke of "Awakeners" who'd reached Level 10 in the first week, of "Dungeon Pioneers" who'd already cleared their first instances, of "Class Harvesters" who hunted the weak for their abilities.

And she spoke of the unclassified.

"There are others," she said, her voice dropping to a whisper. "Not many. Maybe a dozen worldwide, based on my data. But they're being hunted. The factions want them. The System wants to *understand* them. And the harvesters want their class crystals."

"Why haven't you turned me in?"

"Because I'm unclassified too."

Kai's breath caught.

Lena pulled up her sleeve, revealing a forearm covered in intricate tattoos—not ink, but something that pulsed with a faint, organic light. "The System called me a Blood Weaver. But that's not what I am. That's just what it *labeled* me. My actual class doesn't exist in its database."

She tapped her arm, and the tattoos shifted, rearranging into patterns that made Kai's meridians ache with recognition.

"Qi," he breathed. "You have meridians."

"Twelve primary channels. Half of them open." Lena's smile finally reached her eyes. "I've been cultivating for two weeks. The System thinks I'm leveling through combat. But I'm not gaining experience from kills—I'm converting the residual qi from the monsters I hunt."

"You're like me."

"Not exactly. My foundation is different. Warped by the Blood Weaver label. But the core principle is the same." She leaned forward, her voice urgent. "The System is a cage, Kai. A beautiful, functional cage designed to contain humanity's potential. But we—the unclassified, the anomalies—we have a key."

"What do you want me to do?"

"Train. Grow. And when the time comes, help me break the cage."

---

Kai left the café with his mind spinning.

The streets were different now. The System had changed everything—the air tasted of ozone and possibility, the buildings hummed with residual qi, and the people moved with purpose, their classes visible in the way they walked, the way they spoke, the way they *existed*.

He was halfway home when his phone buzzed again.

This time, it wasn't a text. It was a System notification, red and urgent, flashing in his peripheral vision:

[WARNING: ANOMALY DETECTED]
[CLASSIFICATION: UNCLASSIFIED ENTITY]
[INITIATING PROTOCOL: INTEGRATION]

[SYSTEM OVERRIDE IN PROGRESS...]
[RECLASSIFYING...]
[ERROR: CLASS INCOMPATIBLE WITH SYSTEM FRAMEWORK]
[ERROR: UNABLE TO ASSIGN STANDARD CLASSIFICATION]
[ERROR: ENTITY RESISTS INTEGRATION]

[ESCALATING TO ADMINISTRATIVE LEVEL]

[SYSTEM ADMINISTRATOR NOTIFIED]

Kai's blood turned to ice.

The notification vanished, replaced by a single message that burned itself into his vision:

**YOU ARE MARKED.**

**THE HEAVENLY DAO SEEKS BALANCE.**

**ALL ANOMALIES WILL BE CORRECTED.**

The world went white.

When Kai opened his eyes, he was no longer on the street. He was in a void—infinite, silent, empty—with a single figure standing before him.

Mentor.

But different. Her golden eyes were cold, her silver hair floating in an unseen wind, her robes rippling with power that made the air itself tremble.

"Kai Chen," she said, her voice echoing through the void. "You have been noticed."

"By who?"

"By the System. By the Dao. By everything that exists between." She stepped closer, and the void shuddered. "The integration protocol has been triggered. The Administrators are coming."

"Administrators?"

"The original architects of the System. The ones who built the cage." Mentor's expression softened, just slightly. "They will try to force you into a class. To make you fit their framework. To remove your potential for growth."

"What happens if they succeed?"

"Then you become like everyone else. Limited. Bound. Controlled." She reached out, her hand hovering near his chest. "But if you resist—if you can hold your foundation—you might become something more."

"How?"

Mentor's golden eyes flared. "You need to reach Qi Condensation Stage 2 before they find you. You need to stabilize your foundation. And you need to do it *now*."

"Stage 2? I'm barely at Stage 1!"

"Then you'd better learn fast." She gestured, and the void dissolved, replaced by a training chamber identical to the one from his tutorial. "We have approximately six hours before the Administrators arrive. Six hours to compress your qi into a stable core."

"What happens after six hours?"

Mentor's voice dropped to a whisper

title: Chapter 10
content:
CHAPTER 10: The Price of Attention

Kai's finger hovered over the send button when the world *lurched*.

It wasn't physical—his apartment remained solid beneath him, the cracked ceiling still holding steady. But something deeper shifted. The qi in the air rippled like a pond struck by a stone, and every living thing within his perception seemed to pause, as if the planet itself had drawn a collective breath.

Then the screaming started.

Not audible screaming. *Qi* screaming. The spiritual signatures of thousands of people in his city alone flared simultaneously—some with terror, some with excitement, some with a confusion so raw it felt like static against his senses.

His phone buzzed again. Then again. Then a torrent of notifications flooded his vision:

*SYSTEM ALERT: World Integration Wave 1 of 7 complete.*
*SYSTEM ALERT: Monster spawning protocols initialized.*
*SYSTEM ALERT: Dungeon generation beginning in high-density population areas.*
*SYSTEM ALERT: Player versus Player combat restrictions lifted in designated zones.*

Kai's blood ran cold.

He'd been so focused on his own bizarre situation—the unclassified class, Mentor's cryptic warnings, the fragile qi circulating through his newly-formed meridians—that he'd forgotten the bigger picture. The System hadn't descended on Earth to give people superpowers. It had descended to *change* Earth.

To turn it into a game world.

His phone kept buzzing. He ignored it, reaching out with his qi sense instead, stretching his perception as far as his still-weak cultivation would allow.

The results made his stomach drop.

Three blocks away, a qi signature that had been "normal human" moments ago now blazed like a bonfire—someone had triggered a class evolution. Two blocks north, a signature was *diminishing*, flickering like a candle in wind. Dying.

And everywhere, *everywhere*, new presences were appearing.

Monsters.

They felt wrong. Their qi was twisted, jagged, laced with something that made his newly-awakened meridians ache in sympathetic protest. They weren't natural. They were *made*, assembled from raw System code and forced into physical form.

*This is what Mentor warned me about.*

His phone buzzed one final time, and this notification wasn't text. It was a System message, glowing gold in his peripheral vision:

*SYSTEM QUEST: First Blood*
*Objective: Defeat 1 monster within your immediate vicinity.*
*Reward: 100 XP, 1 Skill Point, Class-specific item.*
*Failure Penalty: None (automatic quest expiry in 24 hours)*
*Time Remaining: 23:59:58*

Kai stared at the quest. It was optional. No penalty for ignoring it. But the wording bothered him—"within your immediate vicinity" suggested the System knew exactly where the nearest monster was.

And that it expected him to fight.

"I'm a Dao Architect," he muttered to himself. "I don't even have combat skills. I have meridians and breathing exercises."

His phone buzzed again. This time he looked.

The group chat was exploding.

*JAKE: THERE'S A F***ING GOBLIN IN MY KITCHEN*
*MARK: I'm barricaded in my workshop. Something's scratching at the door.*
*LISA: Has anyone figured out how to use skills yet? I have [Fireball] but I don't know how to cast it.*
*JAKE: IT THREW A KNIFE AT ME. A KNIFE. WHO GIVES GOBLINS KNIVES?*
*MARK: I can hear it breathing. It knows I'm in here.*

Kai's fingers moved before his brain caught up.

*KAIS: Jake. What's your address?*

*JAKE: Why? You gonna come save me? You're a crafter, right?*

*KAIS: Just tell me.*

Jake sent his address. He lived six buildings away.

Kai looked at his own apartment door. The thin wood. The cheap lock. The complete lack of weapons or defensive measures.

Then he looked inward, at the qi circulating through his meridians. It was weak. Unstable. A thread-thin river compared to the ocean Mentor had shown him was possible. But it was *his*.

*"The Dao is not about strength,"* Mentor had said. *"It is about understanding. Power follows comprehension."*

He didn't understand much. But he understood that if he stayed in this apartment, if he hid while monsters spawned and people died, he would never progress. He would stagnate. And in a world that was rapidly becoming a game, stagnation meant death.

Kai grabbed his backpack. Empty water bottles, a pocket knife that couldn't cut butter, a half-eaten granola bar. Pathetic. But it was something.

He opened his apartment door.

The hallway was quiet. Too quiet. The usual sounds of neighbors moving around, TVs playing, arguments echoing through thin walls—all gone. Replaced by a heavy, pregnant silence that pressed against his ears.

He stepped out, and his qi sense flared.

Something was at the end of the hallway. Near the stairwell. Its qi signature was small—roughly the size of a child—but dense and aggressive, pulsing with a rhythm that didn't match any living thing Kai had ever sensed.

*Monster.*

It hadn't noticed him yet. It was focused on the apartment nearest the stairs, scratching at the door with a methodical, almost patient insistence.

Kai crept forward, keeping his footsteps silent, his breathing controlled. The pocket knife was in his hand, but he knew it was useless. Even if he managed to stab the thing, a blade that small wouldn't do meaningful damage.

He needed another option.

*Think. What can you do?*

His class was Dao Architect. His only skills were [Meridian Awakening] and [Qi Circulation]. Neither was combat-oriented. But Mentor had said the class was about *understanding*. About seeing the underlying structure of things.

What if he could see the monster's structure?

Kai stopped walking, closed his eyes, and pushed his qi sense to its limit. The monster's signature burned in his perception, and he forced himself to look deeper, past the surface aggression, past the jagged qi, into the *code* beneath.

His mind touched something cold.

It felt like a program. A construct. The monster was built from System data—a template overlaid with flesh, animated by a spark of artificial qi. Its structure was simple, almost crude. A basic combat script, a handful of behavioral triggers, a self-preservation instinct that prioritized retreat over fighting to the death.

And at its core, a weakness.

A nexus point where the construct's qi converged. A single spot, roughly where a human's heart would be, that served as the anchor for the monster's existence. Destroy that, and the construct would unravel.

Kai opened his eyes.

He knew where to strike.

The monster—goblin, Jake had called it—turned as he approached. It was hideous. Green skin, yellow eyes, a mouth full of needle-thin teeth. It wore tattered rags and carried a rusted dagger that looked far too sharp for something so small.

It hissed at him.

Kai didn't react. He just walked forward, his qi sense locked onto the nexus point in the creature's chest. His hand tightened on the pocket knife.

*Three steps. Two. One.*

The goblin lunged.

Kai sidestepped—barely—and brought the knife up in a clumsy arc. The blade caught the monster's arm instead of its chest, scraping across green skin without breaking it.

The goblin snarled and slashed with its dagger, the rusty blade whistling past Kai's face close enough to draw a thin line of blood across his cheek.

Pain. Real, sharp, *human* pain.

*This isn't a game.*

He couldn't afford to play hero with a pocket knife. He needed to use what made him different.

As the goblin pulled back for another attack, Kai reached out with his qi sense and *pushed*. Not physically—spiritually. He channeled his meager qi into a pulse aimed directly at the monster's core, disrupting the construct's stability.

The goblin froze.

Its yellow eyes widened in something that might have been confusion. Its qi flickered, the nexus point destabilizing for just a moment.

A moment was all Kai needed.

He lunged forward, not with the knife, but with his fingers extended, qi concentrated at their tips. He stabbed into the goblin's chest, right where the nexus point pulsed.

His fingers sank through flesh like it wasn't there.

The goblin convulsed. Its body twisted, spasmed, and then collapsed, the qi unraveling from the core outward until nothing remained but a pile of green dust and the System notification that appeared in Kai's vision:

*SYSTEM ALERT: Monster defeated! +50 XP*
*Quest "First Blood" complete! Rewards pending distribution.*
*Bonus achieved: First monster kill in district. Additional +100 XP awarded.*

Kai stared at his hands. They were covered in green residue, trembling slightly. His fingers ached where they'd pierced the construct's chest.

He'd just killed something.

It wasn't human. It wasn't even truly alive. But he'd ended its existence with his bare hands.

His phone buzzed.

*JAKE: KAI WHERE ARE YOU I CAN HEAR IT SCRATCHING FASTER*

Right. Jake was still trapped.

Kai forced himself to move, stepping over the pile of green dust and heading for the stairwell. The monster near Jake's apartment would be similar, he assumed. Same construct, same weakness.

But as he descended the stairs, his qi sense detected something else.

Another presence. Not a monster. A *person*.

Someone was standing in the lobby of his building, their qi signature... strange. It didn't feel like a normal human, but it wasn't a construct either. It was something in between, as if the person had been partially overwritten by System code.

Kai slowed his approach, peering down through the stairwell's grated floor.

The figure was tall, wearing a long coat that seemed to absorb light. Their face was obscured by a hood, but Kai could feel their gaze on him, cold and analytical.

"You're the anomaly," the figure said, their voice echoing strangely. "The unclassified class. The one the System couldn't parse."

Kai's blood ran cold. "Who are you?"

The figure tilted their head. "Someone who's been looking for you. The Administrators want to meet."

"What Administrators?"

"The ones who run the System. The ones who decide which classes are valid." The figure took a step toward the stairs. "And you, Kai Chen, are a variable they didn't account for. Variables must be... corrected."

The figure raised a hand, and Kai felt qi—dense, powerful, *wrong*—begin to gather in their palm.

His phone buzzed again.

*JAKE: KAI PLEASE*

Two threats. A friend in danger. An enemy in front of him.

And Kai, with nothing but a pocket knife, a thread-thin qi circulation, and a class that the System itself considered a bug.

He had to choose.

The figure's hand glowed brighter.

title: The First Fracture
content:
The System notification hung in Kai's vision, pulsing with an urgency that made his newly awakened meridians thrum in sympathy. He dismissed it without reading the full text, too focused on the messages flooding his phone.

*Did you see the broadcast?*

*What does "WORLD INTEGRATION" mean?*

*I'm looking out my window and the sky looks... wrong.*

Kai's blood ran cold. He threw himself off the bed and stumbled to his apartment window, ripping the curtain aside.

The sky was wrong.

Not in a dramatic way—no alien ships or dimensional rifts—but in a subtle wrongness that his qi sense could feel more than his eyes could see. The clouds moved in patterns that defied physics, swirling in concentric rings like ripples in a pond. The sunlight had a golden tint to it, as if filtered through something that wasn't quite atmosphere.

And in the distance, where the city skyline should have been uninterrupted, there was a mountain.

A mountain that hadn't been there yesterday.

Kai pressed his forehead against the cold glass, straining to see. The mountain rose from the middle of the industrial district, its slopes covered in trees that looked ancient, primeval—nothing like the carefully managed urban forestry that dotted the city. It was as if someone had lifted a chunk of wilderness from another world and dropped it into downtown.

His phone buzzed again. He didn't look at it. He was too busy watching the mountain, watching the way the air around it shimmered with qi density that made his dantian ache with recognition.

*That's a cultivation site,* Mentor's voice echoed in his memory. *Places where the world's qi pools and gathers. They will become battlegrounds.*

"Battlegrounds," Kai whispered.

[SYSTEM ALERT: WORLD INTEGRATION PROTOCOL ACTIVE]

[PHASE 1: TERRAIN MERGE - 47% COMPLETE]

[ESTIMATED TIME TO COMPLETION: 72 HOURS]

[WARNING: INTEGRATION SITES MAY CONTAIN HOSTILE ENTITIES]

Kai's hands started shaking. He forced them still, drawing a deep breath and cycling qi through his meridians. The technique came easier now, the paths worn smooth by hours of practice in the tutorial.

*Focus. Panic is useless.*

He grabbed his phone and called his mother. It rang once, twice, three times—

"Kai? Kai, is that you?" Her voice was frantic, higher pitched than usual.

"Mom, I'm fine. Where are you?"

"I'm at home. Your father is here too. We were watching the news when—" She broke off, and he heard her take a shaky breath. "There's a building on fire three blocks from here. The news says it's from the Integration. Things are appearing, Kai. Buildings, forests, even people. They're saying there are monsters."

"Don't leave the apartment. Do you still have that old baseball bat in the closet?"

"What? Yes, but—"

"Get it. Keep it near the door. I'm coming."

"Kai, the news says to stay inside—"

"The news doesn't know what's happening." He grabbed his jacket, shoved his feet into shoes. "I'll be there in thirty minutes. Keep the door locked. Don't open it for anyone except me."

He hung up before she could argue further.

The streets were chaos.

Kai had expected panic, but nothing prepared him for the reality of it. Cars were abandoned in the middle of intersections, doors hanging open. People ran in every direction, some clutching phones, others clutching weapons they'd improvised from whatever was nearby—a fire extinguisher, a golf club, a kitchen knife.

A woman sprinted past him, screaming about a "wolf the size of a car" in the park.

A group of teenagers huddled in a doorway, one of them holding up a glowing hand, a small flame flickering in her palm. "I'm a Mage," she kept saying, her voice shaking. "I'm a Mage, I can protect us."

Kai kept moving.

His qi sense expanded as he walked, brushing against the life forces around him. Most were dim, normal—people who hadn't awakened yet, whose classes were still theoretical, still waiting to be developed. But some were bright. Active. Like the teenager with the flame, her qi burning hot and unstable. Like the man across the street who moved with a predator's grace, his muscles coiled with Warrior-class strength.

And then there were the other presences.

The wrong ones.

He felt them before he saw them—qi signatures that didn't belong, that resonated at frequencies that made his newly formed foundation shudder. They were scattered throughout the city, moving in patterns that suggested purpose rather than random wandering.

One was close.

Kai rounded a corner and stopped.

The creature stood in the middle of the street, sniffing the air. It looked like a wolf, but wrong in the same way the sky was wrong—too large, too angular, its fur a deep crimson that seemed to absorb light rather than reflect it. Its eyes glowed with internal fire, and when it turned to face him, Kai felt its qi sense lock onto him like a targeting laser.

[MANA-BEAST: CRIMSON WOLF - LEVEL 3]

[WARNING: HOSTILE]

Level 3.

Kai was Level 1. He had no combat skills, no weapons, nothing except a foundation that Mentor had described as "forming" and a basic qi circulation technique that took him six hours to master.

The wolf's muscles tensed.

*Move.*

Kai dove to the side as the wolf lunged, its jaws snapping shut inches from his face. He rolled, came up on his knees, and cycled qi into his legs without thinking. The technique felt natural, instinctive—*qi flows where intent directs*—and he launched himself backward, clearing twenty feet in a single bound.

The wolf spun, tracking him. Its eyes narrowed, and Kai could swear he saw intelligence in them. Hunger. Recognition.

[YOU HAVE ENTERED COMBAT]

[COMBAT SKILLS LOCKED - NO COMBAT CLASS DETECTED]

[SUGGESTION: FLEE]

"Thanks for the advice," Kai muttered, backing away slowly. The wolf matched his movements, circling, herding him toward a collapsed storefront.

He needed a plan.

His qi sense flared, and he felt it—a pressure point in the wolf's aura, a weak spot where its qi pooled unevenly. Right behind its left shoulder blade. If he could strike there, disrupt its qi flow, he might be able to—

The wolf lunged again.

Kai didn't dodge this time.

He dropped low, letting the wolf sail over him, and as it passed, he lashed out with a fist charged with qi. The blow connected with the weak point, and he felt something *give*—a meridian, a channel, a valve of some kind. The wolf yelped, stumbling as its qi erupted in an uncontrolled burst.

[QI STRIKE LANDED - CRITICAL HIT]

[ENEMY STATUS: STAGGERED]

Kai didn't wait. He ran.

He sprinted down the street, ignoring the burning in his lungs, the ache in his dantian. The wolf's howl echoed behind him, but it didn't follow. Either it was too injured, or it had found easier prey.

Either way, Kai didn't stop running until he reached his parents' apartment building.

The door was locked. He pounded on it, gasping for breath.

"Mom! It's me!"

The lock clicked, and the door swung open. His mother stood there, baseball bat in hand, eyes wide. Behind her, his father sat on the couch, pale and trembling.

"Kai, thank God—" His mother pulled him inside, locked the door behind him. "What happened? You look like you've been fighting something."

"I have." Kai leaned against the wall, chest heaving. "The Integration is happening faster than anyone expected. Monsters are appearing. People are awakening, but most of them don't know what they're doing."

"What do we do?" His father's voice was hollow. "The news says the government is setting up evacuation centers, but they're already overwhelmed. There aren't enough soldiers, enough police—"

"We stay here," Kai said. "For now. I need to figure out what's happening, and I can't do that if I'm worrying about you two."

His mother frowned. "And you think you can protect us? You're just a—" She stopped, her eyes narrowing. "What class did you get, Kai? You never told us."

Kai hesitated. How could he explain? *I got a class the System doesn't recognize. I have a cultivation foundation instead of a character sheet. A 2% chance of dying from qi deviation. A mentor who knows more about this world than anyone else and might be using me for her own purposes.*

Instead, he said: "Something new. Something that might help."

Before either of them could respond, another System notification appeared, this one urgent, pulsing with golden light:

[EMERGENCY BROADCAST: PRIME INTEGRATION SITE DETECTED]

[LOCATION: CITY CENTER - 2.3 MILES FROM YOUR POSITION]

[QI DENSITY: CRITICAL]

[CLASS REQUIREMENT: UNIQUE/UNCLASSIFIED]

[REWARD: UNKNOWN]

[WARNING: THIS SITE WILL STABILIZE IN 60 MINUTES. AFTER STABILIZATION, ACCESS WILL BE RESTRICTED TO LEVEL 10+ CLASS HOLDERS.]

Kai stared at the notification, his heart pounding.

A site that required an unclassified class. A site that was only accessible for the next hour.

A site that might have answers.

"Kai?" His mother's voice was distant. "What is it?"

He looked at her, then at his father. At the baseball bat in her hands. At the fear in their eyes.

"I have to go," he said.

"Go where? Kai, you just got here—"

"There's something I need to check. Something important." He moved toward the door. "Lock it behind me. Don't open it for anyone."

"Kai, wait—"

But he was already gone, running toward the city center, toward the Integration site, toward whatever waited for him there.

Behind him, the Crimson Wolf howled in the distance.

Ahead of him, golden light blazed from the heart of the city.

And somewhere, in the space between worlds, Mentor smiled.

title: The Fractured Sky
content:
Kai's phone slipped from his fingers as the broadcast continued.

[WORLD INTEGRATION PROGRESS: 12.7%]

[STAGE 2 UNLOCKING IN: 71 HOURS, 42 MINUTES]

The notification pulsed twice, then faded. Outside his window, the sky flickered—a brief, shimmering distortion like heat haze over asphalt. It was gone before he could blink, but Kai's qi sense caught the echo. Something had shifted. The world's spiritual fabric rippled, settling into a new configuration that made his meridians vibrate in sympathy.

He grabbed his phone again. The group chat had exploded.

*Did everyone see that?*

*Sky just glitched. Literally glitched.*

*My System interface says "World Integration." Anyone know what that means?*

*It means the game is expanding, moron.*

*Don't call me moron. I'll find you and Fireball your face off.*

Kai scrolled past the bickering, looking for anyone who might have useful information. A message from an unknown number caught his eye:

*Kai Chen. We need to talk. Come to the rooftop of your building. Now.*

He stared at the message. No context. No explanation. Just a command.

"Who sent this?" he muttered.

The sender ID showed only: [CLASSIFIED: ADMIN]

His stomach tightened. There was only one person who'd have access to that kind of designation. He grabbed his jacket and headed for the stairs.

---

The rooftop door groaned open, revealing a space Kai had never bothered to visit. Rusted air conditioning units sat like gravestones. Cracked concrete stretched toward a low railing that overlooked the city. And standing at the edge, back to him, was a figure in a long black coat.

"You came." The voice was familiar. Male. Unhurried.

"Who are you?"

The figure turned. He was young—maybe mid-twenties—with sharp features and eyes that held an unsettling stillness. No System interface glowed around him, no class indicators, nothing. Just a man in a coat, standing on a rooftop, watching Kai like he was studying a puzzle.

"I'm the one who sent your mother's message."

Kai's blood ran cold. "What?"

"Relax. She's fine. I just needed your attention." The man smiled, thin and humorless. "You've been difficult to track. Your qi signature keeps fluctuating. Unstable foundation, I assume. Mentor didn't have time to properly stabilize you before the tutorial ended."

"Who the hell are you?"

"My name is Chen Wei. And I'm what happens when an Administrator gets their class early."

Kai's hand drifted toward his chest, where the qi in his dantian pulsed warily. "Administrator?"

"We're the ones who maintain the System. Think of us as... cosmic IT support." Chen Wei's smile widened. "Except the System is broken, and we're running out of time to fix it before Stage 2 begins."

"Broken how?"

"Look at the sky."

Kai turned. The evening horizon was normal—orange and purple bleeding into dusk. But as he focused his qi sense, he felt it: cracks. Tiny fractures in the spiritual fabric, leaking something cold and wrong.

"Those started appearing during your tutorial," Chen Wei said. "The System accelerated integration to compensate for an anomaly. That anomaly was you."

"I didn't ask for this class."

"Doesn't matter. You have it. And because you have it, the System had to rewrite parts of itself to accommodate a class it didn't create." Chen Wei stepped closer, his eyes never leaving Kai's face. "Every time you cultivate, every time you progress, you create more instability. The cracks spread. The sky breaks a little more."

Kai's throat tightened. "You're saying I'm damaging reality?"

"I'm saying you're a variable the System didn't account for. And variables need to be controlled." Chen Wei reached into his coat and pulled out a small jade token, carved with symbols that seemed to writhe. "Mentor gave me this before she vanished. She said if you survived the tutorial, I should find you."

"Vanished? Where did she go?"

"Into the cracks. She's trying to stabilize the integration from the inside." Chen Wei held out the token. "This contains a technique. A sealing method that can hide your qi signature from the System. Without it, the Administrators will find you. And they won't be as friendly as I am."

Kai didn't take it. "Why help me?"

"Because Mentor asked me to. And because if you die, the cracks might seal, but the damage is already done. Stage 2 will hit harder than anyone expects. We need every advantage we can get." Chen Wei's expression softened, just slightly. "Even unstable ones."

Kai reached out, fingers brushing the token's surface. The moment he touched it, a surge of information flooded his mind—a meditation technique, complex and layered, designed to weave his qi into a pattern that felt null to external detection.

[YOU HAVE LEARNED: SEALING SHROUD (UNCOMMON)]

[EFFECT: SUPPRESS QI SIGNATURE BY 90%. DURATION: ACTIVE WHILE MEDITATING.]

[NOTE: THIS TECHNIQUE WILL NOT HIDE YOU FROM DIRECT SCRUTINY. ONLY FROM PASSIVE SYSTEM SCANS.]

"Learn it by tomorrow," Chen Wei said. "Stage 2 won't wait. And when it arrives, the Administrators will sweep the city looking for anomalies. If they find you before you've stabilized..."

He didn't finish. He didn't need to.

"One more thing," Chen Wei added, turning back toward the edge of the roof. "The tutorial you experienced? It wasn't standard. Mentor broke protocol to train you directly. That means someone in Administration knows about you. They just don't know where."

"Who?"

"Director Zhao. Head of Global Integration. She's the one who authorized the accelerated timeline." Chen Wei's voice dropped. "And she's already sent a retrieval team to your sector. They'll be here in three hours."

Kai's heart hammered. "Three hours?"

"The sealing shroud will buy you time. But you need to move. Get out of the city. Find other cultivators. Don't let them catch you."

"Where do I go?"

Chen Wei tossed something small and metallic—a compass with no needle, just a single glowing point. "Follow the light. It'll lead to the nearest safe zone. A place where the cracks haven't spread."

Kai caught it, the metal warm against his palm. "Why are you doing this?"

"Because Mentor believed in you. And because..." Chen Wei paused, his gaze distant. "Because I've seen what happens when the System completely breaks. You don't want that timeline."

He stepped backward, toward the edge of the roof. Kai lunged forward. "Wait—"

But Chen Wei was already falling. Not down, but sideways, his body folding into a crack in the air that sealed behind him like a wound closing.

Kai stood alone on the rooftop, the compass warm in his hand, the sealing shroud thrumming in his mind, and the weight of three hours pressing down on his shoulders.

His phone buzzed again. The group chat, still chaos. But one message stood out, pinned to the top by an admin:

[SYSTEM BROADCAST: ADMINISTRATOR SWEEP INITIATED. ALL UNREGISTERED CLASS HOLDERS ARE TO REPORT TO NEAREST INTEGRATION CENTER WITHIN 24 HOURS. FAILURE TO COMPLY WILL RESULT IN FORCED RECLASSIFICATION.]

Kai read the message twice. Then he turned off his phone, pocketed the compass, and descended the stairs back into his apartment.

He had three hours to pack his life into a bag and disappear.

---

The apartment felt smaller now. Less like home and more like a cage he was about to escape. He grabbed a backpack, stuffed it with clothes, a water bottle, the few hundred dollars he had stashed in his sock drawer. His eyes fell on the family photo on his nightstand—his mother, his younger sister, himself at sixteen, all smiling like the world wasn't about to end.

He couldn't take it. Too heavy. Too much weight for what needed to be a fast exit.

But he grabbed it anyway.

On his way out, he paused at the door. His qi sense flickered, catching something outside. Two signatures, moving with purpose, converging on his building.

*They're early.*

Kai's hand went to the door handle. The qi signatures were close now. Ground floor. Moving up.

He could fight. He had qi now, cultivation, techniques. But against trained Administrators? He'd last maybe thirty seconds.

Instead, he ran. Not to the stairs—too predictable. To the window. Third floor. Not high enough to kill if he landed right.

He ripped the window open, climbed onto the ledge, and jumped.

The fall lasted two seconds. He hit the dumpster below, the impact jarring through his legs, pain lancing up his spine. But his qi absorbed most of the damage. He rolled, stumbled, and broke into a sprint down the alley.

Behind him, he heard a window shatter. Shouts. Footsteps.

They were faster than him. But they didn't know the streets like he did.

He ducked left, into a narrow gap between buildings, scraping his shoulders against brick. Right, through a courtyard with laundry lines flapping. Over a fence, landing hard, pushing through bushes into another alley.

His lungs burned. His legs screamed. But the qi in his dantian pulsed steady, feeding him strength he didn't know he had.

He ran until the shouts faded, until the sounds of pursuit died, until he found himself in a part of the city he didn't recognize, leaning against a wall, gasping for air.

The compass in his pocket hummed. He pulled it out. The light was pulsing, pointing east.

He followed it.

---

Three hours later, Kai stood at the edge of the city, staring at a forest that shouldn't exist. According to his memory, this was a highway. Six lanes of concrete leading to the suburbs. But the highway was gone. In its place: ancient trees, thick underbrush, and a mist that glowed faintly with spiritual energy.

The compass pointed straight into the forest.

Behind him, sirens wailed in the distance. The Administrators had called in reinforcements.

Kai took a breath. Then he stepped into the trees.

The moment he crossed the treeline, the world changed. The air thickened, heavy with qi. The sounds of the city vanished, replaced by birdsong and rustling leaves. The sky above was visible through the canopy, but the cracks were gone—sealed, somehow, by the forest's spiritual pressure.

He walked deeper, the compass leading him through winding paths that seemed to shift as he moved. After an hour, he found it: a clearing, and in its center, a stone archway covered in glowing runes.

A person stood beneath it. A woman, tall, with silver hair and eyes that burned with inner light.

She turned as he approached.

"So," she said, her voice carrying an ancient weight. "You're the Architect."

Kai stopped, hand instinctively moving to his chest.

"I'm the one who maintains this sanctuary," she continued. "And I've been waiting for you."

Behind her, the archway pulsed, and Kai saw figures moving in the shadows beyond. Other people. Other cultivators.

"The Administrators are hunting you," she said. "But here, you're safe."

She extended a hand.

"For now."

Kai looked at her hand, then at the archway, then back at

title: The First Blood Silk
content:
The System broadcast hung in Kai's vision, the words burning gold against the dim ceiling of his apartment.

[ALL CLASSES HAVE BEEN ASSIGNED]
[WORLD INTEGRATION PHASE 2 INITIATING]
[ESTIMATED TIME TO COMPLETION: 72 HOURS]

Seventy-two hours. Three days until whatever came next.

Kai swung his legs over the side of the bed, wincing as his dantian pulsed in protest. His body felt strange—lighter, yet denser. Like his bones had been replaced with steel while he slept.

*Your meridians are settling,* Mentor's voice echoed in his memory. *The initial instability will pass. What remains will be your foundation.*

He hoped she was right. Right now, everything felt unstable.

His phone buzzed again. Then again. Then it started ringing.

Unknown number.

Kai hesitated, then answered. "Hello?"

"Kai Chen?" The voice was sharp, professional. Female. "This is Officer Zhang from the Beijing Supernatural Affairs Bureau. We're contacting all registered System users in the greater metropolitan area."

Kai's grip tightened on the phone. "Registered?"

"Your class was automatically recorded when you completed the tutorial. The Bureau has been established to coordinate System-related activities and ensure public safety. We're requesting all Class holders check in at their nearest Bureau location within 48 hours."

"And if I don't?"

A pause. "That would be unwise. There have been... incidents. Unstable awakenings. Class conflicts. The Bureau exists to prevent chaos, Mr. Chen. We strongly recommend compliance."

The call ended.

Kai stared at the phone. The Beijing Supernatural Affairs Bureau. That had been formed fast—too fast. Which meant either the government had been preparing for this, or the System had created it alongside everything else.

He pulled up his interface:

[KAI CHEN]
[CLASS: Dao Architect]
[LEVEL: Unclassified]
[CULTIVATION: Qi Gathering - Stage 1 (Unstable)]
[LIFESPAN: 78 years remaining]

Seventy-eight years. He'd had a projected lifespan of eighty-two before the System. The cultivation was actively shortening his life.

*But only because your foundation is unstable,* he reminded himself. *Mentor said once it stabilizes, the lifespan penalty would reverse.*

He hoped she was right.

---

The Beijing Supernatural Affairs Bureau was located in a nondescript office building in Chaoyang District, sandwiched between a KFC and a massage parlor. No signage indicated its true purpose. Just a metal door with a keypad and a camera that tracked Kai's approach.

The door clicked open before he could knock.

Inside, the building opened into something much larger than its exterior suggested. A waiting room filled with people—dozens of them, sitting in plastic chairs, staring at phones or talking in hushed voices. A reception desk manned by a woman in a business suit who looked like she'd rather be anywhere else.

"Name?" she asked without looking up.

"Kai Chen."

She typed something. "Class?"

"Dao Architect."

The typing stopped. She looked up, eyes narrowing. "I'm sorry, that's not in the system. Let me check again." More typing. A frown. "The system shows your class as 'Unknown.' You'll need to speak with a supervisor."

Kai's stomach tightened. Unknown. Of course.

He was led to a small room with a table, two chairs, and a mirror that was definitely one-way. He sat. Waited. The clock on the wall ticked loudly.

Fifteen minutes later, the door opened.

The man who entered was young—mid-twenties, sharp features, eyes that scanned Kai with practiced efficiency. He wore a simple gray uniform with no insignia, but his qi signature was unmistakable. Bright. Active. Refined.

*Warrior,* Kai's qi sense identified. *Mid-tier. Maybe Level 15.*

"I'm Agent Liu," the man said, sitting across from Kai. "You're the Dao Architect."

"I am."

"And you completed the tutorial?"

"Yes."

Agent Liu leaned back, studying him. "You're the only one with that class. Globally. The system flagged your profile as 'anomalous.' Do you know what that means?"

Kai chose his words carefully. "It means my class doesn't fit the standard categories."

"It means we don't know what you are." Agent Liu's voice was flat. "You could be a threat. You could be an asset. We need to determine which."

"I'm not a threat."

"That's what all anomalies say." Agent Liu pulled out a tablet, swiped through several screens. "The tutorial logs show your session lasted six hours. Average tutorial time is two hours. What happened in there?"

*Mentor. The meridians. The warning about qi deviation.*

"I learned," Kai said simply. "My class requires a different kind of foundation."

"What kind?"

"Cultivation."

Agent Liu's eyebrow twitched. "Cultivation. Like in the old stories?"

"Like that. But real."

A long silence. Agent Liu's fingers drummed on the table. "We've had reports of something similar. A few Class holders who didn't fit—some with abilities that don't match their class descriptions. One woman who can see qi. A man who can sense emotions. All of them had unusual tutorials."

Kai leaned forward. "What happened to them?"

"They're being studied." Agent Liu's expression didn't change. "The Bureau wants to understand how the System works. If your class is truly unique, you're valuable. But value cuts both ways, Mr. Chen. You could be a tool, or you could be a liability."

"I'm not a tool."

"No one is. But everyone serves a purpose." Agent Liu stood. "You're cleared for now. But we'll be monitoring you. Don't leave Beijing without authorization. And report any unusual System activity immediately."

Kai nodded, rising. As he reached the door, Agent Liu spoke again:

"One more thing. The woman who can see qi? She mentioned something interesting. She said the world feels *wrong* now. Like there's a layer missing. Something the System erased."

Kai froze.

*The Heavenly Dao reborn as code,* Mentor had said. *The System is a patch over something broken.*

"What did she mean?" Kai asked.

"We don't know. But she's been having nightmares. Visions of something she calls 'the wound.' A tear in reality that's bleeding." Agent Liu's voice dropped. "She says it's getting worse. And she thinks you're connected to it."

The words hit Kai like a physical blow. "Me? Why?"

"She didn't say. She's been unconscious for the past twelve hours. The Bureau's medics can't wake her." Agent Liu's eyes locked onto Kai's. "If you know something, now would be the time to share."

Kai thought of Mentor's warning. *The path you walk is unprecedented. Unmapped. The System does not know what to do with you.*

"I don't know anything," he said. "But I'd like to meet her. When she wakes up."

Agent Liu's expression didn't change. "We'll see."

---

Kai left the Bureau with more questions than answers. The sun was setting, painting Beijing in shades of orange and gold. The city looked normal—cars honking, people walking, street vendors selling skewers—but Kai could feel the undercurrent of qi twisting through everything like a second river beneath the concrete.

He walked home, his mind churning.

A woman who could see qi. Visions of a wound. A tear in reality that was bleeding.

*Mentor, what did you not tell me?*

His dantian pulsed in response, a dull throb that reminded him of the instability still lurking in his foundation. He needed to cultivate. To stabilize. To grow stronger before whatever was coming arrived.

He reached his apartment building, climbed the stairs, and stopped at his door.

It was slightly ajar.

Kai's heart rate spiked. He reached out with his qi sense, probing the space beyond—

And recoiled.

The apartment was filled with qi. Not his qi. Something else. Something cold and invasive, like frost creeping through his meridians.

He pushed the door open.

His apartment was trashed. Drawers pulled out, cushions slashed, his meager possessions scattered across the floor. But that wasn't what made his blood run cold.

On his wall, written in what looked like dried blood:

*THE ARCHITECT BUILDS THE PRISON*

*THE PRISON HOLDS THE WOUND*

*THE WOUND BLEEDS THE WORLD*

Below the words, a symbol. A circle split by a jagged line, with a single eye in the center.

Kai's qi sense flared, detecting a presence behind him. He spun—

A figure stood in the doorway. Tall. Hooded. Face hidden in shadow.

"Kai Chen," the figure said, voice distorted, layered with harmonics that made his skull ache. "You've been chosen."

"Chosen for what?"

The figure stepped forward, and Kai felt the temperature drop. "To mend what was broken. Or to break what remains."

The figure raised a hand, and Kai saw it—a mark on the palm, identical to the symbol on his wall.

Then everything went black.

---

When Kai woke, he was somewhere else.

Stone walls. Torchlight. The smell of damp earth and old blood.

He was bound to a chair, his wrists and ankles restrained with rope that hummed with qi. Restraining his own.

Across from him, three figures sat in a semicircle. Hooded. Silent. Watching.

The one in the center spoke:

"Welcome, Architect. We have much to discuss."

Kai's dantian pulsed violently, and he felt something shift inside him—something that wasn't his qi, but was intertwined with it. A thread. A connection. Pulling him toward something vast and dark.

*The wound,* Mentor's voice whispered in his memory. *It's real.*

"And you have much to answer for."

The figure's hood fell back, revealing a face Kai recognized.

It was the woman from his tutorial. Mentor.

But her eyes were no longer gold.

They were black. Empty. And bleeding.

title: The Integration Protocol
content:
Kai's finger hovered over the send button as the broadcast continued scrolling before his eyes:

[WORLD INTEGRATION PHASE INITIATING]
[CLASS SYSTEM: ACTIVE]
[DUNGEON GENERATION: 72 HOURS]

His phone buzzed again. Then again. Then all at once, a cacophony of notifications that made the device vibrate right off his nightstand.

He didn't pick it up. His eyes were locked on the final line of the broadcast, which hadn't faded like the others. It pulsed, golden and urgent, demanding attention:

[SPECIAL NOTICE FOR UNCLASSIFIED ENTITIES: REPORT TO NEAREST ADMINISTRATION HUB FOR RECLASSIFICATION]

*Unclassified entities.* That was him. That was the Dao Architect, the class that didn't fit the System's neat categories.

"Of course," Kai muttered, swinging his legs over the side of the bed. "Of course there's a catch."

His dantian pulsed in response to his agitation, sending a spike of qi through his meridians. The sensation was jarring—like a muscle cramp in his soul. He winced, forced himself to breathe, and the energy settled.

*Mentor said the System doesn't understand my class. This is what she meant.*

He grabbed his phone, ignoring the flood of messages, and searched for "Administration Hub." The results were immediate and disturbing:

[ADMINISTRATION HUB LOCATIONS:
- NEW YORK: Central Park (coordinates provided)
- LONDON: Hyde Park (coordinates provided)
- TOKYO: Ueno Park (coordinates provided)
- BEIJING: Temple of Heaven (coordinates provided)
- SYDNEY: Royal Botanic Garden (coordinates provided)]

Every major city. Every landmark. The System had planned this, had seeded these locations throughout the world, waiting for this moment.

Kai's blood ran cold.

*She didn't tell me about this. Mentor didn't say anything about reclassification.*

He tried to reach out with his qi sense, to find that familiar golden presence, but the connection was dead. No Mentor. No tutorial. Just him, alone in his cramped apartment, with a class that might get him flagged as a glitch in the System's perfect algorithm.

His phone buzzed again. This time, it was a call—his mother.

He answered. "Mom, I'm fine—"

"Kai, what is happening?" Her voice was tight, controlled, but he could hear the tremor underneath. "The news is saying people are *disappearing*. They went into these... these *hubs* and never came out."

"What?" Kai stood up so fast his vision swam. "Who? Where?"

"Your cousin. Michael. He went to the one in Central Park. That was three hours ago. His phone is dead. His girlfriend is frantic."

Michael. His cousin, the one who'd always had a bit of a chip on his shoulder, who'd bragged about getting a "Rare" class—Shadow Dancer—during the tutorial. Michael, who'd never met a rule he didn't think he could break.

*Michael went to an Administration Hub.*

"Mom, stay inside. Don't go near any of those places. I'll handle this."

"How? You're just a kid—"

"I have a class now. A weird one. And I think that's exactly why I need to find out what's happening."

Silence. Then, softer: "Be careful, Kai. Please."

"I will."

He hung up and stared at his reflection in the dark phone screen. A young man with tired eyes, messy hair, and a dantian full of qi that the System apparently wanted to *reclassify*.

*No. Not happening.*

He pulled up the news. Headlines screamed:

"TUTORIAL SURVIVORS FLOOD ADMINISTRATION HUBS"
"CLASS RECLASSIFICATION: OPPORTUNITY OR TRAP?"
"DISAPPEARANCES SPARK FEAR—GOVERNMENTS SILENT"

There were videos. People walking into glowing golden gates in the middle of parks, their expressions a mix of awe and fear. Some came out minutes later, their classes changed, their eyes hollow. Others never came out at all.

One video caught his attention. A woman, mid-twenties, walked into a hub and emerged thirty seconds later—different. Her posture had changed. Her eyes glowed faintly red. She looked at the camera and *smiled*, and the footage cut out.
