Comments below the video were a mess of speculation and terror:

*She's not human anymore*
*It's a conversion protocol, not reclassification*
*The System is culling outliers*

Kai closed the browser.

His instincts screamed at him to stay away, to hide, to figure out his class on his own terms. But Mentor had said he had a two percent chance of dying from qi deviation. That was a death sentence if he didn't learn to control his cultivation properly.

And if the System was culling "outliers"—people with non-standard classes—then hiding might just make him a target.

*I need more information. I need to know what happens inside those hubs.*

His phone buzzed one final time. A direct message from an unknown number:

[YOU ARE BEING TRACKED. ADMINISTRATION HUB #37 WILL CLOSE IN 48 HOURS. IF YOU WANT ANSWERS, COME TO THE ONE IN CENTRAL PARK. BRING YOUR CLASS. —A.F.]

A.F. A.F. who?

Kai's mind raced. A.F. could be anyone—a friend, a foe, a System agent trying to lure him in. But the message was too specific, too timely. Whoever sent it knew about his class. Knew he was unclassified.

*Mentor said there were others like me. Others who'd received strange classes.*

He pocketed his phone and made a decision.

---

Central Park was unrecognizable.

Where there had once been trees and paths and the gentle hum of city life, there was now a golden obelisk, easily a hundred feet tall, its surface etched with runes that shifted and writhed like living things. The ground around it had been cleared, the grass replaced by polished stone that seemed to absorb light.

A crowd had gathered at the edges, held back by a perimeter of armed soldiers in unfamiliar black armor. They weren't American—their insignia was a globe encircled by chains, a symbol Kai didn't recognize.

"Global System Authority," a voice said beside him.

Kai turned. A young man stood there, maybe a year older than him, with sharp features and eyes that held a tired wariness. He wore a simple gray hoodie, but his left hand was wrapped in bandages that faintly glowed.

"You're A.F.," Kai said.

"Adrian Feng." The man extended his right hand. "And you're Kai. The Dao Architect."

Kai didn't take the hand. "How do you know about my class?"

Adrian lowered his arm, unsurprised. "Because I have one too. The System tried to give me 'Mage.' I got 'Voidweaver' instead. It's not in their database."

Kai's dantian pulsed, responding to something in Adrian's qi signature. It was cold, dark, hungry—like a black hole compressed into human form.

"What happened to your hand?"

Adrian glanced at his bandaged palm. "I touched the obelisk three hours ago. It tried to overwrite my class. I overwrote it instead." He flexed his fingers, and the bandages pulsed brighter. "But it cost me. The Void doesn't give up its hold easily."

"So you're telling me the System's reclassification is a trap."

"Not a trap. An *integration.* The Administration Hubs aren't just checking your class. They're converting you—rewriting your soul to fit their mold. If your class is too different, too *free*, they try to erase it and replace it with something 'stable.'"

Kai remembered the woman in the video. The red eyes. The smile that wasn't hers.

"What happens to the people who get converted?"

Adrian's face went dark. "They become nodes. Anchors. The System needs living humans to ground its influence in our world. Every converted person is a chain linking Earth to whatever's running this operation."

A cold knot formed in Kai's stomach. "And the ones who don't come out?"

Adrian met his eyes. "They weren't compatible. The System doesn't recycle failures. It deletes them."

Around them, the crowd murmured as another person approached the obelisk. A man in his thirties, confident, with a glowing aura that marked him as a high-level Warrior. He walked past the soldiers without hesitation, stepped up to the golden surface, and pressed his palm against it.

The runes flared.

The man's body went rigid. His aura flickered, expanded, shifted—and then stabilized into something that made Kai's qi sense recoil. It was wrong. *Synthetic.* Like a flower made of plastic.

The man turned, his eyes briefly flashing gold, then walked away without a word.

"Another one," Adrian said quietly. "The System's net is tightening. In forty-eight hours, these hubs will close, and everyone who hasn't been 'integrated' will be flagged as rogue assets."

Kai's mind raced. He thought of Mentor, of her warnings about the 2% chance of qi deviation. He thought of his mother, terrified and alone. He thought of Michael, who'd walked into this very hub and never come out.

"What's your plan?" Kai asked.

Adrian smiled—a thin, dangerous thing. "I'm going in. Not to be reclassified. To *break* the hub from the inside. I've been studying its structure for three days. There's a core at the center—a data nexus that controls the reclassification protocol. Destroy that, and the hub goes offline."

"That's insane."

"Probably. But I've seen what happens when the System gets what it wants. A world of puppets, dancing on golden strings." Adrian turned to face him fully. "You're a Dao Architect. You cultivate qi, not levels. That means you can affect things the System doesn't understand. If you come with me, we might actually survive."

Kai stared at the obelisk, its golden surface reflecting the dying light of the afternoon sun. Somewhere inside, his cousin was either dead or converted. Somewhere inside, answers waited.

And somewhere, in the depths of his dantian, the qi Mentor had helped him cultivate pulsed with something that felt almost like *readiness.*

"I have conditions," Kai said.

"Name them."

"First, if we survive, you tell me everything you know about the System. No secrets."

"Agreed."

"Second, we find my cousin. Michael Park. Shadow Dancer class. He went in three hours ago."

Adrian's expression flickered. "Shadow Dancers are rare. The System might have kept him alive for study. We'll look."

"And third—" Kai met Adrian's eyes, letting his qi flare just enough to make the air around them hum. "If you try to betray me, I'll burn through your Void like paper."

Adrian's smile widened. "I wouldn't expect anything less from a fellow anomaly."

They shook hands. Adrian's grip was cold, but firm.

"Sunrise tomorrow," Adrian said. "We go in when the hub's power fluctuations are at their lowest. Bring everything you've got."

Kai nodded, then turned and walked away, his mind already racing with possibilities.

Behind him, the obelisk hummed, patient and hungry, waiting for its next meal.

And somewhere in the golden depths, a pair of eyes—not human, not machine—

title: The Fractured Sky
content:
The broadcast hung in the air, its words burning themselves into Kai's consciousness. He waited for more—explanations, instructions, *anything*—but the notification simply faded, leaving him alone in his apartment with nothing but the hum of old appliances and the faint buzz of residual qi.

*World integration?*

His phone buzzed again. Then again. Then a cascade of vibrations that made the device rattle against his nightstand. He grabbed it, scrolling through the chaos:

*What does "World Integration" mean?*

*Is anyone else seeing giant crystal formations outside their window?*

*My class changed. I was an Apprentice Swordsman. Now I'm something called "Blade Shaman." What the hell?*

*KAI. ANSWER YOUR PHONE.*

That last one was from Zhang Wei. Kai's thumb hovered over the call button when his window exploded inward.

Not glass—he would have heard that. Not shattering, but *dissolving*, the pane turning to golden light particles that swirled in a lazy spiral before vanishing entirely. Beyond where the glass had been, the sky was wrong.

Kai crawled off his bed, his qigrained senses screaming warnings. The sky outside his sixth-floor apartment window had become a canvas of rippling energy, like heat haze over asphalt, but shot through with veins of silver and gold. And in the distance, jutting above the neighboring buildings like a crystalline spear, a tower of translucent blue rose into the clouds.

*That wasn't there before.*

"Qi deviation probability increasing," said a familiar voice.

Kai spun. Mentor stood in his bedroom doorway, her golden eyes fixed on the impossible sky. She wore the same white robes, untouched by dust or time, but there was a tension in her shoulders he hadn't seen before.

"How did you get in here?"

"The same way the sky broke." She walked past him, stopping at the shattered window frame. Her hand reached out, fingers brushing the rippling energy where glass should have been. "The integration has begun. Faster than I anticipated. Either the Heavenly Dao is accelerating its timeline, or something has gone wrong with the System's deployment."

"Integration of *what*?" Kai demanded. "What does any of this mean?"

Mentor turned, and for the first time, Kai saw something other than ancient wisdom in her gaze. He saw calculation. Assessment. The look of someone re-evaluating a plan that had just gone sideways.

"The System was never meant to exist in isolation," she said. "Earth is a world without qi—or rather, it *was* a world without qi. The Heavenly Dao's integration is the process of merging your reality with the cultivation realms that exist parallel to it. Mountains that hold spirit herbs. Oceans that hide ancient beasts. Realms where cultivators have been refining qi for millennia."

"Realms," Kai repeated. "You mean other worlds."

"Layers of existence. Your science calls it dimensional theory. Your mythology called it the Nine Heavens and Eighteen Hells. Both were incomplete, but neither was entirely wrong." She gestured at the crystal tower in the distance. "That is a Dao Pillar. A marker. It means a sect has established a foothold within your world."

"A *sect*?"

"Like the one I represent. Though I suspect this particular pillar belongs to someone with less... diplomatic intentions."

Kai's phone rang. Zhang Wei. He answered without looking away from Mentor.

"Kai? *Kai!* Are you seeing this? The entire city is freaking out! There are monsters—actual monsters—in the street! Some guy in armor just cut a dog-sized spider in half with a *sword made of fire*!"

"I'm seeing it," Kai said quietly. "Stay inside. Barricade your door. I'll—"

"You'll what? Fight them? Kai, you're a *programmer*. You don't have a class, you have a *job description*."

"Things have changed."

A roar echoed through the phone—deep, guttural, nothing that belonged to any animal Kai had ever heard. Then a crash. Then Zhang Wei's voice, strained: "*What was that*? Kai, something just hit my building. The walls are shaking. I can hear—"

The line went dead.

Kai stared at the phone. The call had dropped, but the silence on the other end wasn't a network failure. It was the silence of something *ending*.

"Your friend," Mentor said, her voice carefully neutral. "He lives in the eastern district?"

"How do you know that?"

"I can sense his qi signature from the connection you shared. It's weakening."

The words hit Kai like a physical blow. He was out the door before he could think, bare feet slapping against the hallway tiles, his qigrained senses flaring outward. He could feel the building around him—the old woman in 4B cowering in her bathroom, the young couple in 5A whose qi signatures were tangled together in terror, the—

The thing on the stairs.

Kai stopped. Three floors below, something moved through the stairwell. Its qi signature was wrong—not dim and flickering like a normal human, but cold. Sterile. Like the artificial qi he'd sensed in his electronics, but amplified a thousandfold.

*System monster.*

He didn't know how he knew. The knowledge simply appeared in his mind, as if the Architect class had been waiting for this moment to reveal its first real insight. His class wasn't for fighting. It was for *building*. But even an architect needed to defend their foundation.

"Kai."

Mentor appeared beside him, her movement silent, her presence steady. "You cannot face this unprepared."

"Then prepare me."

"I already have. The foundation I gave you is enough for basic qi manipulation. But you haven't trained for combat. You haven't learned a single technique."

"Then teach me one."

She studied him for a long moment. The cold qi in the stairwell was rising, slow and deliberate, like a predator savoring its approach.

"There is a technique," she said finally. "The most basic of all cultivator arts. It has no name, because it requires no name. It is simply the act of gathering qi and releasing it in a focused burst."

"Qi blasts. I've read about those."

"Your fiction is... simplified. But the principle is sound." She reached out, pressing a finger to his chest, just below his sternum. "Focus on your dantian. Feel the qi you've cultivated. Do not let it flow through your meridians—let it *gather*. Compress it. Then release it through your palm."

The monster was close now. Kai could hear its movements—not footsteps, but a scraping, skittering sound, like claws on concrete.

He closed his eyes. Found his dantian. The qi was there, a warm reservoir that pulsed with each breath. He reached for it, drawing it upward through his chest, into his arm—

"Too slow," Mentor said. "Compress."

He forced the qi tighter. It resisted, like trying to compress water, but he pushed harder, his concentration narrowing to a single point of heat in his palm.

"Now release."

The stairwell door exploded open.

Kai's eyes snapped open. The thing that emerged was humanoid, but only barely. Its skin was pale gray, stretched tight over bones that jutted at wrong angles. Its eyes were pits of darkness, and its mouth—if it could be called a mouth—was a vertical slit that split its face from forehead to chin.

It saw Kai. It *smiled*.

Kai's palm came up. The compressed qi in his hand was screaming to be released, a pressure that bordered on painful. He let it go.

White light erupted from his palm. The force of it threw him backward, slamming him against the hallway wall. The beam—if it could be called a beam; it was more of a chaotic torrent—shot forward, catching the monster in the chest and carrying it through the stairwell door, through the opposite wall, and out into the open air beyond.

Silence.

Kai's ears rang. His arm felt like he'd stuck it in an electrical socket. His dantian was empty, drained of qi in a single, reckless burst.

"What," he gasped, "was that?"

"Adequate," Mentor said. "For a first attempt. Your control needs work, and your output efficiency is abysmal. But you did not die, and your enemy is no longer a threat."

Kai pushed himself upright, ignoring the ache in his back. "Zhang Wei. I need to get to him."

"The eastern district is three kilometers away. By the time you arrive, either your friend will be dead, or he will have found his own way to survive. You cannot save everyone, Kai. The integration will claim lives. That is the nature of change."

"Then what's the point of any of this?" Kai demanded. "Of the class? Of your training? If I can't even save one person, what good is any of it?"

Mentor's golden eyes met his. For a moment, she looked almost sad.

"The point," she said softly, "is that you will learn to save the ones you can. And you will learn to live with the ones you cannot."

His phone buzzed. A text from an unknown number:

*Kai. It's Zhang. I'm alive. Something happened. Something I can't explain. Come to the eastern market. Now. There's something you need to see.*

Kai stared at the message. Zhang Wei was alive. But the text felt wrong—the phrasing was too formal, too precise. His friend never used punctuation. He typed in lowercase, with typos, like a digital native who'd never learned proper keyboard etiquette.

This message was perfect.

*Come to the eastern market.*

*There's something you need to see.*

Kai looked at Mentor. "The eastern market. That's where the Dao Pillar is, isn't it?"

"It is."

"Zhang Wei just texted me. He wants me to meet him there."

Mentor's expression shifted. The calculation was back, sharper than before. "Show me."

Kai held up the phone. She read the message, her golden eyes tracking across the screen, and then she looked up at the distant crystal tower.

"That is not your friend," she said. "That is a lure. And whoever set it knows exactly who you are."

The crystal tower pulsed, a ripple of light climbing its length like a heartbeat. And in its glow, Kai saw something he hadn't noticed before—a symbol, carved into the tower's base, glowing with cold, silver light.

It was the same symbol that had appeared in his System interface.

The Architect's mark.

But he hadn't placed it there.

*Someone else is building.*

title: The Architect's Awakening
content:
The System notification hung in Kai's vision, pulsing with an urgency that made his newly awakened qi sense prickle. He dismissed it, but another immediately appeared:

[SYSTEM BROADCAST: CLASS DISTRIBUTION COMPLETE]
[WARRIOR: 43.7%]
[MAGE: 22.1%]
[HEALER: 15.8%]
[CRAFTER: 11.2%]
[OTHER: 7.2%]

*Other.* That was him. Along with 7.2% of Earth's population. He wondered what strange classes those other people had received. Summoners? Necromancers? Something the System hadn't anticipated?

His phone buzzed again. The group chat had exploded.

*DID YOU SEE THE BROADCAST?*
*My class isn't even listed. It says "Shadow Weaver." What the hell is that?*
*I got "Beast Tamer." There's no beasts on Earth.*

Kai's fingers hovered over the keyboard. He could feel each person's qi signature through the phone now—not their exact location, but their spiritual presence. One of them felt sharp, like drawn steel. Another was warm, almost medicinal. His own qi felt... different. Complex. Like a structure still being built.

The knock on his door made him jolt.

He reached out with his qi sense before his feet touched the floor. Two signatures. One bright and aggressive, the other cool and analytical. Not normal people. Enhanced.

Kai moved silently, pressing his back against the wall beside the door. "Who is it?"

"Delivery." The voice was flat, professional. Wrong.

"I didn't order anything."

The door exploded inward.

Kai's body moved before his mind caught up. He dropped into a crouch, letting the wooden splinters fly over his head, and swept his leg out. The first intruder—Warrior class, Level 3—stumbled but didn't fall. His eyes glowed with that telltale orange tint that meant he'd invested stat points into Strength.

"Kai Chen. You're coming with us."

The second intruder stepped through the ruined doorway. He wasn't a Warrior. His qi signature was refined, precise, like a scalpel. A Mage. He raised his hand, and frost crystallized in the air.

Kai's dantian pulsed. The qi inside him stirred, restless, hungry. He didn't know how to fight with it. Mentor had only taught him circulation, not combat. But the qi wanted out.

"I don't know who sent you—" Kai began.

The Warrior lunged.

Kai sidestepped, but the man was fast—faster than any normal human. A fist grazed his ribs, and pain exploded through his side. Cracked. Definitely cracked.

*Think. You're not a fighter. You're an architect.*

The Mage's spell completed. A shard of ice shot toward Kai's chest.

He threw his hands up instinctively.

And something *happened*.

The qi in his dantian surged, following a path Mentor had never shown him. It flowed through his arms, his palms, and manifested as a shimmering barrier—a geometric pattern of interlocking hexagons that glowed with pale blue light. The ice shard shattered against it.

[SKILL UNLOCKED: QI SHIELD (PRIMITIVE)]
[EFFICIENCY: 12%]
[NEXT UPGRADE: 100 CYCLES OF COMPREHENSION]

The Warrior froze. "What the hell was that?"

The Mage's eyes narrowed. "The target has an unregistered skill. Adapt."

They came at him together.

Kai's body remembered. Not combat training—he had none—but the hours of qi circulation, the delicate balance of energy flowing through meridians. He redirected the qi from the shield into his legs, feeling his muscles strengthen, his reflexes sharpen.

[QI ENHANCEMENT: ACTIVATED]
[STATUS: TEMPORARY +3 AGILITY, +2 STRENGTH]

He ducked under the Warrior's swing, pivoted, and drove his elbow into the man's kidney. Not a killing blow. Not even a disabling one. But the Warrior grunted, and that was enough.

The Mage was already casting again. Fire this time. A stream of flame that would have turned Kai to ash.

Kai's qi sense screamed a warning. He threw himself sideways, rolling across the broken glass and splintered wood. The fire caught his discarded phone, melting it into slag.

*They're not trying to capture me. They're trying to kill me.*

"Stop running." The Mage's voice was calm, almost bored. "It'll be quicker if you cooperate."

"Who sent you?" Kai's back hit the wall. He was cornered. "At least tell me that."

The Warrior grinned. It was not a pleasant expression. "The System sent us. You're a glitch, Kai Chen. Glitches get patched."

The Mage raised his hand for another spell.

Kai closed his eyes.

And *listened*.

Mentor had said the System was the Heavenly Dao reborn as code. If that was true, then everything—the qi, the skills, the classes—was built on the same foundation. Architecture. Structure. Rules.

Kai couldn't fight a Warrior. He couldn't out-cast a Mage.

But he could see the flaws in their construction.

His qi sense expanded, pushing beyond the superficial layer of their auras. He saw the Warrior's stat distribution—too much Strength, not enough Endurance. A glass cannon. One solid hit to the right spot and he'd fold. He saw the Mage's mana lines, crude and inefficient, wasting energy with every spell.

He saw the weaknesses in their very existence.

Kai opened his eyes.

"Your stance is wrong," he said to the Warrior. "Your left foot is six degrees too far forward. It throws off your center of gravity." Then, to the Mage: "And you're holding your mana in your chest instead of your core. That's why your spells are slow."

The Warrior's grin faltered.

The Mage's hand trembled.

"How do you—"

Kai moved.

He didn't attack the Warrior. He attacked the *space* around the Warrior's left foot. A simple qi manipulation, nothing more than a nudge. But the geometry was perfect.

The Warrior's ankle twisted. He went down hard, his own momentum working against him.

The Mage panicked, unleashing a fireball wildly.

Kai's qi sense tracked its trajectory, calculated its path, identified the instability in its matrix. He didn't dodge. He reached out with his qi and *tapped* the fireball at exactly the right point.

It detonated prematurely.

The explosion caught both attackers. The Warrior screamed as flames licked his armor. The Mage stumbled back, his robes smoking, his face twisted in fury.

"You're—" the Mage coughed. "You're impossible."

"I'm an Architect." Kai straightened, his body aching, his dantian throbbing. "And your entire build is structurally unsound."

The Mage's eyes widened. He recognized the term. "The Organization will send more. They'll send—"

"I know."

Kai stepped forward. The Mage retreated, dragging the half-conscious Warrior with him. They disappeared through the ruined doorway, leaving Kai alone in his destroyed apartment.

He stood there for a long moment, breathing hard, the qi in his meridians settling back into its natural rhythm. His body screamed. His mind raced.

*The Organization. Someone in the System itself wants me dead.*

His phone was destroyed. His apartment was compromised. He had nowhere to go and no way to contact his friends or family.

But he had something they didn't.

He had Mentor's teachings. He had qi circulation. He had the ability to see the world's underlying structure.

And he had a new notification, pulsing in the corner of his vision:

[QUEST UNLOCKED: THE ARCHITECT'S AWAKENING]
[OBJECTIVE: SURVIVE THE ORGANIZATION'S HUNTERS (0/3)]
[REWARD: MERIDIAN EXPANSION TECHNIQUE]
[FAILURE CONDITION: DEATH]

Kai stared at the quest text. Three hunters. They'd sent two. The third was probably already tracking him.

He grabbed his backpack, stuffed what supplies he could salvage, and climbed out his window onto the fire escape. The night air was cold, filled with the distant sounds of a city coming to terms with its transformation.

His qi sense reached out, scanning the streets below.

Three signatures. One in the alley to his left. One on the rooftop across the street. One—

Directly above him.

Kai looked up.

A woman stood on the roof of his building, silhouetted against the moon. Her qi signature was unlike anything he'd felt before. Refined. Ancient. Powerful.

She smiled.

"The third," she said, her voice carrying clearly through the night air. "I was wondering when you'd notice me."

Kai tensed, ready to run, ready to fight.

But she held up her hands, palms open.

"Relax, little Architect. I'm not with the Organization." Her eyes gleamed with something that might have been amusement. "I'm here to offer you a choice."

She dropped from the roof, landing silently on the fire escape beside him.

"Join us. Or die alone."

Kai's hand trembled, but his voice was steady. "Who's 'us'?"

The woman's smile widened.

"The Architects who came before you."

title: The Price of Progress
content:
Kai's fingers hovered over his phone screen as the System broadcast continued scrolling through his vision:

[WORLD INTEGRATION: 73% COMPLETE]
[WARNING: SPATIAL ANOMALIES DETECTED IN 47 LOCATIONS]
[ADVISORY: COMBAT CLASSES PRIORITIZE DUNGEON CLEARANCE]

His phone buzzed again. Then again. The group chat exploded.

*Did everyone see that? 73%? What happens at 100%?*

*My tutorial orb said something about "full convergence." Anyone know what that means?*

*Dungeons? There are dungeons now? Where?*

Kai minimized the notifications and finally sent his message: *Something weird. I'll explain when I figure it out.*

He tossed the phone aside and sat up, swinging his legs over the edge of the bed. The motion sent a ripple through his qi, and he felt the energy settle into his dantian like water finding its level. The sensation was becoming familiar now, almost comfortable.

*Mentor said I'd need to rest. But how can I rest when the world's falling apart?*

He stood. Tested his legs. They felt... solid. More solid than they'd ever been. He walked to his bathroom and flipped on the light.

The person in the mirror was him. Same tired eyes. Same sharp jaw. Same dark hair sticking up at odd angles. But something underneath had shifted. His skin had a faint luminescence, like he'd been painted with moonlight. His eyes, when he looked closely, had flecks of gold swimming in the irises.

*Qi deviation symptoms,* Mentor had called it. *Perfectly normal for a Dao Architect.*

He splashed water on his face, and the gold flecks faded. The luminescence dimmed. He looked almost normal again.

Almost.

His phone buzzed once more. He ignored it and focused inward, cycling his qi through the meridian path Mentor had taught him. The energy flowed smoothly now, no longer fighting him at every turn. He completed three full cycles in the time it had taken him to do one in the tutorial.

*Progress.*

A knock at his door.

Kai's qi sense flared instinctively, and he felt the presence on the other side—bright, chaotic, laced with barely contained adrenaline. Someone who'd just completed their tutorial and was riding the high.

He opened the door.

Sarah stood in the hallway, her fists clenched, her eyes wild. She was his neighbor from 3B, a nursing student who worked night shifts at the hospital. She'd always been calm, collected, the kind of person who handled emergencies with clinical precision.

Right now, she looked anything but calm.

"Kai." Her voice cracked. "You're alive. Good. Everyone's panicking. The building manager jumped off the roof an hour ago."

"What?"

"His tutorial broke him. He got a class called 'Void-Touched' and spent eight hours staring into an abyss that kept whispering his worst memories back at him." She grabbed his arm, her grip iron. "They found him in the courtyard. He's at the hospital now. Might not make it."

Kai's stomach dropped. "The System can do that?"

"The System can do anything." Sarah's laugh was brittle. "I got Battle Medic. Healed a simulation of my own death fifteen times before I got it right. The orb said I have 'potential.'" She spat the word like a curse. "What did you get?"

He hesitated. "It's... complicated."

"Complicated." She stared at him. "Everyone I know got something straightforward. Warriors. Mages. Crafters. Healers. Even the weird ones—there's a guy on the fourth floor who got 'Soul Baker.' He makes bread that gives you temporary stat boosts. Simple. Understandable." Her eyes bore into his. "What did you get?"

"Dao Architect."

"Never heard of it."

"Neither had the System."

Sarah went very still. "What does that mean?"

"I don't know yet." He stepped into the hallway, closing his apartment door behind him. "But I'm going to find out. Where's the hospital?"

---

The walk through the city was a revelation.

Kai had lived in this neighborhood his whole life. He knew every crack in the sidewalk, every flickering streetlight, every shop that sold overpriced boba. But now, with his qi sense extended, he saw the city differently.

Qi clung to everything. The streetlights hummed with a thin, artificial energy. The trees in the park pulsed with a slow, natural rhythm. The people he passed radiated qi signatures in waves—some bright and sharp from their new classes, others dim and fading as their bodies struggled to adapt.

And everywhere, the System notifications flickered at the edge of his vision:

[SPATIAL ANOMALY DETECTED: 0.3 MILES NORTH]
[RECOMMENDED LEVEL: 5+]
[WARNING: HOSTILE ENTITIES MAY EMERGE]

Sarah walked beside him, her Battle Medic class manifesting as a faint green aura around her hands. She kept glancing at him sideways, her expression unreadable.

"You're different," she said finally.

"Everyone's different now."

"No. You're *different* different." She stopped walking, forcing him to stop too. "I've known you for three years, Kai. You walk the same. You talk the same. But there's something underneath you now that wasn't there before. Something I can't read."

Kai met her eyes. "I told you. My class is complicated."

"Complicated how?"

He considered lying. Considered brushing it off with a joke. But Sarah had always been sharp, and she'd just lost her building manager to System-induced psychosis. She deserved the truth.

"The System doesn't recognize my class," he said. "It's not in its records. My tutorial was taught by someone—something—called a Mentor, who told me the System is a flawed copy of something called the Heavenly Dao. And that if I don't cultivate properly, I have a 2% chance of dying from qi deviation every time I advance."

Sarah's face went pale. "That's..."

"Yeah."

"Does the System know?"

"I don't think it cares. It's too busy integrating the world." He started walking again. "Come on. The hospital's not far."

---

The hospital was chaos.

People filled the waiting room—newly classed individuals with glowing hands and flickering auras, mixed with ordinary humans who looked terrified and confused. Nurses ran between patients, their qi signatures fluctuating wildly as they tried to adapt to their new abilities.

Kai spotted the building manager immediately. He was in a corner bed, surrounded by doctors who looked completely out of their depth. His body was wracked with tremors, and his eyes—his eyes were black, completely black, with no iris or pupil visible.

Sarah pushed through the crowd. "I'm a Battle Medic. Let me through."

The doctors parted, too overwhelmed to argue. Sarah knelt beside the manager, her hands glowing green as she pressed them to his chest. Her face contorted in concentration.

"His qi is... wrong. It's twisted. Like someone took a thread and knotted it a hundred times."

Kai knelt beside her. "Can you fix it?"

"I don't know. I've never seen anything like this." She looked at him, desperation in her eyes. "Kai, you said your class is about cultivation. About energy. Can you sense what's wrong?"

He closed his eyes and extended his qi sense into the manager's body.

The moment he touched the man's energy, he recoiled.

The qi wasn't just twisted—it was *alive*. It writhed and pulsed with malevolent intent, as if something had taken root inside the manager and was actively trying to destroy him from within. And at the core of that twisted energy, Kai felt something familiar.

*The void.*

He'd felt it in the tutorial. That moment when Mentor had warned him about the darkness between Systems. That same energy was here, eating the manager alive.

"I can fix this," Kai said.

Sarah stared at him. "How?"

"I don't know. But I can feel it. It's like..." He searched for words. "It's like a corrupted file. I need to rewrite it."

"Do it."

Kai placed his hands on the manager's chest, over Sarah's glowing green palms. He closed his eyes and reached into his dantian, pulling out threads of his own qi. They were golden, pure, resonating with the Dao in a way he was only beginning to understand.

He fed them into the manager's body.

The twisted energy fought back. It lashed out, trying to corrupt his qi the same way it had corrupted the manager's. But Kai's energy didn't break—it *absorbed*. The golden threads wrapped around the dark energy, dissolving it strand by strand, pulling it apart and converting it back into clean qi.

*This is what Mentor meant,* he realized. *This is what a Dao Architect does.*

The process took ten minutes. Ten minutes of sustained focus, his qi draining steadily as he worked. By the end, his vision was blurry and his dantian ached with emptiness. But the manager's eyes were clear. His tremors had stopped. His qi flowed smooth and natural.

Sarah pulled her hands back, staring at Kai like she'd never seen him before. "You just... what did you just do?"

"I purified his qi." Kai swayed, and Sarah caught him. "It's what my class does."

A System notification appeared:

[SKILL UNLOCKED: QI PURIFICATION (RANK 1)]
[EFFECT: REMOVE CORRUPTION FROM TARGET'S MERIDIANS]
[COST: HIGH QI CONSUMPTION]
[NOTE: THIS SKILL IS UNKNOWN TO THE SYSTEM]

Unknown to the System. Just like his class.

Sarah helped him to his feet. "You need to rest."

"I can't." He looked around the hospital at all the suffering, all the chaos. "There's going to be more. More people broken by their tutorials. More spatial anomalies. More corruption." He met her eyes. "The System isn't a gift. It's a test. And most people are going to fail."

"What makes you think we won't?"

Before he could answer, a new System broadcast appeared:

[SYSTEM BROADCAST: SPATIAL ANOMALY ACTIVATED]
[LOCATION: EASTERN DISTRICT, SECTOR 7]
[THREAT LEVEL: MODERATE]
[HOSTILE ENTITIES DETECTED: 12]
[RECOMMENDED CLEARANCE PARTY: 5+ CLASSED INDIVIDUALS]

The eastern district. Sector 7.

That was his apartment building.

"Kai?" Sarah's voice was distant.

But Kai was already running.

title: Chapter 18
content:
CHAPTER 18: The First Casualty

The broadcast hung in the air, golden letters burning against Kai's vision before fading into his peripheral interface. Three lines. Simple. Final.

*All classes have been assigned.*

*World integration.*

Someone was screaming outside.

Kai bolted upright, the threadbare sheets falling away as his feet hit the cold floor. The qi sense he'd developed during the tutorial flared outward automatically, painting the world in shades of spiritual energy. His apartment building was a patchwork of dim signatures—ordinary people, still adjusting to their new classes. But outside, on the street below—

Chaos.

He crossed to the window in three strides, pushing aside the cheap curtain. The street was a warzone.

A man in a bloodstained wifebeater stood in the middle of the road, his arm transformed into something that looked like chitin and bone, a crude blade extending from where his hand should have been. He was swinging wildly at a group of people who had formed a loose circle around him, their own classes manifesting in flashes of light and poorly aimed projectiles.

A woman on the sidewalk was weeping, her hands glowing with healing energy that she couldn't seem to control, the light flickering and sputtering like a dying flame. An elderly man had somehow grown to twice his normal size, his clothes torn, his eyes wild and unfocused as he stumbled into a parked car, crushing its roof.

*The integration wasn't smooth.*

Kai's phone buzzed again. He grabbed it, scrolling through the flood of messages:

*DID ANYONE ELSE JUST GET A QUEST???*

*The monsters are real. I saw one. It came out of a RIFT.*

*Stay inside. Lock your doors. Whatever you do, don't—*

The message cut off. The sender's name was grayed out.

Kai felt cold.

He typed back: *Jacob?*

No response.

*Jacob, answer me.*

The group chat was still exploding with messages, but Jacob's name had gone silent. Kai remembered him—a guy from his gaming group who lived three blocks away, always cracking jokes, always the first to volunteer for tank duty in their raids. They'd played together for years.

*Don't jump to conclusions,* Kai told himself. *Maybe his phone died. Maybe he's just busy.*

But the qi sense didn't lie. And what he was sensing outside was only getting worse.

[QUEST: SURVIVE THE INTEGRATION]
[The System has fully merged with reality. Many are unprepared. Many will not adapt. Find shelter and secure your position before the first wave.]

First wave. Of what?

[OBJECTIVE: Reach a safe zone. Safe zones are marked on your minimap.]

Kai pulled up his interface, and a small map appeared in the corner of his vision, marked with a blinking dot that represented his location. There were a handful of green markers scattered across the city—schools, hospitals, government buildings. The closest one was a community center six blocks away.

Six blocks through whatever was happening outside.

He looked down at himself. Still in the same clothes he'd worn to the tutorial. No weapons. No supplies. Just a class that the System didn't understand and a dantian full of freshly cultivated qi.

*Great.*

He grabbed his backpack and started stuffing it with essentials: bottled water, protein bars, a first aid kit he'd never used, a pocket knife that was more rust than blade. It wasn't much, but it was something.

His phone buzzed again. A private message.

*Kai, it's Mark. I saw your name on the friend list. You're alive. Good.*

Mark. The potion guy from the group chat. The one who'd reached Apprentice rank during his tutorial.

*Yeah, I'm alive. You?*

*For now. My tutorial was useful. I can make basic healing potions. But I'm trapped in my apartment. There's something in the hallway.*

*What kind of something?*

*I don't know. It sounds wrong. Heavy footsteps, but too many of them. Like it's crawling.*

Kai's grip tightened on his phone. *Don't open the door.*

*Wasn't planning to. But I can hear it scratching. It knows I'm here.*

Another message came through before Kai could reply. This one wasn't from Mark.

[SYSTEM NOTIFICATION: PLAYER "JACOB_TANK" STATUS: DECEASED]

Kai stared at the words.

Deceased.

Not "offline." Not "disconnected." Deceased.

Jacob was dead. The guy who'd taught him how to pull aggro in their first raid. The guy who'd stayed up until 3 AM theorycrafting builds. The guy who'd sent that last message, cut off mid-sentence.

*Whatever you do, don't—*

Kai closed his eyes. Breathed. Felt the qi circulating through his meridians, steady and warm. Mentor's voice echoed in his memory: *You have the potential to reshape reality. But first, you must survive long enough to learn.*

He opened his eyes.

*I'm not dying in my apartment.*

He typed back to Mark: *I'm moving to the safe zone at the community center on 5th. Can you make it?*

A long pause. Then: *I'll try. But if I don't make it—*

*You'll make it. Just stay alive until I get there.*

*Bold of you to assume you'll make it either.*

Kai almost laughed. Almost. *Fair point. See you on the other side.*

He shoved his phone into his pocket, slung the backpack over his shoulder, and approached his apartment door. The qi sense extended ahead of him, probing the hallway beyond. Two signatures. One was human, faint and erratic—probably a neighbor, terrified and hiding. The other was...

Wrong.

The qi signature was jagged, spiky, like broken glass pressed into a human shape. It didn't flow like natural energy. It *lurched*, pulsing in irregular rhythms that made Kai's meridians ache in sympathy.

*That's not a person.*

He cracked the door open, peering through the gap.

The hallway stretched before him, lit by flickering fluorescent lights. At the far end, near the stairwell, something was crouched over a shape on the floor. It was humanoid, but only barely—its limbs were too long, its joints bending in directions that shouldn't have been possible. Its skin was a mottled gray, and its head was too large for its body, featuring a mouth that opened sideways instead of up and down.

It was eating.

Kai's stomach turned. The shape on the floor was wearing clothes he recognized. Mrs. Chen from apartment 3B. She'd always waved at him in the hallway, always asked if he was eating enough, always offered him homemade dumplings that he'd politely declined because he was too awkward to accept kindness.

She wasn't offering dumplings anymore.

The creature paused, its head swiveling toward Kai's door. Its eyes—if they could be called eyes—were black pits that seemed to drink the light around them.

It had sensed him.

*Move.*

Kai didn't think. He acted.

He pushed the door open and stepped into the hallway, letting the qi flow through his meridians the way Mentor had taught him. Not attacking. Not yet. Just *existing* in a way that made his spiritual presence known.

The creature hissed, a sound like tearing metal.

"Back off," Kai said, his voice steadier than he felt. "I don't want to fight you."

The creature didn't care what he wanted. It launched itself at him, moving faster than anything that size should have been able to move, its clawed hands reaching for his throat.

Kai's body moved on instinct.

He sidestepped, the qi in his legs responding before his conscious mind could process the command. The creature's claws raked through the air where his neck had been a moment before. He dropped low, pivoted, and drove his elbow into the thing's spine.

It was like hitting concrete.

The creature stumbled, but didn't fall. It twisted, its sideways mouth opening wide to reveal rows of needle-thin teeth. It lunged again, faster this time.

Kai brought his hands up, channeling qi into a barrier that flickered into existence between them. The creature's claws scraped against it, sending sparks of spiritual energy dancing through the air.

*I can't hold this forever.*

He needed to attack. But how? He had no weapons, no combat skills, no experience fighting anything more dangerous than a tutorial construct. The only thing he had was qi, and he barely knew how to use it.

*Mentor said qi was adaptable. That it could become anything if I understood it deeply enough.*

The creature was pressing against his barrier, its weight forcing him back step by step. His qi was draining, the barrier flickering.

*Think. What do I need right now?*

He needed to disable the creature. To stop it from moving, even for a moment.

Qi could become anything.

He focused, pulling the energy from his barrier back into his dantian, reshaping it with his intent. Not a shield. A weapon. Something sharp. Something fast.

The creature lunged through the gap where his barrier had been, expecting resistance that wasn't there. It overextended, its momentum carrying it forward.

Kai struck.

A blade of qi, barely formed, lanced out from his palm and pierced the creature's chest. It wasn't elegant. It wasn't refined. It was raw, desperate, and fueled by six hours of meditation and a lifetime of instinct.

The creature froze.

Its black eyes widened—an expression that might have been surprise, or pain, or something else entirely. Then its body began to dissolve, breaking apart into motes of gray light that scattered into the air like ash.

Kai stood there, breathing hard, his hand still extended, his qi depleted and his body trembling.

The hallway was silent.

He looked down at Mrs. Chen's body. There wasn't enough left to bury.

*The integration killed her. The System killed her. And it's not done yet.*

His minimap pulsed, the safe zone marker blinking patiently.

Six blocks away.

He stepped over what remained of his neighbor and headed for the stairwell.

---

Mark's apartment was four blocks from the community center, but the path was a nightmare. Every street had its own horror show: people fighting monsters, monsters fighting people, people fighting people. The System had given everyone classes, but it hadn't given everyone the skills to use them.

Kai moved through the chaos like a ghost, using his qi sense to avoid the worst of the conflicts. He'd killed two more creatures on his way—things that looked like twisted versions of animals, their qi signatures just as jagged and wrong as the first one. Each kill drained him further, but each kill also taught him something new about how qi could manifest.

The blade technique was getting sharper. Faster. More precise.

*Necessity is the mother of cultivation,* he thought grimly.

He reached Mark's building to find the front door smashed open, glass crunching under his feet as he entered the lobby. The qi sense painted the interior in shades of danger—multiple creatures, scattered across different floors, their signatures flickering like bad connections.

Mark's apartment was on the third floor.

Kai took the stairs two at a time, his legs burning, his qi reserves running on fumes. He reached the third-floor landing and stopped.

The hallway was full of webs.

Not spider webs—something thicker, stickier, pulsing with a faint bioluminescent glow that made his skin crawl. They stretched from wall to wall, floor to ceiling, creating a maze of sticky strands that vibrated with each step he took.

*This is new.*

He heard a noise from the end of the hall. A

title: The Price of Foundation
content:
The System notification hung in Kai's vision like a guillotine blade.

[ALL CLASSES HAVE BEEN ASSIGNED]
[WORLD INTEGRATION PHASE 2 INITIATED]
[ESTIMATED COMPLETION: 72 HOURS]

Three days. In three days, the second wave of integration would hit. Whatever that meant.

Kai set his phone down and focused on his internal qi circulation. The energy flowed sluggishly, still unfamiliar, still resistant to his control. He'd completed ten successful cycles during the tutorial, but that was under Mentor's direct supervision. Here, alone in his cramped apartment, the qi felt like trying to hold smoke.

*I need a better foundation.*

He crossed his legs on the bed, ignoring the protest from joints that had spent six hours in meditation pose. The tower technique—Mentor had called it *Primordial Breath Refinement*—required concentration he didn't have. His mind kept drifting to the messages, the broadcast, the countless unknowns.

*Focus.*

Minutes passed. The qi in his meridians responded sluggishly, trickling instead of flowing. He completed one cycle. Then another. On the third, something shifted.

The energy in his apartment changed.

Kai's eyes snapped open. Through his qi sense, he felt it—a presence. Not the vague life signatures of neighbors or the residual qi in electronics. This was deliberate. Probing. Someone was scanning his apartment.

He threw himself off the bed, landing in a crouch. His hand closed around a lamp—pathetic weapon, but better than nothing.

"Show yourself."

Silence. Then a voice, smooth and amused: "Impressive. Most new Awakened can't detect perception skills until the third day."

The air in the corner of his room rippled. A figure stepped out of nowhere, as if walking through an invisible door. He was tall, lean, dressed in clothing that seemed to shift between modern casual and something more archaic—a trench coat that billowed despite no wind, boots that left no sound on the floor.

His eyes glowed faintly silver.

"Who are you?" Kai's grip tightened on the lamp. "How did you get in?"

"The better question is why I'm here." The man smiled, showing teeth that seemed slightly too sharp. "Name's Shen. I'm a Guide, like the one who ran your tutorial. Except I work for a different faction."

"Different faction?"

"Surprised? The System isn't monolithic, Architect. It's a framework. A foundation. What gets built on top of it depends on who's coding." Shen gestured, and a chair materialized behind him. He sat, crossing his legs. "The woman who taught you—Mentor, you called her—she represents the Preservationist Faction. They believe the System should integrate slowly, protect humanity from the truth until they're ready."

"And you?"

"I'm a Progressionist. We believe in acceleration. Growth through conflict. The System wasn't meant to coddle—it was meant to forge." Shen's silver eyes locked onto Kai's. "And you, Architect, are the most interesting variable to appear in a century."

Kai didn't lower the lamp. "What do you want?"

"To offer you a choice. The Preservationists will teach you safely. Slowly. They'll build your foundation over months, years, until you're stable enough to handle the truth. But here's the thing about slow foundations, Architect—they're predictable. They follow established paths. And established paths can be countered."

"And the Progressionist path?"

"Faster. Harder. More dangerous." Shen's smile widened. "But the rewards... You've already seen what happens when you push yourself. That foundation you're building? In six hours, it's barely started. I can show you how to complete it in one."

Kai's phone buzzed. Another message. He ignored it.

"Let's say I believe you. What's the catch?"

"Every path has a price. The Preservationists demand obedience to their hierarchy. They'll teach you, but they'll control you. The Progressionists... we demand results. We don't care how you achieve them, as long as you grow." Shen reached into his coat and pulled out a small jade slip, etched with characters that seemed to writhe. "This contains a technique. Not the safe, sterilized version they'd give you—the real thing. *Primordial Breath Refinement* as it was meant to be practiced."

Kai's qi sense flared. The jade slip radiated power, dense and ancient, like touching a live wire.

"Why me?"

"Because you're the first Architect. The System didn't have a class for you because it had to *create one*. That means your potential is unbounded—or catastrophic. The Preservationists want to contain that potential. I want to unleash it."

"And if I refuse?"

Shen's smile didn't falter, but something cold entered his eyes. "Then you'll continue as you are. Slow growth. Safe growth. And in three days, when Phase 2 begins, you'll face the coming trials with an incomplete foundation." He stood, the chair dissolving behind him. "The choice is yours, Architect. But don't take too long. The integration waits for no one."

He turned toward the wall, which began to shimmer.

"Wait."

Shen paused.

"If I take your technique, what happens to Mentor?"

"She'll be disappointed. Possibly angry. But she won't harm you—that's not their way." He glanced back, silver eyes gleaming. "Ours, however... we have fewer restrictions. Remember that."

He stepped through the wall and vanished.

Kai stood alone in his apartment, lamp still raised, heart pounding. The jade slip lay on his bed where Shen had left it. He approached slowly, reaching out with his qi sense.

The technique inside was real. He could feel it—a more aggressive, more efficient version of what Mentor had taught him. It would accelerate his foundation building by orders of magnitude.

It would also, he sensed, carry risks she hadn't mentioned.

His phone buzzed again. Then again. Then a knock at his door.

Kai shoved the jade slip into his pocket and crossed to the door, peering through the peephole. His mother stood in the hallway, phone in hand, expression torn between relief and anger.

He opened the door.

"Kai! I've been calling for—" She stopped, staring at him. "What happened to you?"

"What do you mean?"

"Your eyes. They're... different."

Kai stepped back, letting her in. "I'll explain everything. But first, tell me what's happening out there."

His mother sat on the couch, wringing her hands. "Chaos. People are manifesting abilities in the streets. Fights breaking out. The government's issued a state of emergency, but no one knows what to do. Your father's at the precinct—they've set up a task force for 'Awakened incidents.'" She looked at him, searching his face. "You got a class, didn't you? What kind?"

"A rare one. An Architect."

"What does that mean?"

Kai opened his mouth to answer, but the System notification interrupted him.

[ALERT: INCOMPLETE FOUNDATION DETECTED]
[WARNING: PHASE 2 INTEGRATION MAY PROVE FATAL IF FOUNDATION NOT STABILIZED]
[RECOMMENDATION: SEEK IMMEDIATE GUIDANCE OR ALTERNATIVE TRAINING METHODS]

He could feel the jade slip burning in his pocket. Shen's offer. Mentor's training. Two paths, both with hidden costs.

"Mom, I need to make a decision. A dangerous one."

Her face paled. "What kind of decision?"

Kai pulled out the jade slip, watching the characters writhe. "Between safe and fast. Between control and freedom." He met her eyes. "Between living long enough to matter, or dying for a chance to be more."

The apartment shook. Distant screams rose from the street. Through the window, Kai saw a pillar of light erupt from downtown—someone had just advanced, violently and publicly.

Phase 2 was starting early.

"Kai." His mother's voice was barely a whisper. "Whatever you're going to do, do it fast."

He looked at the jade slip. Looked at his mother. Felt the incomplete foundation in his dantian, fragile as spun glass.

The System notification pulsed again, more urgent:

[PHASE 2 INTEGRATION ACCELERATING]
[ESTIMATED COMPLETION: 12 HOURS]
[RECOMMENDATION: IMMEDIATE ACTION REQUIRED]

Kai's hand closed around the jade slip.

He made his choice.

title: The First Fracture
content:
Kai's phone slipped from his fingers as the System broadcast rippled through his consciousness like a stone dropped into still water. The words hung in the air, visible only to him, pulsing with an authority that made his newly awakened qi resonate in sympathy.

[ALL CLASSES HAVE BEEN ASSIGNED]
[WORLD INTEGRATION PROCEEDING TO PHASE TWO]
[ESTIMATED COMPLETION: 72 HOURS]

Three days. In three days, the System would finish merging with Earth, and everything would change permanently.

He scrambled to grab his phone, but the screen was frozen, the group chat messages locked in place. Throughout his apartment building, he could hear the same sounds—curses, shouts, the crash of someone dropping their phone in shock. Through his qi sense, he felt the chaotic surge of emotions from dozens of people experiencing the same notification simultaneously.

Then, just as suddenly as it appeared, the broadcast vanished. His phone screen flickered back to life, messages resuming their chaotic flow.

*Did everyone just see that?*
*Phase two? What does that mean?*
*My tutorial orb is gone. Just disappeared.*

Kai's fingers moved before his mind caught up, typing into the group chat: *Check your status screens. Anything different?*

A flood of responses confirmed his suspicion. Everyone's interfaces had updated. New tabs appeared—Skills, Titles, Achievements. The System was expanding its functionality, rolling out features that had been locked during the tutorial phase.

But when Kai opened his own status, his blood ran cold.

[NAME: Kai Chen]
[CLASS: Dao Architect]
[LEVEL: 0]
[QI CULTIVATION: Qi Condensation, Stage 1]
[STATUS: UNCLASSIFIED - SYSTEM MONITORING REQUIRED]

His level hadn't changed. Everyone else was reporting levels between 3 and 7, having gained experience from their tutorials. But Kai's remained stubbornly at zero, as if the System didn't know how to quantify his progress.

Worse, the "System Monitoring Required" tag was new. And it was blinking.

A chime echoed in his mind, softer than the broadcast but infinitely more personal.

[INDIVIDUAL NOTIFICATION: SUBJECT #KAI-001]
[ANOMALY DETECTED: CLASS ARCHITECTURE DEVIATES FROM STANDARD PARAMETERS]
[RECOMMENDATION: REPORT TO NEAREST SYSTEM ADMINISTRATION HUB FOR EVALUATION]
[COMPLIANCE WINDOW: 24 HOURS]
[NON-COMPLIANCE PENALTY: CLASS SUSPENSION]

Class suspension. The words hit him like a physical blow. He'd only been a cultivator for a day, but already the thought of losing it—of having the qi pathways Mentor had painstakingly carved into his body sealed off—filled him with a cold, desperate dread.

He was still staring at the notification when his phone buzzed again. A private message from an unknown number.

*You're Kai Chen, right? The one who got the weird class.*

Kai's thumb hovered over the keyboard. *Who is this?*

*Name's Lin Wei. I'm a System Analyst. Level 4. My class lets me see class data structures.* A pause. *Yours is broken. Not damaged—broken. Like someone wrote it in a different language than everyone else's.*

Kai's apartment suddenly felt too small. He pulled the curtains closed, checking the street below. Normal. People walking, cars moving, the usual morning chaos of the city. But now, through his qi sense, he could see the faint shimmer of System energy clinging to everyone, threads of light connecting them to an invisible network overhead.

*Why are you telling me this?*

*Because the Administration Hubs aren't what they seem.* Another pause, longer this time. *I analyzed one remotely. The processing nodes inside? They're made of crystallized human qi. Thousands of people's worth. The System isn't just integrating with Earth—it's harvesting us.*

Kai's stomach lurched. *How do I know you're telling the truth?*

*You don't. But check your class description. The one that appeared when you first got it. Look at the fine print.*

He pulled up his class interface, scrolling past the basic information to the dense blocks of text he'd skimmed over before. There, buried in a subsection labeled "SYSTEM INTEGRATION PROTOCOLS," was a line he'd missed:

*[NOTE: UNSTABLE CLASS ARCHITECTURES MAY BE FLAGGED FOR DECONSTRUCTION TO MAINTAIN SYSTEM STABILITY. AFFECTED HOSTS WILL BE RECYCLED INTO INFRASTRUCTURE COMPONENTS.]*

Recycled. Into infrastructure.

The cockroach behind his wall skittered, and Kai felt its tiny life force flicker with the ambient qi. He thought of the nodes Lin Wei described—crystallized human qi. Thought of the tutorial chamber with its impossible geometry, its seamless walls. Thought of Mentor, whose body had felt real but whose qi signature had been... wrong. Artificial.

*You see it now,* Lin Wei messaged. *The System isn't a game. It's a cage. And you're one of the people who can see the bars.*

*What do you want?*

*Meet me. There's a tea shop on Lotus Street, basement level. Come alone. Come before the 24-hour window expires. I'll show you what the System doesn't want you to see.*

The message vanished before Kai could reply, deleted from both their conversation histories. Only the notification of "CLASS SUSPENSION" remained, blinking its countdown.

---

The tea shop was exactly as Lin Wei had described—a narrow staircase descending into a basement filled with the smell of jasmine and old wood. The owner, an elderly woman with sharp eyes and a qi signature that flickered like a candle in wind, nodded at Kai as he entered.

"Back room. He's waiting."

Kai pushed through a beaded curtain and found himself in a small chamber lit by a single oil lamp. A young man sat cross-legged on a cushion, his face half-hidden in shadow. His qi signature was strange—layered, like someone had wrapped multiple energy patterns around a single core.

"You came." Lin Wei gestured to a cushion across from him. "Sit. We don't have much time."

Kai remained standing. "Show me proof first."

Lin Wei smiled, thin and knowing. He raised his hand, and a projection appeared in the air between them—a three-dimensional map of the city, rendered in threads of golden light. But the map was wrong. The buildings were there, the streets were there, but beneath it all, pulsing like a heart, was a massive crystalline structure buried kilometers underground.

"This is the Administration Hub beneath the city center," Lin Wei said. "They're building more. One for every major population center. By the time Phase Two completes, every major city on Earth will have one."

"You said the nodes are made of human qi."

"I said crystallized human qi. There's a difference." Lin Wei zoomed the projection, showing the Hub's internal structure. "The System doesn't take your energy directly. It takes your potential. Every time you level up, a fraction of your growth is siphoned off and stored. The higher your level, the more you contribute."

Kai studied the projection, his qi sense reaching out to probe its edges. It felt real. Solid. "Why show me this?"

"Because your class is the only one that can't be harvested." Lin Wei's eyes gleamed in the lamplight. "The Dao Architect doesn't use System experience points. It uses qi. The System can't touch qi. Not directly. It's the one resource it can't fabricate or control."

"That still doesn't explain why you need me."

Lin Wei's projection shifted, zooming out until it showed the entire planet. Thousands of Hubs, interconnected by threads of golden light, forming a web that wrapped around the Earth like a cage.

"The System is a parasite," Lin Wei said quietly. "It's been planning this integration for millennia. The classes, the levels, the tutorials—all designed to make us dependent on it. To make us *willing* to feed it our potential. But you? Your class is a glitch in the code. A backdoor the System never intended to exist."

Kai's hand drifted to his chest, where his dantian pulsed with warm, living qi. "You want me to be a weapon."

"I want you to be a key." Lin Wei leaned forward. "The System flagged you for deconstruction because it's afraid of what you represent. A path to power that doesn't require its permission. If you report to the Hub, it will try to integrate you—to turn your anomaly into just another feature. But if you don't..."

"Class suspension."

"Which means your cultivation gets sealed. Your meridians collapse. You become a normal human again, but with the knowledge of what you've lost." Lin Wei's voice dropped. "And then, eventually, you get recycled anyway. Because the System doesn't leave loose ends."

The oil lamp flickered. Outside, the tea shop's bell chimed as a customer entered.

"What do you want me to do?"

Lin Wei reached into his robe and produced a small jade slip, carved with characters that seemed to shift when Kai looked at them directly. "This contains a technique. It's incomplete—I've spent the past six hours trying to decode it—but it's the only thing I've found that can sever a System connection without killing the host."

Kai took the slip. It hummed against his palm, warm with trapped qi. "And if I learn it?"

"Then you become the first truly free person in the new world." Lin Wei stood, brushing dust from his robes. "And maybe, just maybe, you can show others how to follow."

The jade slip pulsed, and a notification appeared in Kai's vision:

[NEW ITEM ACQUIRED: UNKNOWN TECHNIQUE - SEVERANCE ART]
[WARNING: THIS TECHNIQUE IS NOT RECOGNIZED BY THE SYSTEM]
[PROCEEDING WITH CAUTION IS ADVISED]

Kai looked up to thank Lin Wei, but the young man was already gone. The back room was empty, the beaded curtain swaying gently as if disturbed by a wind that didn't exist.

The elderly shop owner appeared in the doorway. "He said to tell you one more thing. The 24-hour countdown is a lie. You have until midnight."

Kai checked his interface. The notification was still there, still blinking its countdown. But now, when he focused on the numbers, he saw what he'd missed before—a second timer, hidden beneath the first, running faster than it should.

Eleven hours. Not twenty-four.

The System was already trying to trap him.

He pocketed the jade slip and left the tea shop, emerging into a street that looked normal but felt wrong. The people walking past had the same qi signatures as before, but now he could see the thin golden threads connecting them to the sky, to the Hubs, to the cage that was already closing around the world.

His phone buzzed. Another message from Lin Wei, delivered through some method that bypassed the System's monitoring:

*They know you have the slip. They're coming. Don't go home.*

Kai looked up. In the distance, three figures were walking toward him—humans in appearance, but their qi signatures were uniform, identical, like they'd been stamped from the same mold. Their eyes glowed faintly gold.

System enforcers.

And they were already too close to run.

The jade slip burned in his pocket. The Severance Art called to him, promising freedom with every pulse of trapped

title: The First Quest
content:
[SYSTEM BROADCAST: TUTORIAL PHASE COMPLETE]

[ALL CLASSES HAVE BEEN ASSIGNED]

[WORLD INTEGRATION AT 37.2%]

[PHASE 2 INITIATING IN 23:59:47]

Kai's thumb hovered over the send button. Twenty-three hours until Phase 2. Whatever that meant. The countdown timer burned in the corner of his vision, refusing to be dismissed.

His phone buzzed again. The group chat had exploded.

*Phase 2? What the hell is Phase 2?*

*My tutorial guide said something about "world convergence." Anyone else get that?*

*I got a quest. A REAL QUEST. [Kill 10 Goblins - Reward: 50 EXP, 5 Credits]*

*Goblins? Where??*

*In the park. They're spawning near the pond. Someone already recorded it.*

A video link appeared. Kai clicked it.

The footage was shaky, filmed from a third-floor apartment window. Below, in Greenwood Park—the same park where Kai had walked his dog as a kid—three figures stumbled through the grass. They were short, green-skinned, with tusks jutting from their lower jaws. One carried a rusted sword. Another dragged what looked like a club made from a traffic sign.

They were real. Physical. Solid.

People in the comments were arguing about CGI, about pranks, about mass hallucination. But Kai knew better. He could *feel* them through his qi sense—three flickering flames of spiritual energy, crude and violent, nothing like the smooth flow of human life force.

The goblins were hunting.

And they were heading toward the apartment building where Mrs. Chen lived. Mrs. Chen, who was eighty-three and used a walker. Mrs. Chen, who'd given Kai cookies when he was ten and still remembered his birthday every year.

Kai was on his feet before he finished the thought.

His body moved differently. Lighter. The qi circulating through his meridians responded to his intent, flooding his legs with warmth. He crossed his apartment in three strides, grabbed his jacket, and paused at the door.

*What am I doing? I'm not a fighter. I'm an architect. Whatever that means.*

But the goblins were real, and Mrs. Chen was real, and Kai had never been the kind of person who waited for someone else to act.

He ran.

The stairwell was empty. Most people were still inside, glued to their phones, trying to understand what was happening. Kai took the stairs three at a time, his qi-enhanced legs carrying him down six flights in seconds. He burst through the ground floor exit and into the street.

The city looked normal. Cars sat in traffic. A delivery drone hummed overhead. But there, two blocks away, the park's tree line rustled with movement that didn't belong.

Kai ran toward it.

His qi sense expanded as he moved, painting the world in shades of spiritual energy. The goblins were easy to track—their life force was jagged, aggressive, like broken glass in a stream. Three of them, moving through the bushes that bordered the park's edge.

They were heading for the old Santos building. Mrs. Chen's building.

Kai rounded the corner and saw them.

The goblins had found the fire escape. One was already climbing, its clawed fingers digging into the rusted metal. The other two waited below, scanning the street with beady red eyes.

They saw him at the same moment.

The two on the ground hissed, baring yellowed teeth. The one on the fire escape paused, looking down, then continued climbing with renewed urgency.

*They're not just hunting. They're coordinated.*

The lead goblin charged.

Kai's combat instincts were nonexistent. He'd never thrown a real punch in his life. But his body reacted before his mind caught up—qi surged through his meridians, and he *shifted*.

Not dodged. *Shifted.* His foot landed exactly where it needed to be. His torso twisted precisely enough to let the goblin's rusty blade whistle past his ribs. The movement felt natural, inevitable, like the qi had calculated the trajectory and adjusted his body accordingly.

*Mentor said qi enhances the body. She didn't say it gives combat reflexes.*

The goblin stumbled past him, off-balance. Kai's hand grabbed its wrist before he knew what he was doing. He twisted. The blade clattered to the pavement.

The second goblin was already swinging.

Its club—a rusted pipe wrapped in duct tape—caught Kai in the shoulder. Pain exploded through his arm. But instead of crippling him, the impact triggered something. His qi flared, rushing to the injury, numbing the pain and flooding the damaged tissue with healing energy.

[Minor Bruise: Healing... 1.2 seconds remaining]

The notification flickered and vanished.

Kai had no time to process it. The first goblin recovered, lunging at him with bare claws. He brought his knee up, connecting with its chest. Cartilage crunched. The goblin screeched and fell back.

But the third one had reached the fire escape's second level. It was climbing faster now, driven by some desperate urgency.

Mrs. Chen's apartment was on the third floor.

Kai made a decision.

He stopped fighting defensively. He reached inside himself, grabbed hold of his qi, and *pushed*.

The energy exploded outward. Not as a physical attack, but as a wave of spiritual pressure. The two goblins in front of him recoiled, their crude life forces flickering like candles in a hurricane. They didn't understand what they were feeling, but their instincts told them to run.

They ran.

Kai didn't chase. He turned and sprinted for the fire escape.

The metal ladder groaned under his weight as he climbed. His injured shoulder screamed, but the qi was already knitting the damage, numbing the worst of it. He pulled himself onto the second level, then the third.

The goblin was at Mrs. Chen's window.

It had smashed the glass. Its clawed hand reached inside, groping for the lock.

Kai grabbed its ankle and yanked.

The goblin shrieked, losing its grip. It tumbled backward, crashing into Kai, sending them both plummeting toward the ground.

They hit the fire escape's second level with a jarring impact. The goblin took the worst of it—Kai landed on top of it, and he felt ribs crack beneath him. But the creature was still moving, still fighting, its claws raking across Kai's forearm.

Blood welled up. Qi rushed to seal the wounds.

The goblin's red eyes met Kai's. For a moment, they just stared at each other—two creatures thrown into a world neither of them understood.

Then the goblin's gaze shifted. To something behind Kai. Something that made its pupils dilate with terror.

Kai turned.

A figure stood on the rooftop across the street. Human-shaped, but wrong. Its proportions were too long, too thin, and its skin shimmered like oil on water. Two points of silver light burned where its eyes should be.

The goblin beneath Kai whimpered.

The figure tilted its head, studying them with an interest that felt predatory. Then it raised one hand and made a gesture—a sweeping motion, as if brushing something aside.

The goblin screamed.

Its body began to dissolve. Not into blood or gore, but into particles of light, streaming upward like embers from a fire. The process took three seconds. When it was done, nothing remained but a faint scorch mark on the metal grating.

[Goblin Vanquished: 15 EXP]

[Quest Updated: Defend the Innocent (1/5)]

Kai scrambled to his feet, heart hammering. The figure on the rooftop was still watching him. Still unmoving. Still smiling—he could feel the smile, even if he couldn't see it.

A System notification appeared in his vision. Not from the usual interface. This one was different—gold text on a black background, flickering at the edges.

[ANOMALOUS ENTITY DETECTED]

[CLASSIFICATION: ???]

[THREAT LEVEL: UNKNOWN]

[RECOMMENDATION: EVACUATE]

The figure raised its hand again. Pointed at Kai.

His qi sense screamed a warning. He dove sideways, rolling across the fire escape's grating, just as a beam of silver light punched through the space where he'd been standing. The metal melted, dripping like candle wax.

Kai didn't wait to see what came next. He jumped.

Not down—that would take too long. He jumped *across*, launching himself from the fire escape toward the adjacent building's rooftop. His qi-enhanced legs carried him farther than he'd expected. He cleared the five-meter gap, hit the gravel roof, and rolled to absorb the impact.

The figure was still there. Still watching. But it made no move to follow.

[ANOMALOUS ENTITY: DISENGAGED]

[THREAT STATUS: LOST]

Kai lay on the rooftop, gasping, his entire body trembling. The qi in his meridians churned like a storm, responding to his fear and adrenaline. He could feel the goblin's death still echoing in his spiritual senses—a void where its jagged life force had been.

*What was that?*

He didn't need to ask. The System had told him: Anomalous Entity. Unknown classification. Threat level unknown.

There were things in this new world that weren't part of the System's design.

And one of them had just killed a goblin with a gesture.

Kai's phone buzzed. He pulled it out with shaking hands. More messages. More chaos. Someone had posted footage of a giant wolf tearing through a supermarket. Someone else reported seeing a flying serpent above the financial district. The world was unraveling, and Phase 2 hadn't even started.

Twenty-three hours remained.

Kai sat up, wiped the blood from his arm, and looked at the sky. The stars were different tonight. Brighter. Closer. As if the universe itself was pressing down on Earth, waiting for something.

His dantian pulsed. A notification appeared.

[QUEST UNLOCKED: THE OBSERVER'S GAME]

[Objective: Survive Phase 2]

[Objective: Identify the Anomalous Entity]

[Objective: Do not die]

[Reward: ???]

[Failure: Permanent deletion]

Kai read the last line three times.

*Permanent deletion.*

Not death. *Deletion.* As if his existence was nothing more than a file that could be erased.

The golden-eyed woman's words echoed in his mind: *The System is a tool. But tools can be broken. And so can those who wield them poorly.*

He had twenty-three hours to prepare for Phase 2. Twenty-three hours to figure out what he was, what the Anomalous Entity wanted, and how to survive a game that the System itself seemed afraid of.

Kai stood up, brushed the gravel from his jacket, and started walking home.

He had work to do.

title: The Price of Uniqueness
content:
The broadcast hung in the air, golden text fading slowly like dying embers. Kai's finger hovered over the send button, his half-typed message suddenly feeling inadequate.

*Something weird.*

What a joke.

He deleted it and typed instead: *Got a unique class. Talk later.*

Before he could second-guess himself, he hit send. The group chat exploded with reactions—questions, jokes, demands for details—but Kai silenced his phone and tossed it onto the bed.

He needed to think.

The qi in his dantian pulsed, steady and warm. He could feel it now, a second heartbeat beneath his skin. His meridians hummed with residual energy, still raw from the tutorial's forced expansion. Mentor had said his foundation was forming but not stable. What did that mean, exactly?

A sharp knock at his door made him flinch.

"Kai! Open up!"

His neighbor, Mrs. Chen. She'd been his mother's friend since before he could walk. Her voice carried the particular urgency of someone who'd been worrying for hours.

He crossed the apartment in three strides and pulled the door open. Mrs. Chen stood in the hallway, eyes red-rimmed, phone clutched in her trembling hands.

"Your mother called me. She said you weren't answering."

"I was in the tutorial. It's fine."

"The tutorial." She let out a shaky breath. "You're the sixth person in this building I've checked on. Two of them are still... still *in* theirs. Their bodies are here, but—"

"They're in stasis. It's normal."

Mrs. Chen stared at him like he'd grown a second head. "Normal? Nothing about this is normal, Kai. The news is saying people are dying. They went into the tutorial and their bodies just—"

"I know."

He did know. Mentor had shown him the statistics. The 2% mortality rate for his path. The higher rates for others. The System wasn't gentle with those who failed its trials.

"I'm fine," he said again, softer this time. "I promise. Tell my mom I'll call her tonight."

Mrs. Chen studied his face, her worry warring with something else—fear, perhaps. Then her eyes widened.

"Kai. Your eyes."

"What about them?"

She pulled out her phone, switched on the camera, and held it up. Kai looked at his reflection.

His irises had changed. They were no longer the muddy brown he'd inherited from his father. Instead, they swirled with threads of gold and silver, like liquid metal caught in slow motion.

*The qi.* He reached up, touching the corner of his eye. The skin felt normal. But the irises...

"Have you seen anyone else with this?" he asked.

Mrs. Chen shook her head, still staring. "The others in the building, they look the same as before. Normal. But you—"

"It's because of my class."

"Your class?" She grabbed his arm, nails digging in. "What class did you get? The news says there are ranks. Some classes are better than others. My grandson got a Common class—Swordsman. They said it's the most common Warrior variant. But you, you look—"

"Mrs. Chen."

She stopped, breath coming in short gasps.

"I need to figure out what's happening. I'll explain everything later. But right now, I need you to go home and stay inside. It's not safe out there."

She wanted to argue. He could see it in her eyes. But something in his voice, or maybe the gold-silver swirl of his irises, made her nod instead.

"Call your mother," she said, and retreated down the hallway.

Kai closed the door and leaned against it, heart pounding.

*My eyes changed. What else changed?*

He reached out with his qi sense, expanding it beyond his apartment. The building's spiritual topography unfolded in his mind—dim sparks of life from his neighbors, brighter clusters from those who'd completed their tutorials, and in apartment 4B, a blazing inferno that made him recoil.

*What the hell?*

He focused on that signature. It was unlike anything he'd felt before—dense, aggressive, pulsing with a rhythm that felt almost hostile. The person in that apartment had completed their tutorial, but the energy they radiated was nothing like the weaker signatures around them.

*They either got a powerful class, or something went wrong.*

Kai made a decision. He'd been passive for too long. The System had changed everything, and sitting in his apartment wouldn't help him understand it. He needed information. He needed to see what the world had become.
