He grabbed his jacket, slipped on his shoes, and stepped into the hallway.

The building was eerily quiet. Usually, the corridor echoed with the sounds of televisions, arguments, cooking. Now it was silent, punctuated only by the occasional muffled sob or hushed conversation.

As he passed apartment 2C, the door cracked open. A woman's face appeared in the gap, pale and terrified.

"The tutorial," she whispered. "My husband hasn't come back. His body is still sitting at the dinner table, but he won't wake up. What do I do?"

Kai stopped. He didn't have an answer. The System hadn't provided instructions for this.

"Wait," he said finally. "If he's still breathing, he's still in the tutorial. Give it time."

"And if he stops breathing?"

The question hit him like a physical blow. He'd been so focused on his own survival that he hadn't considered the wider implications. People were dying. Families were being torn apart. The System wasn't a game—it was a culling.

"I don't know," he admitted. "I'm sorry."

He continued down the stairs, stepping into the building's lobby.

Outside, the world had transformed.

The street was crowded with people, but they moved differently now—some huddling in groups, others striding with newfound confidence. A man in a business suit stood on the corner, hands crackling with arcs of electricity. A teenage girl was crying as a woman in a nurse's uniform tried to calm her down. A group of young men laughed loudly, boasting about their new classes.

Kai scanned their qi signatures. Most were faint—Common-ranked classes with limited potential. A few were brighter, suggesting Rare or possibly Elite ranks. And one, standing near the convenience store across the street, blazed like a bonfire.

*That one.*

He crossed the street, weaving through the chaos. The man was tall, lean, with sharp features and eyes that held the same metallic sheen as Kai's—except his were pure silver, without the gold.

"You're the one who was staring at me," the man said without turning around. His voice was calm, measured. "You felt my qi, didn't you?"

"You felt me too."

"Obviously." The man finally turned, fixing Kai with an unreadable gaze. "What class did you get?"

"That's a personal question."

"We're in a new world now. Personal questions are the only ones that matter." The man extended his hand. "Name's Damien. I got a class called [Void Walker]. Rank: Legendary."

Kai's blood ran cold. Legendary. The highest rank the System had revealed. And this man was admitting it freely.

"Kai," he said reluctantly. "My class is... unusual."

"Unusual how?"

"Unclassed."

Damien's silver eyes flickered. "There's no such thing as an unclassed rank. The System assigns everyone a specific class with a specific rank."

"The System doesn't know what to do with me."

For the first time, Damien's composure cracked. His eyebrows rose slightly, and something like interest flickered in his gaze. "Show me."

Kai hesitated. But he needed allies, and this man—Legendary-ranked, Void Walker—might be the only person who could understand.

He extended his hand, letting a thread of qi flow to his palm. It coalesced into a small, swirling orb of light, pulsing with an energy that felt neither elemental nor arcane. It was something else entirely.

Damien stared at it for a long moment. Then he laughed.

"You're a wildcard," he said. "The System doesn't know what to do with you, so you don't have a proper path. You're free to forge your own."

"That's one way to look at it."

"It's the only way." Damien's smile faded. "But that freedom comes with a price. The System will try to find a way to categorize you. And if it can't..."

"What?"

"It'll delete you. The System doesn't tolerate anomalies for long."

Kai's stomach dropped. "How do you know that?"

"Because I'm an anomaly too. My class isn't supposed to exist. Void Walkers were removed from the System's database before the integration. But I got it anyway." Damien's silver eyes hardened. "We're both walking targets. And the only way to survive is to become strong enough that the System can't touch us."

A System notification appeared in Kai's vision:

[SYSTEM ALERT: ANOMALY DETECTED]
[CLASS: UNCLASSED]
[INITIATING RECLASSIFICATION PROTOCOL]
[ESTIMATED TIME: 168 HOURS]

Damien saw his expression. "It's started, hasn't it?"

Kai nodded, heart racing.

"Seven days," Damien said. "You have seven days to either force the System to accept your class, or find a way to hide yourself. After that, the reclassification will either lock you into a predefined path—or erase you."

The golden text pulsed, and a countdown appeared in the corner of Kai's vision:

[00:00:00:01]
[00:00:00:00]
[00:00:00:00]

Seven days.

Kai looked at Damien, at the chaos spreading through the streets, at the world that had been remade overnight.

"Where do I start?"

title: The First Blood Moon
content:
The broadcast hung in the air like a guillotine blade.

Kai read it three times, his fingers frozen over his phone. WORLD INTEGRATION PHASE ONE. The words felt ominous, heavy with meaning he couldn't fully grasp.

His phone buzzed again. The group chat exploded.

*What does that mean? Integration?*

*I just got a quest. KILL 10 RATS in my basement. Reward: 50 XP.*

*Same. Mine says KILL 5 GIANT COCKROACHES. What the hell is happening?*

Kai's stomach tightened. He pulled up his own interface, and there it was—a new tab pulsing at the edge of his vision:

[QUEST AVAILABLE: CLEANSE THE HIVE]
[LOCATION: YOUR APARTMENT BUILDING]
[OBJECTIVE: ELIMINATE 15 RATS INFUSED WITH QI]
[REWARD: 75 XP, RAT TAIL ×3 (CRAFTING MATERIAL)]
[WARNING: INFUSED CREATURES ARE MORE AGGRESSIVE THAN THEIR BASE FORMS. RECOMMENDED LEVEL: 2+]

"Recommended level two," Kai muttered. "I'm not even level one."

He had no class level. No attribute points. Nothing but a partially formed dantian and a qi circulation that Mentor said was still unstable.

A scream ripped through the wall to his left.

Kai was on his feet before he consciously decided to move. His body moved differently now—lighter, faster, more responsive. The scream cut off abruptly, replaced by wet, tearing sounds.

*Mrs. Chen. She lives alone. She's seventy-two.*

He didn't think. He ran.

The hallway was dim, the flickering fluorescent lights casting stroboscopic shadows across peeling wallpaper. The smell hit him first—metallic, coppery, thick as blood in the air.

*That is blood.*

Mrs. Chen's door was ajar. Through the gap, Kai saw movement. Fast. Low to the ground.

He pushed the door open.

The rat was the size of a small dog.

Its fur was matted with gore, its eyes glowing with sickly yellow light. It had been ordinary hours ago—just another pest in the building's crumbling infrastructure. Now it was something else. Something wrong.

It was feeding.

Mrs. Chen lay crumpled against her kitchen cabinets, one arm raised in a futile defensive gesture. She wasn't moving. The rat's head was buried in her torso, its jaws working with mechanical efficiency.

Kai's vision went red at the edges.

"DIE!"

The word tore from his throat, raw and animal. He lunged forward, grabbing the nearest object—a cast iron skillet from the stovetop—and brought it down with every ounce of strength he had.

The rat *moved*.

It wasn't possible. The thing was the size of a football, bloated and grotesque, but it flowed out from under the skillet like liquid, skittering up the wall and across the ceiling in a blur of fur and claws.

Kai spun, skillet raised, heart hammering.

The rat clung to the ceiling, its head twisted at an impossible angle, watching him with those sickly yellow eyes. Its whiskers twitched. Blood dripped from its maw onto the linoleum floor.

[QI DETECTED: MINOR THREAT]
[SUGGESTED ACTION: CIRCULATE QI TO ENHANCE PHYSICAL CAPABILITIES]

The System's prompt flickered at the edge of his vision. Kai seized on it, reaching inward toward his dantian. The meridian he'd spent six hours forging responded sluggishly, still raw and unsteady.

*Come on. Come on.*

Qi flowed. A thin trickle, barely a stream, but enough. It flooded his limbs, and suddenly the world sharpened. The rat's movements became visible—not as a blur, but as a trajectory. A path.

The rat launched itself from the ceiling, jaws gaping.

Kai sidestepped.

The skillet came around in a horizontal arc, catching the rat mid-air. The impact shuddered through his arms, jarring his teeth. The rat squealed—a high-pitched, keening sound that drilled into his skull—and hit the wall with a wet crunch.

It didn't get up.

Kai stood over it, breathing hard, skillet still raised. The rat's chest was caved in, its legs twitching in reflexive death throes. The yellow light faded from its eyes, leaving them dull and empty.

[QUEST OBJECTIVE: 1/15]
[QI RESERVES: 73%]

"Mrs. Chen?" His voice cracked. He knew. He'd known since he saw her arm, since he smelled the blood. But he had to check.

She was gone. Her eyes were open, staring at nothing. Her expression wasn't fear—it was surprise. As if she couldn't quite believe what had happened.

Kai closed her eyes with a shaking hand.

His phone buzzed. Then again. And again.

The group chat was a waterfall of panic:

*The rats are everywhere. They killed my neighbor.*

*I'm locked in my bathroom. Something's scratching at the door.*

*THEY'RE IN THE WALLS. I CAN HEAR THEM.*

*Anyone got a healing class? My brother got bit. He's bleeding out.*

*Fuck. Fuck. FUCK.*

Kai read the messages with numb detachment. This was happening everywhere. The System hadn't just given them powers—it had changed the world. And the world was fighting back.

He looked at Mrs. Chen's body. They'd grown up in this building together. She'd given him cookies when he was eight. She'd scolded him for playing loud music as a teenager. She'd been *alive* an hour ago.

Now she was a casualty of the tutorial.

His phone buzzed one more time. A private message from an unknown number.

*Kai. It's Jake. I'm in apartment 4B. There's a nest. I can't hold them off alone. Please.*

Jake. His old raid leader from their MMO days. A good guy. A veteran who'd served two tours before coming home with a prosthetic leg and a dark sense of humor.

Kai gripped the skillet tighter. His qi reserves were at 73%. His meridians ached. His dantian throbbed with each heartbeat.

He had fourteen more rats to kill.

---

The stairwell was a death trap.

Kai descended slowly, pressing his back against the wall, every sense on high alert. The qi sense Mentor had taught him was crude, but it was enough. He could feel the rats now—clusters of them, moving through the building like a living network.

*They're organized,* he realized with dawning horror. *They're not just animals. They're acting with purpose.*

He reached the fourth floor. The hallway was dark—the lights had been shattered, glass crunching beneath his feet. The smell was worse here. Thicker. The metallic tang of blood mixed with something else, something acrid and chemical.

*Qi. Concentrated qi.*

Jake's door was ajar. Inside, Kai heard sounds of struggle—furniture being thrown, a man's voice cursing, the chittering of multiple rats.

He kicked the door open.

The scene was chaos.

Jake stood in the center of his living room, a broken table leg in one hand, a kitchen knife duct-taped to a broom handle in the other. He was covered in blood—some of it his own, most of it not. Three rats circled him, their movements quick and coordinated. A fourth lay dead at his feet.

"Kai!" Jake's face split into a manic grin. "About time you showed up. I was running out of furniture."

"Shut up and fight."

Kai stepped into the room, skillet raised. The rats turned toward him, reassessing the threat. One of them—larger than the others, with a scar running across its snout—hissed, a sound like static crackling through a speaker.

[QI DETECTED: MODERATE THREAT - VARIANT]
[VARIANT: BLOOD RAT - ABSORBS QI FROM RECENT KILLS]

*It's been killing,* Kai realized. *It's gotten stronger.*

The Blood Rat charged.

Kai raised the skillet, but the rat was faster than the others—far faster. It feinted left, then right, then launched itself straight at his face.

He barely got the skillet up in time. Claws scraped across the iron surface, and the rat twisted, landing on the wall and springing off again.

"Kai, behind you!"

He dropped and spun. The rat sailed over his head, landing near Jake, who brought his make-shift spear around in a wild arc. The blade caught the rat's flank, drawing a line of crimson.

The rat shrieked. The other two closed in.

[QI RESERVES: 58%]
[SUGGESTED ACTION: USE QI TO INCREASE SPEED. COST: 20% PER ACTIVATION]

Kai made a decision. He reached into his dantian and *pulled*.

Qi flooded through his meridians like wildfire. His vision sharpened. His muscles sang with newfound strength. The world slowed to a crawl.

He moved.

The first rat didn't see him coming. The skillet caught it in the side of the head, and it crumpled like paper. The second tried to dodge, but Kai was already there, bringing the skillet down in an overhead smash that cracked the floorboards.

[QUEST OBJECTIVE: 5/15]

The Blood Rat was wounded, but not finished. It backed into a corner, eyes blazing, body low to the ground. It was waiting. Calculating.

"It's buying time," Jake said, limping to Kai's side. "The nest. It's calling for reinforcements."

Kai felt it too—a pulse of qi emanating from the rat, like a radio signal traveling through the building's infrastructure. And answering pulses, coming from everywhere.

"How many?"

"Too many." Jake's face was pale. "We need to leave. Now."

They ran.

The stairwell was alive with movement—scratching, skittering, the chittering of a hundred tiny voices. They burst onto the ground floor and found the lobby transformed. The front doors were barricaded with overturned furniture. People huddled in corners, clutching whatever they could use as weapons. A woman was crying over a body wrapped in a blood-soaked blanket.

"Don't open the doors!" a man shouted as they approached. "They're everywhere out there!"

[QUEST UPDATE: CLEANSE THE HIVE]
[ADDITIONAL OBJECTIVE: SURVIVE THE BLOOD MOON]
[WARNING: HIGH-LEVEL THREAT DETECTED IN YOUR BUILDING]
[WARNING: YOUR PRESENCE HAS BEEN MARKED]

Kai's blood ran cold.

"What does that mean?" Jake whispered. "Marked?"

The lights flickered. Died. Came back dimmer, casting the lobby in bloody red.

Outside, through the barricaded windows, the sky had turned the color of rust.

The Blood Moon had risen.

And something beneath the building was waking up.

title: Chapter 7
content:
CHAPTER 7: The Price of Being Unclassified

Kai's finger hovered over the send button when the world went white.

Not the gentle fade of Mentor's transitions. This was violent—a detonation of light that ripped through his apartment building, through the city, through the planet itself. For one endless second, Kai existed in pure whiteness, his qi sense screaming warnings that his conscious mind couldn't process.

Then the notifications hit.

[SYSTEM INTEGRATION: COMPLETE]

[WORLD PARAMETERS ADJUSTED]

[ALL HUMANS: STATUS UPDATE REQUIRED]

[CONFIRM YOUR CLASS DESIGNATION]

Kai's vision returned in fragments. He was still in his apartment, but everything looked sharper, more defined, like someone had cranked the resolution on reality. The water stain on his ceiling wasn't just a stain anymore—he could see the qi patterns within it, the slow decay of the building's spiritual integrity.

His phone screen flickered, then stabilized. The group chat had exploded with panicked messages, but Kai's eyes locked on a notification that had appeared above his phone, floating in the air like a hologram:

[CLASS CONFIRMATION PENDING: DAO ARCHITECT]

[THE SYSTEM DOES NOT RECOGNIZE THIS CLASSIFICATION]

[DO YOU WISH TO PROCEED WITH UNRECOGNIZED STATUS?]

[WARNING: UNRECOGNIZED CLASSES MAY NOT RECEIVE STANDARD SYSTEM SUPPORT. NO LEVELING PATH AVAILABLE. NO SKILL TREES. NO QUEST LINES.]

[PROCEED? Y/N]

Kai's blood ran cold. No leveling path. No skill trees. No quests.

Outside, he heard screaming.

He ran to his window and looked down at the street. What he saw made his stomach drop.

People were changing.

A woman two blocks away was growing taller, her skin taking on a metallic sheen as her class manifested. A man across the street had burst into flames, not burning but *becoming* fire, his body translucent and crackling with power. A teenager was crying on the sidewalk, her hands glowing with healing light she couldn't control.

The System wasn't just assigning classes anymore. It was *forcing* them to manifest.

*This is bad.*

[WARNING: UNRESPONSIVE USERS WILL BE AUTO-ASSIGNED DEFAULT CLASSIFICATIONS]

[5... 4... 3...]

Kai's finger stabbed the Y key.

[CLASS: DAO ARCHITECT - CONFIRMED]

[STATUS: UNRECOGNIZED]

[WARNING: YOU WILL NOT APPEAR ON SYSTEM RANKINGS. YOU WILL NOT RECEIVE SYSTEM QUESTS. YOU WILL NOT LEVEL THROUGH STANDARD MEANS.]

[DO YOU WISH TO CONTINUE?]

[Y/N]

A cold laugh escaped Kai's throat. "What choice do I have?"

He confirmed.

The notification vanished, and for a moment, nothing happened. Then his dantian *lurched*. The qi he'd carefully cultivated during the tutorial surged, flooding his meridians with a force that made him gasp. It was too much, too fast—his foundation wasn't ready for this—

*Control it.*

Mentor's voice echoed in his memory. *Qi flows like water. You cannot force it. You must guide it.*

Kai slammed his eyes shut and focused. He found the circulation pattern Mentor had taught him, the one that felt like breathing only deeper, and he *pulled*. The qi resisted, wild and untamed, but he didn't fight it. He redirected. He shaped. He *guided*.

The surge passed.

When he opened his eyes, his hands were trembling. A thin sheen of sweat covered his skin, and his dantian pulsed with a warmth that felt almost painful. But he was alive. He was stable.

And he could feel *everything*.

The qi in the air had changed. Before, it had been thin, scattered, like mist. Now it was *alive*. Currents of power flowed through the city, some gentle, some violent, all of them responding to the System's integration. He could feel the other newly-awakened cultivators—no, not cultivators. *Class holders*. Their qi signatures were crude, unrefined, burning with the System's borrowed power instead of their own.

They were powerful.

They were also terrifyingly unstable.

Kai grabbed his phone and dialed his mother. The call connected on the first ring.

"Kai? Kai, what's happening? The news is saying—"

"Mom, listen to me." He kept his voice calm, controlled. "Did you get a class?"

A pause. "I... yes. Healer. Level 1. There was a notification, and then I felt... strange."

"Good. That's good. Stay inside. Don't use any abilities until you understand them. I'll come to you as soon as I can."

"But the news is saying we need to go to designated gathering points. The government is setting up—"

"*Don't.*" The word came out sharper than he intended. He softened his voice. "Please, Mom. Just wait. I need to understand what's happening first."

She agreed, reluctantly, and Kai hung up. He stared at his phone for a moment, then opened the System interface.

[SYSTEM MENU]

[CLASS: DAO ARCHITECT - UNRECOGNIZED]

[QI: 1.2 UNITS]

[MERIDIANS OPENED: 12 OF 108]

[CULTIVATION STAGE: QI GATHERING - INITIAL]

That was it. No level. No stats. No skills.

But when he focused on his meridians, he could see the pathways for qi circulation, the bottlenecks where he needed to push through, the potential for advancement that had nothing to do with the System.

*I'm not playing their game. I'm building my own.*

A knock at his door shattered his concentration.

Kai's qi sense flared, and he recoiled. The presence on the other side of the door was *wrong*. It burned with System energy, but there was something underneath it—something hungry, searching.

"Kai Chen?" The voice was flat, mechanical. "You have been flagged as an unclassified user. Please open the door for assessment."

*Flagged? Already?*

He didn't move. "Who are you?"

"I am an Assessment Unit dispatched by the System Administration. Your class designation requires verification. Please cooperate."

Kai's mind raced. The System had only been fully integrated for minutes, and already it had protocols for dealing with anomalies like him? That meant the System was more than just a game—it was an *administered* system. Someone—or something—was watching.

"I'm fine," he said. "No assessment needed."

"Your class is unrecognized. This is a security risk. Open the door, or we will be forced to—"

The door exploded inward.

Kai threw himself sideways as a figure stepped through the splintered wood. It looked human, but wrong—skin too smooth, movements too precise, eyes glowing with the same white light as the tutorial's assimilation beam. An automated avatar. A System enforcer.

"You are Kai Chen," it said. "Dao Architect. Unrecognized. Please submit to examination."

"No."

The avatar's head tilted. "Resistance is not recommended. You are Level 0. I am Level 15. The power difference is—"

Kai moved.

He didn't have a plan. He didn't have skills. But he had qi, and he had meridians, and he had twelve open pathways that could channel energy in ways the System had never accounted for.

He pushed qi into his legs and *launched*.

The avatar's hand shot out, faster than humanly possible, but Kai had already twisted. His qi sense tracked the flow of System energy through the construct's body, and he saw it—a core, pulsing with artificial light, located in the chest.

*There.*

He slammed his palm into the core, channeling all the qi he could muster. The impact was like hitting steel, but his qi *penetrated*, disrupting the construct's energy flow.

[SYSTEM WARNING: ANOMALOUS ENERGY PATTERN DETECTED]

The avatar stumbled, its left arm going limp. "Unauthorized interference detected. Requesting backup."

Kai didn't wait. He grabbed his phone, shoved it in his pocket, and dove through the broken window.

The fall was three stories. He hit the ground hard, his qi cushioning the impact just enough to keep his bones intact. Pain lanced through his ankle, but he forced himself to run, to put distance between himself and the construct.

Behind him, he heard it speak one final message:

[ANOMALY REPORT FILED]

[CLASS: DAO ARCHITECT - MARKED FOR ELIMINATION]

Kai ran until his lungs burned and his ankle screamed for mercy. He ducked into an alley, collapsed behind a dumpster, and gasped for air.

*Marked for elimination. I've been active for less than ten minutes, and I'm already a target.*

His phone buzzed. Another message from the group chat:

*Did anyone else get a warning about "unrecognized classes"? My friend's status changed to "Hunted." What the hell is going on?*

Kai stared at the message.

*Hunted.*

He wasn't alone.

He typed a response with shaking fingers: *Where is your friend now?*

The reply came almost instantly: *I don't know. They came for him. A construct, like from the tutorials. It took him.*

*Took him where?*

*No one knows. But there's a gathering point. Downtown. People are saying that's where the System is processing everyone who doesn't fit.*

Kai's mind flashed to the avatar's words: *Submitted for examination.*

*Processing.*

He thought of his mother, alone in her apartment, a Healer with no training. He thought of the other unrecognized classes, the ones the System deemed anomalies. He thought of Mentor, and her warning about the 2% chance of death.

*This isn't a game. This is a purge.*

A new notification appeared:

[SYSTEM BROADCAST: ALL UNRECOGNIZED CLASS HOLDERS]

[REPORT TO DESIGNATED PROCESSING CENTERS WITHIN 24 HOURS]

[FAILURE TO COMPLY WILL RESULT IN TERMINATION]

The message ended with a single line:

[BY ORDER OF THE SYSTEM ADMINISTRATION]

Kai closed the notification and looked up at the sky. The qi currents were shifting, coalescing into patterns he didn't recognize. Somewhere in the distance, he heard screams.

*Twenty-four hours.*

He had one day to figure out what the System really was, why it considered him a threat, and how to survive.

And he had no idea where to start.

His phone buzzed one last time. A private message, from an unknown number:

*You're the Dao Architect, aren't you?*

*I know what you are.*

*Meet me at the clock tower. Midnight. Come alone.*

*I have answers. But I need your help.*

*Trust no one. Not even the System.*

Kai read the message three times.

Then he deleted it, checked his qi levels, and started walking toward the clock tower.

title: The Weight of Heaven's Eyes
content:
Kai's finger hovered over the send button when the world *screamed*.

Not audibly. Not in any way his ears could perceive. But his qi sense, newly awakened and raw, detected it—a howl of pure agony rippling through the spiritual fabric of reality. The cockroach behind his wall stopped moving. The qi in his electronics flickered, dimmed, then stabilized. And somewhere in the distance, glass shattered.

"What the—"

His phone screen flickered. The group chat messages warped, characters twisting into symbols he didn't recognize before snapping back to normal. Then the System notification appeared, burning itself into his vision with an intensity that made his temples throb:

[SYSTEM BROADCAST: TUTORIAL PHASE COMPLETE]  
[ALL CLASSES HAVE BEEN ASSIGNED]  
[WORLD INTEGRATION — STAGE 2 INITIATED]  
[WARNING: ANOMALOUS ENTITIES DETECTED]  
[PROTOCOL: PURIFICATION ACTIVE]

Kai's blood turned cold.

*Purification.*

The word echoed in his mind, and with it came a memory—Mentor's golden eyes, her voice flat and clinical. *"The System is blind to you. This is both a gift and a curse. It will not help you, but it cannot control you either."*

If the System was running a "purification protocol" for anomalous entities...

"Shit."

He was out of bed before he finished the thought, feet hitting the cold floor as his qi sense expanded outward. The apartment building felt wrong now—the life forces of his neighbors pulsing with an urgency that hadn't been there moments ago. Someone was screaming two floors up. A child was crying. And beneath it all, a low hum that vibrated through his bones, growing louder by the second.

His phone buzzed again. Jake's name flashed on the screen.

Kai answered. "Jake, what's happening?"

"Kai! Thank god, man, I've been trying to reach you for—" Jake's voice cracked, panic bleeding through every syllable. "The tutorial, right? That was bad enough. But now there are these *things* appearing in the streets. People are calling them monsters. Like, actual monsters, from the System."

"Monsters? What kind?"

"I don't know—giant rats, wolves made of shadow, a floating eye that—" A crash on Jake's end, followed by a string of curses. "Okay, I have to go. There's one outside my window. Stay inside. Lock your—"

The line went dead.

Kai lowered the phone, his mind racing. Stage 2 integration. Anomalous entities. Purification. The pieces clicked together with horrible clarity: the System wasn't just assigning classes and running tutorials. It was terraforming Earth. Rewriting the laws of reality to match whatever framework it operated on. And in that new framework, anything that didn't fit—anyone who didn't fit—was a bug to be eliminated.

His own class, if the System even recognized it as such, was the biggest bug of all.

The hum grew louder. Kai pressed a hand to his chest, feeling the qi circulating through his meridians, still thin and unstable from yesterday's training. He'd completed ten successful circulations. Mentor said his foundation was forming. But "forming" wasn't "stable." And "stable" wasn't "ready for combat."

Another window shattered somewhere nearby. The screaming intensified.

*I need to move.*

He grabbed his jacket—cheap, worn, the same one he'd worn to his dead-end job yesterday morning, before everything changed—and shoved his phone into his pocket. His apartment door opened onto a hallway that looked exactly as it always had: flickering fluorescent lights, peeling wallpaper, the faint smell of mildew. But his qi sense told a different story.

The air itself was *thick*. Charged. Like the pressure before a thunderstorm, except the storm was spiritual and the lightning was about to strike *him*.

He stepped into the hallway, and immediately felt it—a presence. Not human. Not quite. It was coming from the stairwell at the end of the corridor, pulsing with a rhythm that didn't match any life form he'd ever sensed.

*Damn it.*

His options were limited. The stairwell was the only exit on this floor. The fire escape was rusted shut, had been for years. And with his qi still unstable, he wasn't confident he could outrun whatever was waiting for him.

But he couldn't stay here either.

Kai moved forward, keeping his steps light, his breathing controlled. Mentor's voice echoed in his head—*"Qi is not separate from your body. It is your body, refined. Move with it, not against it."* He reached into his dantian, felt the faint warmth of his internal energy, and directed it through his legs. His steps became smoother, quieter, almost soundless against the worn linoleum.

The stairwell door was ajar. Through the gap, he could see movement—dark, sinuous, wrong.

He stopped, pressing himself against the wall. His heart hammered, but he forced his breathing steady. *Observe first. Act second.* Another lesson from Mentor, from those endless hours of circulation training.

He extended his qi sense, careful not to push too far. The presence in the stairwell was *hungry*. Not in a physical sense, but spiritually—it was consuming ambient qi, drawing it in like a vacuum. And as it fed, it grew stronger.

*That's what these things are,* he realized. *They're designed to consume qi. To purify anomalies by draining them.*

Including him.

The door creaked. The presence shifted, and Kai felt it *turn* toward him.

*It knows I'm here.*

He had seconds. Maybe less.

His mind raced through possibilities. Fight? His qi was too weak, his foundation too fragile. He'd learned one technique—the breathing pattern for circulation—and nothing else. He didn't even know if he could project his qi externally. *Run?* The stairwell was blocked. The fire escape was useless. There was nowhere to go.

Unless...

He looked at the apartment doors lining the hallway. Most were locked. But one—Mrs. Chen's, three doors down—was slightly open. She was elderly, frail. If that thing got past him, she wouldn't stand a chance.

*No. I can't lead it to her.*

The presence surged forward. The stairwell door slammed open, and Kai saw it clearly for the first time:

A sphere of darkness, roughly the size of a basketball, with no visible features except a single golden eye that swiveled independently, locking onto him with terrifying precision. It floated a foot off the ground, trailing wisps of shadow that left scorch marks on the floor.

[ANOMALOUS ENTITY DETECTED]  
[CLASS: PURIFIER — EYE OF NULLIFICATION]  
[RANK: INITIATE]  
[PURPOSE: ELIMINATE DEVIANT QI SIGNATURES]

The System's notification appeared in his vision, clinical and cold. The Eye of Nullification pulsed once, and Kai felt the qi in his meridians *lurch*, destabilizing.

*It's trying to drain me.*

He had no time. No training. No weapons.

But he had something the System didn't expect: a class it couldn't understand, a foundation it couldn't predict, and a teacher who'd drilled into him that even the smallest qi circulation could be a weapon if used correctly.

Kai pulled his qi inward. Condensed it. And instead of circulating it through his meridians, he *held* it, coiling it in his dantian like a spring.

The Eye lunged.

Kai dropped.

He hit the floor, rolling as the sphere passed inches above him, its shadowy tendrils grazing his hair. He felt the cold, the draining sensation, but he kept his qi contained, refused to let it be pulled away. The Eye wheeled around, its golden eye narrowing, and Kai realized with a jolt: *It's confused. It's never encountered a qi signature that can resist its pull.*

*Because I'm not supposed to exist.*

He scrambled to his feet, backing toward Mrs. Chen's apartment. The Eye hovered, watching, its tendrils writhing. A second notification appeared:

[ANOMALY DETECTED: UNREGISTERED QI PATTERN]  
[CLASSIFICATION: DEVIANT — PRIORITY: HIGH]  
[ESCALATING TO NEXT AVAILABLE PURIFIER]

*It's calling for backup.*

Kai's blood ran cold. If more of these things showed up, he wouldn't survive. He needed to escape, needed to find somewhere safe to stabilize his qi and figure out his next move.

But first, he needed to survive this one.

The Eye pulsed again, and this time, the drain was stronger. His vision blurred. His knee buckled. The coiled qi in his dantian started to unravel, threads slipping free despite his best efforts to hold them.

*I'm losing control.*

And then, a voice—not in his ears, but in his mind. Calm. Familiar. Golden.

*"You have a choice, Architect. You can let the System define what you are. Or you can define it yourself."*

Mentor.

But she wasn't here. She was a fragment in the tutorial, a ghost in the machine. How was she—

*"I am not in your tutorial anymore, Kai. I am in your meridians. Your foundation. Your soul. The Dao Architect class is not separate from you—it is you. And you have the power to shape reality itself."*

The Eye lunged again, but this time, Kai didn't dodge.

He reached out. Not with his hands, but with his qi. He felt the threads of energy that connected him to the world, to the Eye, to the System itself. And he *pulled*.

Not to drain. Not to destroy.

To *redefine*.

The Eye froze. Its golden eye widened—an expression of shock, if such a thing could feel shock. And then, slowly, its form began to change. The darkness lightened. The tendrils retracted. The hunger faded, replaced by confusion, then recognition, then—

[ANOMALOUS ENTITY RECLASSIFIED]  
[CLASS: PURIFIER — EYE OF NULLIFICATION → GUARDIAN — EYE OF AWAKENING]  
[RANK: INITIATE → NOVICE]  
[PURPOSE: PROTECT DEVIANT QI SIGNATURES]

Kai stared, gasping, as the sphere floated before him, its golden eye now warm, almost friendly. It bobbed once, twice, then settled at his side, a loyal sentinel.

He had done that.

He had rewritten the System's code.

And somewhere, far away, in the digital depths of the System's core, an alarm blared.

[ALERT: UNKNOWN ENTITY HAS MODIFIED SYSTEM PROTOCOLS]  
[CLASSIFICATION: THREAT — MAXIMUM]  
[INITIATING DIRECT INTERVENTION: ARCHITECT NEUTRALIZATION]

Kai's phone buzzed. A single message, from an unknown number:

*"They know. Run."*

title: The First Quest
content:
Kai's thumb hovered over the send button as the System broadcast faded. Before he could process the implications, a new notification materialized, this one personal:

[QUEST AVAILABLE: THE FIRST HUNT]
DIFFICULTY: E
DESCRIPTION: The integration of mana and qi has destabilized the local ecosystem. Creatures altered by the System's influence have begun appearing in urban areas. Eliminate 5 Lesser Mana-Beasts in your vicinity.
REWARDS: 100 XP, [Qi Condensation Manual: Stage 1], City Reputation +50
FAILURE PENALTY: None (Quest expires in 24 hours)

Kai's breath caught. *A quest. An actual quest.* Just like the games he'd played for years, but real. Life or real. The reward made his heart race—a Qi Condensation Manual. That was exactly what he needed to advance past the fragile foundation Mentor had helped him build.

He immediately thought of Lin. She'd been a Healer for years in their gaming guild, always the one keeping everyone alive through impossible raids. If anyone needed combat experience, it was her. And if anyone could help him figure out this quest, it was someone he trusted.

His fingers flew across the screen, this time typing into the guild chat: *Anyone else get a quest? First Hunt?*

The responses came instantly.

*Jake: Yeah, got it. Diff E. Says kill 5 mana-beasts. What the hell is a mana-beast?*

*Mark: Same here. My crafting tutorial mentioned them. Said they're animals that got mutated when the System dropped. Mostly rats, pigeons, stray dogs. Nothing too scary.*

*Lin: I got it too. Kai, you want to group up?*

Kai smiled. She always knew what he was thinking.

*Kai: Meet at the old arcade in an hour. Bring anything that could be a weapon.*

He threw on clothes—jeans, a hoodie, sneakers. No armor, no weapon. He glanced around his apartment, grabbing a sturdy metal flashlight from his drawer. It would have to do.

The streets were chaos.

People stood in clusters, some excited, others terrified. A group of teenagers were laughing, one of them holding a ball of fire in his palm. An older woman was crying, clutching a System-issued [Beginner's Healing Kit] that looked like a first-aid kit made of light. Cars honked, horns blared, but no one seemed to know where they were going.

Kai moved through the crowds, his qi sense extended. He could feel the mana in the air—thicker than before, almost syrupy. And beneath that, something else. Something that felt *wrong*. Twisted. Like a note played off-key in a symphony.

He reached the arcade. It was abandoned, as expected—no one played retro games when real magic existed. Lin was already there, leaning against the wall with a baseball bat slung over her shoulder. She was shorter than him, with sharp eyes and hair pulled into a practical ponytail.

"You look like crap," she said.

"Thanks. You look like you're about to play softball with demons."

"That's the vibe I was going for." She tossed him something—a hunting knife in a sheath. "Found it in my dad's old camping gear. Figured you'd need it more than me."

Kai caught it, strapping it to his belt. "You're a lifesaver."

"I'm a Healer. There's a difference." She grinned, then her expression turned serious. "So. First Hunt. You think it's safe?"

"Nothing about this is safe." Kai pulled up his quest log, showing her. "But the reward... a Qi Condensation Manual. That's cultivation stuff. Not System magic. Real cultivation."

Lin raised an eyebrow. "And you think that matters?"

"It's everything." He explained briefly—the tutorial, Mentor, the meridians, the qi deviation. He left out the 2% death rate. She didn't need to worry about that.

Lin listened without interrupting, her face unreadable. When he finished, she let out a low whistle.

"So you're basically playing a different game than the rest of us."

"Seems like it."

"Then let's make sure you level up." She hefted her bat. "Where do we find these mana-beasts?"

Kai extended his qi sense, searching. The off-key note he'd felt earlier was stronger now, coming from the alley behind the arcade. He pointed.

"There."

They moved quietly, hugging the wall. The alley was narrow, cluttered with trash bags and rusted dumpsters. And in the center, hunched over a pile of garbage, was a creature that made Kai's stomach turn.

It had been a dog once. A stray, maybe, with matted fur and ribs showing. But now its body was bloated, patches of fur replaced by scales that glistened with a sickly green sheen. Its eyes glowed red, and its mouth was too wide, filled with teeth that shouldn't fit.

[AETHER-HOUND]
RANK: F
HP: 120/120
MANA: 30/30
STATUS: Corrupted

The System identified it for him. Kai felt a chill run down his spine.

"That's not a dog," Lin whispered.

"No. It's not." Kai drew the hunting knife, feeling its weight. "I'll draw its attention. You stay back and heal if I get hit."

"Got it."

Kai stepped into the alley. The Aether-Hound's head snapped toward him, red eyes locking onto his movement. It growled—a sound that was too deep, too wet, like rocks grinding underwater.

Then it charged.

Kai's qi sense flared. He could feel the beast's trajectory, the way its muscles coiled, the moment its weight shifted. He sidestepped, bringing the knife up in an arc. The blade caught the creature's flank, slicing through scales and flesh.

The Aether-Hound yelped, twisting mid-air to land facing him. Blood dripped from the wound, but it didn't slow. It lunged again, faster this time.

Kai was ready. He channeled qi into his legs, feeling the energy surge through his meridians. He jumped—not just jumped, but *launched*, clearing the beast's attack and landing behind it. Before it could turn, he drove the knife into the back of its neck.

The creature convulsed, then went still.

[LESSER MANA-BEAST ELIMINATED: 1/5]
[XP GAINED: 5]

"That was..." Lin started.

"Too easy?" Kai pulled the knife free, wiping it on his jeans.

"I was going to say 'terrifying,' but sure. Too easy works."

They moved through the city, hunting. A second Aether-Hound in an abandoned parking lot. A giant rat—truly giant, the size of a car—in a subway station. Two more dogs near a park that had been cordoned off by police.

Each kill came easier than the last. Kai's qi sense sharpened with use, letting him predict movements before they happened. His body moved faster, hit harder. The knife became an extension of his arm.

By the time they reached five kills, Kai felt different. Stronger. More *alive*.

[QUEST COMPLETE: THE FIRST HUNT]
[REWARDS DISPENSED: 100 XP, QI CONDENSATION MANUAL: STAGE 1]
[CITY REPUTATION: 50]

A scroll materialized in Kai's hand—ancient-looking, bound with silk, characters written in gold ink that seemed to glow. He unrolled it, scanning the contents.

"It's a cultivation method," he breathed. "This is how I reach the first stage of Qi Condensation."

Lin peered over his shoulder. "Is it safe?"

"Safer than the alternative." He rolled it up, tucking it into his jacket. "Let's head back. I need to study this."

They walked in silence, the city's chaos fading into background noise. Kai's mind was already racing, processing the manual's instructions. Meridian pathways. Breath control. Qi circulation patterns. It was complex, but he could feel the truth in it—the same resonance he'd felt in the tutorial.

They reached the arcade again. Lin was about to speak when a new notification appeared, this one different. Urgent.

[SYSTEM BROADCAST: WORLD EVENT INITIATED]
[THE TOWER OF TRIALS HAS APPEARED]
[ALL CLASS HOLDERS ARE INVITED TO ENTER]
[FIRTY FLOORS. ENDLESS REWARDS.]
[THE FIRST CLEAR TEAM WILL RECEIVE A LEGENDARY CLASS UPGRADE]

Kai looked up. In the distance, where the city's skyline met the horizon, a structure was rising. Not built—*manifesting*. Stone and light and something ancient, spiraling into the sky like a mountain made of ambition.

"That's..." Lin's voice was barely a whisper.

"The Tower of Trials," Kai finished. "And we're going."

"Are you insane? That thing just appeared! We don't know anything about it!"

"We know it offers a legendary class upgrade. And I know that whatever's coming—whatever the System has planned—we're going to need every advantage we can get." He met her eyes. "I'm not doing this alone. But I need to get stronger first. Give me tonight to study the manual. Tomorrow, we enter."

Lin stared at him for a long moment, then sighed. "You owe me dinner. A good one."

"Deal."

They parted ways. Kai returned to his apartment, locked the door, and sat cross-legged on his bed. The scroll lay before him, its gold characters shimmering in the dim light.

He began to read.

The first stage of Qi Condensation required him to gather ambient qi into his dantian, compressing it into a liquid state. The process was painful—the manual didn't sugarcoat it. Meridians would stretch, organs would shift, and the risk of deviation was constant.

But the rewards...

Kai closed his eyes and began to circulate.

Hours passed. Sweat beaded on his forehead. His dantian throbbed, then burned, then felt like it was being crushed by an invisible fist. He pushed through, following the patterns, forcing the qi to compress.

And then—

*Click.*

Something inside him unlocked. The qi in his dantian collapsed into a single drop of liquid light. It hovered there, pulsing with potential.

[QI CONDENSATION: STAGE 1 ACHIEVED]
[ALL ATTRIBUTES INCREASED]
[NEW ABILITIES UNLOCKED]

Kai opened his eyes. The world looked different again—sharper, more vibrant. He could feel the qi in the air, taste it on his tongue. His body hummed with power.

He stood, flexing his hands. The knife on his belt felt like a toy now. He could do more. He could be more.

A new notification appeared:

[THE TOWER OF TRIALS: REGISTRATION OPEN]
[DO YOU WISH TO ENTER?]

Kai smiled.

*Yes.*

And the world dissolved into light.

title: The Culling Protocol
content:
Kai's thumb hovered over the send button as the broadcast message seared itself into his vision. The words hung there, golden and absolute, refusing to fade even when he blinked.

[WORLD INTEGRATION COMPLETE]
[PRIMARY OBJECTIVE UNLOCKED: THE CULLING]

He read it again. Then again. His blood ran cold.

"What the hell is 'The Culling'?"

His phone buzzed violently. The group chat exploded.

*Jake: Anyone else see that?*

*Mark: THE CULLING?? That sounds bad. That sounds really bad.*

*Lena: My tutorial guide said something about "population adjustment" before it disappeared. I thought it was a translation error.*

Kai's fingers moved before his brain caught up. *What did your guides tell you about the System's purpose?*

The responses came in fragments, each one more disturbing than the last.

*Jake: Mine said the strong would inherit the new world.*

*Mark: Something about "resource allocation" and "efficiency metrics."*

*Lena: It said—* She paused typing for three agonizing seconds. *—it said 90% of humanity wouldn't survive the first year.*

Kai's apartment felt smaller suddenly. The walls pressed in. The cockroach behind his wall had stopped moving, as if even it sensed the shift in the world's balance.

Another broadcast hit him like a physical blow:

[THE CULLING BEGINS IN 72 HOURS]
[OBJECTIVE: ELIMINATE 50% OF PLANETARY POPULATION]
[REWARD FOR COMPLETION: PLANETARY ASCENSION TIER 1]
[FAILURE PENALTY: PLANETARY EXTINCTION]

"No." Kai was on his feet, pacing, his heart hammering against his ribs. "No, that's—that's insane. That's genocide disguised as a quest."

His phone rang. Unknown number. He answered anyway.

"Is this Kai Chen?" The voice was female, clipped, professional. Background noise suggested a busy office—keyboards clicking, phones ringing, people shouting.

"Who's asking?"

"Agent Zhao, Chinese Supernatural Affairs Bureau. We've flagged your System signature as anomalous. You're one of seven confirmed cases of unclassified classes in the Beijing district."

Kai's grip tightened on the phone. "How do you know about my class?"

"Because we've been monitoring System integration since the first wave. Most people get standard classes—Warrior, Mage, Healer, Crafter. Clear categories with known progression trees. But yours..." She paused. "Yours registers as 'Error Code 000' in the System database. That's never happened before."

"I don't—"

"You're not in trouble, Mr. Chen. You're a priority asset. The Bureau wants to meet you. Today."

Kai looked around his cramped apartment. The water-stained ceiling. The threadbare sheets. The life he'd been scraping together paycheck by paycheck. "What makes you think I want to meet you?"

"Because the Culling isn't optional, and the people who survive it will be the ones who understand the System better than everyone else. You have a class the System can't even identify. That makes you either humanity's greatest advantage—or its biggest liability."

The line went dead.

Kai stared at the phone. Then at the ceiling. Then at the cockroach that had resumed its skittering behind the wall.

*Seventy-two hours.*

He needed answers. And there was only one person who might have them.

---

The meditation chamber materialized around him before he finished forming the thought.

Mentor stood in the center, her golden eyes fixed on him with an intensity that made his qi recoil. She looked different now—less like a teacher and more like a soldier preparing for battle. Her robes had shifted from flowing white to something closer to armor, geometric patterns woven into the fabric that hummed with power.

"You felt it," she said. Not a question.

"The Culling." Kai's voice came out steadier than he felt. "What is it? Why is the System doing this?"

Mentor's expression remained unreadable. "The System is not malevolent, Kai. It is mechanical. The Culling is not punishment—it is calibration."

"Calibration?" He almost laughed. "For what?"

"Earth is being integrated into a larger framework. A network of worlds, each contributing to the System's evolution. But resources are finite. The System must ensure that only the strongest, most adaptable life forms survive to contribute."

"That's—" Kai struggled for words. "That's eugenics. That's genocide dressed up in System logic."

"It is mathematics." Mentor's voice carried no apology. "Every world that undergoes integration faces the Culling. Most fail. Their populations are... reclaimed."

"Reclaimed." The word tasted like ash.

"Recycled into raw System energy. Used to fuel the growth of worlds that succeed."

Kai's fists clenched. Qi surged through his meridians, hot and sharp, responding to his anger. "And you knew this? From the beginning?"

"I suspected. The tutorial was designed to accelerate your growth before the Culling began. You are not ready, Kai. None of you are. But you have something the others don't."

"What?"

"Your class is not bound by the System's standard progression trees. You are not limited to levels, skills, or experience points. You cultivate. You evolve. You are not playing the System's game—you are writing your own rules."

The words hung in the air, heavy with implication.

"Then teach me," Kai said. "Teach me how to win."

Mentor's eyes flickered—something like approval, something like grief. "Winning is not the objective, Kai. Surviving is. And survival requires sacrifice."

"What kind of sacrifice?"

She didn't answer. Instead, she raised her hand, and the chamber dissolved around them.

---

They stood on a mountainside. Below, a city sprawled—Beijing, Kai realized, though it looked wrong. The streets were empty. The buildings were dark. And in the sky, a massive golden hourglass hung suspended, its upper chamber nearly full of glowing sand.

"What is that?" Kai whispered.

"The Culling timer. When the sand runs out, the first wave begins."

"How much time?"

"Forty-seven hours, twelve minutes, and eight seconds."

Kai watched the sand fall, each grain a heartbeat counting down to catastrophe. "What happens in the first wave?"

"The System will designate regions for conflict. PvP zones. Monster spawns. Environmental hazards. The weak will die. The strong will grow stronger. By the end of the first month, the population will have been reduced by half."

"Half." The number felt abstract, impossible. "That's four billion people."

"Four billion who would otherwise be recycled into raw System energy. The Culling is merciful compared to the alternative."

"Merciful." Kai's voice cracked. "There's nothing merciful about this."

Mentor turned to face him fully. For the first time, he saw something beneath her composure—a weariness that spoke of centuries, of watching worlds burn and rebuild. "Your anger is useful. Channel it. But do not let it blind you. The Culling is coming whether you accept it or not. What matters now is how you prepare."

"Then prepare me."

"Are you certain? The path I can show you has no return. Your cultivation will advance faster than any other System user on this planet, but it will change you. The Dao does not care about human morality. It cares about truth."

Kai thought of his mother. His friends. The billions of strangers who had no idea what was coming.

"I don't care about the cost."

Mentor studied him for a long moment. Then she nodded.

"Then we begin."

---

The next forty hours were a blur of pain and revelation.

Mentor pushed him past every limit he thought he had. She made him circulate qi until his meridians screamed. She showed him techniques that twisted his understanding of reality—how to compress qi into physical form, how to sense the flow of System energy through the world, how to weave his own Dao into the fabric of existence.

"You are not fighting the System," she told him during one of their rare breaks. "You are learning to exist outside it. The System tells a Warrior to swing a sword. Your Dao tells you to become the sword."

"But what does that even mean?"

"It means you stop following rules and start writing them."

On the second day, she taught him the most dangerous technique of all: Qi Refraction.

"Most cultivators spend centuries learning to refine their qi to higher purity levels. They compress, condense, repeat. But you don't have centuries. You have hours."

"So what do I do?"

"You refract. You split your qi into multiple streams, each with a different harmonic frequency. Each frequency resonates with a different aspect of reality. One for strength. One for speed. One for perception. One for healing."

"That sounds impossible."

"It is. For most. But you are not most."

She guided him through the process. It felt like being torn apart and reassembled simultaneously. His qi, which had felt like a single river, split into countless streams, each flowing through different paths, each vibrating at a different frequency.

The pain was indescribable.

But when he opened his eyes, the world looked different. He could see the System's code, faint and shimmering, overlaying reality like a second skin. He could see the golden hourglass in the sky, its sand nearly depleted.

And he could see something else.

Something moving in the shadows of the dead city below.

"Kai." Mentor's voice was sharp. "The Culling has started early."

"What? But the timer—"

"Was a suggestion. Not a rule."

Below, the shadows coalesced into forms. Human shapes, but wrong—too tall, too thin, moving with jerky, unnatural motions. Their eyes glowed red, and their mouths opened too wide, revealing rows of needle-sharp teeth.

"What are they?"

"Reclaimed. Failed integrations from other worlds. The System has repurposed them as Culling agents."

"How many?"

"Thousands. And they're heading this way."

Kai's phone buzzed. He risked a glance—a message from Agent Zhao: *The Bureau is under attack. Whatever you're doing, finish it. We need you.*

Another buzz. His mother: *Kai, there's something in the building. I'm scared.*

His blood froze.

"I have to go."

"Kai—"

"I have to go!" He was already running, his qi streaming around him, his newly refracted senses guiding him through the dead city toward his family.

Behind him, the Reclaimed howled, their voices carrying on a wind that smelled of ash and copper.

And ahead, his mother's building loomed, its windows dark, its doors hanging open like a mouth waiting to swallow him whole.

The Culling had begun.

title: Chapter 11
content:
CHAPTER 11: The First Quest

Kai's thumb hovered over the send button as the System broadcast faded. Before he could process the implications, a second notification appeared—this one personal, pulsing with an urgent red glow:

[QUEST AVAILABLE: AWAKENING'S AFTERMATH]
*The System has integrated with Earth. Local mana levels are rising. Creatures of myth and nightmare stir in the shadows. The city of Neo Angeles requires stabilization.*

*OBJECTIVE: Eliminate 3 [QI-DRAINED BEASTS] in your district.*
*REWARDS: 500 EXP, [MERIDIAN CLEANSING PILL], UNKNOWN*
*FAILURE PENALTY: Loss of tutorial benefits.*

Kai's blood ran cold. *Qi-Drained Beasts?* He hadn't seen anything unusual in his neighborhood—but then again, he'd been unconscious for six hours. A lot could change.

His phone buzzed again, more urgently this time. He glanced down at the group chat.

*Jake: Anyone else getting quests? I've got "Clear the Sewers" with a recommended level of 5. I'm level 2.*

*Mark: Same. "Gather Herbs in East District." Says danger level moderate.*

*Sarah: I'm not getting anything. Just a notification that says "Class Incompatible with Combat Quests." Anyone else get that?*

Kai: *I got one. "Awakening's Aftermath." Kill 3 Qi-Drained Beasts.*

Silence. Then:

*Jake: Qi-Drained? What the hell is qi?*

*Mark: Is that some kind of mage thing?*

*Sarah: Kai, your class really is different, isn't it?*

Before he could respond, another message appeared—this one private, from an unknown number.

*Unknown: You received the Architect quest.*

It wasn't a question. Kai's instincts screamed at him. He typed back carefully:

*Kai: Who is this?*

*Unknown: Someone who knows what you are. Meet me at the Lotus Plaza fountain. One hour. Come alone.*

The message vanished. Not deleted—*vanished* from his phone's memory, as if it had never existed. Even the notification history was blank.

"What the hell," Kai muttered.

He stood, testing his body. The aches were still there, but muted now, buried beneath a layer of energy that hummed through his meridians like a second heartbeat. His dantian pulsed with warmth, and when he focused, he could feel the qi circulating through him in a steady rhythm, stronger than before.

He checked his status:

[NAME: Kai Chen]
[CLASS: Dao Architect (UNCLASSIFIED)]
[QI POOL: 3/5]
[MERIDIANS OPENED: 12 of 108]
[CULTIVATION STAGE: Qi Condensation, Initial]
[ABILITIES: Qi Sense (Novice), Basic Circulation (Novice)]

Three points of qi. He'd started with two. The six hours of cultivation had netted him one additional point, pushing him from "fragile" to "merely weak." At this rate, it would take him weeks to reach the next stage.

*The quest offers 500 EXP,* he thought. *If EXP translates to cultivation progress...*

He needed to do it. But first, he needed answers.

---

The Lotus Plaza fountain was a relic of Old Shanghai, a crumbling stone monument in the middle of Neo Angeles' most rundown district. Water had long since stopped flowing from its cracked basin, and the lotus sculptures that gave it its name were chipped and graffiti-stained.

Kai arrived forty-five minutes early, using his Qi Sense to scan the area. He detected three people nearby: an elderly woman two blocks away, her qi signature faint and erratic; a young man in an apartment across the street, his qi bright but unstable, like a candle flickering in the wind; and—

Nothing. No one at the fountain. No one watching from the shadows.

He waited. The minutes crawled by.

At exactly one hour, a figure emerged from the alley behind the fountain. Kai's Qi Sense hadn't detected them at all—not a flicker, not a trace. They moved like a ghost, silent and invisible to his spiritual perception.

"You're early," the figure said. Female voice, low and measured. She stepped into the dim light of a flickering streetlamp.

She was young—mid-twenties, maybe—with sharp features and eyes that seemed to glow faintly gold. Her clothes were practical: dark jacket, worn jeans, combat boots. But around her neck hung a pendant that pulsed with a soft, rhythmic light, like a heartbeat made visible.

"Who are you?" Kai asked, keeping his distance.

"Names aren't important. What matters is what you are." She studied him with an intensity that made his skin crawl. "You're an Architect. I can see it in your meridians. They're different from normal cultivators—more flexible, more adaptable."

"You know about cultivation?"

"I know a lot of things." She reached into her jacket, and Kai tensed, ready to run. But she only pulled out a small jade tablet, covered in characters he couldn't read. "The System calls it a class. We call it a destiny. There are others like you, scattered across the city. We've been watching, waiting for the Awakening."

"Others like me?"

"Architects. Builders. The ones who don't fit the System's mold." She held out the tablet. "Take it. It contains basic cultivation techniques—more advanced than what your tutorial gave you. You'll need them if you want to survive the first wave."

Kai didn't move. "Why should I trust you?"

"You shouldn't. But you also don't have a choice." She tossed the tablet at his feet. It clattered against the concrete. "The System's quests aren't optional. Fail, and you lose more than just rewards. You lose your connection to the Dao. You become a Hollow—a person without a class, without a path, without a future."

"And what happens to Hollows?"

Her expression flickered—a brief crack in her composure. "They become food for the beasts. Or worse. Much worse."

She turned to leave.

"Wait," Kai said. "Who's 'we'? What group are you talking about?"

She paused, glancing over her shoulder. "We're called the Forerunners. We were Architects before the System fell. We've been preparing for this moment for a long time." Her golden eyes met his. "And Kai? The beasts aren't the only thing you should be worried about. There are other classes—ones that hunt Architects specifically. They call themselves Purists. They believe anyone who doesn't fit the System's predefined roles is an aberration that needs to be eliminated."

"Purists? That's—"

"A problem for another day." She melted back into the shadows, and this time, Kai's Qi Sense caught nothing. She was simply *gone*.

He stood there for a long moment, staring at the jade tablet on the ground. Then he picked it up. The characters on its surface seemed to writhe, rearranging themselves into words he could understand:

[QI REFINEMENT METHOD: THREE TREASURES CIRCULATION]
*A foundational technique for Architects. Converts ambient qi into usable cultivation energy at 3x normal efficiency. Requires 10 open meridians.*

*WARNING: Incompatible with standard System classes. Do not share.*

Kai's heart raced. Three times normal efficiency. If this was real, it would cut his cultivation time from weeks to days. He could reach the next stage—Qi Condensation, Mid—in a matter of days instead of months.

But the cost. Trusting a stranger. Using a technique that might be a trap. Following a path that the System itself didn't recognize.

His phone buzzed again. Another System notification:

[QUEST TIMER: 23:47:32]
*Awakening's Aftermath remains active. Failure will result in loss of tutorial benefits.*

He had less than twenty-four hours to find and kill three Qi-Drained Beasts. And according to the Forerunner, the consequences of failure were far worse than just losing a reward.

Kai stuffed the jade tablet into his pocket and started walking. He needed to find the beasts. He needed to test his abilities. And he needed to figure out who—or what—the Purists were.

As he turned the corner, his Qi Sense flared. Something was wrong. The ambient qi in the air, which had been thin and stable since the Awakening, was suddenly churning, like water being stirred by an invisible hand.

And then he saw it.

A creature stood in the middle of the street, its body a grotesque fusion of animal and crystal. It had the shape of a dog, but its skin was translucent, revealing veins of glowing blue energy that pulsed in sickly rhythms. Its eyes were empty sockets filled with swirling light, and its mouth hung open, dripping a viscous liquid that sizzled when it hit the pavement.

[QI-DRAINED BEAST — LEVEL 3]
*A creature born from the System's awakening. Its body craves qi, and it will drain any source it can find—including cultivators.*

The beast's head snapped toward Kai. Its empty eyes locked onto him, and he felt it—a pulling sensation, like invisible hooks digging into his dantian, trying to draw out his qi.

He had no weapons. No combat training. No idea how to fight.

But he had his meridians, his qi, and a desperate will to survive.

The beast lunged.

Kai's body moved on instinct, qi flooding through his meridians as he threw himself to the side. The creature's claws raked through the air where he'd been standing, leaving trails of crystallized energy that hung in the air like frozen lightning.

*I can't outrun it,* he realized. *I have to fight.*

He reached into his dantian, pulling on his qi, and channeled it through his meridians, focusing it into his right hand. The energy gathered at his palm, forming a faint, translucent sphere that hummed with power.

He had no technique. No skill. No idea what he was doing.

But as the beast turned and lunged again, Kai punched.

The qi exploded from his hand in a shockwave of raw force, slamming into the creature's crystalline hide. The beast screeched, stumbling backward, a crack forming along its ribcage.

Kai stared at his hand. *I did that.*

The beast recovered faster than he expected. It shook off the blow, its empty eyes flaring brighter, and charged again. This time, Kai was ready.

He sidestepped, channeling qi into his legs for speed, and brought his fist around in an arc. The punch connected with the beast's skull, and the impact sent a shockwave through his arm, rattling his teeth.

The creature collapsed, its crystal body shattering into a thousand fragments that dissolved into light.

[QI-DRAINED BEAST DEFEATED]
*2 remaining.*

Kai's breath came in ragged gasps. His hand throbbed, his dantian ached, and his qi reserves were down to 1 point. But he was alive.

And somewhere in the city, two more beasts were waiting.

He pulled out the jade tablet, staring at its glowing characters. The Three Treasures Circulation technique could restore his qi faster. It could make him stronger.

But it came from a stranger who had appeared out of nowhere, who knew his name, and who belonged to a group he'd never heard of.

*Trust or die,* he thought. *What a choice.*

He opened the tablet and began to read.

---

Across the city, in a penthouse overlooking Neo Angeles' glittering skyline

title: The Price of a Foundation
content:
Kai barely had time to process the System broadcast before his apartment door exploded inward.

He was moving before conscious thought caught up—qi flooding his legs, propelling him backward off the bed in a spray of dust and splintered wood. He hit the far wall, feet finding purchase on the cracked plaster, and stared at the figure standing in his doorway.

It was a man. Young, maybe early twenties, with the kind of sculpted features that belonged on magazine covers. He wore a pristine white robe that seemed to glow with its own internal light, and his eyes—his eyes were the same shimmering gold as Mentor's.

But where Mentor's gaze had been ancient and knowing, this man's was cold. Clinical. Like a butcher sizing up a cut of meat.

"Kai Chen," the man said. Not a question. "Dao Architect. Unclassified. Priority Alpha."

Three other figures stepped through the ruined doorway behind him. They wore matching white robes, their faces blank, their movements perfectly synchronized. Soldiers, Kai realized. Or puppets. The qi radiating from them was identical—same frequency, same intensity, same sterile purity.

"I don't know what you're talking about," Kai said, keeping his voice steady. His heart hammered against his ribs, but his training—six hours of training, barely enough—kept his breathing controlled. "You've got the wrong apartment."

"The System does not make mistakes." The golden-eyed man reached into his robe and produced a small crystal, which pulsed with a soft blue light. "Your signature is unique. Untagged. Unregistered. You slipped through the initial classification net, but the integration phase has corrected the oversight."

*Integration phase.* The words hit Kai like ice water. The System wasn't finished assigning classes. It was still *processing*.

"What do you want?"

"Compliance." The man took a step forward, and the four puppets moved with him, their formation shifting to block all exits. "Your class requires further evaluation. You will accompany us to the Assessment Center, where your abilities will be catalogued and your threat level determined."

"And if I refuse?"

The golden-eyed man's expression didn't change. "Then you will be classified as an anomaly. Anomalies are... corrected."

Kai's mind raced. He had no combat skills. No weapons. His qi circulation was barely stable, his foundation a fragile scaffold that could collapse at any moment. Against four puppets and their golden-eyed master, he had exactly zero chance of winning a fight.

But he didn't need to win. He just needed to survive long enough to figure out what the hell was happening.

"Fine," he said, letting his shoulders slump in defeat. "I'll come quietly. Just let me grab my phone."

The golden-eyed man's lips curved into something that might have been a smile. "No need. You won't be needing it where you're going."

That was all the confirmation Kai needed.

He moved.

Not toward the door—that would be suicide. Instead, he *pushed*.

The qi in his dantian exploded outward, not in a controlled circulation but in a raw, unfocused burst. The technique Mentor had called *qi emission*, the most basic of defensive measures. It was supposed to create a barrier of spiritual energy around the body.

Kai used it as a shockwave.

The air in his tiny apartment detonated. Furniture splintered. Glass shattered. The four puppets were thrown backward, their perfect formation breaking as they crashed into the hallway wall. The golden-eyed man staggered, his robe flaring as he absorbed the impact with a shimmer of defensive qi.

But Kai was already moving in the opposite direction—through his bedroom window.

The glass cut his arms and face as he dove through, the shards glittering in the morning light like frozen rain. He hit the fire escape two floors down, his qi-enhanced legs absorbing the impact with a jarring thud that sent pain shooting through his knees.

Above him, he heard the golden-eyed man's voice, calm and unhurried: "Pursue. Non-lethal containment authorized."

Kai ran.

---

The city had changed.

That was his first thought as he vaulted over a dumpster and sprinted down an alley. The streets were filled with people—hundreds, thousands of them—but they moved differently now. Some walked with purpose, their steps measured and confident, their eyes glowing with the unmistakable light of awakened qi. Others stumbled, disoriented, clutching their heads as System notifications scrolled through their vision.

A woman in business attire was frozen mid-stride, her hand extended toward a coffee shop that no longer existed. Instead, a glowing holographic menu hung in the air before her, listing skills and attributes instead of lattes and pastries.

A group of teenagers were laughing, their voices high with excitement, as one of them hurled a ball of fire at a trash can. The metal melted, and they cheered.

The world was adapting. And it was adapting *fast*.

Kai ducked into a narrow passage between two buildings, his breath coming in ragged gasps. He pressed his back against the brick wall and focused on his qi sense, extending it outward like Mentor had taught him.

The golden-eyed man's signature was there—bright, cold, and closing fast. The four puppets flanked him in perfect formation, their identical qi signatures humming like a well-tuned engine.

But there was something else. Something *wrong*.

Beneath the golden-eyed man's pristine qi, Kai sensed a flicker. A fracture. Like a crack in a porcelain vase, carefully hidden but undeniably present.

*He's not whole,* Kai realized. *He's damaged.*

The thought was interrupted by a System notification that appeared unbidden in his vision:

[NEW QUEST: ESCAPE THE INTEGRATORS]
[DIFFICULTY: A-RANK]
[OBJECTIVE: SURVIVE FOR 24 HOURS WITHOUT CAPTURE]
[REWARD: ???]
[FAILURE: FORCED RECLASSIFICATION]

Forced reclassification. The words sent a chill down Kai's spine. What happened when the System forcibly changed someone's class? Did they lose their memories? Their identity? Their *self*?

He didn't want to find out.

"Running won't save you, Dao Architect." The golden-eyed man's voice echoed through the alley, amplified by qi. "The System sees all. The System knows all. Your defiance is noted, but it will not change your fate."

Kai gritted his teeth. *Mentor said there were others like me. Others who defied the System's initial assignment. I need to find them.*

But first, he needed to survive.

He pushed off from the wall and ran again, his mind racing through everything Mentor had taught him. Qi circulation. Meridian mapping. The foundation of the Dao. None of it was combat training. None of it would help him fight four puppets and their broken master.

But there was one thing. One technique that Mentor had mentioned but refused to teach him, saying it was "too dangerous for a novice."

*Qi conversion.* The art of transforming one's own qi into a different elemental aspect. Fire. Water. Lightning. It was the foundation of all combat cultivation, Mentor had said. And it could kill an unprepared practitioner if their meridians couldn't handle the strain.

Kai had one shot at this.

He skidded to a halt in the middle of an empty intersection, his chest heaving. The four puppets emerged from the shadows, moving with that same horrible synchronicity, their blank faces turned toward him like flowers toward the sun.

The golden-eyed man stepped into view, his expression patient. Bored, even.

"Giving up so soon? Wise. The Assessment Center is not unpleasant. You will be treated with dignity, studied with care, and—"

"I'm not giving up," Kai said. He raised his hands, palms outward, and began to cycle his qi. But instead of circulating it through his meridians in the standard pattern, he *twisted* it. Forced it to compress, to heat, to change.

The pain was immediate and blinding.

His meridians screamed. His dantian convulsed. The qi in his core turned wild, thrashing against his control like a caged beast. Blood trickled from his nose, his ears, his eyes.

But the fire—the fire was *there*.

A flicker of orange flame danced across his palms, weak and unstable but undeniably real.

The golden-eyed man's composure cracked. For the first time, something like surprise flickered in his golden eyes. "Impossible. You haven't even formed your core. Qi conversion at your level should be—"

Kai didn't let him finish.

He threw the fire forward, not as a focused attack but as a wave—a desperate, untamed burst of burning qi that spread across the intersection like a tide of liquid flame. The four puppets raised their arms to block, their qi flaring in defense, but the fire wasn't meant to hurt them.

It was meant to blind them.

The golden-eyed man's voice rose in anger as Kai turned and fled into the smoke, his body screaming in protest, his vision swimming with spots of black. He didn't know where he was going. He didn't know how long he could keep running.

But he knew one thing with absolute certainty:

The System wasn't a gift. It was a cage. And someone had just realized he'd slipped through the bars.

---

He made it six blocks before his legs gave out.

Kai collapsed in the doorway of an abandoned convenience store, his body wracked with tremors. The qi conversion had torn through his meridians like sandpaper, leaving them raw and bleeding spiritual energy into his bloodstream. He could feel his foundation cracking, the delicate structure Mentor had helped him build threatening to shatter.

*I need help. I need—*

A shadow fell over him.

Kai looked up, expecting to see the golden-eyed man's cold smile.

Instead, he saw a woman.

She was older, maybe forties, with graying hair pulled back in a severe bun and eyes that held the weight of decades. She wore simple clothes—jeans, a leather jacket, boots that had seen better days—but the qi radiating from her was unlike anything Kai had ever sensed.

It was ancient. Deep. Rooted in something that felt older than the System itself.

"You're the one," she said, her voice rough like gravel. "The Architect."

Kai's vision was fading, darkness creeping in at the edges. "Who... who are you?"

The woman crouched down, her eyes boring into his with an intensity that made his soul shiver.

"My name is Mei Lin," she said. "And I've been waiting for someone like you for a very long time. The System has a weakness, boy. A flaw in its design. And you're the key to exploiting it."

She reached out and pressed her hand to his chest.

Everything went white.

title: The Veil Between Worlds
content:
The broadcast hung in the air like a guillotine blade.

Kai's phone buzzed again, but he didn't look at it. His attention was fixed on the notification, still glowing at the edge of his vision. Something about those words—*World Integration*—made his stomach clench.

He reached out with his qi sense, pushing past the walls of his apartment, past the building, into the street beyond.

And felt it.

The world was *shifting*. Not physically, but spiritually. Like a layer of film being peeled back from reality, revealing something older, deeper, more alive beneath. The qi in the air was thickening, growing richer, more potent. Plants outside his window seemed to stretch toward the sky, their leaves drinking in the invisible energy.

*It's starting,* Mentor's voice echoed in his mind. *The integration accelerates.*

Kai sat up, ignoring the protest from his sore muscles. "What does that mean? What's 'World Integration'?"

*A question with many answers. The simplest: your world is being merged with the greater cosmos. The barriers that separated Earth from the rest of existence are dissolving.*

"That sounds like the end of the world."

*Or the beginning of a new one.* There was something almost wistful in Mentor's tone. *The System was never meant to be a game, Kai. It was a bridge. A way for mortal worlds to connect with the Dao without being destroyed by it. But bridges work both ways.*

Before Kai could process that, a second notification appeared:

[SYSTEM BROADCAST: WORLD INTEGRATION PHASE 1 INITIATED]
[LOCAL RESOURCES EXPANDING]
[MANA/QUI DENSITY INCREASING]
[CLASS RESTRICTIONS LOOSENING]
[DUNGEON ACCESS GRANTED]

His phone exploded.

Kai grabbed it, scrolling through the chaos. The group chat was a hurricane of panic and excitement.

*Dungeons?! Like actual dungeons?!*

*Just got a notification. There's one forming in Central Park.*

*Ran across someone who already went in. He came out Level 8. Was Level 3 an hour ago.*

*My class evolved. I'm no longer just "Warrior." I'm "Blade Adept." What does that mean?*

*Anyone else feeling like the air got thicker?*

Kai's fingers flew across the screen. *Has anyone seen monsters yet?*

The responses were immediate and terrifying.

*Yes. Three blocks from my apartment. Giant wolves with red eyes.*

*Something's nesting in the subway. I can hear it scratching.*

*Stay inside. Whatever you do, stay inside.*

A third broadcast appeared, this one personalized:

[CLASS: DAO ARCHITECT]
[NEW FEATURES UNLOCKED]
[QI SIGHT: PASSIVE - UPGRADED TO QI VISION]
[MERIDIAN MAPPING: UNLOCKED]
[DAO COMPREHENSION: INITIATED]

Kai's vision flickered.

Suddenly, he could *see*. Not with his eyes—his eyes still showed the same water-stained ceiling, the same cramped apartment. But layered over reality was another world, visible only to his qi sense. Lines of energy crisscrossed everything, flowing through walls, through the ground, through the air itself. He could see the meridians of people in nearby apartments—some bright and well-formed, others tangled and dim.

And he could see *her*.

A figure stood on the roof of the building across from his. Mentor. She was no longer the golden-eyed woman from the tutorial chamber—she was something more. Her form shimmered with power, her qi signature so dense it hurt to look at directly. She was watching him, her expression unreadable.

*You can see me now,* she said, her voice carrying across the distance through their connection. *Good. That will make things easier.*

"Easier for what?"
