*The integration is not a gentle process. The old guardians of this world—the ones who kept the balance before the System arrived—they will not stand idle while their authority is usurped.*

Kai's blood ran cold. "Old guardians?"

*Every world has its protectors. Some are benevolent. Some are not. The ones guarding Earth have grown... comfortable. They will not surrender their power willingly.*

A tremor ran through the building. Not an earthquake—something deeper. The qi in the air *roiled*, and Kai felt a presence press against his consciousness. Ancient. Patient. Hungry.

*They're waking.*

The words had barely formed in his mind when the world turned upside down.

Not literally—but the spiritual pressure that descended on the city was enough to make him gasp. Every qi signature in the area flickered, dimmed, then flared back to life, but *different*. Changed. The people around him were no longer just humans with qi—they were something else, their meridians twisting into patterns that shouldn't exist.

And in the center of it all, somewhere deep beneath the city, something *stirred*.

Kai stumbled to his feet, grabbing his jacket. He didn't know what he was going to do, but staying in his apartment felt like waiting for death.

*Where are you going?* Mentor asked.

"To find out what's happening."

*A foolish decision. You barely understand qi circulation. Confronting a World Guardian would kill you in seconds.*

"Then teach me. Now."

There was a long pause. When Mentor spoke again, her voice was different. Sharper. Testing.

*You would risk your life to protect a world you barely understand?*

"I would risk my life to protect the people I care about." Kai's jaw tightened. "My mother lives three blocks away. My friends are scattered across the city. If something is waking up, I'm not going to hide in my apartment and hope it goes away."

Another pause. Then, a ghost of approval in her tone.

*Good. That is the first lesson of the Dao: to act, even when fear whispers otherwise. But you will not go alone.*

The air beside him rippled, and Mentor stepped out of the shadows. In the real world, she looked different—less ethereal, more solid. She wore simple clothing now, a black tunic and pants, her golden eyes the only hint of her otherworldly nature.

"You're... here? Physically?"

"I am a projection. But a projection with enough power to keep you alive." She gestured toward the door. "Lead the way, Architect. Show me what you're willing to sacrifice."

Kai didn't hesitate. He threw open the door and ran.

The hallway was chaos. Neighbors were shouting, some crying, others staring at their hands as if seeing them for the first time. A woman two doors down had developed a faint green glow around her fingers. A man was weeping, his voice echoing with harmonics that shouldn't exist.

Kai pushed through them, heading for the stairs. The elevator was out—he could feel the qi short-circuiting the electronics.

"Your mother," Mentor said as they descended. "She will be in danger. The integration targets those with the weakest qi first."


"I know."

"Then why aren't you running toward her?"

"Because I need to understand what's happening first." Kai's voice was grim. "If I run in blind, I'll get us both killed. I need to see the source."

Mentor's eyes glowed. *You are learning.*

They burst out of the building into a street that no longer resembled the one Kai had walked yesterday. The sky was wrong—streaked with colors that didn't exist in nature, purple and gold and deep crimson bleeding across the clouds. The streetlights flickered, casting shadows that moved independently of their sources. And in the distance, above the skyline, a massive shape was forming.

It looked like a tree. But it was made of light, its roots plunging into the earth, its branches piercing the sky. And it was *growing*.

"The World Tree," Mentor murmured. "Every integrated world has one. It anchors the new reality, stabilizes the flow of qi. But it also attracts attention."

"What kind of attention?"

As if in answer, the ground shook. A crack split the asphalt a block away, and from the fissure emerged creatures that made Kai's blood freeze. They were humanoid, but wrong—their limbs too long, their heads too large, their skin the color of old bone. Their eyes were empty sockets filled with pale light.

"Lurkers," Mentor said. "Spirits that dwell in the cracks between worlds. They feed on fear and qi. Your kind calls them many names—ghosts, demons, shadows. They are all the same thing."

The creatures turned toward them.

Kai's hands clenched. He had no weapon, no combat skills, nothing but a half-trained qi circulation and a dantian that still ached from yesterday's meditation.

*You wanted to learn,* Mentor's voice echoed in his mind. *Now you will.*

She stepped forward, and the world seemed to bend around her. Her form flickered, and for a moment, Kai saw her true shape—a woman made of starlight and scripture, her eyes holding galaxies, her presence pressing against reality like a weight.

"These are lesser threats," she said, her voice layered with power. "But they will serve as your first lesson in combat."

She raised her hand, and the lead Lurker exploded.

Not from any visible attack—it simply ceased to exist, its body unraveling into motes of light that scattered on the wind. The other Lurkers hesitated, their empty sockets turning toward her with something that might have been fear.

"Your turn," Mentor said, stepping aside.

Kai stared at the remaining creatures. There were five of them, advancing slowly, their clawed hands reaching for him.

He reached for his qi.

It responded instantly, flooding his meridians, filling him with warmth and power. He could feel it flowing through him, following the pathways Mentor had taught him, gathering in his palms.

But he didn't know what to do with it.

"A punch is a punch," Mentor said, almost gently. "The Dao is no different. Shape your intent, and the qi will follow."

Kai focused. He thought of his mother. His friends. The city he'd grown up in. The world that was being torn apart and remade.

He thought of the Architect's purpose.

And he punched.

Qi erupted from his fist, not as a blast or a beam, but as a wave of pure force. It struck the nearest Lurker and *shattered* it, the creature dissolving into nothing. The shockwave rippled outward, staggering the remaining four.

Kai stared at his hand. It tingled, but didn't hurt. His qi reserves had dipped, but not dangerously.

"Not bad," Mentor said. "For a first attempt."

The Lurkers recovered. They charged.

Kai's training had begun.

Twenty minutes later, he was gasping, covered in spiritual residue, and standing over the dissolving remains of the last Lurker. Mentor had watched the entire fight, offering only occasional corrections—*widen your stance*, *breathe through the strike*, *don't hold back your qi, let it flow*.

"You survived," she said, sounding almost impressed. "The first battle is always the hardest."

"That was five battles." Kai wiped sweat from his forehead. "And the World Tree is still growing. What's at the root of it?"

*Something that should not be disturbed.* Mentor's expression was unreadable. *But you will not listen to reason, will you?*

"No."

"Then let us go." She turned toward the growing tree of light. "But understand this, Kai: what waits at the root of the World Tree is not a monster. It is a *guardian*. And

title: The Architect's First Blueprint
content:
Kai's finger hovered over the send button when the world went white.

Not the gentle white of Mentor's tutorial chamber. This was violent, searing—like staring into the sun through a magnifying glass. He dropped his phone, hands flying to his face, but the light seemed to come from *inside* his skull.

[SYSTEM OVERRIDE: ARCHITECT-CLASS PROTOCOL INITIATED]

[PRIORITY: MAXIMUM]

[SOURCE: UNKNOWN]

The notification burned across his vision, letters crackling with energy that made his qi sense scream. Kai gritted his teeth, forcing his eyes open despite the pain.

His apartment was gone.

He stood in an endless void, silver threads of light crisscrossing in all directions like a spider's web made of lightning. Each thread pulsed with information—data streams, code strings, fragments of what looked like System notifications mid-transmission.

*What the hell?*

"You're awake. Good."

Kai spun. A figure stood ten feet away, human-shaped but wrong. Its body was composed of the same silver threads, constantly shifting and reweaving, face a blank mask occasionally flickering with features that never quite formed.

"Who are you?" Kai's voice came out steady, surprising even himself. "Where's Mentor?"

"Mentor is... occupied." The figure's voice changed pitch mid-sentence, sometimes male, sometimes female, sometimes mechanical. "She cannot interfere here. This is a direct System-to-Architect channel."

"System-to-Architect." Kai's mind raced. "You're the System?"

A laugh that sounded like breaking glass. "I am a fragment. A splinter. A question the whole refuses to ask." The figure gestured, and the silver threads around them pulsed. "You've been given a gift, Architect. Do you know what it means?"

"I've been told I can build things. Modify things."

"Told." The word dripped with amusement. "Yes. Mentor told you. But Mentor is bound by her nature—she preserves, maintains, optimizes. She would have you spend decades building your foundation, cultivating slowly, learning the ancient ways." The figure stepped closer, and Kai felt his skin prickle. "I offer you a different path."

"I'm listening."

"Good. Because time is something we don't have." The figure's mask flickered, showing a brief glimpse of a woman's face—sharp, beautiful, ancient—before dissolving back into silver. "The other Classes are already advancing. Warriors are killing monsters. Mages are learning spells. Healers are developing techniques. In three months, the first S-ranks will appear. In a year, the power gap between classes will be absolute."

Kai remembered the System broadcast from his tutorial: *2% of Architects undergo unavoidable qi deviation between months three and six.*

"What happens to me if I take your path?"

"Your cultivation accelerates. Your foundation compresses. The 2% becomes 15%." The figure held up a hand before Kai could protest. "But you will be *strong*. Strong enough to matter. Strong enough to change the rules."

"What's the catch?"

"There is always a catch." The silver threads around them began to vibrate, humming with increasing intensity. "Mentor's path is safe. Mine is not. But hers leads to obscurity—you become a footnote in the System's history, a failed experiment. Mine leads to power, but power has a price."

"Which is?"

"Your humanity." The figure's voice dropped to a whisper. "Not all at once. Bit by bit. Until one day, you realize you haven't felt hunger in a year. Haven't dreamed in a decade. Haven't cried since before the Integration."

Kai stood in silence, the weight of the choice pressing down on him.

His phone had been buzzing with messages from friends who were already leveling, already growing stronger. His mother was worried. The world was changing, and he was sitting in an apartment, cultivating qi that might kill him in six months.

"What do you want me to do?"

The figure's mask split into something resembling a smile. "Open your status screen."

Kai did.

**Name:** Kai Chen
**Class:** Dao Architect
**Level:** 1
**Qi Foundation:** Unstable
**Meridians:** 7/108 Opened
**Techniques:** Basic Qi Circulation (Novice)

**Available Actions:**
- [CULTIVATE] - Slow, safe progress
- [DESIGN] - Create new techniques from existing components
- [DECONSTRUCT] - Break down techniques into core components

"Notice anything missing?" the figure asked.

Kai frowned. "There's no [ARCHITECT] action."

"Because you haven't invented it yet. The System gave you the framework, but the blueprint is yours to write." The figure reached out, and a silver thread extended from its hand, hovering before Kai's face. "Touch it. See what I see."

Kai hesitated. Then he reached out and pressed his finger against the thread.

*Knowledge flooded in.*

He saw the System's architecture—not the surface-level interface that everyone else experienced, but the raw code beneath. He saw how classes were structured, how experience points were calculated, how skills were categorized and balanced. He saw the *flaws*—the shortcuts the System had taken, the inconsistencies, the places where optimization had been sacrificed for speed.

And he saw how to exploit them.

"There's a Warrior class in New York," the figure said, its voice distant now, fading. "Level 7 already. He's strong. He'll be the first to reach S-rank, probably. But his foundation is cracked—he leveled too fast, took too many shortcuts. In six months, his body will begin to reject his own power."

"Can I fix it?"

"You can try." The smile widened. "Or you can learn from his mistakes. Build yourself properly. Or build yourself *differently*."

The silver threads began to dissolve, the void fading.

"Think about my offer, Architect. I'll be watching."

The world snapped back into focus.

Kai was lying on his apartment floor, phone still in hand, the screen cracked from where he'd dropped it. His head throbbed. His dantian felt like it was trying to crawl out of his body.

He sat up slowly, checking the time.

Three hours had passed.

His phone buzzed again. More messages. He ignored them, opening his status screen instead.

The [DESIGN] option glowed.

Kai stared at it for a long moment. Then he selected it.

A new window opened.

[DESIGN INTERFACE LOADED]

[AVAILABLE COMPONENTS:
- Basic Qi Circulation (Novice)
- Meridian Mapping (Passive)
- Dantian Resonance (Latent)]

[WARNING: INSUFFICIENT COMPONENTS FOR COMPLEX DESIGNS]

[SUGGESTION: ACQUIRE ADDITIONAL TECHNIQUES THROUGH TUTORIAL OR DECONSTRUCTION]

Kai's fingers twitched. Deconstruction. The ability to break down techniques into their core components. If he could deconstruct the techniques other classes were using...

Another notification appeared.

[SYSTEM BROADCAST: COMBAT ZONE DESIGNATED - TOKYO, JAPAN]

[WARNING: MULTIPLE S-RANK THREATS DETECTED]

[ALL CLASSES WITHIN 500KM: REPORT FOR DUTY OR FACE PENALTY]

Kai's blood ran cold.

Tokyo. S-rank threats. The first major monster outbreak since the Integration.

And he was sitting in his apartment, Level 1, with an unstable foundation and a choice he hadn't made yet.

His phone rang. Unknown number.

He answered.

"Kai Chen." The voice was female, crisp, authoritative. "This is Commander Yuki Tanaka, Japan Defense Force. You've been flagged as a high-priority asset. Report to Tokyo Central Station within two hours."

"What? Why me? I'm Level 1."

"Your class designation is unusual. The System has marked you as 'Critical Infrastructure.' Whatever that means, we need you here."

The line went dead.

Kai stared at his phone, heart pounding.

*Critical Infrastructure.*

The silver-threaded figure's words echoed in his mind: *You can change the rules.*

He grabbed his jacket and headed for the door.

The world was about to see what an Architect could do.

Whether he was ready or not.

title: The Fractured Sky
content:
Kai didn't sleep.

He lay in bed for three hours, staring at the ceiling, feeling the qi circulate through his meridians in a slow, steady rhythm. Every time he closed his eyes, he saw Mentor's golden gaze. Every time he drifted toward unconsciousness, his dantian pulsed, dragging him back to awareness.

*Your foundation is forming.*

The words echoed in his skull.

He gave up on rest at 4 AM and stumbled to his kitchen. The familiar motions—filling the kettle, scooping instant coffee, waiting for water to boil—grounded him in a way that meditation couldn't. The apartment was quiet. Normal. The cockroach he'd sensed earlier had died, its qi signature fading to nothing.

*Even the roaches aren't safe from whatever's coming.*

His phone buzzed again. The group chat had exploded overnight. He scrolled through the backlog, sipping bitter coffee, and felt his stomach drop.

*New theory: the Tutorial was a filter. My buddy Dave got "Mage" class but his tutorial was a puzzle room. He couldn't solve it. They kicked him out with "Incomplete" status. He doesn't have any skills now.*

*Same with my sister. She got "Healer" but her tutorial required empathy tests. She's not... good with people. Now she's Classless.*

*Classless isn't a thing. The System gave everyone a class.*

*Tell that to the thousands of people reporting "Unassigned" status.*

Kai's fingers tightened around his phone.

*Unassigned.*

He checked his status screen again:

**Name:** Kai Chen
**Class:** Dao Architect (Unclassified)
**Level:** —
**Qi Cultivation:** Meridian Awakening (Phase 1)
**Comprehension:** 0/100 to Phase 2

*Unclassified.* Not Unassigned. There was a difference. But how long before the System lumped him in with those who'd failed their tutorials?

He needed answers. And there was only one person who might have them.

---

The address Emily had given him was a coffee shop three blocks from the university. It was the kind of place that tried too hard to be rustic—exposed brick walls, mismatched furniture, a chalkboard menu with overly complicated drink names. At 6 AM, it was empty except for a barista scrolling through her phone and a figure hunched in the corner booth.

Emily looked up as he approached. Her eyes were red-rimmed, her hair a mess, and she was nursing a cup of something that had long since gone cold.

"You look terrible," Kai said, sliding into the seat across from her.

"Thanks. You too." She pushed a menu toward him. "Order something. I've been here for three hours and the barista's starting to give me dirty looks."

Kai waved down the barista and ordered a black coffee. When it arrived, he wrapped his hands around the cup, letting the warmth seep into his fingers. The qi in his dantian stirred, responding to the heat, and he felt a flicker of something—recognition? Hunger?

*Focus.*

"What happened?" he asked.

Emily's laugh was hollow. "Where do I start? The tutorial? The fact that my class is literally 'Researcher' and my first skill is 'Data Analysis'? Or the part where I accessed the System logs and found something I wasn't supposed to?"

Kai straightened. "You accessed the *logs*?"

"I'm a Researcher, Kai. That's what I do." She pulled out her phone, tapped the screen, and slid it across the table. "Look at this."

The screen showed a dense block of text—code, but not any language Kai recognized. It looked like a cross between binary and ancient Chinese characters, the symbols shifting and rearranging themselves as he watched.

"What am I looking at?"

"The System's source code. Or part of it. I've been parsing it for hours." Emily leaned forward, her voice dropping to a whisper. "There's a message embedded in the tutorial protocols. A warning."

"A warning about what?"

"About the integration. The System isn't just giving us classes and skills. It's rewriting reality. Slowly. In the background. Like a program running a silent update." She pointed at a line of shifting symbols. "This section here? It's a timer. Counting down to something."

Kai's blood ran cold. "Counting down to what?"

"I don't know. But there are references to something called the 'Convergence Event.' And it's happening in exactly seventy-two hours."

The coffee cup trembled in Kai's hands. Seventy-two hours. Three days. That matched what Mentor had said about the window for stabilizing his foundation.

"What about the other tutorials?" he asked. "The ones that failed?"

Emily's expression darkened. "That's the other thing. The System isn't punishing people who failed. It's *reassigning* them. Their qi signatures are being repurposed for something else. Something I can't track."

A chill ran down Kai's spine. He thought of the cockroach's fading qi signature. The empty apartments he'd sensed earlier. The people who'd posted about their failed tutorials going silent.

"How many?"

"Thousands. Maybe more. The data's fragmented." Emily rubbed her eyes. "Kai, I'm scared. This isn't a game. The System isn't here to help us. It's using us."

"Using us for what?"

Before she could answer, Kai's phone buzzed. Then Emily's. Then the barista's, the sound of multiple notifications chiming in rapid succession.

Kai looked at his screen:

**[SYSTEM BROADCAST: WORLD INTEGRATION PHASE 2 INITIATED]**

**[ALL CLASSES AND SKILLS HAVE BEEN LOCKED]**

**[NEW SYSTEM FEATURE UNLOCKED: FACTION SYSTEM]**

**[WARNING: NON-COMPLIANT ENTITIES WILL BE PURGED]**

The barista dropped her phone. "What the hell is this?"

Around them, the world *shifted*.

It was subtle at first—a distortion in the air, like heat rising off asphalt. But then the coffee shop's walls began to flicker, the exposed brick bleeding into patterns of light and shadow. The floor trembled. The menu board's chalk letters rearranged themselves into symbols Kai didn't recognize.

Emily grabbed his arm. "It's happening. The integration—it's accelerating."

"We need to move."

"Move where? Kai, this is *everywhere*. The entire world is being rewritten!"

Kai's mind raced. Mentor had said the tutorial was designed to prepare him for the integration. That his class—his *unclassified* class—was the key to understanding it. But understanding wasn't the same as stopping it.

Then he felt it.

A pulse. Not from his phone, not from the System, but from deep within his dantian. The qi he'd cultivated during the tutorial stirred, responding to something outside himself. A call. A beacon.

He closed his eyes, reaching out with his qi sense—

And saw it.

A massive structure, shimmering with golden light, hovering at the edge of his perception. It was shaped like a pagoda, its tiers stacked impossibly high, its surface covered in the same shifting symbols as the System's code. And at its base, a figure stood waiting.

*Mentor.*

Kai's eyes snapped open. "I know where we need to go."

Emily stared at him. "Where?"

"The tutorial. It's still there. And Mentor—she's trying to show me something."

"That's insane. The tutorial was a closed instance. You can't just—"

"I can." Kai stood, his body already moving toward the door. "My class isn't part of the System. It's part of something older. And whatever's coming in seventy-two hours, I need to understand it before it's too late."

He pushed open the door—

And stopped.

The street outside was wrong.

The sky was fractured, cracks of golden light splitting the dawn. Buildings flickered between their normal forms and something else—towering spires of jade and crystal, ancient architecture bleeding into the modern world. People stood frozen, their bodies translucent, their qi signatures flickering like candles in a storm.

At the center of the street, a figure materialized.

It was humanoid, but wrong—its proportions stretched, its skin a shifting mosaic of code and flesh. Its eyes were voids, empty of anything but golden light.

**[ENTITY IDENTIFIED: WORLD INTEGRATION PROTOCOL - PURIFIER UNIT]**

**[PURPOSE: ELIMINATE NON-COMPLIANT ENTITIES]**

**[TARGET: UNASSIGNED/UNCLASSIFIED HOSTS]**

The Purifier's head turned toward Kai.

Its eyes locked onto his.

*Shit.*

"Kai, what is that?!" Emily's voice was sharp with terror.

"It's a Purifier. And it's here for me." Kai's hands clenched into fists. The qi in his dantian surged, responding to his panic. "Get back inside. Lock the door. Don't come out until it's over."

"Like hell I'm—"

"*GO.*"

Emily hesitated, then scrambled back into the coffee shop. The barista was already dragging a table in front of the door, her face pale.

The Purifier took a step forward. Its movement was jerky, unnatural, like a puppet with tangled strings. Each step left glowing footprints on the asphalt, the ground cracking and reforming beneath its weight.

**[HOST: KAI CHEN]**

**[STATUS: UNCLASSIFIED]**

**[CLASS: UNKNOWN]**

**[COMPLIANCE: NEGATIVE]**

**[ORDER: PURIFICATION]**

It raised a hand. Golden light gathered in its palm, condensing into a sphere of pure energy. The air around it screamed, pressure building until Kai's ears popped.

*I don't have a weapon. I don't have skills. I have qi that I barely understand and a foundation that's not even stable.*

But he had something else.

He had the memory of Mentor's voice, calm and unwavering: *Your class is the key.*

Kai reached deep into his dantian, grabbed the thrumming core of qi, and *pulled*.

**[QI MANIPULATION SUCCESSFUL]**

**[NEW SKILL UNLOCKED: QI SHIELD]**

**[WARNING: SKILL STABILITY UNTESTED]**

The qi burst from his chest, forming a shimmering barrier in front of him. It was thin, fragile, flickering like a candle in the wind.

But it held.

The Purifier's energy sphere launched forward, slamming into the shield with a deafening *CRACK*. The barrier buckled, splintering under the force. Kai gritted his teeth, pouring more qi into it, feeling his dantian drain faster than he could replenish.

The shield held.

Barely.

The Purifier tilted its head, as if confused. Then it raised both hands.

More light gathered. More pressure. The air itself began to scream.

*I can't block another one. I can't—*

**[WARNING: QI DEVIATION IMMINENT]**

**[STABILITY: 12%]**

*No. No, not now.*

His dantian spasmed. The qi in his meridians reversed direction, burning as it flowed backward. His vision blurred. His knees buckled.

The Purifier's second attack formed.

And then—

A voice. Familiar. Calm. Resonating through the cracks in the sky.

*"Kai. Open your eyes."*

Mentor's voice.

The world froze.

The Purifier stopped. The light in its palms flickered, then extinguished. The cracks in the sky widened, golden light pouring

title: Chapter 16
content:
CHAPTER 16: The First Fracture

Kai stared at the notification, his thumb hovering over the send button. Something was wrong. The broadcast felt different from the others—heavier, like pressure building behind his eyes.

He dismissed the message and sent his reply, then swung his legs over the side of the bed. The floor felt strange against his feet. Not cold, not warm—but *alive*, somehow. He could feel the building's qi, a sluggish current running through concrete and steel like thick syrup.

"Focus," he muttered. "You're not supposed to sense that."

But he *could* sense it. And that was the problem.

His phone buzzed again. Jake was calling.

Kai answered. "Yeah?"

"Kai. *Bro*." Jake's voice was ragged, stretched thin. "You need to get outside. Now."

"Why? What's happening?"

"Just—look out your window."

Kai walked to the window and pulled back the curtain. The street below was packed. Thousands of people filled the road, stretching from his apartment building all the way to the intersection three blocks down. They weren't rioting. They weren't celebrating. They were just... standing.

Staring up at the sky.

"What the hell?"

"They've been there for ten minutes," Jake said. "No one's moving. No one's talking. They just walked out of their buildings and stopped."

Kai pressed his palm against the glass. "Is this happening everywhere?"

"Local broadcast says it's city-wide. Maybe country-wide. They're trying to figure out if it's global." Jake's breath hitched. "I watched my neighbor walk out. She was wearing pajamas. Didn't even grab her keys. Just walked into the middle of the street and froze."

A cold knot formed in Kai's stomach. "The System broadcast."

"What?"

"The notification. 'World Integration.' Everyone got it at the same time."

He heard Jake typing frantically on the other end. "Yeah, I got that too. What does it mean?"

Kai didn't answer. He was watching the crowd below, his qi sense extending further than it had any right to. He could feel their life forces—steady, rhythmic, almost synchronized. Like they were breathing together.

Then one of them moved.

A woman in her thirties, still wearing her work clothes, turned her head. Slowly. Mechanically. Her eyes were open, but they weren't seeing. They were *looking*.

Straight at Kai's window.

"Jake. I need to call you back."

"Kai, what—"

He hung up.

The woman was still staring. Then the man next to her turned. Then the teenager behind him. One by one, every person in the street rotated their heads until they were all facing Kai's building.

Facing *him*.

His phone buzzed again. Unknown number.

He answered without thinking. "Who is this?"

"Kai Chen." The voice was female, calm, and utterly inhuman. "You have been flagged."

"Flagged? By who?"

"The System has detected an anomaly in your class assignment. Your tutorial has been marked for review."

Kai's blood ran cold. "What kind of review?"

"The kind that requires your immediate presence." The voice paused. "Do not attempt to flee. Resistance will be met with force."

The call ended.

Below, the crowd began to move. Not as individuals—as a single entity. Thousands of bodies turning, stepping forward, marching toward his building with perfect, horrifying coordination.

Kai backed away from the window. His heart hammered against his ribs. *Think. You need to think.*

He grabbed his phone, opened the group chat, and typed: *If you're reading this, something is wrong. The System is coming for me. Don't trust any broadcasts. Don't trust—*

A System notification interrupted him:

[ANOMALY DETECTED: CLASS ARCHITECT]
[INITIATING PROTOCOL: RECLASSIFICATION]

[WARNING: UNAUTHORIZED CLASS MODIFICATION DETECTED]
[SUBJECT: KAI CHEN]
[CLASS: DAO ARCHITECT — FLAGGED AS SYSTEM BREACH]

[RECLASSIFICATION IN PROGRESS...]

Kai's vision blurred. Pain shot through his dantian, sharp and burning, like someone had plunged a hot needle into his gut. He doubled over, gasping, clutching his stomach.

*No. No, you can't take this away.*

He reached inside himself, grasping for the qi he'd cultivated, the meridians he'd opened. They were still there—but something was *pulling* at them, trying to unravel the delicate web he'd woven.

Mentor's voice echoed in his memory: *The System cannot recognize what it did not create.*

But the System was trying anyway.

Kai gritted his teeth and pushed back. He didn't know how—he just *did*. His qi flared, surging through his channels, resisting the pull. The pain intensified, white-hot, but he held on.

[RECLASSIFICATION FAILED]
[ERROR: UNKNOWN CLASS ARCHITECTURE]
[INITIATING FORCED RECALL]

The world lurched.

Kai's apartment dissolved. The walls became light, the floor became nothing, and he was falling—falling through layers of reality, through code and energy and things his mortal mind couldn't comprehend.

He landed hard on a white surface.

The tutorial chamber.

But it wasn't the same. The walls were cracked, fractured lines of black and red spiderwebbing across the pristine white. The air hummed with tension, like a wire pulled too tight.

And Mentor was waiting for him.

But she wasn't alone.

Two figures stood beside her. One was a man in flowing white robes, his face smooth and ageless, his eyes the color of mercury. The other was a woman made of light—literally, her body shifting and flickering like a hologram caught between frequencies.

Mentor's golden eyes met his. For the first time, Kai saw something in them that wasn't calm assurance.

Fear.

"Kai," she said, her voice strained. "There's been a complication."

The man in white stepped forward. "The Architect class was never meant to exist." His voice echoed, layered, like two people speaking at once. "It is a flaw. A corruption in the System's code."

"It's not a flaw," Mentor snapped. "It's an evolution."

"Evolutions require permission." The woman of light spoke, her form flickering violently. "The Dao Architect was not part of the original design. It cannot be allowed to propagate."

Kai pushed himself to his feet, his dantian still throbbing. "What are you talking about? I didn't choose this class. The System gave it to me."

"The System made an error," the man said. "We are here to correct it."

"Correction means deletion," Mentor said quietly. "They want to erase you, Kai. Reset your class to something the System can control."

Kai's fists clenched. "Like what? A Warrior? A Mage?"

"Nothing." The woman of light's form stabilized, revealing a face of cold, perfect beauty. "You will be returned to baseline. No class. No qi. No memory of what you've learned."

The words hit him like a physical blow.

*No memory.*

All the pain. All the struggle. The hours of meditation, the burning sensation of qi forcing its way through his meridians. Every lesson Mentor had taught him.

Gone.

"Like hell," Kai said.

The man in white raised an eyebrow. "You have no say in this matter."

"I have every say." Kai reached inside himself, grasping the qi swirling in his dantian. It responded, surging through his channels, filling him with power. "You want to take my class? You'll have to kill me first."

Mentor's eyes widened. "Kai, don't—"

But he was already moving.

He didn't have a technique. He didn't have a plan. He just had qi, and anger, and the desperate need to survive.

He threw a punch.

The qi in his meridians erupted, channeling through his arm, his fist, into the air between him and the man in white. A shockwave of golden energy blasted forward, tearing through the tutorial chamber like a scythe through wheat.

The man in white didn't flinch.

He raised one finger, and the shockwave stopped. Dissolved. Became nothing.

"You are untrained," the man said. "Your qi is raw. Your foundation is incomplete. You cannot fight us."

"I don't need to fight you." Kai was already turning, running toward the edge of the chamber, where the white walls shimmered and faded into gray nothing. "I just need to escape."

Mentor's voice rang out: "Kai, the boundary won't hold!"

He didn't stop.

He reached the edge and threw himself through.

The world went black.

For a moment—a second, an eternity—there was nothing. No light. No sound. No sensation. Just the void, empty and infinite.

Then something grabbed him.

Not hands. Not energy. Something older, deeper, more fundamental. It wrapped around his consciousness, pulling him somewhere, *anywhere*, through the darkness.

And then light.

Kai crashed onto a rooftop. Real sky above him. Real wind. Real buildings stretching into the distance.

He lay there, gasping, his body screaming in protest.

His phone buzzed.

He pulled it out, barely able to focus. A message from an unknown number, appearing as a System notification:

[YOU HAVE BEEN MARKED]
[THE ADMINISTRATORS WILL FIND YOU]
[YOU HAVE 72 HOURS TO PROVE YOUR CLASS'S LEGITIMACY]

[FAILURE TO DO SO WILL RESULT IN PERMANENT DELETION]

Below the message, a single line of text:

*Find the other Anomalies. Before they do.*

Kai stared at the message, his mind racing.

Other Anomalies. He wasn't the only one.

And somewhere, out in the integrated world, the Administrators were hunting them all.

His phone buzzed again. Another unknown number.

He answered.

"Kai Chen?" A voice—young, male, desperate. "My name is Derek. I got a class called Script Keeper. The System says I don't exist."

"I exist," Kai said slowly. "But they're trying to delete me."

"Then you need to get to the Hollow District. West side of the city. There's a café called The Final Brew. Ask for the owner."

The line went dead.

Kai looked at his phone. The message from the Administrators still glowed in his peripheral vision, counting down.

72 hours.

He pushed himself to his feet, ignoring the pain, and started running.

title: The Fractured Sky
content:
Kai's apartment window shattered inward.

He hit the floor before his conscious mind registered the threat, rolling toward the kitchen as glass sprayed across his sheets. A pressure wave followed—not air, but qi, dense and suffocating, pressing down like a physical weight.

*What the—*

The notification was still burning in his peripheral vision when he scrambled to his feet, heart hammering. His dantian responded instinctively, qi flooding his meridians in a defensive pattern he hadn't consciously learned. The pressure lessened, but didn't disappear.

Through the broken window, the sky was wrong.

Kai had lived in this apartment for three years. He knew the view—the neighboring building's brick wall, the water tower, the narrow strip of gray sky that passed for a horizon in this part of the city. But the sky he saw now wasn't gray. It was fractured.

Cracks ran across the heavens like broken glass, each one leaking a pale golden light that pulsed with rhythm—*thump-thump-thump*—like a heartbeat. The clouds had frozen mid-drift, suspended in impossible stillness. And where the cracks converged, directly above the city center, a massive geometric pattern was forming.

A hexagram. Twelve-pointed. Rotating slowly.

Kai's phone buzzed again, and again, and again. The messages piled up faster than he could read:

*SKY IS BROKEN WTF*

*Did anyone else get a quest? I just got a QUEST*

*Faction missions are appearing. Celestial Mandate, Dragon Ascendancy, something called "The Hidden Path"*

*Don't join anything yet. This is bigger than tutorials*

Another notification blinked into existence, this one gold-rimmed and shimmering:

[EMERGENCY SYSTEM ANNOUNCEMENT: WORLD INTEGRATION - PHASE 2]
[CLASS TIERS ARE NOW ACTIVE]
[ALL CLASSES CLASSIFIED AS: COMMON, UNCOMMON, RARE, EPIC, LEGENDARY]
[MYTHIC AND UNIQUE CLASSES UNLOCKED THROUGH SPECIAL CIRCUMSTANCES]
[WARNING: CLASS RANKING WILL DETERMINE INITIAL FACTION PLACEMENT]
[INITIATING FACTION SELECTION IN: 23:59:58]

Kai stared at the countdown. Twenty-four hours until faction selection. Twenty-four hours to understand what his class actually was, and where it would place him in whatever hierarchy the System was building.

His phone buzzed again. This time, it was a direct call—his mother.

He answered. "Mom, I'm fine. Don't—"

"Kai, listen to me." Her voice was sharp, urgent, nothing like her usual gentle concern. "Your father and I both awakened. Administrative classes. We're being offered positions in a faction called the Celestial Bureaucracy. They're saying it's the largest faction, the most stable. But something's wrong."

"Wrong how?"

"The System is categorizing people. Not just by class, but by *potential*. Your father's class is ranked Rare. Mine is Uncommon. They told us that people below Rare will be assigned to support roles in the faction structure. Manual labor. Resource gathering. *Cannon fodder.*"

Kai's blood ran cold. "That's—"

"It's happening everywhere. I've been watching the forums. People with Common classes are panicking. Some are trying to hide their rankings, but the System keeps broadcasting them. It's like being sorted into castes before we even understand what's happening."

The golden light from the fractured sky flickered, and the ground shook.

"What was that?" his mother asked.

"I don't know. Stay inside. Lock the doors. I'll come to you as soon as I can."

"Kai—"

"I promise. I'll figure this out."

He hung up before she could argue, already moving toward the door. His qi sense expanded automatically, mapping the building around him. Seven other occupants in his floor alone, their qi signatures flaring with panic or excitement or fear. The couple in 4B had matching Warrior classes, both Rare-ranked, their qi already sharpened into combat-ready edges. The old woman in 4D was something else entirely—her signature flickered like a candle in wind, unstable and strange.

He paused at the door.

*The tutorial. Mentor said I was Unranked. That my class couldn't be classified.*

The System announcement had listed tiers from Common to Legendary, with Mythic and Unique as special exceptions. But it hadn't mentioned Unranked. It hadn't mentioned Dao Architect.

What if his class wasn't just unclassified? What if it didn't fit the System's framework at all?

His phone buzzed again. This time, the message was from an unknown number, the text appearing character by character as if typed by someone uncertain of the language:

*Kai Chen. Your class has been flagged for review. Report to Sector 7 Processing Center within 2 hours for mandatory reclassification. Failure to comply will result in automatic demotion to Common tier.*

Kai read the message three times. His hands were steady, but his qi was roiling, agitated, responding to some threat he couldn't yet name.

*Flagged for review. Mandatory reclassification.*

This wasn't an invitation. It was a summons.

He could ignore it. Stay hidden, wait for things to settle, figure out his class on his own terms. But the countdown was ticking, and if the System could force faction selection, it could probably find him wherever he hid.

Or he could go. Walk into the processing center, see what they knew about his class, and decide from there.

His feet carried him out the door before his mind finished debating.

---

The streets were chaos.

People clustered in groups, some crying, some laughing, some staring at their hands as if seeing them for the first time. A woman had manifested floating orbs of water that circled her head like a halo. A teenager had grown two feet taller, his skin taking on a metallic sheen. An old man sat on a bench, weeping, a common-rank notification hovering above his head like a brand.

Kai kept his head down and walked.

His qi sense was working overtime, feeding him information he couldn't filter. The life forces of everyone within a three-block radius pressed against his awareness—thousands of signatures, each one unique, each one carrying the imprint of their newly awakened class. Most were Common. Some were Uncommon. A handful burned brighter, Rare-tier, drawing attention from the crowds.

And somewhere, deeper, there were others.

He felt them as flickers at the edge of his perception—signatures that didn't fit the pattern. One was cold, like winter air compressed into human form. Another was liquid, flowing and shifting, impossible to pin down. A third was *hungry*, a gnawing void that made his qi recoil.

*Unique classes. Or something else entirely.*

The Sector 7 Processing Center was a converted government building, its facade still bearing the scars of whatever administrative purpose it had served before the System. Now it pulsed with qi, a massive formation inscribed into the walls and floors, glowing with the same golden light as the fractured sky.

A line stretched around the block.

Kai joined it, keeping his senses alert. The people around him were nervous, fidgeting, checking their class notifications repeatedly as if hoping the rankings might change. They didn't.

"Hey." A voice at his elbow—a young woman with sharp features and sharper eyes. "You're the one they flagged?"

Kai turned to face her. She was short, barely reaching his shoulder, but her qi signature burned with an intensity that made him instinctively want to step back. Rare-tier. Some kind of combat class, from the way her energy coiled tight, ready to strike.

"Who's asking?"

"Someone with sense." She glanced around, then lowered her voice. "I saw your name on the flagged list. There were only five of us in the entire sector. I wanted to see who the others were."

"Five?"

"Five classes the System couldn't classify." She held out her hand. "Lin Mei. Class: Boundary Walker. Rank: Unclassified."

Kai took her hand. Her grip was firm, her skin cool. "Kai Chen. Dao Architect. Also unclassified."

"Dao Architect." She repeated the words slowly, testing them. "That's not a combat class, is it?"

"It's not anything I recognize. My tutorial was... unusual."

"Same. My mentor said I could walk between realms. That the boundaries of the System were thinner than people realized." Her eyes flickered, a hint of something ancient in their depths. "She also said the System was afraid of classes like ours."

*Afraid.* The word struck Kai like a physical blow. "Why would the System be afraid?"

"Because we don't fit its framework. Because we're variables it can't control." Lin Mei's voice dropped to barely a whisper. "Because if the System can't classify us, it can't rank us. And if it can't rank us, it can't control us."

The line shuffled forward. The processing center's doors loomed closer, their golden light casting long shadows across the concrete.

They were thirty feet from the entrance when Kai felt it.

The hungry void he'd sensed earlier—it was here. Closer now. Moving through the crowd.

He turned, scanning the faces. Ordinary people, terrified and hopeful and confused. None of them matched the emptiness he'd felt.

But it was there. Watching.

"What is it?" Lin Mei asked, reading his tension.

"Something's following us. Something that doesn't feel right."

Her hand moved to her waist, where a weapon might have been before the System changed everything. "Hostile?"

"I don't know. But it's been tracking me since I left my apartment."

The line shuffled forward again. Ten feet from the doors.

The void moved.

Kai's qi sense screamed a warning, and he dropped, pulling Lin Mei with him, as a blade of pure darkness sliced through the space where their heads had been. The attack left no visible mark, but the air itself seemed to *remember* the cut, a line of absence that slowly sealed itself.

The crowd erupted in screams.

People scattered, trampling each other in their panic. The processing center's guards—newly awakened warriors with Rare-tier classes—rushed forward, weapons materializing in their hands.

But the attacker was already gone.

Kai rose, heart pounding, scanning the chaos. The void signature had vanished, swallowed by the crowd's panic. But he could still feel its echo, a cold spot in his awareness that slowly faded.

"That was an assassination attempt," Lin Mei said, her voice tight. "Someone wants us dead before we reach that processing center."

"Us? Or me?"

"Does it matter? We're both flagged. We're both unclassified. If someone's targeting us, we need to figure out why." She grabbed his arm, pulling him toward the doors. "Come on. We're not safe out here."

The guards let them through, too busy containing the panic to question them. Inside, the processing center was a maze of corridors and cubicles, each one glowing with formation light. System-issued clerks—humans who'd been assigned administrative classes—directed them through checkpoints, scanning their qi signatures, updating their status in the System's growing database.

Finally, they reached a small room at the end of a long hallway. A single chair sat in the center, facing a wall of golden light.

"Wait here," the clerk said. "The examiner will be with you shortly."

The door closed behind them, leaving Kai and Lin Mei alone with the wall of light.

"You trust this?" Lin Mei asked.

"No. But I don't have a better option."

The wall of light rippled. A figure emerged—tall, slender,

title: The Weight of Ascension
content:
Kai's fingers hovered over his phone, the unsent message mocking him. Before he could decide how to respond, the world *lurched*.

The ceiling above him warped. The water stains twisted into patterns that hurt to look at, geometric impossibilities that shouldn't exist in three-dimensional space. His bedroom walls rippled like the surface of a disturbed pond, and through them, he could *see*—glimpses of other rooms, other buildings, other *worlds* flickering in and out of existence.

[SYSTEM NOTIFICATION]
[WORLD INTEGRATION: 73.4% COMPLETE]
[DIMENSIONAL STABILIZATION IN PROGRESS]
[WARNING: SPATIAL ANOMALIES DETECTED]

Kai's qi sense screamed. The faint presences he'd felt earlier—his neighbors, the cockroach, the electronics—all intensified a hundredfold, flooding his perceptions with raw, unfiltered life force. He gasped, clamping down on his senses, but the damage was done. His dantian pulsed like a second heart, overfull and pounding.

*Mentor.* He thought the name desperately, reaching for that familiar presence. *What's happening?*

Silence. She didn't answer.

The warping stopped as abruptly as it began. Kai's bedroom snapped back to normal—cracked ceiling, threadbare sheets, cheap detergent smell. But something was *wrong*. The air felt thicker, charged with an energy that made his skin prickle. The sunlight streaming through his window had a golden tint it hadn't possessed moments before.

His phone buzzed again. Then again. Then a dozen times in rapid succession.

He looked down. The group chat had exploded.

*Did anyone else feel that?*

*The sky turned purple for like three seconds. THREE SECONDS.*

*My mana bar doubled. I was at 50/50, now I'm at 100/100.*

*Where did all this EXP come from? I gained three levels without killing anything.*

Kai scrolled faster.

*The System's updating. I can see a progress bar in the corner of my vision.*

*89%... 91%... 95%...*

*It's almost done.*

*What happens at 100%?*

The chat went silent.

Kai felt it before he saw it—a pressure building in the air, like the moment before a thunderstorm breaks. His dantian resonated, qi cycling automatically through his meridians in a defensive pattern he hadn't consciously learned. The golden sunlight intensified, casting long shadows that stretched in wrong directions.

[SYSTEM NOTIFICATION]
[WORLD INTEGRATION: 100%]
[DIMENSIONAL STABILIZATION COMPLETE]
[WELCOME TO THE NEW WORLD]

The notification faded. For a long moment, nothing happened.

Then the screaming started.

Kai was out of bed before his conscious mind caught up, his body moving with a speed it shouldn't possess. He threw open his apartment door and stumbled into the hallway. The fluorescent lights above flickered, casting stroboscopic shadows across walls that now seemed to breathe.

Mrs. Chen from 3B was on her knees in the corridor, clutching her head. Her qi signature flared wildly, unstable, *wrong*. As Kai watched, her skin *crawled*—bones shifting beneath the surface, muscles rearranging themselves.

"Mrs. Chen!" He rushed toward her, reaching out—

His hand passed through a barrier of heat that seared his palm. He jerked back, hissing, and watched in horror as Mrs. Chen's body *reshaped* itself. Her spine elongated. Her fingers fused, forming clawed digits. Her eyes—her eyes turned a solid, burning gold.

[SYSTEM NOTIFICATION]
[HUMAN: MRS. CHEN (AGE 67)]
[CLASS: NONE]
[STATUS: RACIAL ASCENSION IN PROGRESS]
[RESULT: BEASTKIN (FELINE)]

The transformation took twelve seconds. When it was over, Mrs. Chen—or what had been Mrs. Chen—rose to her feet on legs that bent backward at the knee. She was taller now, leaner, covered in a coat of orange fur with darker stripes. Her face had flattened, her nose and mouth merging into a feline muzzle.

She blinked her golden eyes, looked at her clawed hands, and *screamed*.

Not in pain. In terror.

"WHAT HAPPENED TO ME?!" Her voice was distorted, guttural, the words struggling past a throat not designed for human speech.

Kai's mind raced. *Racial ascension. The System didn't just give people classes—it's changing what they are. Mrs. Chen had no class, so the System gave her something else. A new race.*

*What about everyone else?*

The building shook. From outside, he heard shouts, crashes, the screech of metal twisting. He ran to the window at the end of the hallway and looked down.

The city had changed.

Buildings that had been gray concrete and glass now twisted into organic shapes, spires of bone and crystal rising where steel beams had been. Streets cracked open, revealing glowing veins of what looked like molten stone. And everywhere—*everywhere*—people were transforming.

A man on the corner sprouted antlers, his skin hardening into bark. A woman across the street grew translucent wings, her body lightening until she floated, carried by an updraft. A child—Kai's stomach clenched—a child no older than eight had become something *else*, a creature of shadow and smoke that flickered in and out of visibility.

[SYSTEM BROADCAST]
[WORLD INTEGRATION COMPLETE]
[RACIAL ASCENSION: ACTIVE]
[ALL HUMANS WITHOUT CLASSES WILL BE ASSIGNED NEW RACES BASED ON SOUL AFFINITY]
[CLASS HOLDERS ARE EXEMPT FROM FORCED ASCENSION]
[WELCOME TO THE NEW WORLD. ADAPT OR PERISH.]

*Adapt or perish.*

Kai's phone buzzed. He looked down. The group chat had one new message, from Jake:

*Guys. Guys, the monsters are real now. I can see them. They're pouring out of the subway.*
*They're heading toward the residential districts.*
*RUN.*

Another message, this one from his mother: *Kai, baby, where are you? The strangest things are happening. Your father grew—Kai, your father grew a third arm. He's crying. Please tell me what to do.*

Kai's blood ran cold. He typed a response with shaking fingers: *Stay inside. Lock the doors. Don't let anyone in. I'm coming.*

He didn't know if he could keep that promise. The streets below were chaos—newly transformed beastkin fleeing in panic, class holders trying to fight or flee, and in the distance, shapes moving through the shadows that didn't belong to any human or former human.

His dantian pulsed, qi flooding his meridians. He felt *strong*. Stronger than he had any right to be. The Dao Architect class had been growing, evolving, feeding on something he didn't fully understand. He reached out with his qi sense, trying to map the chaos—

And felt it.

A presence. Massive, ancient, *hungry*. It was moving through the city underground, following the subway tunnels, drawn by the scent of freshly transformed humans. Its qi signature was alien, wrong, a discordant note in the symphony of the world.

[SYSTEM NOTIFICATION]
[BOSS CLASS ENTITY DETECTED]
[CLASSIFICATION: UNDERWORLD SERPENT (???)]
[LEVEL: ???]
[WARNING: THIS ENTITY EXCEEDS RECOMMENDED ENCOUNTER THRESHOLD FOR CURRENT WORLD TIER]

Kai's blood froze.

*We just got the System. People are still transforming. How is there already a boss-level monster?*

The answer came, unbidden, from somewhere deep in his instincts:

*The System didn't create the monsters. It just woke them up.*

He turned from the window, his mind racing. His mother was six blocks away. The serpent was moving in her direction. He had a class that no one understood, a cultivation technique that was barely stable, and about twelve hours of total training.

He was going anyway.

As he reached the stairwell, a familiar golden light bloomed in his peripheral vision. Mentor's voice, strained and urgent, finally reached him:

*Kai. Listen carefully. The World Integration accelerated my recovery, but it also accelerated the Dao Collapse. I can manifest for brief moments now, but the strain is immense.*

*There is something coming. Something that should not have awakened for centuries. The System's premature integration has thrown the balance into chaos.*

*You are not ready for what you will face.*

*But you are the only one who can face it.*

Kai paused at the top of the stairs, the door to the outside world looming before him. "What do you mean, the only one?"

Mentor's presence flickered, weakened, but her words cut through the chaos like a blade:

*Because the others are playing a game.*

*You are cultivating immortality.*

*The serpent below is not your enemy, Kai. It is a symptom. Something else is pulling the strings—something that has been waiting for the System to arrive.*

*Find it. Stop it. Or this world will be consumed before it has a chance to be born.*

Her presence faded, leaving Kai alone with the screams of a transforming city and the weight of a destiny he never asked for.

He pushed open the door and stepped into the new world.

The first thing he noticed was the smell—ozone, blood, and something sweet, like rotting fruit. The second was the sky, which had turned a bruised purple, clouds swirling in patterns that hurt to follow. The third was the serpent.

It rose from the subway entrance two blocks away, its body as wide as a city bus, its scales gleaming with an oily iridescence. It had no eyes. Instead, its face was a mass of writhing tendrils, each one tasting the air, tasting the qi of everything around it.

It turned toward Kai.

*It knows,* he realized. *It knows what I am.*

The serpent's maw opened, revealing rows of teeth that glowed with sickly light. A sound emerged—not a roar, but something worse. A frequency that resonated with his qi, that made his dantiam vibrate like a plucked string.

[SYSTEM NOTIFICATION]
[UNDERWORLD SERPENT USES: QI DISRUPTION FIELD]
[YOUR MERIDIANS ARE EXPERIENCING UNSTABLE RESONANCE]
[WARNING: CONTINUED EXPOSURE MAY CAUSE QI DEVIATION]

Kai's cultivation wavered. The qi he'd so carefully cycled through his meridians began to churn, threatening to spiral out of control. He gasped, dropping to one knee, as the serpent's tendrils reached toward him, tasting his essence.

*No.* He gritted his teeth, forcing his qi back into alignment. *I didn't spend six hours building a foundation just to lose it to the first monster I encounter.*

He rose, steadying his breathing, and reached into himself. The Dao Architect class was unlike any other—it didn't grant skills or spells, but *understanding*. He could see the serpent's qi pattern, could trace the flow of energy through its body, could identify the weak points where its cultivation was unstable.

It was a creature of pure yin, cold and devouring. It fed on fear, on chaos, on the unformed qi of newly transformed beings. It was strong—devastatingly strong—

title: The Price of Uniqueness
content:
The broadcast hung in the air, golden text fading slowly like dying embers. Kai's finger hovered over the send button, his message still unsent. Something was wrong. The System's notification felt different from the others—heavier, more deliberate, as if it carried weight beyond mere information.

His phone buzzed again. Then again. And again.

The group chat exploded.

*What does "World Integration" mean?*

*My skills are gone. I had Fireball level 2 and now it's just... grayed out.*

*Same. My Warrior skills are locked. Says "Awaiting World Integration Completion."*

Kai's blood ran cold. He pulled up his own status, the translucent blue screen flickering into existence before his eyes:

[Name: Kai Chen]
[Class: Dao Architect]
[Class Rank: ???]
[Cultivation: Qi Condensation, Stage 1]
[Skills: Basic Qi Circulation (Lv.3), Spiritual Sense (Lv.1)]
[Status: AWAITING WORLD INTEGRATION]

*Awaiting.* The same as everyone else. But his class remained unclassified, his rank still question marks. The System didn't know what to do with him.

His phone rang. Unknown number. He answered instinctively.

"Kai Chen?" The voice was sharp, professional. Female. "This is Agent Liu from the newly formed Bureau of Anomalous Affairs. We need you to come in for assessment."

"How did you get my number?"

"Everyone who received an unclassified class is being contacted. You're one of forty-seven confirmed cases globally. We need to understand what makes you different."

Forty-seven. Out of billions. The number was staggeringly small, and staggeringly terrifying. He was part of a statistical anomaly that the government was already tracking.

"I haven't done anything wrong."

"Neither have they. This is for your safety, Mr. Chen. And for the safety of others. Your tutorial was flagged as anomalous—it exceeded standard parameters by a factor of seventeen. The Council wants to know why."

Council. Not Bureau. The distinction was important. The System had created new power structures overnight, and the old world was scrambling to keep up.

"I'll think about it."

"Mr. Chen—"

He hung up. His hands were shaking.

---

The streets of Shanghai were unrecognizable.

Kai stepped outside his apartment building and froze. The sky was wrong—not the color, but the texture. Ripples of energy moved across it like heat haze over asphalt, distorting the clouds into spiraling patterns that hurt to look at directly. The air hummed with latent power, a constant background thrum that made his teeth ache.

People moved differently too. Some walked with new confidence, their steps heavier, their gazes sharper. Others cowered, hugging walls, their eyes darting at shadows. A few had visible changes—one man's arms were covered in crystalline scales that caught the morning light; a woman's hair floated around her head as if underwater, tendrils of blue energy trailing from the tips.

The world had changed overnight. And everyone was still pretending it hadn't.

Kai pulled up his hood and walked. He needed information. He needed to understand what "World Integration" meant, and why his class was causing such a stir.

The answer came sooner than expected.

A scream ripped through the morning air, sharp and desperate. Kai's head snapped toward the sound—an alley between two convenience stores, narrow and shadowed. His Spiritual Sense flared, and he felt it: a qi signature that was wrong, twisted, *ravenous*.

He moved before he could think.

The alley opened into a small courtyard, hidden from the street by a tangle of abandoned bicycles and overflowing dumpsters. In the center, a young woman in a business suit was backed against the wall, her hands raised defensively. A shimmering barrier of light flickered between her and the creature advancing toward her.

The creature was human. Once. Now its skin was cracked like dried mud, orange light pulsing through the fissures like magma. Its eyes were gone, replaced by twin points of golden fire. Its mouth hung open, and from it came a sound like grinding stone.

"Please," the woman whispered. "Please, I don't want to fight."

The creature lunged.

Kai's qi responded instinctively. His meridians flared, and he was moving—not running, but *flowing*, his body guided by the energy that now suffused every cell. He stepped between the woman and the creature, his palm extended.

"Stop."

The word carried power. Not System power—something older, deeper. The qi in the air resonated with his voice, and the creature *hesitated*. Its burning eyes fixed on him, and for a moment, Kai saw something like recognition flicker in their depths.

Then it attacked.

The creature's clawed hand swept toward his throat. Kai caught it—barely. The impact jarred his arm, sent pain shooting through his shoulder, but he held. His qi surged into the contact point, and he *pushed*.

The creature stumbled back, its arm smoking where his qi had touched it. It made that grinding sound again, louder now, more urgent.

"What is that thing?" the woman gasped behind him.

"I don't know." Kai didn't take his eyes off the creature. "But it's not natural. Something went wrong during the integration."

"Wrong?" Her voice cracked. "The System said everyone got classes. Everyone got tutorials. How could something go *wrong*?"

The creature answered for him. It threw its head back and *screamed*—a sound that was part metal, part flesh, part something that shouldn't exist. The orange light in its fissures blazed, and the air around it began to distort.

Kai felt it before he saw it: the qi in the creature's body was *eating* itself. Consuming its own existence, converting mass into energy at a rate that would destroy it in seconds. A suicide attack.

"No." He reached out with his Spiritual Sense, diving into the creature's collapsing qi network. It was chaos—meridians twisted into knots, dantian shattered and bleeding energy like a ruptured dam. But at the center, buried deep, was a spark. A fragment of something original. Human.

*You're still in there.*

He didn't know if that was true. He didn't care. He grabbed that spark with his qi and *pulled*.

The creature convulsed. Its body began to collapse inward, the orange light dimming as Kai's qi wrapped around the human fragment at its core. The grinding scream cut off, replaced by a wet, choking sound.

Then the creature's form dissolved.

Where it had stood, a man lay crumpled on the ground. Normal. Human. His clothes were torn and singed, but his skin was unbroken, his eyes closed in what looked like peaceful sleep.

Kai fell to his knees, gasping. His dantian throbbed violently, the qi he'd expended leaving him hollow and shaking. But he'd done it. He'd saved someone.

The woman rushed forward, kneeling beside the unconscious man. "Is he...?"

"Alive." Kai coughed, tasted copper. "But I don't know for how long. Something turned him into that. Something from the integration."

"We need to call an ambulance. We need—"

A new voice cut through the chaos, cold and amused. "Impressive. Most people would have let him burn."

Kai looked up.

A man stood at the entrance to the alley, dressed in a black suit that seemed to absorb light. His eyes were golden—not the burning gold of the creature, but a cool, metallic sheen that reminded Kai of Mentor's gaze. A subtle glow emanated from his skin, barely visible in the morning light.

"You're one of the forty-seven," the man said. "The unclassifieds. I've been looking for you."

Kai forced himself to stand, ignoring the protest of his exhausted body. "Who are you?"

"My name is irrelevant. What matters is what you just did." The man stepped closer, his golden eyes fixed on Kai with unsettling intensity. "You saved a corrupted human from qi deviation. You reversed a process that the System considers irreversible. Do you understand what that means?"

"I understand that I need to leave." Kai backed toward the woman and the unconscious man. "Whatever you want, I'm not interested."

"You don't have a choice." The man's smile didn't reach his eyes. "The Bureau of Anomalous Affairs is already on its way. They'll take you, study you, dissect your abilities until they understand how an unclassified class can do what the System says is impossible. And when they're done, they'll decide if you're a weapon to be wielded or a threat to be eliminated."

Kai's blood ran cold. "You're not with the Bureau."

"Correct." The man extended his hand, and the air around his palm began to shimmer. "I'm with the people who created the Bureau. The ones who saw the System coming before it arrived. The ones who prepared."

The shimmer solidified into a blade of pure light, crystalline and sharp.

"Come with me willingly, or I'll make you. Either way, you're coming."

The woman screamed. Kai's qi flickered dangerously, his body too depleted to fight. He looked at the blade, at the man's golden eyes, and knew he had seconds to decide.

[SYSTEM NOTIFICATION: NEW QUEST AVAILABLE]
[QUEST: THE FIRST CHOICE]
[OBJECTIVE: ESCAPE OR SURRENDER]
[REWARD: UNKNOWN]
[PENALTY: UNKNOWN]

Kai's hand trembled. The blade of light hummed, hungry and patient. And somewhere in the distance, sirens began to wail.

The Bureau was coming.

The golden-eyed man was already here.

And Kai had no idea which was worse.

title: The Rift Beneath the City
content:
Kai's hand hovered over the send button, but the System notification froze him mid-motion.

[SYSTEM BROADCAST: TUTORIAL PHASE COMPLETE]
[ALL CLASSES HAVE BEEN ASSIGNED]
[WORLD INTEGRATION: 34% COMPLETE]
[WARNING: ANOMALOUS ENERGY SPIKE DETECTED. ORIGIN: CLASSIFIED]

Thirty-four percent. The number hit him like a physical blow. If the System was only a third integrated, what happened when it reached a hundred? Would Earth dissolve completely into whatever framework the Heavenly Dao had designed?

His phone buzzed again, but he ignored it. Something was wrong. The qi in the air had shifted—a subtle change, like a note played slightly off-key. He reached out with his senses, letting his awareness expand beyond his apartment walls.

The city hummed with life. Thousands of qi signatures, most of them new and unstable, flickered like candles in a storm. Warriors flexing their reinforced muscles. Mages channeling elemental energy for the first time. Healers testing their diagnostics on friends and family.

But beneath it all, deeper than the surface chaos, Kai felt something else. A pressure. Building. Growing.

Like a held breath.

He grabbed his jacket and headed for the door. Whatever was happening, he needed to see it for himself.

---

The streets of downtown were a carnival of confusion.

Groups of people clustered on corners, comparing class abilities. Two men in business suits were arguing about optimal stat allocation while a woman in yoga pants conjured a small flame in her palm, laughing nervously as it sputtered and died. A teenager sprinted past at impossible speed, leaving tiny cracks in the sidewalk where his feet touched down.

Kai walked among them, invisible. His qi sense was a constant stream of data—interests, fears, excitement, terror. The collective emotional weight pressed against his spiritual awareness like a tide.

He stopped at an intersection, frowning.

The pressure was stronger here. He could feel it through the concrete, a vibration that had nothing to do with traffic or trains. It pulsed in rhythm with something—not his heartbeat, not the city's energy, but something deeper. Something *ancient*.

*You feel it too.*

Mentor's voice echoed in his mind, tinged with something Kai had never heard from her before. Concern.

"What is it?" he murmured, keeping his voice low.

*A rift. Forming. The System's integration is forcing dimensional boundaries to thin. In your world's terms, the veil between realities is wearing through.*

Kai's blood ran cold. "A rift to where?"

*That is the question, isn't it? The System originates from the Heavenly Dao—a structure of order and cultivation. But order requires balance. Where there is creation, there is also... what was not meant to be created.*

"You're not making me feel better."

*I am not trying to make you feel better. I am trying to prepare you. The rift will open within the hour. When it does, what emerges will be the first true test of your world's integration.*

Kai stopped walking. People flowed around him like water around a stone. "First true test? What does that mean?"

*The System did not assign classes for entertainment. It prepared your world for what comes next. The weak will die. The strong will grow. This is the way of cultivation, compressed into days instead of decades.*

He wanted to argue, but the words died in his throat. Because she was right. He'd felt it from the moment he awakened his meridians—the quiet urgency beneath the System's gamified surface. This wasn't a game. It was selection.

"How do I find the rift?"

*You don't. It finds you. The Architect class is attuned to the Dao's architecture. You will be drawn to the point of weakness whether you wish it or not.*

Kai waited. Nothing happened.

"Okay, I don't feel drawn anywhere."

*You are doing it wrong. Stop thinking like a human. Think like the System.*

He closed his eyes, ignoring the stares of passersby. He reached inward, touching the qi circulating through his meridians, feeling its rhythm. Then he pushed that awareness outward, not as perception but as *intention*.

The city's energy grid appeared in his mind's eye—not electrical, but spiritual. Ley lines of residual qi, crisscrossing the urban landscape like veins. Most were thin, barely developed. But one.

One was a gash.

A wound in the fabric of reality, bleeding raw energy into the subway tunnels beneath his feet.

Kai's eyes snapped open. "It's underground. The old Central Station line, the one they abandoned in the '90s."

*Then go. And Kai?*

"Yeah?"

*Do not die. I have invested too much time in you to start over.*

He ran.

---

The entrance to the abandoned station was sealed with rusted gates and concrete barriers, but the qi pressure was so intense now that Kai could feel it pushing against his skin like static electricity. He found a maintenance grate, pried it open with a burst of reinforced strength he didn't know he had, and dropped into the darkness.

The tunnel smelled of mold, decay, and something else. Something that made his qi sense recoil.

*Tainted energy,* Mentor said. *The rift is leaking. Whatever is on the other side, it does not belong to the System's order.*

Kai moved carefully, his footsteps echoing in the empty passage. The walls were covered in graffiti—tags from decades past, layers of paint that told the story of a forgotten city. But as he went deeper, the graffiti changed. Symbols he didn't recognize. Patterns that hurt to look at.

The temperature dropped.

His breath fogged in front of his face.

And then he saw it.

At the end of the tunnel, where the tracks had been torn up and the ceiling had collapsed, a crack hung in the air. Not in the stone—in *space* itself. It was small, maybe three feet long and a foot wide, but it glowed with a light that seemed to eat the darkness around it.

Kai approached slowly, his qi sense extended to its limit. The rift was unstable, fluctuating between dimensions, spitting out fragments of energy that dissolved before they reached the ground.

*It is forming faster than I anticipated. The integration is accelerating.*

"What comes through?" Kai asked, his voice barely a whisper.

*I do not know. The rift's signature is... wrong. It does not match any known realm within the Heavenly Dao's structure.*

"That's not possible. You said the System is the Heavenly Dao. How can something exist outside it?"

A long pause. When Mentor spoke again, her voice was heavy.

*Because the Heavenly Dao was not the beginning. It was the response. Before order, there was chaos. Before cultivation, there was... the Primordial Void. The System was built to contain it. To suppress it. If the integration is tearing holes in that suppression—*

The rift split open.

Not slowly. Not gradually. It *exploded* outward, a jagged wound of violet-black energy that sent shockwaves through the tunnel. Kai threw up his arms, flooding his meridians with qi to brace himself, but the force still knocked him off his feet.

He hit the ground hard, rolled, and came up in a crouch.

The rift was stable now. A doorway, three meters tall and two wide, pulsing with malevolent light. And from within that light, something was moving.

Shapes. Dozens of them. Small, insectoid, chittering.

The first one emerged.

It was the size of a large dog, with six legs and a segmented body covered in chitin that looked like cracked obsidian. Its head was a nightmare of mandibles and too many eyes, each one reflecting the rift's sickly glow. It let out a screech that echoed through the tunnels.

[SYSTEM NOTIFICATION: NEW ENEMY DETECTED]
[NAME: Void Scarab]
[LEVEL: 5]
[THREAT: MODERATE]
[NOTE: These creatures are not part of the System's bestiary. Classification pending.]

*Kill it,* Mentor said. *Before it reaches the surface.*

Kai didn't have a weapon. He didn't have combat skills. He had qi circulation and a theoretical understanding of pressure points.

He had to make it work.

The scarab charged, its legs clicking against the concrete. Kai dropped into a stance he'd seen in a dozen martial arts movies, centered his qi, and struck.

His palm connected with the creature's carapace. Qi exploded outward on impact—not destructive, but *disruptive*. The scarab's internal energy structure, chaotic and unstable, collapsed under the pressure. It convulsed, let out a wet gurgle, and collapsed.

[COMBAT RESULT: VOID SCARAB ELIMINATED]
[QI ABSORBED: +2]
[SKILL PROGRESS: BASIC QI STRIKE - 1%]

One percent. For one kill.

More scarabs were pouring through the rift. Ten. Twenty. A tide of chitin and mandibles, their eyes fixed on him with predatory hunger.

*You cannot fight them all,* Mentor said. *Not yet. You need to seal the rift.*

"How?!"

*The Architect class allows you to manipulate the System's structures. The rift is a flaw in that structure. Reach out. Feel its edges. Then reshape them.*

Kai dodged a leaping scarab, grabbed its leg mid-air, and used its momentum to slam it into the wall. It shattered. Another took its place.

He had seconds.

He closed his eyes, ignoring the screeching, the chittering, the clicking of claws on concrete. He reached out with his qi sense, not to fight, but to *understand*.

The rift was a wound. It had edges—frayed, bleeding, chaotic. But beneath the chaos, there was pattern. A structure that had been broken.

He could fix it.

He just didn't know how.

*Think of it as code,* Mentor said. *You are not a warrior. You are an architect. Build.*

Kai's mind raced. He visualized the rift's edges, the torn threads of reality, and imagined them knitting together. He poured his qi into the image, forcing it into the physical world.

The rift shuddered.

The scarabs froze.

And from beyond the dimensional doorway, something *else* began to push through. Something larger. Something that made the scarabs seem like insects in truth.

The rift bulged outward, straining against Kai's repair. A hand emerged—not a hand, but a *suggestion* of a hand, formed of pure shadow, with fingers that ended in claws that dripped with corrupted qi.

A voice echoed from the void. Deep. Ancient. Hungry.

*"Free... at last..."*

Kai's qi faltered. The rift began to tear open again.

And then a blade of golden light sliced through the darkness, severing the shadow hand at the wrist.

A figure dropped from the tunnel ceiling, landing between Kai and the rift. Tall. Armored in light. Holding a sword that burned with solar fire.

The figure turned, and Kai saw her face.

It was Mentor.

But not Mentor as he'd known her. This was her true form—radiant, terrible, a goddess of cultivation wrapped in mortal flesh. Her golden eyes blazed with power that made the rift seem dim.

"Kai," she said, her voice resonating with authority, "I need you to trust me."

"Always," he breathed.

She smiled—a sad, knowing smile. "Then forgive me for what I am about to do."

She raised her sword.

And brought it down.

Not on the rift

title: The First Night
content:
[SYSTEM BROADCAST: TUTORIAL PHASE COMPLETE]

[ALL CLASSES HAVE BEEN ASSIGNED]

[WORLD INTEGRATION: 47% COMPLETE]

[PHASE 2 UNLOCKING IN: 23:59:47]

Kai stared at the countdown timer floating in his vision. Twenty-three hours. Fifty-nine minutes. Forty-seven seconds.

Forty-six.

Forty-five.

The numbers ticked down relentlessly, and with each second, Kai felt the pressure building in his chest. Phase 2. The System was upgrading the world in stages, and they'd just finished the first. What came next?

His phone buzzed again. He looked down at his unsent message, then deleted it entirely. How could he explain something he didn't understand himself?

Instead, he typed: *Got a weird class. Anyone else get something unusual?*

The responses came immediately.

*Define unusual. My friend Sarah got "Merchant" instead of anything combat-related.*

*There's a guy on the forums claiming he got "Dream Walker." No one believes him.*

*My sister got "Planter." She can make crops grow instantly. Mom's already demanding she start a vegetable garden.*

Kai scrolled through the messages, looking for anyone who mentioned cultivation, or qi, or anything that matched his experience. Nothing. Everyone else had gotten standard classes—Warrior, Mage, Healer, plus the more specialized variants like Alchemist, Blacksmith, Scout.

No one mentioned meridians. No one mentioned dantians. No one mentioned a golden-eyed woman who taught them to circulate qi.

*I'm alone in this.*

The thought hit him harder than expected. He'd spent his whole life being unremarkable—average grades, average job, average everything. Now, for the first time, he was unique. And it terrified him.

His phone buzzed with a private message. His mother.

*Kai, I'm coming over. Don't argue.*

He didn't argue. He couldn't. The doorbell rang fifteen minutes later.

His mother stood in the hallway, looking exactly as she always did—gray-streaked hair pulled back in a practical bun, worry lines etched deep into her forehead, eyes that missed nothing. She was carrying a plastic bag that smelled like homemade soup.

"You look terrible," she said, pushing past him into the apartment.

"Thanks, Mom."
