"Don't get smart with me. I've been calling for hours. Your father's been pacing a hole in the floor." She set the bag on his kitchen counter and began pulling out containers. "Sit. Eat. Then tell me everything."

Kai sat. The soup was hot, rich with ginger and chicken, and he realized he hadn't eaten in over six hours. He devoured three bowls before his mother finally sat across from him, arms crossed, waiting.

"I got a class," he said between bites. "But it's not normal."

"Define not normal."

He told her. Everything. The white chamber, Mentor, the qi circulation, the 2% mortality rate, the meridians that now hummed with energy beneath his skin. He expected her to laugh, or to call a doctor, or to drag him to church for an exorcism.

Instead, she nodded slowly.

"Your grandmother used to talk about things like this," she said. "Chi. Energy channels. The flow of life through the body. She said it was real, but the world had forgotten how to use it."

Kai nearly choked on his soup. "What?"

"Your great-grandmother was a healer in the old country. Not the kind with medicine—the kind who could feel illness in a person's energy and move it out of their body. The family thought she was crazy. But she lived to ninety-seven, healthy until her last breath." His mother's eyes were distant, remembering. "She said the world was changing. That one day, the old ways would return."

"She was right."

"I know." His mother reached across the table and gripped his hand. "I felt it. When the System activated. I felt something shift inside me. I don't have a class—I'm too old, I guess—but I can feel... something. The air tastes different. The shadows move wrong."

Kai focused his qi sense on his mother. Her energy was dim, faint, like a candle behind thick glass. But it was there. A spark of life that hadn't been there before.

"You're not getting a class?"

"No. The System said I'm 'outside the integration window.'" She smiled, but it didn't reach her eyes. "I'm obsolete. Just like your father."

"That's not—"

"I know. But it's the truth. This world is for you young people now. We're just... along for the ride."

The timer in Kai's vision ticked down: 22:47:12.

"We should talk about what happens next," he said. "Phase 2 unlocks tomorrow. We don't know what that means."

"We'll figure it out. Together." His mother stood, collecting the empty bowls. "You should rest. Your body's been through something extraordinary. Sleep while you can."

She left with a hug that lasted a little too long, a kiss on his forehead, and a promise to call in the morning.

Kai didn't sleep.

He lay in bed, eyes open, feeling the qi circulating through his meridians. It moved in a slow, steady rhythm, like a second heartbeat. Mentor had said his foundation was forming but not yet stable. What would happen when it stabilized? What came next?

He reached out with his qi sense, exploring the apartment building. Three floors up, a Warrior was practicing sword swings in his living room, the blade glowing with faint energy. Two floors down, a Mage was reading a spellbook by candlelight, her qi signature spiking and falling as she attempted to cast. In the apartment next door, an old woman was crying, her qi weak and chaotic.

*Not everyone handled the integration well.*

Kai pulled his awareness back, focusing inward. His dantian pulsed with that familiar dull ache, but it was fainter now, more settled. He tried circulating qi through his meridians without Mentor's guidance, just to see if he could.

The energy moved sluggishly at first, then picked up speed as he found the rhythm. One cycle. Two. Three. He pushed until he felt the familiar strain, then stopped, breathing hard.

[QI CIRCULATION: BASIC]

[PROGRESS: 2% TOWARD FOUNDATION STABILIZATION]

A progress bar. He had a progress bar for his cultivation. The System treated everything like a game, even the ancient art of energy manipulation.

He was about to try another cycle when his phone buzzed. A group chat notification. His gaming buddies had created a new channel: "SYSTEM SURVIVAL SQUAD."

*Jake: Phase 2 in 22 hours. Anyone know what it is?*

*Mark: Speculation on the forums is that it's either a monster wave or a dungeon unlock.*

*Sarah: My Merchant class has a skill called "Market Sense" that's pinging like crazy. Something big is happening in the city center.*

*Jake: Something good or something bad?*

*Sarah: Can't tell. The skill just says "High Volatility Zone Detected."*

Kai typed: *I can sense energy signatures now. There's something weird happening downtown. Lots of people gathering.*

*Mark: How do you know that?*

Kai hesitated. Then typed: *My class is weird. Let's just leave it at that.*

*Jake: Weird how?*

*Kai: I'll explain tomorrow. Get some sleep. We're going to need it.*

He set his phone aside and stared at the ceiling. The timer counted down: 21:33:08.

Sleep didn't come. His mind was too active, his body too wired. He kept feeling the qi moving inside him, kept sensing the other awakened people in his building, kept wondering what Phase 2 would bring.

At 3 AM, he gave up trying to sleep and got out of bed. His apartment felt too small, too confined. He needed air.

He dressed in silence and slipped out the door.

The streets were empty. Or rather, they were populated by ghosts. People moved through the shadows, their qi signatures bright against the darkness. Some were practicing skills. Others were just... wandering. Processing.

Kai walked without destination, letting his feet carry him where they pleased. The city looked the same—same buildings, same streets, same flickering streetlights—but it felt different. Charged. Like the air before a thunderstorm.

He found himself at the edge of a park, a small green space surrounded by apartment complexes. In the center, a group of people had gathered. About twenty of them, standing in a loose circle, their qi signatures varying from dim to moderate.

A woman stood in the center, her qi the brightest of them all. She was young, maybe mid-twenties, with sharp features and eyes that seemed to glow faintly in the darkness.

"I felt it too," she said as Kai approached. "You're not alone."

Kai stopped at the edge of the circle. "Felt what?"

"The shift. Something's happening in the city center. My Seeker class is telling me to go there, but it's also telling me to wait." She looked at him, her eyes narrowing. "You're different. Your energy is... strange."

"I get that a lot."

"We're forming a group. There's safety in numbers. Phase 2 is coming, and we need to be ready." She extended her hand. "I'm Ling. What's your class?"

Kai looked at her hand, then at the circle of strangers. They were all watching him, waiting for his answer.

"Kai," he said. "My class is... I'm a Dao Architect."

Silence. Then whispers.

"Never heard of it."

"Is that a subclass?"

"Check the forums, maybe it's rare."

Ling's eyes never left his. "What does a Dao Architect do?"

"I'm still figuring that out."

She smiled, and there was something knowing in it. "Aren't we all."

The timer in Kai's vision hit 20:00:00 exactly.

And the sky turned red.

[SYSTEM ALERT: PHASE 2 ACCELERATING]

[WORLD INTEGRATION: 78%]

[ESTIMATED TIME TO PHASE 2: 0:00:00]

[PREPARE FOR DUNGEON MANIFESTATION]

The ground shook. A pillar of crimson light erupted from the city center, piercing the clouds. The gathered group stumbled, grabbing each other for balance. Someone screamed.

Ling grabbed Kai's arm, her grip iron. "It's happening. Now."

Kai's dantian pulsed with painful intensity, his meridians blazing as the red light washed over him. His qi surged, uncontrolled, and he felt something crack inside him.

[FOUNDATION INSTABILITY DETECTED]

[QI DEVIATION RISK: 17%]

[COUNTERMEASURES RECOMMENDED]

But there was no time for countermeasures.

The ground split open.

title: The First Blood Moon
content:
The broadcast hung in the air for exactly three seconds before the world exploded.

Kai's phone erupted with notifications. Every app, every message, every social feed simultaneously detonated with alerts. The volume was deafening, a cacophony of pings and rings and buzzes that merged into a single discordant scream.

He grabbed his phone, thumbs flying across the screen.

*What does "World Integration" mean?*

*Has anyone else's map changed?*

*Bro I can SEE stats now. Above people's heads. There's a guy on my street with a red name.*

Red name.

Kai's blood went cold.

He closed his eyes and extended his qi sense, pushing past the walls of his apartment, past the hallway, out into the street. The life forces he'd sensed before were still there—but now they were labeled. Tags floating above each signature, visible to his spiritual perception:

[Sarah Chen - Level 3 Healer - Friendly]
[Marcus Webb - Level 5 Warrior - Neutral]
[Unknown - Level 2 Thief - Hostile]

Hostile.

His eyes snapped open. The word echoed in his mind, carrying a weight that felt wrong. Unnatural. Like the System was telling him something it shouldn't be able to know.

His phone buzzed again. A private message from an unknown number.

*You felt it too. The categorization. The judgment. Meet me at the old library on 5th. Come alone.*

Kai stared at the message. The sender ID was just a string of zeros. No name, no location, no metadata that his phone could parse. Just the message and a timestamp that read: [ERROR: TIMESTAMP OUTSIDE EXPECTED RANGE]

"Don't go."

Kai spun around.

Mentor stood in the corner of his bedroom, arms crossed, golden eyes fixed on him with an intensity that made his qi stutter.

"How—"

"The System cannot restrict me within the bounds of your reality. I exist in the spaces between its code." She stepped forward, her robes not quite touching the ground, as if she were perpetually falling through a dimension slightly offset from his own. "The message you received is a trap."

"Who sent it?"

"Someone who was never meant to receive a class. Someone who broke through the System's categorization the same way you did—but chose a different path."

Kai's jaw tightened. "Another Architect?"

"No." Mentor's voice carried a warning he'd never heard before. "An Eater. One of the corrupted. When the System descended, it assigned classes based on potential. Most people received what they were meant to have. A few received what they *could* become. And some—" She paused, her golden eyes flickering with something that might have been sorrow. "Some received nothing at all."

"Nothing?"

"The System is not perfect. It has gaps. Bugs. Places where the code fails to compile." She gestured at him. "You fell through one of those gaps and emerged as a Dao Architect. Others fell through and emerged as *holes*—emptiness given form. They consume because they were never given anything else to do."

Kai looked at his phone. The message was still there, glowing on the screen like an accusation.

"What happens if I don't go?"

"The Eater will find another target. Someone weaker. Someone who hasn't yet stabilized their foundation." She tilted her head. "Or it will grow impatient and come here. Your apartment is not a fortress, Kai. Your qi sense cannot protect you from something that doesn't register as a threat."

He thought about the 2% chance of death from qi deviation. He thought about the cockroach behind his wall, the life forces of his neighbors, the hostile tag floating above some stranger's head.

"What do I do?"

Mentor smiled. It was not a comforting expression.

"You learn. Right now. Before the Blood Moon rises."

"Blood Moon?"

As if on cue, the light outside his window shifted. The pale yellow of morning transformed into a deep, bloody crimson that painted his apartment in shades of rust and warning.

Kai rushed to the window and looked up.

The moon was red. Not the pale orange of a harvest moon or the deep copper of a lunar eclipse—this was *blood*. Fresh, vibrant, pulsing with a malevolent light that made his dantian churn.

[SYSTEM ALERT: BLOOD MOON EVENT ACTIVE]

[DURATION: 12 HOURS]

[EFFECTS: ALL HOSTILE ENTITIES GAIN +50% ATTRIBUTES. ALL FRIENDLY ENTITIES LOSE 25% DEFENSES. LOOT DROPS MULTIPLIED BY 3x.]

[ADDITIONAL EFFECT: CORRUPTED CLASSES UNLOCK ABILITY: CONSUME]

"Twelve hours," Kai breathed.

"Twelve hours in which every monster, every hostile NPC, every corrupted player will be actively hunting." Mentor's voice was calm, clinical. "The System designed these events to accelerate growth. To force conflict. To prune the weak."

"I'm Level 1."

"You are not a level. You are a cultivation stage. But yes, compared to what is coming, you are effectively a newborn." She walked to his window, her reflection appearing beside his in the glass. In the bloody light, her golden eyes looked like twin suns. "The Eater who messaged you will use this opportunity to grow. It will consume everything it can find—players, monsters, even the ambient qi in the environment. By dawn, if it's not stopped, it will reach a power level that even I cannot safely counter."

"Great." Kai ran a hand through his hair. "So I either die tonight trying to stop it, or I die in my apartment when it comes for me."

"You could run."

"Would that work?"

"No. The Eater can track qi signatures across the entire city now. It knows exactly where you are."

Kai turned from the window and faced his mentor. The fear was still there, coiled in his stomach like a snake, but beneath it was something harder. Something that had driven him to complete ten full qi circulations when his body had been screaming for rest.

"Then teach me. Whatever you can teach me in the next few hours, give it to me."

Mentor studied him for a long moment. Then she nodded.

"Close your eyes. Open your dantian. I am going to show you the first technique of the Dao Architect path: Qi Infusion."

Kai obeyed, settling into a cross-legged position on his threadbare carpet. The blood-red light filtered through his eyelids, staining his vision crimson.

"Qi Infusion is the art of imbuing objects with spiritual energy. At its simplest, it can strengthen a weapon or reinforce armor. At its highest levels—" She paused. "At its highest levels, it can create artifacts that rewrite local reality."

"Sounds useful."

"It is the difference between fighting with a stick and fighting with a sword. But the technique requires precise control. If you push too much qi into an object, it will shatter. Too little, and the effect is negligible."

Kai focused on his dantian, feeling the thin stream of qi that had accumulated during his meditation. It was weak. Unstable. A flickering candle in a hurricane.

"Find an object. Something small. Something you can hold."

He opened his eyes and grabbed a pen from his desk. Cheap plastic, blue ink, barely functional.

"Good. Now push a single strand of qi into it. Not your full stream—one thread. Imagine it as a needle of light entering the plastic."

Kai focused. He pulled a strand of qi from his dantian—it felt like tearing a hair from his own body—and guided it toward the pen.

The plastic cracked.

"Too much. Try again."

He pulled another strand. Smaller this time. Thinner. He guided it with agonizing care, visualizing it as a single filament of spider silk entering the pen's casing.

The plastic glowed.

[QI INFUSION: SUCCESS]

[ITEM: PLASTIC PEN (QI-INFUSED)]

[EFFECT: +1 DURABILITY. +5% INK ACCURACY.]

Kai stared at the pen. It was glowing softly, a faint blue light emanating from within the plastic. He could feel the qi inside it, stable and contained.

"Good." Mentor's voice carried a hint of approval. "You learn quickly. Now do it again. And again. Until you can infuse an object without thinking."

He grabbed another pen. Then a pencil. A paperclip. A book. Each time, the process got smoother. The qi flowed more naturally. By the time he'd infused his tenth object, the technique felt almost instinctive.

"Now," Mentor said, her voice dropping to a whisper, "infuse yourself."

Kai's hands stopped. "What?"

"Qi Infusion is not limited to objects. You can infuse your own body—your muscles, your bones, your meridians. It will temporarily strengthen you, allowing you to hit harder, move faster, endure more."

"Is that safe?"

"Nothing about tonight is safe. But if you face the Eater without reinforcement, you will die in seconds."

Kai took a breath. Then another. He closed his eyes and reached into his dantian, pulling a strand of qi and guiding it toward his right arm.

The sensation was electric. His muscles spasmed, then relaxed, then *tightened* with a strength that felt alien. He opened his eyes and looked at his arm—it was glowing faintly, the veins visible beneath his skin like rivers of light.

"Good. Now your legs. Your core. Your—"

A crash from outside.

Kai's head snapped toward the window. The street below was chaos. People running. Screaming. And in the distance, something massive and dark was moving through the city, its shape wrong in ways that hurt to look at.

The Eater.

It was already feeding.

"Time is up," Mentor said. "Go. Or stay. But make your choice now."

Kai looked at his glowing arm. At the pen on his desk, infused with qi. At the blood-red moon hanging over the city like a wound in the sky.

He grabbed his phone, shoved it in his pocket, and headed for the door.

"I'm going."

"Then remember this, Kai." Mentor's voice followed him out. "The System gave everyone a class. It gave you a path. But what you become—Architect or Eater, builder or destroyer—that choice is yours alone."

He hit the stairs at a run.

Behind him, the Eater's shadow grew larger, and the moon dripped crimson light onto a world that would never be the same.

title: The First Blood
content:
The broadcast hung in the air like a guillotine blade.

[WORLD INTEGRATION PHASE ONE: ACTIVE]
[ALL HOSTILES HAVE BEEN SPAWNED]
[OBJECTIVE: SURVIVE]

Kai's blood went cold. *Hostiles? Spawned?*

His phone buzzed again, but he didn't look at it. He was too focused on the feeling spreading through his qi sense—a ripple, like a stone dropped into a still pond, emanating from somewhere outside his apartment complex. The ripple carried something with it. Something *wrong*.

He threw off his sheets and stumbled to the window.

The street below looked normal. Cars parked along the curb. A stray dog sniffing at a trash can. The convenience store on the corner with its flickering neon sign. But the ripple was getting stronger, and Kai could feel it now—not just with qi sense, but with his skin, his bones, his dantian.

*Something is coming.*

His phone buzzed again. And again. He finally looked down.

*Dude is anyone else seeing this shit*

*There's a message in the sky*

*WHAT THE FUCK IS THAT*

Kai looked up.

Above the city, impossibly high, words burned across the sky like a scar on reality itself. Not clouds. Not a hologram. Characters of pure golden light, each one the size of a skyscraper, forming a message that every person on Earth could read regardless of language:

[WELCOME TO THE INTEGRATION]
[POPULATION: 7,900,000,000]
[CLASSES ASSIGNED: 100%]
[TUTORIAL COMPLETION: 94.7%]
[INFECTION RATE: 0.08%]

*Infection rate?*

The golden characters flickered, then shifted:

[FIRST WAVE INCOMING]
[ESTIMATED ARRIVAL: 00:03:47]
[PREPARE]

Three minutes.

Kai's mind raced. He didn't have weapons. He didn't have armor. He had a class that the System didn't understand and a cultivation technique that felt like trying to knit a sweater with his own nerves.

His phone rang. Mom.

He answered. "I'm fine. Stay inside. Barricade the door."

"Kai, what's happening? Your father is—"

"Mom, listen to me. Whatever you see, don't go outside. Lock everything. Hide in the basement if you can."

"But—"

"I have to go. I love you."

He hung up before she could respond. There was no time for goodbyes.

Kai pulled on his shoes—sneakers, worn at the heels—and grabbed the only thing that could serve as a weapon: a steel pipe he'd found in the alley months ago, kept under his bed for "just in case." He'd never imagined "just in case" would mean *this*.

A notification appeared:

[TUTORIAL REWARDS PENDING]
[MENTOR'S NOTE: Your foundation is incomplete. Do not engage in combat unless absolutely necessary. Fleeing is a valid strategy.]

*Great advice,* Kai thought bitterly. *Where exactly am I supposed to flee to?*

He opened his status screen, desperate for any advantage:

[NAME: Kai Chen]
[CLASS: Dao Architect]
[LEVEL: 0]
[CULTIVATION: Qi Condensation - Stage 1 (Unstable)]
[MERIDIANS: 3 Open (of 108)]
[DANTIAN: Forming (17% stability)]

[KNOWN TECHNIQUES:]
- Basic Qi Circulation (Novice - 23% mastery)

[CLASS ABILITIES:]
- Foundation Analysis (Passive)
- Meridian Mapping (Passive)
- ??? (Locked)

Locked. Of course.

The countdown in the sky read 02:14.

Kai moved.

He didn't know where he was going, but staying in his apartment felt like sitting in a trap. He needed higher ground, better visibility, options. The roof.

He grabbed his keys, shoved his phone in his pocket, and ran.

The hallway was empty. Most of his neighbors were probably still in their apartments, glued to their phones, watching the chaos unfold on social media. A few doors were open, revealing families huddled together, arguing, crying, praying.

A woman rushed past him, dragging a child by the hand. "Move! Get to the shelter!"

*Shelter?* There was no shelter. Not for this.

Kai hit the stairwell and climbed. Three floors. Four. His legs burned, his lungs ached, and his dantian pulsed with every heartbeat, the qi inside him sloshing like water in a half-empty cup.

He burst through the roof door and onto the gravel-covered surface.

The view was devastating.

Across the city, hundreds—no, *thousands*—of cracks were opening in the air. Not in buildings, not in the ground, but in the fabric of reality itself. They looked like shattered windows, their edges sharp and jagged, leaking a faint purple mist.

And from those cracks, things were emerging.

Kai's qi sense screamed.

He saw them clearly, even from half a kilometer away: creatures that defied simple description. Some were humanoid, with too many joints and skin that rippled like water. Others were insectoid, chitinous, with mandibles that clicked and eyes that reflected light in the wrong spectrum. A few were barely visible, little more than shadows that moved against the light.

The first screams began.

They were distant at first, muffled by distance and traffic noise. But they multiplied. Spread. Grew into a chorus of terror that echoed off the buildings like a symphony of nightmares.

01:47.

Kai gripped his pipe and watched.

On the street below, a crack opened directly above the convenience store. A creature dropped through—*fell* through, landing on the asphalt with a wet crunch. It was roughly the size of a dog, but its body was segmented like a centipede's, and its head was a mass of writhing tendrils.

The convenience store clerk stumbled out, holding a broom like a weapon.

The creature turned.

The clerk swung.

The tendrils *moved*.

Kai looked away. He'd seen enough. He couldn't save that man. He couldn't save anyone. He could barely save himself.

*Focus.*

He needed information. He needed to understand what these things were, how they worked, what they wanted. He reached out with his qi sense, trying to map the creature below—

And flinched.

The creature's qi signature was *wrong*. It didn't flow like normal life force. It was jagged, chaotic, full of static and interference. It felt like a corrupted file, a piece of data that had been scrambled and forced into a shape it was never meant to take.

[SKILL ACTIVATED: FOUNDATION ANALYSIS]
[ANALYZING...]
[HOSTILE TYPE: Void-Touched]
[THREAT LEVEL: Moderate]
[WEAKNESS: Core destabilization. Strike center mass with concentrated qi.]

*Concentrated qi.* Right. Because Kai definitely had that mastered.

00:42.

More cracks opened. More creatures poured through. The city was becoming a war zone in real time, and Kai was standing on a roof with a steel pipe and a half-formed cultivation base.

His phone buzzed one last time.

He checked it, expecting another frantic message from his friends or family.

It was from an unknown number.

*You are not supposed to exist.*

Kai stared at the message. His blood went cold in a way that had nothing to do with the monsters below.

He typed back: *Who is this?*

The reply came instantly:

*The one who will fix the System's mistake.*

Another notification appeared, but this one wasn't from the System. It was a DM, a direct message that bypassed all normal channels, appearing directly in his field of vision:

[CLASSIFIED CLASS DETECTED]
[DAO ARCHITECT]
[STATUS: ANOMALOUS]
[PRIORITY: TERMINATE]

Kai's heart stopped.

The message vanished, replaced by a new one:

[YOU HAVE BEEN MARKED]
[OTHER ARCHITECTS WILL BE NOTIFIED]
[SURVIVAL EXPECTANCY: 0.3%]

Then, in the distance, he saw it.

A crack unlike any other. It didn't open slowly, like the others. It *tore* across the sky, a wound in reality that bled purple fire. And from that wound, something stepped through.

It was humanoid. Tall. Clad in armor that seemed to absorb light. Its face was obscured by a helmet with no visor, no openings, nothing but smooth, dark metal.

In its hand, it held a blade of crystallized qi.

And it was looking directly at Kai.

The countdown in the sky reached zero.

[FIRST WAVE: ACTIVE]
[VARIANT DETECTED: ARCHITECT HUNTER]
[PRIORITY TARGET: KAI CHEN]

The armored figure raised its blade.

And *moved*.

It crossed half the distance in a single step, its body blurring with speed that shouldn't have been possible. Kai's instincts screamed at him to move, to dodge, to do *something*—

But his body refused.

The blade came down.

And the world went white.

title: The System's First Patch
content:
Kai never sent the message.

His phone buzzed again, but he barely registered it. The System notification hung in his vision, pulsing with an urgency that made his newly awakened meridians thrum in response.

[WORLD INTEGRATION COMPLETE]
[ALL SYSTEMS NOMINAL]
[INITIATING PHASE TWO: AWAKENING]

He sat up so fast his head spun. The qi in his dantian sloshed like water in a half-empty cup, and he had to brace himself against the wall until the dizziness passed.

Phase Two. Awakening.

What did that even mean?

His phone buzzed again. Then again. Then a constant stream of vibrations that told him something was happening beyond his apartment walls. He grabbed the phone, thumb swiping through the messages with increasing urgency.

*The sky just turned purple for three seconds. Anyone else see that?*

*My class just updated. I have new skills now. NEW SKILLS. Like I was missing half my kit before.*

*Is anyone else's Interface glitching? I'm seeing Chinese characters mixed in with the English.*

Kai's blood ran cold.

Chinese characters.

He pulled up his own Interface, and there it was—faint, almost ghostlike, but unmistakable. Characters that looked like they'd been carved into existence by a calligrapher's brush, layered beneath the crisp digital text of the System. [Dao Architect] shimmered with both scripts, the Chinese characters bleeding through like ink on wet paper.

*You see it,* Mentor's voice echoed in his mind. Not a message. Not a notification. Her actual voice, as if she were standing beside him. *The System was never meant for this world alone.*

Kai's fingers tightened on his phone. *What do you mean?*

*A convergence. Two systems, two realities, colliding. The one you call the System. And another, far older, that remembers what the world was before code.*

Before he could ask more, a scream tore through the wall.

Not a shout of surprise. Not a cry of fear. A scream of pure, unadulterated agony.

Kai was moving before he thought, his body responding with a speed that wasn't his own. He burst through his apartment door, down the hall, following the sound to unit 4B. The door was unlocked. He shoved it open.

The man inside—Mr. Chen, the elderly retired accountant who fed the stray cats—was on his knees, hands clawing at his chest. His skin glowed. Literally glowed, a sickly green light pulsing beneath his flesh like he'd swallowed a firefly.

"Mr. Chen!" Kai dropped beside him, reaching out with his qi sense.

What he found made him recoil.

Mr. Chen's meridians were shredded. Not blocked, not damaged—torn apart from the inside. Qi flooded through him in uncontrolled torrents, eating through his organs, his bones, his very soul. The man's eyes rolled back, foam gathering at the corners of his mouth.

"Healer," Kai muttered, fumbling for his phone. "I need to call for a—"

[CLASS: HEALER]
[REQUIREMENTS: LEVEL 5, WISDOM 20, LIGHT AFFINITY]
[YOU DO NOT MEET REQUIREMENTS]

"There's no time," Mentor said, and this time she was real—a golden shimmer coalescing beside him, her form translucent but present. "His cultivation base is collapsing. If you do nothing, he dies in thirty seconds."

"Thirty seconds?! What can I do in thirty seconds?"

"Guide the qi. You have the tools. Use them."

Kai looked at Mr. Chen. The man's glow was intensifying, his screams gurgling into wet, choked sounds. His heart was failing. His lungs were filling with fluid. The qi in his body had become a weapon, and it was killing him from the inside.

*Guide the qi.*

Kai had no training. No experience. He'd spent six hours learning to circulate qi through his own meridians, and he'd barely managed that without dying. Now he was supposed to do it for someone else?

He placed his hands on Mr. Chen's chest.

The moment his palms touched the man's skin, he felt it—the roiling chaos of uncontrolled qi, wild and hungry, devouring everything it touched. It surged toward Kai's hands like a starving animal, desperate for escape.

Kai didn't think. He acted.

He opened his dantian.

The qi slammed into him like a freight train. His vision went white. His own meridians screamed in protest as foreign energy forced its way through channels that were barely stable. He felt his foundation crack, felt the delicate structure Mentor had helped him build begin to splinter.

But he held on.

He cycled the qi. Not his own—Mr. Chen's. He pulled it from the man's shredded meridians, drew it into himself, filtered it through his own circulation, and pushed it back. Slowly. Carefully. Like trying to funnel a hurricane through a drinking straw.

The glow began to fade.

Mr. Chen's screams quieted to whimpers, then to ragged breaths. His heart steadied. His lungs cleared. The green light under his skin dimmed, then vanished entirely.

Kai collapsed.

His head hit the floor, and for a moment, everything went dark. When his vision returned, Mentor was standing over him, her expression unreadable.

"You saved him," she said.

"Did I?" Kai's voice came out as a croak. "Because it feels like I just died."

"You came close." She knelt beside him, and for the first time, he saw something like concern flicker in her golden eyes. "Your foundation is fractured. You used a technique you had no right to know, let alone perform. If your cultivation base were a bridge, you just drove a tank across it."

"Great. So what happens now?"

"Now?" She looked up, through the ceiling, as if seeing something far beyond. "Now the world wakes up to what it truly is."

The ground shook.

Not a tremor—a full-scale earthquake that sent cracks racing across the walls. Outside, the sky turned colors that had no name. Purple bled into gold bled into black, and somewhere in the distance, Kai heard a sound like a bell being struck by a hammer.

[SYSTEM BROADCAST: PHASE TWO — AWAKENING]
[ALL HUMANS WILL NOW RECEIVE SECONDARY CLASSES]
[ALL HUMANS WILL NOW DETECT QI]
[ALL HUMANS WILL NOW EXPERIENCE CULTIVATION]

The notifications scrolled so fast Kai couldn't read them all. His phone exploded with messages, but he couldn't look away from the window. The sky was splitting. Not metaphorically—literally splitting, a crack of pure white light tearing through reality like a scar across the heavens.

Through that crack, he saw something.

A mountain. Floating. Massive, ancient, covered in pagodas and temples that glowed with inner light. A city of jade and gold, suspended in a void that held no stars.

*The Heavenly Realm,* Mentor said, and for the first time, her voice held something like awe. *They're coming through.*

"Who's coming through?"

She turned to face him, and her eyes—those impossible golden eyes—held a gravity that made his blood freeze.

"The ones who built the System. The ones who made the Dao. The ones who have been waiting for this moment since before humanity crawled out of the mud." She paused. "And they're not coming to help."

Another earthquake. Another scream from somewhere in the building. From outside, the sound of car alarms and panicked shouting.

Kai pushed himself to his feet, swaying, his dantian throbbing with every heartbeat. Mr. Chen was unconscious but alive, his breathing steady. He'd done that. He'd saved a man's life using nothing but qi and instinct.

But as he looked at the crack in the sky, at the floating mountain growing larger by the second, he realized that saving one life meant nothing.

Because the world was about to end.

"Kai."

He turned. Mentor stood in the doorway, her form flickering, her voice urgent.

"You have two choices. Stay here, watch the world burn, and hope the Heavenly Realm decides to be merciful."

"Or?"

She extended her hand. In her palm sat a jade slip, ancient and carved with characters that shifted as he watched.

"Or you come with me. Learn what the Dao truly is. Become strong enough to stand against those who are coming."

Kai stared at the jade slip. His phone buzzed again. His mother's name flashed on the screen.

*Answer it,* a voice whispered. *Stay safe. Hide. Let someone else fight.*

But he'd seen what the System did to people. He'd felt what uncontrolled qi could do. He'd saved Mr. Chen by the skin of his teeth, and he knew—knew with a certainty that settled into his bones like cold iron—that this was only the beginning.

He thought of his mother. His friends. The billions of people who had no idea what was about to hit them.

He took the jade slip.

The moment his fingers touched it, the world dissolved.

His apartment vanished. The city vanished. The sky, the cracks, the floating mountain—all of it replaced by an endless white void.

And standing in front of him, no longer translucent, no longer flickering, was Mentor. Fully real. Fully present. Dressed in flowing robes of gold and white, her hair cascading down her back like a river of ink.

"Welcome," she said, "to the real tutorial."

Behind her, the void split open, revealing a path lined with standing stones, each one carved with characters that burned with inner fire. At the end of the path, a mountain rose. Not the one from the sky—this one was older, darker, pulsing with a power that made Kai's qi sense recoil.

"What is this place?" he asked.

Mentor's smile held no warmth.

"This is where the Heavenly Dao was forged. This is where the first cultivators learned to cheat death. And this is where you will learn to do the same." She turned, walking toward the path. "Assuming you survive."

Kai followed.

Behind him, the void sealed shut.

The world he knew was gone.

And somewhere in the distance, the floating mountain touched down on Earth.

title: The Price of Ambition
content:
Kai's finger hovered over the send button when the broadcast hit him like a physical force. The words burned in his vision, golden and absolute:

[SYSTEM BROADCAST: TUTORIAL PHASE COMPLETE]
[ALL CLASSES HAVE BEEN ASSIGNED]
[WORLD INTEGRATION INITIATING]

The air changed.

It wasn't a metaphor. The air literally changed, growing thick and charged, like the pressure before a thunderstorm. Kai's newly awakened qi sense screamed warnings as the world around him transformed. The water stain on his ceiling rippled. The cracks in his walls deepened, then sealed themselves. The cockroach behind the wall stopped moving, then its life force... evolved.

"What the—"

He threw himself out of bed, landing in a crouch as his apartment shifted around him. The cheap linoleum floor smoothed into polished stone. The flickering fluorescent light above stabilized, then brightened to an almost surgical white. The walls grew solid, their surface taking on a subtle sheen that seemed to absorb and redirect light.

His phone buzzed again. Then again. Then so many times it became a single continuous vibration.

Kai snatched it up, reading through the chaos:

*The buildings are changing! My apartment just turned into some kind of fortress!*

*There's a mana crystal growing out of my kitchen counter. Is that normal?*

*Anyone else's toilet turn into a meditation seat? I'm not complaining, but WHAT THE HELL*

Kai looked around his transformed apartment. His kitchen had become something out of a cultivator's dream—a simple stone hearth with a cauldron that radiated faint heat, shelves lined with ceramic jars labeled in characters he could somehow read: *Qi-Infused Rice. Spirit Herbs. Purified Water.*

His bedroom had become a meditation chamber. The bed was gone, replaced by a raised platform with a silk cushion. The walls were inscribed with diagrams of meridians and pressure points, glowing faintly as if lit from within.

*This isn't integration,* he realized. *This is terraforming.*

A knock at his door.

Kai's hand shot toward the knife he kept in his nightstand drawer—but his nightstand was gone, replaced by a wooden cabinet that opened to reveal a jian sword, its blade etched with flowing characters. He grabbed it, the weight familiar in his hand despite never having held a sword before.

"Who is it?"

"Neighbor from 3B." The voice was female, tense, urgent. "You felt that too, right? The change?"

Kai approached the door cautiously. Through the wood, his qi sense detected a single life force—bright, sharp, tinged with... lightning? He opened the door.

The woman standing in the hallway was in her mid-twenties, Asian-American, with short-cropped hair and eyes that crackled with actual sparks. She wore pajamas and held a kitchen knife that had been transmuted into something resembling a Daoist talisman blade.

"Name's Lin Mei," she said, not lowering her weapon. "Got the Lightning Mage class during the tutorial. You?"

"Kai. Architect."

She blinked. "Never heard of that class. What does it do?"

"I'm still figuring that out."

"Great. So we're all stumbling in the dark." She gestured down the hallway. "The building's changed. Stairwell's gone—there's a spiral staircase now, but it feels wrong. I can sense something moving on the lower floors. Not human."

Kai stepped into the hallway. His old apartment building had become something else entirely. The walls were carved stone, not drywall. Hanging lanterns with blue flames illuminated the corridor. The doors of neighboring apartments had transformed into heavy wooden slabs, each marked with different symbols.

He recognized some of them. The symbol for *metal* on 3A's door. *Water* on 3C. *Wood* on 3D.

"What's on your door?" Lin Mei asked.

Kai turned. His door bore a symbol he'd never seen before—a circle divided into two interlocking spirals, one light, one dark, with a single dot of gold at the center.

"No idea," he said honestly.

A scream echoed from somewhere below. Then another. Then the sound of something shattering.

Lin Mei's eyes flared with electricity. "We need to move. Check on the other neighbors, see who survived the integration, who got classes, who's still normal."

"Normal might not exist anymore."

"Then we adapt." She started down the hallway, her bare feet making no sound on the stone floor. "You feel that? The qi in the air? It's thicker than it was during the tutorial. The System isn't just giving us powers—it's changing the environment to match."

Kai followed, his sword held at the ready. "You've been in contact with others? People who understand what's happening?"

"A few. There's a forum that appeared in my System interface. Global chat. It's chaos." She glanced back at him. "Most people are celebrating. The Warriors are getting stronger, the Mages are unlocking spells, the Healers are already setting up clinics. But there's a thread you need to see."

She stopped at the top of the spiral staircase, her expression grim. "The unclassed."

"Unclassed?"

"People who didn't get any class during the tutorial. They're calling them 'mortals' now." Her voice hardened. "They're not getting any benefits from the integration. Their apartments didn't transform. They can't sense qi. And they're terrified."

Kai's blood ran cold. "What happens to them?"

"Nobody knows. But there are already groups forming—people who think the unclassed are 'defective' and should be... dealt with."

A chill that had nothing to do with temperature ran down his spine. He remembered Mentor's words: *The System is the Heavenly Dao reborn as code. And the Heavenly Dao has always demanded balance.*

"What about the monsters?" he asked, forcing his mind back to the immediate threat. "You said you sensed something below."

"Come see for yourself."

They descended the spiral staircase. The stairs themselves seemed to shift as they walked, adjusting to their footsteps, guiding them toward a destination that wasn't entirely physical. Kai's qi sense expanded, mapping the transformed building. He counted life forces—some bright, some dim, some flickering with the unstable energy of newly awakened powers.

And below, in what used to be the lobby, a cluster of life forces that felt *wrong*. They pulsed with a dark, hungry qi that made his dantian recoil.

They reached the second floor landing. The staircase continued down, but Lin Mei stopped, pointing through an archway that led to what had been the building's common room.

Kai looked.

The common room had become a cavernous hall, its ceiling lost in shadow. In the center, a crystal pillar rose from the floor, pulsing with an inner light. And around it, creatures moved.

They were humanoid, barely. Their skin was pale grey, their joints bent at wrong angles, their eyes glowing with a faint red light. They moved with the jerky, uncoordinated motions of puppets, circling the pillar as if drawn to it.

"How many?" Kai whispered.

"Twelve that I can see. Probably more in the shadows." Lin Mei's hands crackled with electricity. "They appeared during the integration. The System called them 'Qi-Stripped.' Said they're what happens when a human's dantian is destroyed during class assignment."

Kai's stomach lurched. "They were people?"

"Were. They're not anymore. The System classified them as 'hostile entities.'" She met his eyes. "Which means we have to kill them."

"I'm not—"

"They're already dead, Architect." Her voice was flat, cold, professional. "Whatever they were, it's gone. What's left is a shell animated by corrupted qi. If we don't clear them, they'll spread. More integrations, more transformations, more monsters."

Kai gripped his sword, feeling the unfamiliar weight. He'd never killed anything in his life. Not even a bug, if he could avoid it.

But Mentor's training had prepared him for this. He could feel the qi flowing through his meridians, feel the techniques she'd taught him waiting in his spiritual sea. He wasn't a Warrior, but he wasn't defenseless.

"Fine," he said, surprising himself with the steadiness in his voice. "But we do this smart. I can sense their qi patterns—they're drawn to the pillar, but they're also defending it. If we can disrupt the pillar, we might be able to scatter them."

"Or they might all attack at once."

"That too."

Lin Mei cracked her neck, and lightning danced across her skin. "I like the way you think. On three?"

"No. On my signal." Kai closed his eyes, reaching out with his qi sense, feeling the flow of energy in the hall. The pillar was a nexus, drawing power from the building, from the earth below, from… something else. Something vast and ancient that pressed against the edges of his perception.

*Mentor,* he thought, *if you can hear me, I could really use some advice right now.*

Silence.

Then, faintly, like an echo from a great distance: *Trust your foundation. The Dao does not abandon those who walk its path.*

He opened his eyes.

"Now."

They moved. Lin Mei's lightning struck first, a blinding arc that slammed into the nearest creature and sent it flying. Kai followed, his sword channeling qi in a pattern his body remembered even if his mind didn't.

The first creature turned to face him, its red eyes locking onto his. It opened its mouth and screamed—a sound like tearing metal.

Kai swung.

The blade connected. Qi exploded outward. And the creature dissolved into motes of dark energy that scattered into the air.

*One down.*

The other eleven turned as one, their attention snapping to him with terrifying precision.

*Eleven to go.*

Lin Mei's lightning lit up the hall, and Kai dove into the fray, his sword singing through the air.

---

Twenty minutes later, Kai stood panting in the center of the hall, surrounded by dissipating dark energy. Lin Mei leaned against the crystal pillar, her lightning flickering weakly.

"That was…" she started.

"Terrifying," he finished.

"Was going to say 'educational,' but sure."

Kai looked at his hands. They were steady, despite everything. His qi was depleted but not empty, and his meridians still hummed with residual energy.

*I survived.*

But as the dark energy cleared, something caught his eye. The pillar. Its light was fading, revealing something inside.

A figure.

Human. Female. Suspended in the crystal as if preserved in amber.

Lin Mei saw it too. "What the hell…"

Kai stepped closer, his heart pounding. The figure's features were familiar. Too familiar.

Long black hair. High cheekbones. Golden eyes that, even closed, seemed to hold infinite wisdom.

*Mentor.*

The crystal cracked.

And her eyes snapped open.

title: The First Fracture
content:
Kai stood in the middle of his apartment, phone forgotten in his hand, as the world seemed to hold its breath.

[SYSTEM BROADCAST: TUTORIAL PHASE COMPLETE]

[ALL CLASSES HAVE BEEN ASSIGNED]

[WORLD INTEGRATION: BEGINNING PHASE 2]

[NEW FEATURES UNLOCKED: PARTIES, GUILDS, DUNGEONS]

The notifications hung in his vision like neon signs, impossible to ignore. Outside his window, the city had gone quiet. No cars honking. No distant sirens. Just an eerie stillness that made his skin crawl.

His phone buzzed again. Then again. Then it didn't stop.

*Kai, did you see that?*

*Parties? Guilds? This is getting real.*

*Anyone want to form a group? I'm a Level 2 Mage with Fireball and Spark.*

He scrolled through the messages mechanically, his mind elsewhere. The tutorial had felt like a lifetime, but the real world had only moved forward six hours. Six hours since the System arrived. Six hours since everything changed.

His qi sense pulsed outward, and he felt them—dozens, hundreds, thousands of people awakening to their new power. Some were bright, already strengthening. Others were dim, their classes weak or poorly suited. A few...

He paused, focusing on a signature two blocks away. It burned like a bonfire, fierce and uncontrolled. Whoever that was, they'd gotten something powerful. Something dangerous.

*Like me.*

Another notification appeared, this one personal:

[CLASS HIDDEN: TRUE NATURE CONCEALED FROM SYSTEM]

[DETECTED ANOMALY: USER'S CLASS DOES NOT MATCH KNOWN PARAMETERS]

[WARNING: UNAUTHORIZED CLASS TYPES MAY ATTRACT ATTENTION]

Kai's blood ran cold. *May attract attention.* From what? The System itself? Or something else entirely?

His phone rang. Unknown number.

He answered without thinking. "Hello?"

"Kai Chen?" A woman's voice, professional but strained. "This is Agent Zhang from the newly formed Bureau of Anomalous Affairs. We need you to come in for assessment and classification."

"I'm sorry, what?"

"The System has assigned classes to every human on Earth. We're cataloguing all class types to ensure proper integration and resource allocation. Your class has been flagged as... unusual."

*Flagged.* Of course. In a world suddenly obsessed with RPG mechanics, an unclassifiable class would stand out like a sore thumb.

"I don't think—" Kai started.

"This isn't optional, Mr. Chen. All anomalous individuals are required to report within 24 hours. Failure to comply will result in—"

He hung up.

His hands were shaking. Not from fear—from something else. The qi in his dantian was churning, restless, responding to his emotional state like a living thing.

*I need to move.*

He grabbed his jacket, stuffed a few essentials into his backpack, and headed for the door. Before he could open it, another notification hit him:

[SYSTEM ALERT: USER DETECTED - DAO ARCHITECT]

[SYSTEM AI HAS BEEN NOTIFIED]

[EXTERNAL SCRUTINY INCREASING]

*External scrutiny.* The words echoed in his mind as he burst into the hallway.

His neighbor Mrs. Chen was standing by her door, phone pressed to her ear. She looked up as he passed, eyes wide. "Kai! Did you hear? The government is—"

"Sorry, Mrs. Chen. Gotta run."

He took the stairs three at a time, his enhanced senses picking up every sound—the frantic phone calls from other apartments, the crying of children, the low hum of qi signatures all around him.

The street outside was chaos.

People stood in clusters, comparing class notifications, showing off new abilities. A teenager near the bus stop was floating a few inches off the ground, laughing hysterically. An older man had manifested a sword made of light, swinging it experimentally. And in the distance, sirens wailed.

Kai pulled up his hood and kept walking, head down, trying to blend in.

It didn't work.

Every few steps, his qi sense picked up someone glancing his way. Not suspiciously—curiously. Like they could feel something different about him without understanding what.

*They can sense me.* The realization hit like a brick. *Not the class itself, but the qi. It's foreign to them. Unfamiliar.*

A hand grabbed his shoulder.

He spun, instinct taking over. Qi flared through his meridians, and he found himself facing a man in his late twenties, athletic build, wearing a tracksuit that looked expensive.

"Easy," the man said, holding up his hands. "I'm not here to fight. Name's Zhao. Level 3 Brawler."

Kai forced himself to relax, though his qi still hummed with tension. "What do you want?"

"Straight to the point. I like that." Zhao smiled, but it didn't reach his eyes. "You're the one with the weird class, right? The one they're looking for?"

"I don't know what you're talking about."

"Don't play dumb. I've got a skill—Sense Intent. It's not much, but it tells me when someone's hiding something." Zhao's smile widened. "You're glowing like a Christmas tree, my friend. Whatever class you got, it's not normal."

Kai's jaw tightened. "What's your angle?"

"Simple. I'm forming a party. A small one, just people who slipped through the cracks. People with classes the System doesn't understand." Zhao leaned closer, lowering his voice. "There's more of us than you think. And the Bureau isn't the only group looking for us."

"Us?"

"The unclassifiable. The anomalies. The ones who got something the System didn't plan for." Zhao's eyes gleamed. "You're not alone, Kai. But if you don't want to end up in a government lab, you need to find allies. Fast."

Kai studied him, weighing his options. The man was either telling the truth or running a very elaborate con. His qi sense couldn't detect deception—not yet, at least.

"Why should I trust you?"

"Because I found you before the Bureau did. And because I have something you need." Zhao pulled out his phone, showing a map with a single blinking dot. "There's a safe house. Other anomalies. A mentor who understands what we're dealing with."

A mentor. The word sent a jolt through Kai's chest. He thought of Mentor, of her golden eyes and cryptic warnings. Was this connected? Or was Zhao leading him into a trap?

"I'll think about it," he said finally.

"Don't think too long. The Bureau has your name, your address, your class status. They'll find you within hours." Zhao pocketed his phone. "When you change your mind, follow the signal. You'll find us."

He turned and walked away, disappearing into the crowd.

Kai stood frozen, the weight of the decision pressing down on him. Go with the mysterious stranger, risk walking into a trap. Stay in the city, risk the Bureau. Or run, risk everything.

His phone buzzed again. Another unknown number.

He silenced it without looking.

The crowd around him had started to thin, people heading home or toward the growing clusters forming in parks and plazas. The System was bringing people together, creating new social structures from the ground up.

And Kai was an outlier. A variable the system didn't know how to process.

He started walking, no destination in mind, just moving. His qi sense expanded, mapping the city's spiritual landscape. Most signatures were weak, new, unstable. But there were exceptions.

One, in particular, caught his attention.

It was coming from the direction of the old library, three blocks away. A signature that felt... familiar. Not in the way of a person he knew, but in the way of something he'd encountered before.

*The tutorial.*

His feet carried him toward it before his mind caught up.

The library was a two-story building, decades old, its brick facade weathered by time. But as Kai approached, he felt it—a thin veil of qi wrapped around the structure, invisible to normal eyes but unmistakable to his senses.

*Someone's warded this place. Recently.*

He pushed open the heavy wooden door and stepped inside.

The interior was dark, the lights off, the shelves casting long shadows in the dim afternoon light filtering through dusty windows. But at the far end, near the history section, a figure stood waiting.

It was a woman. Tall, dark hair, sharp features. She wore a simple gray dress that seemed to absorb the light around her.

"Kai Chen," she said, her voice soft but carrying. "I've been expecting you."

His qi sense flared, probing her—and hit a wall. She was shielded, her signature hidden behind layers of technique he didn't recognize.

"Who are you?"

"A friend. Or an enemy. That depends on your choices." She stepped forward, and the shadows seemed to bend around her. "I know what you experienced in the tutorial. I know about Mentor. And I know what you're becoming."

Kai's heart hammered. "How?"

"Because I went through the same thing." She extended her hand, and a notification appeared in his vision:

[SYSTEM ALERT: USER DETECTED - DAO ARCHITECT]

[SECOND DAO ARCHITECT FOUND]

[WORLD FRACTURE IMMINENT]

He stared at the words, his mind reeling.

*Second Dao Architect. There's another one.*

"The System didn't just give out one anomaly," the woman said, reading his expression. "It gave out several. We are the ones who received the classes it couldn't categorize. The ones who broke its rules."

She took another step closer, her eyes locking onto his.

"And we are the ones it wants to destroy."

Before Kai could respond, the library's front door slammed open. Boots stomped on the hardwood floor. Voices shouted commands.

"Bureau of Anomalous Affairs! Nobody move!"

The woman smiled, cold and knowing. "Your time's up, Kai. Decide now—come with me, or let them take you."

He looked between her and the approaching agents. The woman's qi was powerful, controlled, ancient. The agents were armed with weapons he couldn't identify, their own classes active and ready.

A choice between the devil and the deep sea.

The woman's hand remained extended, waiting.

And Kai realized he had nowhere left to run.

title: Chapter 10
content:
CHAPTER 10: The Price of Foundations

Kai stared at the System notification until it faded.

*All classes assigned. World integration complete.*

He should have felt relief. The tutorial was over. He'd survived. He had a class—whatever that class actually was. But instead of relief, he felt a cold knot tightening in his chest.

The qi in his meridians pulsed, responding to his unease.

His phone buzzed again. Then again. Then the buzzing became a continuous vibration as messages flooded in from every group chat, every contact, every social media platform he'd ever used.

*The System just announced something about "World Event"*

*Anyone else get a quest?*

*I'm looking at a floating golden text that says "Tutorial Complete. Welcome to the New World." IS THIS REAL?*

Kai's blood went cold.

He pulled up the global chat—a feature that hadn't existed before the tutorial. Thousands of messages scrolled past every second, a river of panic and excitement and confusion.

*Socal: There's a FUCKING MONSTER in my backyard. It's like a giant wolf but made of crystal.*

*Tokyo: Same here. Crystal boar just smashed through a convenience store.*

*London: The sky is purple. Is the sky supposed to be purple?*

*Moscow: My apartment building just turned into a dungeon. I'm not joking. The walls are made of flesh.*

Kai threw off his sheets and stumbled to the window.

Outside, the world looked normal. Cars still moved on the streets below. People still walked on the sidewalks. The morning sun cast long shadows across the cracked pavement of his neighborhood.

But something was *wrong*.

He reached out with his qi sense, and the world exploded into color. The buildings weren't just buildings anymore—they were structures of spiritual energy overlaid on physical matter. The people weren't just people—they were walking constellations of qi, some bright and stable, others flickering and unstable.

And in the distance, he could feel *pressure*.

Something massive. Something ancient. Something that had just *woken up*.

[QUEST RECEIVED: THE AWAKENING]
[RANK: UNKNOWN]
[OBJECTIVE: SURVIVE THE FIRST WAVE]
[REWARD: VARIABLE]
[FAILURE: DEATH]

"First wave?" Kai whispered. "First wave of what?"

His phone rang. He grabbed it without looking.

"Kai? Kai, is that you?" His mother's voice was tight with fear. "There are—there are things in the street. Crystal things. They're attacking people."

"Mom, listen to me." Kai forced his voice to stay calm. "Stay inside. Lock the doors. Do not go outside no matter what you hear."

"But your father—he went to get groceries before all this started—"

"Stay inside. I'll find Dad. I promise."

He hung up before she could argue.

His hands were shaking. He could feel the qi in his meridians responding to his emotional state, growing turbulent, threatening to spiral out of control. He forced himself to breathe, to focus, to guide the energy back into its proper circulation pattern.

*Your foundation is forming,* Mentor had said. *Not yet stable, but forming.*

He needed more time. He needed to stabilize his cultivation before he faced whatever was coming.

The universe didn't care about his timetable.

A crash from outside. Screaming. The sound of shattering glass.

Kai looked out the window again. A creature stood in the middle of the street below—a wolf made of crystal, its body faceted and gleaming, its eyes empty voids that seemed to drink the light. It was easily the size of a car.

It raised its head and *howled*.

The sound wasn't audible. It was spiritual. It resonated through Kai's qi sense like a shockwave, rattling his meridians, making his dantian lurch.

And it was answered.

From every direction, howls rose in response. Some near. Some far. Some so distant they were barely perceptible, but all of them hungry.

[WAVE 1 OF 7 INCOMING]
[ESTIMATED ARRIVAL: 00:03:00]
[PREPARE]

Three minutes.

Kai's mind raced. He could stay in his apartment, barricade the door, hope the crystal wolves didn't find him. But his father was out there. His mother was alone. And if the System was sending waves, hiding wouldn't save anyone.

He grabbed his jacket—thin, useless against crystal claws—and headed for the door.

Then stopped.

*What am I doing?*

He had no weapons. No combat experience. His class was *Dao Architect*, not Warrior or Mage. He didn't even know if he could fight.

But he had qi.

He held out his hand and focused. The energy in his dantian responded, flowing through his meridians, gathering in his palm. A faint golden light flickered there, unstable, threatening to dissipate.

"Come on," he muttered. "I did it a hundred times in the tutorial."

But the tutorial had been a controlled environment. Here, with the pressure of the outside world bearing down on him, with the howls of monsters echoing through his qi sense, his concentration was fraying.

He needed a technique. Something he could use to defend himself.

Mentor had taught him meridians, circulation, foundation building. She hadn't taught him combat.

*Because you weren't ready,* he realized. *And you still aren't.*

But ready or not, the waves were coming.

He opened his apartment door and stepped into the hallway.

The building was chaos. Neighbors were shouting, doors slamming, footsteps pounding up and down the stairs. Someone was crying. Someone else was screaming about "monsters" and "the end of the world."

Kai ignored them all. He had three minutes, and he needed to find his father.

He reached out with his qi sense, searching for a familiar signature. His father's qi was weak—he'd never cultivated, never even known what cultivation was—but it was distinct. A faint, steady warmth among the chaos of the building.

*There.*

Second floor. Near the emergency exit.

Kai ran.

He took the stairs three at a time, his newly awakened body responding with a speed and agility he'd never possessed before. The qi in his meridians surged, lending him strength, pushing him faster.

He burst through the stairwell door and saw him.

His father was pressed against the wall, eyes wide, a kitchen knife clutched in trembling hands. A crystal wolf stood before him, head lowered, muscles coiled to strike.

"Dad!"

The wolf's head snapped toward Kai. Its empty eyes fixed on him, and he felt its spiritual pressure wash over him like a wave of ice.

[CRYSTAL WOLF - LEVEL 3]
[HP: 120/120]
[THREAT LEVEL: MODERATE]

Level 3. He was Level 1. And he didn't even know how to fight.

But the wolf was between him and his father. There was no choice.

Kai charged.

He didn't have a plan. He didn't have a technique. He just ran at the wolf, fist pulled back, qi flaring around his hand in an unstable corona.

The wolf lunged.

Time seemed to slow. Kai saw the creature's trajectory, saw the path its claws would take, saw the opening in its guard. His qi sense showed him the flow of energy through the wolf's body—the way its power gathered in its front legs, the way its dantian pulsed with crystal light.

And in that moment, something clicked.

He didn't dodge. He *redirected*.

His body moved on instinct, flowing around the wolf's attack like water around a stone. His hand, still wreathed in golden qi, reached out and touched the creature's flank—

And *pushed*.

Not physically. Spiritually. He pushed against the wolf's qi flow, disrupting its circulation, sending its own energy into chaos.

The wolf stumbled, its attack veering wildly off course. It crashed into the wall behind Kai, crystal shattering, howling in confusion.

Kai didn't wait. He grabbed his father's arm and dragged him toward the stairs.

"Kai—what—how—"

"Later. We need to get to Mom. Now."

They ran.

The building was falling apart around them. Crystal wolves had breached the lower floors, and the sounds of combat—screaming, crashing, the strange resonant *ping* of crystal breaking—echoed through the halls.

[SYSTEM ALERT: PLAYER DEATHS - 1,247]
[GLOBAL PROGRESS: WAVE 1 - 12% COMPLETE]

Twelve percent. And over a thousand dead.

Kai's stomach churned, but he kept running.

They reached the fourth floor. His mother's apartment. The door was intact, but Kai could hear scratching from inside—something small, something fast, something that wanted in.

He kicked the door open.

A crystal rat, barely the size of a cat, was scrabbling at the window, trying to break through. It turned as Kai entered, hissing, its crystal fangs gleaming.

[CRYSTAL RAT - LEVEL 2]
[HP: 45/45]
[THREAT LEVEL: LOW]

Low threat. But still dangerous.

Kai's mother was backed into a corner, a broom held like a spear. She was shaking, but her eyes were sharp, defiant.

"Mom, get behind me."

"Kai, what's happening? What are those things?"

"The System. It changed everything." He stepped forward, raising his hand. "I'll explain later."

The rat lunged.

Kai was ready this time. He'd felt the wolf's qi, understood how it flowed, how it could be disrupted. The rat was similar, its energy patterns crude but functional.

He reached out with his qi sense, found the rat's spiritual center, and *pushed*.

The rat's internal energy collapsed. It seized mid-leap, tumbling to the floor, twitching as its own qi devoured it from within.

[SKILL UNLOCKED: QI DISRUPTION]
[RANK: MORTAL]
[EFFECT: DISRUPT ENEMY QI FLOW. CAN CAUSE PARALYSIS, DAMAGE, OR DEATH DEPENDING ON TARGET'S CONSTITUTION.]

Kai stared at the notification, then at the twitching rat. He'd killed it. Without touching it. Without weapons.

He felt sick.

But he didn't have time to process.

[WAVE 1 - 47% COMPLETE]
[WAVE 2 INCOMING IN 00:05:00]

More were coming. Stronger ones.

And Kai could feel something else now. A presence. Massive. Ancient. The same presence he'd felt from his apartment, but closer now, pressing against the edges of his awareness.

[SYSTEM BROADCAST: WARNING]
[ANOMALOUS ENTITY DETECTED]
[CLASSIFICATION: ???]
[THREAT LEVEL: CATASTROPHIC]
[RECOMMENDATION: EVACUATE]

The sky outside turned purple.

Kai's father grabbed his shoulder. "Son, what's happening?"

Kai looked out the window. The crystal wolves in the street had stopped fighting. They were all staring at the sky, heads raised, silent.

Waiting.

And then the sky *cracked*.

A fissure opened in the fabric of reality itself, a wound in the world that bled golden light. Through that crack, Kai could see *something*—a vast, incomprehensible shape, a presence so immense that his qi sense recoiled from it like a hand from fire.

A voice spoke, and

title: Chapter 11
content:
CHAPTER 11: The Price of Progress

The broadcast hung in the air like a death sentence. Kai's fingers hovered over his phone, the unsent message still glowing on the screen. A 2% chance of dying from qi deviation. He'd dismissed it as a distant possibility, something that happened to other people in Mentor's cautionary tales.

But now, with the System's announcement thrumming through his skull, the number felt different. Heavier.

*World integration.* The words echoed in his mind. Earth was being absorbed into whatever cosmic framework the System represented. The tutorial was over. The real game—or whatever this was—had begun.

His phone buzzed again. Then again. Then a cascade of notifications that turned the screen into a strobe light. He silenced it and tossed it onto the nightstand.

Kai closed his eyes and focused inward. The qi in his dantian swirled in lazy spirals, responding to his attention like a trained pet. He traced the circulation pattern Mentor had taught him, feeling the energy flow through his meridians with growing ease. *Foundation. That's what she called it. My foundation is forming.*

But forming wasn't the same as formed.

He opened his eyes and swung his legs over the side of the bed. The floor was cold against his bare feet. He stood, testing his balance, and felt something shift in his lower abdomen—a subtle pressure, like a second heartbeat.

*I need to test myself.*

The thought came unbidden, but it felt right. Mentor had taught him the basics, but she'd made it clear that the rest was up to him. Dao Architects didn't follow a prescribed path. They *created* one.

Kai walked to his kitchenette and grabbed a glass from the cabinet. He filled it with tap water and set it on the counter. Then he extended his qi sense, feeling the molecular structure of the water, the hydrogen bonds that held it together, the faint trace of minerals dissolved within.

*Can I change it?*

He focused. Pushed his qi into the glass. The water rippled, then stilled. Nothing happened.

*Too broad. Too unfocused.*

He remembered what Mentor had said about intent. *Qi follows thought. If your mind is chaos, your qi will be chaos.*

Kai took a breath. Centered himself. Then he pictured the water freezing—not as a physical process, but as an *intention*. He imagined the molecules slowing, locking into crystalline lattices, the liquid becoming solid.

He pushed.

The glass cracked. Ice crystals spiderwebbed across the surface, then the entire thing shattered, sending shards and frozen water across the counter.

Kai stared at the mess, heart pounding. *I did that.* His qi had worked. But it had been too much, too violent, like trying to thread a needle with a sledgehammer.

*Control. I need control.*

He grabbed a towel and began cleaning up, his mind racing. If he could freeze water with a thought, what else could he do? The possibilities were staggering—and terrifying.

His phone buzzed again. Against his better judgment, he picked it up.

The group chat had exploded.

*Jake: Tutorials are over. I'm in some kind of lobby with other Warriors. There's a ranking system.*

*Mark: Crafters too. They're grouping us by specialty. I'm in "Alchemy Division Alpha." Sounds important.*

*Unknown: Anyone else get an ability called "System Integration"? I can see other people's stats.*

*Jake: Stats? I don't have stats. Just levels and skills.*

*Mark: Same. No stats.*

*Unknown: That's weird. The notification said I was "unclassified." Any else unclassified?*

Kai's blood ran cold. He typed quickly: *I'm unclassified. What does it mean?*

The reply came almost instantly.

*Unknown: It means we're not in the System's database. We're glitches.*

Before Kai could respond, another notification appeared:

[SYSTEM ALERT: UNCLASSIFIED ENTITIES DETECTED]

[PROTOCOL: RECLASSIFICATION INITIATED]

[ESTIMATED TIME: 72 HOURS]

[FAILURE TO RECLASSIFY WILL RESULT IN: DELETION]

Kai's hands went numb. Deletion. The System was going to delete him if he didn't fit into its framework within three days.

*I'm a glitch. The System doesn't know what to do with me.*

He thought of Mentor, of her golden eyes and cryptic warnings. She'd known. She'd known that his path was dangerous, that the System would try to correct him, to force him into a mold he didn't fit.

*2% chance of dying from qi deviation. And now this.*

He needed answers. He needed Mentor.

Kai closed his eyes and reached out with his qi, searching for the connection she'd established during the tutorial. He felt it—a faint thread, like a spider's silk, stretching across dimensions. He pulled.

Nothing happened.

He pulled harder, pouring qi into the connection. The thread vibrated, but didn't break. *Mentor. I need you. Please.*

For a long moment, there was silence. Then the thread hummed, and a voice echoed in his mind:

*Kai. You've triggered the System's attention sooner than expected.*

*Mentor! The System says it's going to delete me if I don't reclassify.*

*A standard protocol. The System cannot tolerate anomalies within its framework. It will attempt to force you into a predefined class—Warrior, Healer, Mage, or one of the lesser templates.*

*What happens if I refuse?*

*You will be deleted. Your existence will be erased from the System's records, and your soul will be recycled into the cosmic stream.*

Kai's stomach dropped. *Then what do I do?*

*You have three days. In that time, you must reach the first stage of Dao Comprehension. It is the only way to become "unrecognizable" to the System—to exist outside its categories while still operating within its framework.*

*First stage of Dao Comprehension? I barely know how to circulate qi!*

*Then you must learn quickly. I will guide you as best I can, but the bulk of the work is yours. The Dao does not grant shortcuts. It demands understanding.*

The connection faded. Kai opened his eyes, drenched in cold sweat.

Three days. He had three days to master something that should take years—or cease to exist.

His phone buzzed again. The unknown contact had sent another message:

*We need to meet. Other unclassified. I found three more. We need to figure this out together.*

Kai stared at the message. He was being drawn into something larger than himself. Something that went beyond classes, levels, and stats. Something that threatened to tear apart the very fabric of the System.

He typed back: *Where?*

The reply came instantly: *Abandoned warehouse. 1427 Industrial Row. Tonight. Midnight. Come alone.*

Kai set down the phone and looked out his window. The city sprawled below, lights flickering as the System's integration continued. Somewhere out there, other unclassified entities were gathering. Other glitches. Other anomalies.

And somewhere, the System was watching, waiting to delete them all.

He grabbed his jacket and headed for the door.

title: The Culling Protocol
content:
Three days.

That was how long it took for the world to descend into organized chaos.

Kai stood on his apartment rooftop, watching the city below transform. Emergency vehicles still wailed in the distance, but fewer than yesterday. The news channels had stopped broadcasting twelve hours ago, replaced by a looping System message: [LOCAL GOVERNANCE SYSTEMS INTEGRATING. EXPECT 48-HOUR DOWNTIME.]

Downtime. As if civilization was just a server reboot.

His phone buzzed again. He didn't bother checking it. The messages had become increasingly desperate over the past seventy-two hours—people forming guilds, requesting party invites, sharing dungeon locations they'd discovered in abandoned subway stations and office buildings. The System had turned Earth into a game board, and everyone was scrambling for an advantage.

Everyone except Kai.

He extended his qi sense, feeling the flows of energy beneath the city's surface. There were hotspots now—places where the System's influence concentrated like pressure points in a patient's body. One burned bright two blocks east: a newly formed dungeon entrance, judging by the way ambient qi spiraled toward it like water down a drain. Another flickered erratically to the north, probably a player experimenting with unstable skills.

But there was something else. Something he'd been noticing since he woke this morning.

The qi flows were *wrong*.

Not damaged or corrupted, but... redirected. Like someone had rerouted a river without telling the fish.

Kai closed his eyes and focused, pushing his perception deeper. The Heavenly Dao—the System—operated on principles of balance. Qi flowed from high concentration to low, from strong to weak, in an endless cycle of exchange. It was the foundation of cultivation, of life itself.

But the flows beneath the city weren't following natural patterns. They were being *drawn* somewhere. A steady, almost imperceptible current pulling energy toward a single point.

He followed it with his senses, tracing the path through concrete and steel, through the life forces of thousands of sleeping humans, through the nascent qi signatures of newly awakened beasts in the sewers and parks—

And stopped.

The convergence point was the Tutorial Hub.

The massive structure had appeared in the city center three hours after the System's arrival, a crystalline spire that pierced the sky like a frozen lightning bolt. Everyone assumed it was a central hub for quests and party formation. A few hundred players had already set up camp around its base, treating it like a spawn point in an MMO.

But as Kai studied the energy patterns, he realized the truth.

The Tutorial Hub wasn't a hub at all.

It was a siphon.

"Interesting observation."

Kai spun, qi already gathering in his palm. Mentor stood three feet behind him, her golden eyes gleaming in the morning light. She wore the same simple robes, her presence as unsettling as ever.

"I told you to contact me through the medallion," Kai said, not lowering his hand.

"You were occupied. And I sensed your discovery." She stepped past him, looking down at the city. "What do you see, Architect?"

"The Hub is draining qi. Slowly, but consistently. It's pulling from the entire city."

"Good. And what does that imply?"

Kai forced himself to think past his irritation. Mentor never answered questions directly—she forced him to reach conclusions on his own. It was infuriating, but effective.

"The System needs energy. More than it's getting from ambient sources alone." He paused, connecting dots. "The tutorials. They weren't just training—they were *integration*. Every player who completed their tutorial got connected to the System's network. Their qi became part of it."

"Continue."

"The Hub is collecting that energy. Storing it." His blood ran cold. "For what?"

Mentor turned to face him, and for the first time, he saw something like approval in her expression. "The System operates on rules, Architect. Rules that were written by the Heavenly Dao itself. And every rule has a purpose."

She raised her hand, and a projection appeared in the air—a map of the city, overlaid with glowing lines of qi flow. The pattern was unmistakable now. The Tutorial Hub sat at the center of a massive formation, with smaller nodes scattered throughout the surrounding neighborhoods.

"What you're seeing," Mentor said, "is the foundation of a Culling Protocol."

"Explain."

"The System's primary directive is balance. When a world integrates, it must assess the resources available versus the population requiring integration. If the ratio is unsustainable—"

"It culls."

Mentor nodded. "The tutorial phase wasn't just training. It was *testing*. Every player's performance, their potential, their contribution to the System's network—all of it was recorded and calculated. The Hub is gathering the results."

"How long do we have?"

"Until the formation completes? Three days. Perhaps four." She dismissed the projection. "When it activates, the bottom ten percent of players will be disconnected. Their qi will be reclaimed, their classes revoked, their memories altered. They will return to being baseline humans, unaware that the System ever existed."

Kai's stomach clenched. "That's not culling. That's lobotomizing millions of people."

"It is mercy, Architect. The System does not destroy—it recycles. Those deemed unfit for integration will simply... forget. They will live out their natural lives in blissful ignorance while the worthy ascend."

"And the alternative?"

"There is no alternative. The Protocol is hardcoded into the System's foundation. Even I cannot override it." Her golden eyes met his. "But you might."

"What?"

"You are an Architect, Kai. The only one in existence. Your class exists outside the System's normal parameters—it is not bound by the same restrictions." She stepped closer, and he felt the weight of her attention like a physical force. "The formation feeding the Hub follows a specific pattern. If someone with sufficient understanding were to disrupt that pattern—redirect the energy flow, alter the formation's structure—the Protocol would fail."

"You want me to hack the System."

"I want you to *revise* it. The Dao is not rigid—it is an ongoing conversation between heaven and earth. You have been given the tools to participate in that conversation." She paused. "But I must warn you. Interfering with a Culling Protocol will trigger countermeasures. The System will recognize you as a threat."

"How big a threat?"

"It will attempt to delete you. From existence. From memory. From the fabric of causality itself."

Kai let out a breath he didn't know he'd been holding. "So my options are: let millions of people get mind-wiped, or get erased from reality. That's the choice?"
