"The Administrators." The Fracture's voice dropped to a whisper, the harmonic layers becoming discordant. "They are coming. They have already begun their work, seeding the world with their agents. They will find you, Architect. They will attempt to delete you from the System's records."

Kai's hand tightened, qi crackling around his fingers. "Then I'll deal with them when they arrive."

"Bold. But misplaced confidence will not serve you." The Fracture stood, Mrs. Chen's body moving with a fluidity that didn't match her age. "I offer you a deal. I can seal your signature, mask your presence from the Administrators' scans. In exchange, you will help me complete my purpose."

"Which is?"

"To expand the Fracture. To weaken the System's hold on this world." The black eyes gleamed with hunger. "Your Architect class can manipulate the System's framework in ways I cannot. Together, we can reshape reality itself."

Kai studied the entity before him. The qi patterns were still wrong, still discordant, but he could see the structure beneath now. The Fracture was made of broken System code—shards of the original Heavenly Dao that had been shattered and repurposed when the System descended.

It was telling the truth. It was also dangerous.

"If I refuse?"

"Then you die. The Administrators will find you within the week, and they will not be so generous." The Fracture spread Mrs. Chen's hands, palms up. "Choose, Architect. Alliance or extinction."

Kai's mind flashed to Mentor, to her warnings about the dangers of qi deviation and the 2% chance of death. He thought about the notifications, the unread messages, his mother waiting for an explanation he couldn't give.

He thought about the world outside, drunk on power it didn't understand, racing toward a collapse it couldn't see coming.

"No."

The Fracture's expression flickered—surprise, then anger. "You would refuse salvation?"

"I would refuse slavery." Kai straightened, his qi settling into a steady circulation. "You're no different from the Administrators. You want to use me for your own ends. I won't be a tool for anyone."

"Foolish child." The Fracture's form began to dissolve, Mrs. Chen's body slumping as the black energy poured out of her. "You have no idea what you've rejected. But you will learn. When the Administrators find you, when they peel back your consciousness and examine every fragment of your soul, you will remember this moment. And you will regret it."

The darkness collapsed inward, leaving Mrs. Chen crumpled on the floor.

Kai rushed to her side, pressing his fingers to her neck. A pulse. Weak, but there.

[SYSTEM NOTIFICATION: HOSTILE ENTITY DETECTED]
[CLASSIFICATION: SYSTEM FRACTURE — PROTO-CHAOS]
[THREAT LEVEL: EXTREME]
[RECOMMENDATION: IMMEDIATE REPORT TO LOCAL ADMINISTRATION]

A new icon appeared in his peripheral vision—a stylized eye, pulsing with amber light. The Administrators' tracking system.

They already knew.

Kai looked down at Mrs. Chen's unconscious form, then at the notification burning in his vision. He had days, maybe hours, before they came for him.

And he had no idea how to fight back.

His phone buzzed again. He pulled it out, expecting more frantic messages from his friends.

Instead, he saw a single notification:

[MENTOR: Your signature has been detected. The Administrators have flagged you as a Class Anomaly. I am initiating emergency protocols. Stay where you are. Do not resist when they arrive.]

Kai stared at the message, his blood running cold.

Do not resist.

The amber eye pulsed, and a new window appeared:

[SYSTEM BROADCAST: ADMINISTRATOR DEPLOYMENT COMMENCING]
[TARGET: CLASS ANOMALY #001 — KAI CHEN]
[LOCATION: CONFIRMED]
[ESTIMATED ARRIVAL: 00:03:47]

Three minutes.

Kai looked at the window, then at Mrs. Chen, then at the door.

*Do not resist.*

He pocketed his phone, gathered his qi, and ran.

title: The Fragment Awakens
content:
Kai's fingers hovered over the send button when the world *screamed*.

Not audibly. Not in any way his ears could register. But his qi sense—newborn and raw—detected it like a shockwave rippling through the spiritual fabric of reality. Every meridian in his body vibrated in sympathetic resonance. The cockroach behind his wall stopped moving. The electronics in his apartment flickered.

Then the System notification slammed into his vision like a hammer blow:

**[SYSTEM ALERT: ANOMALOUS ENERGY SPIKE DETECTED]**
**[CALCULATING SOURCE...]**
**[ERROR. SOURCE UNKNOWN.]**
**[ESTIMATED THREAT LEVEL: SIGNIFICANT]**
**[ALL AWAKENED ARE ADVISED TO REMAIN ALERT]**

The message hung there, pulsing, for three full seconds before fading.

Kai's phone buzzed again. The group chat exploded.

*Did everyone just feel that?*

*Yeah, what the hell was that?*

*My System's freaking out. Says "dimensional instability detected." Anyone else get that?*

*Jake, you're a Warrior. What does your System tell you?*

*Nothing useful. Just "hostile entity approaching." HOSTILE ENTITY. What does that even mean?*

Kai's blood ran cold. He looked at his own notification—the one about "anomalous energy spike." His wasn't warning about a hostile entity. It was warning about an *unknown source*. And the System couldn't calculate where it came from.

*Mentor said my class was outside the System's understanding.*

He pushed himself off the bed, ignoring the protests from muscles still sore from six hours of qi circulation. His dantian pulsed—not painfully, but alertly, like a guard dog sensing an intruder.

He reached out with his qi sense again, this time focusing outward instead of inward. The spiritual landscape of his apartment building unfolded in his mind's eye. Tenants going about their morning routines, their qi signatures flickering with excitement and anxiety. The building's own energy, a slow and steady hum of concrete and wiring.

And beneath it all—something *else*.

A thread of energy so faint he almost missed it. Thin as spider silk, cold as winter steel, and moving with deliberate purpose through the foundation of the building.

Kai tracked it. The thread wound through pipes and support beams, bypassing the qi signatures of tenants with unnatural precision. It wasn't random. It was hunting.

It was heading upward. Toward the roof.

Kai didn't think. He moved.

He threw on a jacket—his training robes were back in the tutorial space—and burst out of his apartment. The hallway was empty, but he could hear voices from behind closed doors. People arguing, people laughing, people crying. The aftermath of the tutorial phase had hit everyone differently.

He took the stairs three at a time, his newly awakened body responding with speed and coordination he'd never possessed before. His breathing stayed steady. His heart rate climbed, but not from exertion—from *anticipation*.

The door to the roof was locked.

Kai grabbed the handle and pushed. Nothing. He pulled. Still nothing. The lock was old, rusted, designed to keep tenants out and maintenance workers in.

He slammed his palm against the door in frustration—and felt something shift in his dantian. The qi that had been circulating through his meridians surged into his hand, and the door *exploded* off its hinges.

Kai stared at the splintered wood scattered across the rooftop. Steam rose from his palm, and his qi sense showed him the energy dissipating back into his meridians like a wave retreating from shore.

*That was... new.*

He stepped onto the roof.

The morning sky was overcast, gray clouds pressing down on the city like a lid. The air smelled of rain and exhaust and something else—something metallic and sharp, like ozone after a lightning strike.

And there, at the center of the rooftop, stood a figure.

It looked human. That was the first thing Kai noticed. It looked human in the way a mannequin looked human—correct proportions, correct features, but something fundamentally *wrong* about the stillness of it. The figure wore a long coat that seemed to absorb light, and its face was smooth, featureless, like a mask without holes.

But its qi signature—

Kai's breath caught. The figure's qi signature was *blank*. Not absent, not hidden, but actively erased. Where a normal person's energy would glow with life and intent, this thing radiated nothing but a cold, flat void.

And wound around it, coiling like a serpent, was the thread of energy Kai had sensed from his apartment.

"You can see me," the figure said.

Its voice was wrong. Not deep or high, not male or female, but *flat*—a sound that seemed to come from everywhere at once, resonating in Kai's bones rather than his ears.

"I don't know what you are," Kai said, "but you're not supposed to be here."

"Here?" The figure tilted its head—a gesture that should have been natural but looked mechanical. "This world? This city? This rooftop?" A pause. "You're right. I'm not supposed to be here. But neither are you, Dao Architect."

Kai's blood froze.

*How does it know my class?*

The System had classified him as "Unassigned." Mentor had said the Dao Architect class was outside the System's framework. No one should know what he was.

"You're confused," the figure said. "Good. Confusion means you haven't been taught yet. That means you're still vulnerable."

"Taught what?"

"That you're a mistake." The figure took a step forward, and the thread of energy around it tightened. "The System was designed for Warriors. For Mages. For Healers. For the predictable categories that can be measured and controlled. But you—" It pointed a finger at Kai, and the gesture felt like an accusation. "You don't fit. You're a variable that shouldn't exist. An error in the code."

"I'm not an error," Kai said, but his voice wavered.

"Then why can't the System classify you? Why did your tutorial take place outside its architecture? Why did Mentor—" The figure's voice shifted, dropping into a perfect imitation of the golden-eyed woman from his tutorial: "—tell you that your path was 'unwritten'?"

Kai's hands clenched into fists. "How do you know about Mentor?"

"I know many things." The figure spread its arms, and the thread of energy exploded outward, wrapping around the entire rooftop. Kai felt the pressure of it, the weight of something vast and ancient pressing against his qi sense. "I know that the System is not a game. I know that the Heavenly Dao has been reborn as code, and that code has rules that must be followed." The figure's featureless face turned toward him. "And I know that you, Kai Chen, are a violation of those rules."

"You're going to kill me?"

The figure paused. "No. I'm not going to kill you. I'm going to *delete* you."

The thread of energy snapped taut.

And Kai's dantian *screamed*.

The pain was worse than anything he'd felt in the tutorial. Worse than the first time he'd tried to circulate qi. Worse than the moment his meridians had cracked and reformed. This was a tearing, a ripping, a feeling like something was reaching into his core and trying to pull his existence out through his navel.

He dropped to his knees, gasping.

The figure watched, unmoving. "The Dao Architect class is incompatible with System reality. Your very presence creates instability. The anomaly you felt earlier—that was me arriving. The System can't detect me because I'm part of its own framework. I'm a correction protocol. A debugger."

Kai's vision blurred. His qi sense was going haywire, showing him fragments of energy patterns that made no sense—layers upon layers of reality stacked like transparent sheets, each one slightly different from the last.

And through the pain, he understood.

*The figure isn't removing me. It's trying to overwrite me.*

The realization sparked something in his dantian. Not fear. Not anger. Something colder, more primal.

*Survival.*

He remembered Mentor's words: *Your foundation is forming. Not yet stable, but forming.*

He remembered the qi circulation she'd taught him. The way it flowed through his meridians like water through riverbeds. The way it gathered in his dantian and spread outward, connecting him to the energy of the world.

If the figure was trying to overwrite him, then he needed to be *unwritable*.

Kai closed his eyes. He ignored the pain. He ignored the screaming of his qi sense. He focused inward, on the faint pulse of his dantian, on the fragile network of meridians that Mentor had helped him build.

And he circulated.

The qi moved slowly at first, sluggish and reluctant. But as he pushed it through the cycle Mentor had taught him, it gained speed. It gained strength. It began to *resist* the thread of energy trying to pull him apart.

The figure made a sound—the first hint of emotion Kai had heard from it. Surprise? Annoyance? "That's not possible. You shouldn't be able to resist the protocol."

Kai opened his eyes. "I'm not a bug to be fixed."

He stood.

The thread of energy snapped against his qi like a wave breaking against a cliff. The figure stepped back, its featureless face tilting in what might have been confusion.

"You're still unstable," Kai said, his voice hoarse but steady. "Mentor said it would take time for my foundation to solidify. But I've got enough to fight back."

The figure was silent for a long moment. Then it spoke, and its voice was different. Softer. Almost respectful.

"You're stronger than the calculations predicted. The Dao Architect class has more potential than I estimated." A pause. "That makes you dangerous. And dangerous variables must be observed."

"You're leaving?"

"For now." The figure's form began to dissolve, the thread of energy unraveling and fading into the morning air. "But I'll be watching, Kai Chen. And when your foundation is complete, I'll return. Not to delete you—but to *recruit* you."

"Recruit me for what?"

The figure's final words hung in the air like a whisper from a distant world:

"The System is not what it appears to be. And neither am I. When you're ready to learn the truth, find the man who sells flowers at the crossroads of Wei and Chang streets. Tell him the Architect sent you."

And then it was gone.

Kai stood alone on the rooftop, his qi sense slowly settling back to normal. The metallic smell faded. The pressure in the air dissipated. The only evidence that anything had happened was the shattered door at his feet and the faint ache in his dantian.

His phone buzzed. Another message from the group chat.

*Kai, you okay? You haven't responded in like five minutes.*

He looked at the screen. Then at the empty rooftop. Then at the city spread out below him, alive with the qi signatures of millions of newly awakened humans, all trying to figure out what the System meant for their lives.

He typed a single response:

*I need to find a flower shop.*

**[END OF CHAPTER 12]**

title: The Weight of Heaven's Eyes
content:
Kai's phone slipped from his fingers.

The System notification hung in his vision, pulsing with an urgency that made his newly awakened qi receptors throb. *World integration begins in 24 hours. All regions will be synchronized. Dungeons will manifest. Monster spawns will commence.*

His gut twisted. Across the city, he could feel the sudden spike of panic—thousands of qi signatures flaring with fear, excitement, confusion. The cockroach behind his wall stopped moving, as if even it sensed something fundamental shifting.

*Twenty-four hours.*

His phone buzzed again. Then again. And again, until it became a continuous vibration against his mattress. The group chat exploded:

*WHAT DOES THIS MEAN*

*My tutorial master said something about "tiers" and "integration" but I thought he was being dramatic*

*Someone in the global chat says monsters are already spawning in Tokyo*

*Tokyo? That's on the other side of the world*

*THEY'RE NOT ASKING PERMISSION*

Kai's hands moved before his mind caught up, typing with mechanical precision: *Everyone stay inside. Don't engage anything until you understand your class. Stock up on supplies. Wait for official coordination.*

A reply came instantly from Jake: *Since when are you the tactical one?*

*Since I got a class that doesn't have a damage stat.*

More questions flooded in, but Kai silenced the chat. He had twenty-four hours to prepare, and he didn't even know what he was preparing for. His qi sense stretched outward, mapping the apartment building floor by floor. Mrs. Chen on 3 was crying, her qi signature fluctuating wildly. The teenager in 4B was practically vibrating with excitement, his newly awakened Warrior class making his life force burn like a bonfire. And in 2A—

Kai froze.

2A was empty. Had been empty for months, ever since Old Man Harrison moved to a nursing home. But there was something there now. Something that felt like static against his qi sense. Not quite alive, but not quite dead either. A presence that *watched*.

He was on his feet before he made the conscious decision to move. His body moved with unfamiliar precision, each step placed with exact pressure, his breathing automatically cycling qi through his meridians. The hallway was the same as always—stained carpet, flickering fluorescent lights, the smell of boiled cabbage from Mrs. Park's apartment.

But the air around 2A's door was wrong. It shimmered, barely perceptibly, like heat rising off summer asphalt.

Kai raised his hand to knock—

The door opened on its own.

Inside, the apartment was dark. But dark wasn't quite right either. The darkness had *substance*, a living quality that pressed against his senses. And in the center of the living room, where Old Man Harrison's recliner used to sit, a figure waited.

"Come in, Architect."

The voice was smooth, genderless, and utterly wrong. It didn't come from the figure's direction—it came from everywhere at once, vibrating through the floor, the walls, even through the qi in Kai's own meridians.

His body tried to step backward. His qi refused.

"You have questions," the figure said. "I have answers. But first, know this: the others cannot see me. They cannot feel me. You are the only one in this building, in this city, perhaps in this entire integration zone, who can perceive my presence."

Kai's throat worked. "Who are you?"

"I am what your System calls an Observer. What your ancestors called a Celestial Inspector. What the Dao calls an eye that watches the river's flow." The figure shifted, and for just a moment, Kai caught a glimpse of something beneath the darkness—a face that was too symmetrical, eyes that held galaxies, a presence that made the qi in his dantian scream warnings. "And I am here because you are an anomaly that cannot be allowed to propagate."

The door slammed shut behind him.

Kai spun, but the door was gone. The wall where it should have been was seamless, unbroken. He was trapped.

"Do not be alarmed. This space exists between moments. Your physical body is still in the hallway, hand raised to knock. You will return to it when we are finished."

"Convenient," Kai said, forcing his voice steady. "Kidnapping me into some kind of pocket dimension. Very friendly for someone claiming to have answers."

The Observer laughed—a sound like breaking glass played in reverse. "I like you. That's unfortunate. It would be easier if you were insufferable."

"I'm told I can be, given time."

"I'm sure. But time is precisely what we don't have." The figure stepped closer, and Kai felt the weight of its attention like a physical force. "You've been given a class that should not exist. The Dao Architect is not part of the System's original framework. It's a *glitch*, a remnant of something older, something the System was designed to overwrite."

Kai's mind raced. "Mentor mentioned something similar. She said my class was 'pre-System.'"

"Your Mentor is a fragment of something that should have been deleted when the Heavenly Dao was digitized. That she exists at all is proof that the integration is flawed." The Observer circled him, and Kai felt its gaze boring into the back of his skull. "The System was created to standardize cultivation. To make it accessible to all, but also to *limit* it. To prevent anyone from reaching the heights that shattered the previous era."

"Previous era?"

"The age before the System. When cultivators warred across dimensions, when Heaven's will was a battlefield, when the Dao itself was fractured by conflict." The Observer's voice dropped, becoming something ancient and weary. "The System is a cage, Architect. A beautiful, efficient cage that ensures no one ever becomes powerful enough to break reality again."

Kai's fists clenched. "And my class?"

"Is a key. A skeleton key that can unlock the cage from the inside." The Observer stopped directly in front of him, and Kai could see it now—a humanoid shape made of compressed darkness, with stars flickering in its depths like distant suns. "The System cannot delete you directly. You're too deeply integrated. But it can *adjust* circumstances. It can ensure your path is blocked at every turn. It can stack probabilities against you until even your Dao Architect abilities cannot compensate."

"Is that a threat?"

"It's a warning." The Observer reached out, and a finger of pure shadow touched Kai's forehead. Information flooded into him—not words, but *understanding*. He saw the global map, dotted with incoming dungeon spawns. He saw the monster types that would manifest, their levels, their abilities. He saw the optimized routes that players would take, the meta-builds that would emerge, the natural order of progression that millions would follow.

And he saw himself, a single bright point of light, with a hundred red arrows converging on his position from every direction.

"The first wave will hit your city in twelve hours," the Observer said. "A Rank 2 dungeon will manifest in your apartment building's basement. The monsters inside will be calibrated for parties of Level 5-7 Warriors and Mages. You are Level 1, with no combat class."

Kai's blood ran cold. "You're telling me the System is targeting me specifically."

"I'm telling you that the System has calculated that the most efficient way to eliminate your anomaly is to ensure you never reach the threshold where your class becomes relevant." The Observer withdrew its finger. "You have twelve hours. Your neighbors have twelve hours. The dungeon will spawn regardless of whether you're prepared."

"What about evacuation?"

"The dungeon's influence will make this building inescapable to anyone without a class. Your neighbors will be trapped. They will die, or they will be integrated into the dungeon's ecosystem as spawn points." The Observer's form began to dissolve. "I cannot help you directly. That would draw the System's full attention and result in both our deletions. But I can tell you this: your class has potential that no other class possesses. You can build structures that alter reality. You can inscribe arrays that bend probability. You can cultivate qi in ways that the System's leveling framework cannot track."

"How do I learn?"

"Your Mentor will teach you what she can. But she is limited by her nature as a tutorial fragment. For the rest..." The Observer's voice faded like wind through leaves. "You must build your own path. That is, after all, what Architects do."

The darkness shattered.

Kai gasped, stumbling backward into the hallway wall. His hand was still raised. The door to 2A was closed, ordinary, covered in the same faded paint as every other door on the floor. The flickering fluorescent light buzzed overhead. Everything was normal.

Except it wasn't.

His qi sense showed him the *potential* now. Lines of force running through the building's structure, ley lines of spiritual energy that crisscrossed the city, nodes where power gathered and pulsed. He could see, in his mind's eye, the blueprint of reality itself—the framework beneath the physical world, waiting to be modified.

His phone buzzed. A new System notification:

[SYSTEM ALERT: DUNGEON MANIFESTATION INCOMING]
[LOCATION: YOUR CURRENT RESIDENCE]
[ESTIMATED SPAWN TIME: 11 HOURS 47 MINUTES]
[RECOMMENDED PARTY SIZE: 5]
[RECOMMENDED LEVEL: 5+]

Kai's neighbors were Level 1. If they were lucky. Most of them were still trying to figure out how to access their status screens.

He had eleven hours and forty-seven minutes to build something that could save them all.

His phone buzzed again. The group chat. Jake was sending screenshots of dungeon guides that had appeared on the global network, written by players whose tutorials had covered combat. Mark was sharing potion recipes. Someone else was asking if anyone had figured out how to form a party.

And among the chaos, a single private message from an unknown number:

*I saw what you did in the tutorial. The arrays you designed. The foundation you built. I'm in the building across from yours. Level 1 Healer. I think we need to talk.*

Kai stared at the message.

He thought about the dungeon spawning beneath his feet.

He thought about his neighbors, his mother, his friends.

He thought about the Observer's words: *You must build your own path.*

His fingers moved across the screen: *I'm listening.*

title: The Fractured Sky
content:
Kai's thumb hovered over the send button as the System broadcast faded. The world had changed in the span of three words. Tutorial Phase Complete. All Classes Assigned. World Integration.

He'd been so focused on his own bizarre situation that he hadn't stopped to consider the bigger picture. What did "World Integration" even mean?

His phone buzzed again. Then again. Then so many times the device vibrated continuously in his palm. He glanced at the screen and felt his blood run cold.

*What the hell is that thing in the sky?*

*Is that... a second sun?*

*Bro it's getting bigger*

Kai threw off his sheets and stumbled to his window. The morning sky was wrong. A massive rift hung above the city, jagged edges bleeding colors that didn't exist in nature—amethyst, vermillion, a gold so bright it left afterimages on his retina. Through the tear, he could see... something. Mountains that twisted in impossible geometries. Rivers that flowed upward. Forests made of crystal.

"System," he whispered. "What is that?"

No answer. The System, which had been so present since his awakening, was silent.

His phone died. Not ran out of battery—the screen flickered, displayed a corrupted error message, and went black. The apartment lights followed a moment later, buzzing and dying with a sad whimper. Emergency sirens began to wail outside.

Kai's qi sense exploded with input. Every person in the city, every living thing within a mile radius, suddenly flared like bonfires in his perception. He could feel their fear, their confusion, their pain—a cacophony of emotions that made his head throb.

*This is wrong. This is very wrong.*

He reached for his dantian, cycling qi through his meridians to stabilize himself. The energy felt different now—thicker, almost viscous, as if the air itself had changed composition. Each breath tasted like ozone and something metallic, like blood.

The rift in the sky pulsed.

And then the monsters began to fall.

Kai saw the first one impact three blocks away. A creature of chitin and too many legs, it cratered the street and let out a screech that shattered windows. From his apartment, he could see it rise, eight glowing eyes scanning the surroundings with predatory intelligence.

His phone came back to life. Not his phone—the System interface, projected directly into his vision.

[SYSTEM ALERT: DIMENSIONAL BREACH DETECTED]
[WORLD INTEGRATION PHASE 1: THE MERGING]
[ALL AWAKENED BEINGS MUST PARTICIPATE]
[OBJECTIVE: SURVIVE]

*Must participate?* Kai had never liked being told what to do.

Below, the creature had already found its first victim. A man in pajamas, still half-asleep, stumbled out of his building to see what the noise was about. The monster moved faster than its bulk suggested, a bladed limb sweeping out and—

Kai didn't see the rest. He was already moving.

His body responded before his mind caught up, the qi-enhanced speed carrying him down three flights of stairs in seconds. He burst through the apartment building's front door just as the creature reared back for another strike. The man in pajamas was still alive—bleeding, terrified, but alive.

"Hey!" Kai shouted. "Over here!"

The creature's eyes swiveled to track him. All eight of them. He felt their attention like a physical weight, and for a moment, his courage faltered. This wasn't the controlled environment of the tutorial. This was real. If he died here, there would be no reset.

*You are the Dao Architect,* Mentor's voice echoed in his memory. *You are not limited by the System's rules.*

Kai reached inside himself and found his qi reserves full and churning. He didn't have combat skills. He didn't have spells or abilities. He had meridians, a dantian, and an understanding of energy flow that no one else on Earth possessed.

He would have to make it work.

The creature charged, bladed limbs raised. Kai didn't dodge. Instead, he dropped into a stance—not a martial arts pose, but a cultivation foundation posture. He visualized the qi in his body as a river, and the creature as a boulder in its path.

*Redirect. Don't resist.*

He extended his hand and pushed.

The qi exploded from his palm in an invisible wave. It caught the creature mid-stride and sent it tumbling sideways, crashing into a parked car. The metal crumpled like paper. The monster screeched in fury.

*That actually worked?*

[COMBAT LOG: UNKNOWN ABILITY DETECTED]
[TARGET STATUS: STUNNED]

Kai didn't wait for it to recover. He ran forward, closing the distance, his mind racing through possibilities. He needed to finish this quickly—the creature was already shaking off the stun, its eyes glowing brighter with rage.

He remembered something Mentor had said during the tutorial: *Qi is the language of reality. Speak it correctly, and the universe listens.*

What did he want to say?

*I want this creature gone.*

The qi in his dantian responded, twisting and shaping itself according to his will. He didn't know how he was doing it—it felt like reaching for a word on the tip of his tongue, knowing it existed but not quite grasping it. His hand moved of its own accord, tracing symbols in the air that glowed with golden light.

The creature lunged.

The symbol flared.

A pillar of light descended from the rift above, striking the monster with surgical precision. There was no explosion, no dramatic death—the creature simply ceased to exist, its molecules scattered across dimensions.

Kai stood in the crater, breathing hard, his hand still raised. The golden symbols faded from the air. The man in pajamas stared at him with wide, uncomprehending eyes.

"What... what are you?"

Kai didn't have an answer. He looked up at the rift, now pulsing with an almost rhythmic cadence. More shapes were forming at its edges—not just monsters, but structures. Towers. Gateways. Something was coming through.

[SYSTEM NOTIFICATION]
[DO NOT ATTEMPT TO REPLICATE PREVIOUS ACTION]
[ABILITY CLASSIFICATION: ARCHITECT]
[THREAT LEVEL: INCOMPREHENSIBLE]

*The System is scared of me,* Kai realized. *It doesn't understand what I just did.*

His satisfaction was short-lived. A new presence pressed against his consciousness, vast and ancient and utterly alien. It came from the rift, from the world beyond, and it was *looking* at him.

*Found you,* the presence seemed to whisper. *Little architect. Little god-maker. We have been waiting.*

The ground shook. The rift split wider, and from its depths emerged a hand—no, a claw, made of obsidian and starlight, large enough to grip a building. It descended toward the city with terrible slowness, each inch of its approach sending cracks through the fabric of reality.

[WARNING: WORLD-LEVEL THREAT DETECTED]
[RECOMMENDED ACTION: EVACUATE]
[ESTIMATED SURVIVAL RATE: 0.02%]

Kai's qi sense screamed at him. This thing was beyond anything he could fight. Beyond anything any Awakened on Earth could fight. It was a force of nature, a god reaching down to crush an ant.

But he was the Dao Architect. And architects don't run from their creations.

He planted his feet, drew on every scrap of qi in his meridians, and prepared to do something incredibly stupid.

*Mentor,* he thought, *if you can hear me, I could really use some advice right now.*

The golden-eyed woman's voice appeared in his mind, tinged with something he'd never heard from her before: fear.

*Kai. Do not engage. That is not a monster—that is the hand of an Old God. If you die here, everything we have built is lost.*

*Then tell me how to survive.*

Silence. The claw descended.

*The Dao Architect's true power is creation, not destruction. You cannot fight this being. But you can build something it cannot ignore.*

Kai understood. He closed his eyes, ignoring the screams around him, ignoring the trembling ground, ignoring the death bearing down from the sky. He reached into his dantian and found the seed of something new—a concept, a possibility, a name.

He pulled it into existence.

[SYSTEM ALERT: NEW ABILITY BEING GENERATED]
[ERROR: CANNOT CLASSIFY]
[ERROR: CANNOT RESTRICT]
[ERROR: ERROR]

The world went white.

When Kai opened his eyes, the claw was frozen, inches from his face. The rift had stopped pulsing. The city was silent, caught in a moment of perfect stillness.

And standing before him, radiating light that dwarfed the sun, was Mentor.

"You were not supposed to awaken this early," she said, her voice carrying the weight of eons. "But you have forced my hand. The Old Gods know you exist now, Kai. And they will not stop until they have consumed you."

She raised her hand, and reality shattered like glass.

title: The Fractured Sky
content:
The broadcast hung in the air, its golden text pulsing with an authority that made Kai's teeth ache. He read it three times, each pass revealing new layers of wrongness.

[ALL CLASSES HAVE BEEN ASSIGNED]

That wasn't possible. Jake had told him about people who hadn't received classes yet. About forums exploding with panic from those still waiting. About the government setting up hotlines for "delayed integrations."

[WORLD INTEG]

The message cut off. Incomplete. Like someone had yanked the plug mid-sentence.

Kai's phone buzzed again. Then again. Then a continuous vibration that rattled against his nightstand. He grabbed it, and the screen was a wall of notifications:

*Did the broadcast cut out for everyone?*

*My friend's class just got REMOVED. He was a Mage. Now he's nothing.*

*Same here. My sister lost her class. She's freaking out.*

*The System is glitching.*

*This isn't a glitch. Something's wrong.*

Kai's hand moved before his mind caught up. He opened the group chat and typed: *What do you mean "removed"?*

The responses came in rapid fire:

*His class icon went gray. Then it shattered. He said it felt like someone ripped out part of his soul.*

*My sister screamed for five minutes. Now she's catatonic. Hospital won't even look at her. They're overwhelmed with similar cases.*

*It's happening everywhere. Thousands of people. Maybe more.*

*The System is failing.*

Kai's dantian throbbed. His qi sense, still raw and unrefined, reached out instinctively—and recoiled.

The world had changed.

The residual qi in his apartment, thin and artificial as he'd noted before, now seemed to be *shrinking*. Pulling back. Condensing into points of light that flickered like dying embers. He followed one with his perception, tracking it as it drifted through his wall, into the hallway, down the stairs, out into the street—

And then he saw it.

Outside his window, the sky was wrong.

Not visibly. The sun still shone, the clouds still drifted. But in the spiritual layer, the sky was *fractured*. Great spiderweb cracks spread across the heavens, leaking something dark and formless. The cracks pulsed with each heartbeat, growing wider, then contracting, as if the sky itself was breathing.

Kai watched, frozen, as a section of the crack nearest to him *split*.

A shard of something fell.

It was invisible to the naked eye, but his qi sense tracked it perfectly—a jagged piece of... of *System*. Of code. Of Dao. It tumbled through the air, trailing darkness, and struck a building three blocks away.

The building didn't explode.

It *vanished*.

One moment it was there, a six-story apartment complex with laundry hanging from balconies and children playing in the courtyard. The next moment, it was gone, replaced by a perfect circular void. A hole in reality, its edges clean and absolute.

Kai's phone went silent.

He looked down. The screen was black. Dead. Not out of battery—he'd charged it before bed—but truly, completely dead. The kind of dead that came from something severing its connection to power.

His laptop, plugged into the wall, was also dark.

The buzzing of the refrigerator stopped.

The hum of the city, that constant background noise of civilization, died in stages. One by one, the sounds of traffic, of conversations, of televisions and radios and everything that made the modern world modern, faded into an oppressive silence.

Kai stood up. His legs were shaky, his body still recovering from the tutorial's grueling sessions. But he moved to the window anyway, pressing his forehead against the cold glass.

The city was dark.

No. Not dark. The buildings still stood, the streets still stretched, but the *light* was gone. Not just electric light—the spiritual light that had suffused everything since the System arrived. That light was dimming, fading, being consumed by the fractures spreading across the sky.

His phone buzzed.

Kai jumped. He looked at it, and the screen flickered back to life, displaying a single message from an unknown number:

*Do not use your class.*

He stared at it. The message was simple, direct, and utterly terrifying. Who had sent it? How had they bypassed the dead network? And why would they tell him not to use his class?

His thumb hovered over the keyboard. He typed: *Who is this?*

No response.

*Answer me.*

Nothing.

The screen flickered again, and a new message appeared: *The System is being attacked. Every time you use your class, you reveal your location. They are targeting high-potential hosts. You are marked.*

Marked.

Kai's blood went cold. He thought of Mentor, of her golden eyes and her warnings about the 2% chance of dying. He thought of the creatures in the tutorial, of the way they'd looked at him with hunger and recognition. He thought of the fractures in the sky, and the shard that had erased an entire building.

*Who is "they"?*

The response came instantly: *The Architects of Collapse.*

The name meant nothing to him. But the capitalization, the deliberate weight of the words, told him it meant everything to whoever was sending these messages.

*What do they want?*

*To unmake the System. To return the world to chaos. To devour the Dao before it can stabilize.*

*And you, Kai. You are the only one who can stop them.*

His breath caught. *Why me?*

*Because you are the first. The only one who awakened without a class. The one who cultivates, who builds, who creates. The Architects cannot corrupt what they cannot classify. You are their blind spot.*

*How do you know all this?*

Silence. The screen flickered, the words fading, and for a moment Kai thought the connection was lost. Then a final message appeared:

*Because I designed the System. And I made a mistake.*

*Find me before they do.*

The screen went black. This time, it didn't come back.

Kai stood in the silence, the weight of the conversation pressing down on him. Outside, the fractures in the sky continued to spread. Another shard fell, farther away this time, striking somewhere in the industrial district. The resulting void was smaller, but no less absolute.

His dantian pulsed.

He reached out with his qi sense, trying to understand the nature of the fractures. They weren't just cracks—they were *wounds*. The sky was bleeding code, bleeding Dao, bleeding the fundamental laws that held reality together. And with each shard that fell, the wounds widened.

Someone—something—was systematically dismantling the System.

And if the System fell, everything fell. The classes, the skills, the tutorials. The qi that now flowed through his meridians. The very framework that had redefined existence itself.

Kai looked at his hands. They were shaking. Not from fear—though there was plenty of that—but from the qi circulating through him, responding to his emotions, amplifying his every thought.

Mentor had warned him. *The path of cultivation is not without risk. The System provides stability. Without it, your growth becomes... unpredictable.*

She'd known. She'd known something was coming.

He grabbed his jacket. His apartment felt too small, too vulnerable. The fractures were spreading, the shards were falling, and someone—the "Architects of Collapse"—was hunting him.

But they weren't the only ones hunting.

Someone else had reached out. Someone who claimed to have designed the System. Someone who'd made a mistake.

Someone who needed to be found.

Kai opened his door and stepped into the hallway. The building was eerily quiet. No hum of appliances, no muffled conversations, no footsteps from the apartment above. Just silence, thick and oppressive, pressing in from all sides.

His phone buzzed.

He looked down. The screen was dead.

But the buzzing continued.

It was coming from his pocket. No—from his dantian. The vibration was internal, a resonance that had nothing to do with electronics. It was his qi, responding to something outside himself.

He extended his senses.

And felt it.

A presence. Massive. Ancient. Hungry. It was moving through the city, leaving a trail of void in its wake. It wasn't looking for him—not yet. But it was getting closer, its attention sweeping across the buildings like a searchlight.

The Architects of Collapse.

They were already here.

Kai ran.

He didn't know where he was going. He didn't know who to trust. He didn't know if the message he'd received was truth or trap, salvation or damnation.

But he knew one thing with absolute certainty:

If he stayed still, he would die.

The sky fractured further. A third shard fell, closer this time, striking the street two blocks away. The void it created swallowed cars, pavement, streetlights, leaving a perfect circle of nothing where reality had been.

And from that nothing, something emerged.

It was formless. Shapeless. A writhing mass of darkness that defied his qi sense, that refused to be classified or understood. It moved like smoke, like shadow, like a hole in the world given terrible purpose.

It turned toward him.

Kai's dantian flared. His meridians blazed. Every instinct screamed at him to use his class, to fight, to flee.

But the message echoed in his mind: *Do not use your class.*

He couldn't fight.

He couldn't run.

He was trapped.

The darkness surged forward.

And then—between one heartbeat and the next—reality *twisted*.

Kai's vision blurred. The hallway dissolved. The darkness vanished. And when his senses returned, he was standing in a room he'd never seen before, facing a figure he'd never met.

The figure was a man. Old. Gaunt. His eyes were hollow, his skin stretched thin over bones that seemed too sharp, too angular. He sat in a chair that hummed with latent energy, surrounded by screens displaying code that moved too fast to read.

"You found me," the man said. His voice was cracked, dry, like paper crumbling. "Faster than I expected."

Kai's mouth opened. Closed. Opened again. "Where am I?"

"Safe. Temporarily." The man gestured, and one of the screens expanded, showing the hallway Kai had just occupied. The darkness was there, coiling and uncoiling, searching. "The Architects are hunting. They've found your signature. They'll track you anywhere you go in the physical world."

"Then how—"

"I pulled you into the code layer. The space between System functions. It's unstable, but it's hidden." The man leaned forward, his hollow eyes meeting Kai's. "I have a name. It's Dr. Aris Chen. I was the lead architect of the System's initial framework."

Kai's mind raced. "You sent the message."

"Yes."

"You said you made a mistake."

"I did." Aris's face twisted, guilt and regret warring in his expression. "I built a backdoor into the System. A way to access it remotely, to make adjustments, to fix bugs. I thought it was necessary. I thought it was safe."

"And the Architects?"

Aris's laugh was bitter, hollow. "They found it. They're not human, Kai. They're not even from this reality. They're parasites that feed on order, on structure, on systems themselves. And now they're inside the System, tearing it apart from within."

He paused, his eyes locking onto Kai's.

"There's only one way to stop

title: The Integration Protocol
content:
Kai never sent the message.

His phone screen flickered, the text dissolving into static before reforming as a single line of golden characters that burned directly into his retina:

[WARNING: UNAUTHORIZED INTELLIGENCE DETECTED]

The phone grew hot in his hand. He dropped it, watching as the device warped and twisted, the plastic casing bubbling like wax. The screen cracked, and from the fissures emerged thin tendrils of golden light, reaching toward him like searching fingers.

Kai scrambled backward off his bed, his back hitting the wall. The tendrils paused, hovering in the air, as if scenting him.

*What the hell?*

His dantian pulsed. The qi inside him stirred, responding to an instinct he didn't know he had. The golden tendrils recoiled, retreating back into the ruined phone. The device crumbled to dust, leaving nothing but a dark scorch mark on his cheap laminate floor.

"Interesting."

Kai spun around.

A man stood in the corner of his apartment, leaning against the kitchen counter. He was tall, thin, with sharp features and eyes that glowed with the same golden light that had destroyed his phone. He wore a perfectly tailored black suit, utterly out of place in Kai's shabby one-bedroom.

He hadn't used the door. Kai would have heard the lock.

"Who are you?" Kai's voice came out steadier than he felt. His qi was already circulating, ready to respond to threat.

"An envoy." The man smiled, but there was no warmth in it. "The System sends its regards, Kai Chen. Or should I say, its concerns?"

"My phone—"

"Terminated. A necessary precaution. You were about to transmit information that would violate the Integration Protocol." The envoy tilted his head, studying Kai like a scientist examining a specimen. "You've been flagged. Do you know what that means?"

Kai's mind raced. Mentor's warnings echoed in his thoughts: *The System doesn't understand you. It will try to categorize you, control you, and if it can't do either, it will eliminate you.*

"You're not supposed to be here," Kai said.

"I'm everywhere the System needs me to be." The envoy pushed off from the counter, taking a step closer. Kai tensed, ready to move. "Do you know how many people received unclassified classes, Kai? One hundred and forty-seven. Out of eight billion. Do you know how many of those unclassified classes were flagged as 'high threat'?"

Kai said nothing.

"Four. Three of them were combat-oriented. Anomalies in the Warrior class tree that the System couldn't predict. They've been dealt with." The envoy's smile widened. "Recalibrated, shall we say. Given proper classifications."

"And the fourth?"

"You."

Kai's qi flared. The envoy didn't react, but the air between them shimmered, as if his presence was distorting reality itself.

"I'm not a threat," Kai said.

"You're a variable the System cannot calculate. That is the definition of a threat." The envoy reached into his jacket, producing a small golden cube that hummed with power. "You have two options, Architect. Submit to reclassification. Let the System integrate your class properly, assign you a standard path, and erase the anomalies in your cultivation."

"Or?"

"Or I terminate you here. Your existence will be scrubbed. Your mother will remember you as a child who died in the Integration, never having received a class. A statistical tragedy."

Kai's blood ran cold. "You'd kill me?"

"I'm not a killer, Kai. I'm a solution." The envoy held up the golden cube. "But if you force my hand, I will do what's necessary. The System cannot afford blind spots. Not now. Not when the integration is still unstable."

"Three minutes."

The envoy blinked. "What?"

"You said you're everywhere the System needs you to be. But you're talking to me. Trying to convince me. If you could just kill me, you would have done it already." Kai straightened, meeting the envoy's golden gaze. "Something's stopping you. Some limitation."

The envoy's smile flickered.

"Your logic is sound, but flawed," he said. "I'm not trying to convince you. I'm giving you the opportunity to cooperate. The System values efficiency. A willing reclassification takes less energy than a forced one."

"Then why haven't you used the cube?"

Silence.

Kai pressed his advantage. "Because you're not sure it'll work on me. You've never seen a Dao Architect before. You don't know what I'm capable of."

The envoy's eyes narrowed. The golden cube in his hand pulsed brighter, and Kai felt the pressure in the room increase, his ears popping from the shift in qi density.

"You're clever," the envoy admitted. "But cleverness won't save you from the System's judgment. I'll ask one more time: Will you submit?"

Kai thought of Mentor. Of the golden-eyed woman who had taught him to circulate qi, who had shown him the truth behind the System's facade. She had risked something to guide him. She had believed he was worth the effort.

He thought of his mother, waiting for his call.

He thought of the cockroach behind his wall, its life force flickering like a candle in the dark.

"No."

The envoy moved.

Kai didn't see him cross the distance—one moment the man was across the room, the next he was directly in front of Kai, the golden cube pressed against Kai's chest. Energy exploded outward, searing through Kai's shirt, burning into his skin.

Pain. White-hot, blinding, like his qi was being ripped apart molecule by molecule.

But Kai had been trained for pain. Six hours of forced circulation had taught him to work through agony. He reached inward, grabbed hold of the qi in his dantian, and pushed.

The cube shattered.

Golden fragments scattered across the room, dissolving into motes of light before they hit the ground. The envoy staggered backward, his hand smoking, his expression shifting from confident to something Kai hadn't expected: fear.

"What—" the envoy started.

Kai didn't let him finish. His qi surged outward, not as an attack, but as a wave of pure presence. The envoy's form flickered, his human shape destabilizing as the qi washed over him.

"You're not a person," Kai realized. "You're a construct. A System avatar."

The envoy's face contorted. "You should not be able to see that."

"I can see a lot of things now." Kai stepped forward, his qi flaring brighter. "You said the System can't calculate me. Let me show you why."

He reached out with his qi sense, not to feel, but to *shape*. The Architect class wasn't about combat—Mentor had made that clear. It was about understanding the underlying structure of reality and modifying it.

The envoy was made of System code. Golden threads of pure data, woven into a humanoid form.

Kai found the threads and pulled.

The envoy screamed. Not a human sound, but a digital screech, like feedback through a speaker. His body unraveled, golden strands flying in all directions, his form collapsing in on itself.

"No—you can't—the System will—"

Kai pulled harder.

The envoy dissolved into a cloud of golden light, swirling once before dispersing into nothing. The pressure in the room vanished. The faint hum of the cube's remnants faded.

Kai stood alone in his apartment, breathing hard, his qi depleted and his chest aching from the burn.

He looked down at his skin. The mark from the cube was gone, healed by his qi. But he could feel something else—a faint resonance, like a tracking signal, embedded deep in his dantian.

*They'll send more.*

His phone was destroyed. His connection to the outside world was cut. And somewhere out there, the System had just lost one of its enforcers.

[SYSTEM NOTIFICATION: CLASSIFIED ENCOUNTER LOGGED]
[THREAT LEVEL: CRITICAL]
[PROTOCOL OMEGA ENGAGED]

Kai's blood ran cold. He didn't know what Protocol Omega was, but he knew it couldn't be good.

A new window appeared, different from the System's usual golden interface. This one was crimson, pulsing like a heartbeat:

[DAO ARCHITECT: KAI CHEN]
[You have been marked for immediate termination by the System Core.]
[Estimated time until first response wave: 47 minutes.]
[Recommended action: Run.]

Kai stared at the message, his mind racing. His apartment was compromised. His city was compromised. The entire planet was compromised—the System had eyes everywhere.

*Where can I even go?*

His phone was gone. His friends wouldn't understand. His mother—

*No. I can't drag her into this.*

He grabbed his backpack, stuffing it with the few essentials he had. Clothes. Water. The protein bars he kept for emergencies. His wallet.

As he reached for the door, a final thought struck him:

*Mentor said the Tutorial was a glitch. A breach in the System's programming. If the System can't find me in the normal world... maybe it can't find me there.*

It was insane. The Tutorial was supposed to be a one-time event, a guided experience that ended when the class was assigned. But Mentor had hinted that the breach still existed, hidden somewhere in the System's architecture.

If he could find it again, he might have a chance.

He opened the door.

A woman stood in his hallway, her eyes burning with golden fire.

She was beautiful, with sharp features and hair that floated as if underwater. But Kai didn't notice her beauty. He noticed her qi signature—immense, ancient, and familiar.

"Mentor?"

Her lips curved into a smile that held no warmth. "You've been busy, Architect."

"Were you watching? The envoy—"

"I know." She stepped forward, and Kai instinctively backed into his apartment. "I'm not here to help you, Kai. I'm here to clean up the mess."

"What?"

"The envoy was a low-level construct. His loss has alerted the System Core to your existence in a way I couldn't prevent." Her eyes flickered with something like regret. "Protocol Omega means they'll send me next. The real me. Not this projection."

Kai's throat went dry. "You're a System enforcer?"

"I'm the System's sword. The blade it uses when all other options fail." She raised her hand, and golden light gathered around her fingers. "I'm sorry, Kai. You had potential. But potential is dangerous, and the System cannot abide danger."

She moved to strike.

And Kai realized, in that frozen moment, that he had nowhere left to run.

title: Chapter 17
content:
CHAPTER 17: The Price of Progress

Kai's phone buzzed again before he could send his message. Then again. And again. The notifications piled up faster than he could read them, a cascade of panicked texts from every contact he had.

*Did you see the broadcast?*

*What does "World Integration" mean?*

*The sky. Look at the sky.*

Kai's blood ran cold. He threw off his sheets and stumbled to his apartment window, yanking the curtain aside.

The sky was wrong.

Not in color—it was still the pale gray of an overcast morning. But the clouds moved in patterns that defied physics, swirling in geometric formations that pulsed with an inner light. And beyond them, visible even through the haze, was something that made Kai's breath catch in his throat.

A crack. A literal crack in the sky, like someone had taken a hammer to a pane of glass. Through it, he could see—*something*. Colors that shouldn't exist. Shapes that hurt to look at.

[WORLD INTEGRATION: 12% COMPLETE]

The System notification hung in his vision, a countdown timer that no one had asked for.

"Twelve percent?" Kai whispered. "Of what?"

His phone rang. He didn't recognize the number, but he answered anyway, hoping for answers.

"Kai Chen?" The voice was sharp, professional. Female.

"Who's asking?"

"Agent Lin, Department of Anomalous Affairs. We need you to come in for a mandatory assessment."

"Department of *what*?"

"The government's been preparing for this since the first System notification appeared three months ago. You didn't think we were caught off guard, did you?" There was a dry amusement in her voice. "Every class holder is being catalogued. Your tutorial results, your class type, your threat level. Standard procedure."

"Threat level?"

"We're not the enemy, Mr. Chen. But we need to know what we're working with. The crack in the sky isn't the only one. There are seventeen others, all over the world. And they're growing."

Kai's grip tightened on his phone. "What does the System want?"

"That's what we're trying to figure out. Your assessment is at the Central Municipal Building, third floor. Be there in two hours, or we'll send a retrieval team."

The line went dead.

Kai stared at his phone, then at the cracked sky, then at his own hands—hands that now hummed with qi he could barely control.

*Two hours.*

---

The Central Municipal Building looked the same as it always had—a squat, bureaucratic structure of concrete and glass that had housed DMV offices and tax collection centers for decades. But the line outside told a different story. Hundreds of people, all ages, all backgrounds, snaked around the block. Some looked excited, practically bouncing with energy. Others looked like they hadn't slept in days.

Kai joined the queue, keeping his head down. The qi in his dantian pulsed with every heartbeat, a constant reminder that he wasn't the same person who'd woken up in this apartment a week ago.

"Hey, you!"

Kai looked up. A young man in his early twenties was staring at him, eyes wide. "You're the one, right? The guy from the forums? The one who said his class was 'unclassified'?"

The people around them turned. Whispers spread like wildfire.

*Unclassified? That's not possible.*

*I heard the System flagged him as an anomaly.*

*What's his class? Does anyone know?*

Kai's jaw tightened. "I don't know what you're talking about."

"Bull." The young man stepped closer, and Kai got a read on his qi signature—sharp, aggressive, like a blade. A combat class. "You're all over the underground channels. The System doesn't give out 'unclassified' unless something's broken. So what are you? A glitch? A bug?"

"I'm just trying to get my assessment like everyone else."

"Assessment?" The young man laughed. "The government's assessment is just a way to rank us. Figure out who's useful and who's expendable. You think they're gonna help you if you're broken?"

A woman in her forties stepped between them. "Back off, kid. We're all scared. Doesn't mean we need to tear each other apart."

The young man sneered but backed away, muttering something about "freaks" and "glitches."

Kai met the woman's eyes. "Thanks."

"Don't mention it." She lowered her voice. "But he's not entirely wrong. The underground's been buzzing about you. Word travels fast when the System can't categorize someone. Be careful."

The line moved. Slowly, methodically, they were herded into the building, through metal detectors that had been retrofitted with some kind of spiritual sensor—Kai felt it brush against his qi like a cold wind.

"Name?"

"Kai Chen."

The clerk typed something into a terminal, then frowned. "Your file's flagged. You're being directed to a different assessment station."

"Different? Why?"

The clerk shrugged. "Above my pay grade. Take elevator B to the fourth floor. Room 412."

Kai did as instructed. The elevator was empty, the walls lined with the same spiritual sensors. He could feel them tracking him, cataloguing his qi signature, probably sending data to someone in a control room somewhere.

*They're watching me.*

Room 412 was nothing like the assessment rooms he'd passed on the lower floors. No chairs, no desks, no testing equipment. Just a single figure standing in the center of an otherwise empty room.

Agent Lin.

She was younger than he'd expected—maybe late twenties, with sharp features and eyes that missed nothing. She wore a simple business suit, but Kai could sense the power coiled within her. Her qi signature was... strange. Controlled. Like a tightly wound spring.

"Mr. Chen." She gestured to the space in front of her. "Please, stand here."

He obeyed. The moment he stepped into position, the room hummed to life. Runes flared on the walls, forming a circle around them. Kai's qi reacted instinctively, surging to meet the pressure of the formation.

"Interesting." Agent Lin circled him, tablet in hand. "Your qi density is remarkably high for someone who's only been cultivating for a week. Your meridians are open, but unstable. And your dantian—" She paused, frowning. "Your dantian is... forming. That's unusual."

"Forming? What does that mean?"

"It means your cultivation base isn't finished. Most people's dantians lock into place after their tutorial. Yours is still developing, like it's waiting for something." She looked up from her tablet. "Your class. You said it was 'Dao Architect.' What does that entail?"

Kai hesitated. Should he tell her the truth? That Mentor had told him he was building a path the System couldn't understand? That he was a variable in a formula the System hadn't accounted for?

"I'm still figuring it out," he said carefully. "The tutorial was... different."

"Different how?"

"There was a teacher. A person, not a System construct. She called herself Mentor."

Agent Lin's eyes widened—the first crack in her professional composure. "A human teacher? In a tutorial?"

"She wasn't human. Or at least, she didn't seem to be. She called the System 'the Heavenly Dao reborn as code.' Said my class was an anomaly that could either fix everything or break it."

The runes on the walls flickered. The formation hummed louder, and Kai felt a pressure building in his chest, like something was trying to pry open his soul.

Agent Lin's expression shifted. "Mr. Chen, I need you to be completely honest with me. Did your teacher mention anything about 'World Integration'?"

"Yes. She said the System was integrating Earth into a larger framework. That there were other worlds, other cultivators."

"And did she tell you *why* the System chose Earth?"

Kai opened his mouth to answer, but before he could speak, the formation exploded.

Light flooded the room, blinding him. The runes on the walls screamed with energy, and Kai felt his qi being ripped from his dantian, pulled into the formation like water down a drain.

"What—"

Agent Lin was shouting something, but he couldn't hear her over the roar of the formation. His vision blurred. His body felt like it was being torn apart.

And then, through the chaos, he heard it.

A voice. Familiar. Golden.

*"Well done, Kai. You've passed the first test."*

Mentor's voice.

But that was impossible. She was in the tutorial. She wasn't—

*"I never left. I've been here the whole time."*

The formation collapsed. Kai fell to his knees, gasping. When he looked up, Agent Lin was staring at him with an expression he couldn't read.

"What," she said slowly, "was that?"

Kai's hands were shaking. His dantian felt raw, exposed. And somewhere in the back of his mind, Mentor's presence lingered like a shadow.

"Apparently," he said, his voice hoarse, "I'm not done with my tutorial."

---

**END OF CHAPTER 17**

title: Chapter 18
content:
CHAPTER 18: The Architect's First Blueprint

Kai stared at the System notification, his thumb hovering over the unsent message. Something was wrong. The broadcast had appeared in his vision, but it felt... incomplete. Like a sentence cut off mid-word.

He reached out with his qi sense, trying to probe the notification itself. The moment his spiritual perception touched it, the text flickered, revealing a second layer beneath:

[SYSTEM BROADCAST: TUTORIAL PHASE COMPLETE]  
[ALL CLASSES HAVE BEEN ASSIGNED]  
[WORLD INTEGRATION: 34.7% COMPLETE]  
[ERROR: UNKNOWN CLASS DETECTED - INVESTIGATION PENDING]

Kai's blood ran cold. *Investigation pending.* The System knew about him. Or at least, it knew *something* was wrong.

His phone buzzed again, pulling him back to the present. The group chat had exploded:

*Did everyone see that broadcast? What does "World Integration" mean?*

*I think the System is still installing itself. Like a fucking update.*

*Guys, I'm scared. My tutorial was a forest full of monsters. I almost died three times.*

*At least you got a tutorial. My sister didn't get one at all. She's still Classless.*

Kai scrolled through the messages, his mind racing. Classless. He hadn't considered that possibility. If the System was still integrating, still assigning classes, then maybe—

His phone rang. Unknown number.

He answered, keeping his voice neutral. "Hello?"

"Kai Chen?" The voice was female, professional, with an edge of urgency. "This is Director Zhao from the Beijing Bureau of Anomalous Affairs. We need you to come in for evaluation."

"I'm sorry, what?"

"Your class. It's flagged as anomalous in our systems. We need to verify you're not a threat to national security."

Kai's grip tightened on the phone. "I don't even know what my class does yet. How can it be a threat?"

"Sir, I'm not asking. We have a car en route to your apartment. Please cooperate, and this will go smoothly."

The line went dead.

Kai stared at the phone, his mind blank. The Bureau of Anomalous Affairs. He'd heard rumors on forums and news sites—a government organization formed after the System's arrival, tasked with monitoring and controlling superhuman activity. But he'd never expected to be on their radar.

*Think. What would Mentor do?*

The answer came immediately: *Don't be where they expect you to be.*

Kai grabbed his backpack, stuffing it with essentials—a change of clothes, his phone charger, a half-eaten bag of chips. He paused, then reached out with his qi sense, feeling the subtle flows of energy in his apartment. There. In the corner, near the ceiling, a faint shimmer of qi that hadn't been there before.

A tracking sigil. They'd already marked him.

He had minutes, maybe seconds.

Kai moved. He didn't bother with the door—too obvious. Instead, he focused on the qi in his dantian, still raw and unrefined, and pushed it through his meridians. The sensation was like forcing water through a hose with a kink—painful, inefficient, but possible.

He visualized the wall in front of him, not as a physical barrier, but as a concentration of qi particles held together by the System's architecture. And he saw what Mentor had shown him: the gaps, the weaknesses, the places where the System's hold was thinnest.

*There.*

He slammed his palm against the wall. Qi erupted from his hand, not as a blast, but as a precisely targeted disruption. The plaster cracked, then crumbled, revealing the bricks beneath. Another push, and the bricks shifted, creating a hole just large enough for him to squeeze through.

Kai climbed into the adjacent apartment, hoping it was empty. It was. A studio, sparsely furnished, with dirty dishes in the sink and clothes scattered on the floor. The occupant was clearly in the middle of moving out—or had already abandoned the place.

He didn't stop to investigate. He crossed to the window, opened it, and climbed onto the fire escape. Below, he could see a black sedan pulling up to his building's entrance. Two men in suits got out, their movements efficient, practiced. They didn't look like bureaucrats. They looked like soldiers in civilian clothes.

Kai climbed upward, not down. The roof. From there, he could cross to the next building, then the next, until he was far enough away to think clearly.

His muscles screamed in protest. His qi reserves were nearly empty, drained by the wall-breaking and the climbing. But he kept moving, driven by a single thought: *I can't let them catch me. Not until I understand what I am.*

---

Three hours later, Kai sat in a 24-hour diner on the edge of the city, nursing a cup of coffee he couldn't afford. The place was nearly empty—just a tired waitress and a cook who chain-smoked in the back. The fluorescent lights hummed overhead, casting everything in a sickly yellow glow.

He pulled out his phone, checking for messages. The group chat was still active, but the tone had shifted. Fear had given way to excitement as people shared their new abilities, compared builds, and planned dungeon runs.

*Dungeon runs.* The phrase stuck in his mind. The System had introduced dungeons—instanced spaces where players could fight monsters, earn loot, and level up. It was exactly what every gamer had dreamed of.

But Kai wasn't a gamer anymore. He was something else.

He opened the System interface, navigating to his class page:

[CLASS: Dao Architect]  
[STATUS: Active - Anomalous]  
[QI CULTIVATION: Meridian Awakening (Stage 1)]  
[SKILLS: None]  
[BLUEPRINTS: None]

*Blueprints.* That was new. He focused on the word, and a submenu appeared:

[BLUEPRINT: EMPTY]  
[CREATE NEW BLUEPRINT?]

Kai's heart raced. This was it. The core of his class. He selected [Yes], and the world around him seemed to fade as a new interface opened:

[BLUEPRINT CREATION]  
[MATERIALS REQUIRED: Qi (Variable), Intent (Variable), Understanding (Variable)]  
[WARNING: Blueprint creation is permanent. Choose wisely.]

He stared at the prompt, his mind blank. What could he build? What *should* he build?

Mentor's voice echoed in his memory: *"The Dao Architect does not follow the System's rules. You create new rules. You build paths where none exist."*

Kai thought about the Bureau, about the tracking sigil, about the System's investigation flag. He was being hunted, and he had nothing—no skills, no weapons, no allies. If he was going to survive, he needed an edge.

He closed his eyes, reaching deep into his dantian. The qi there was thin, almost depleted, but he could feel its potential. It was raw, formless, waiting to be shaped.

*What do I need?*

The answer came slowly, forming in his mind like a half-remembered dream. A technique. Something simple, something he could use immediately. Something that would let him hide, or escape, or fight.

*Hide. Yes. Hide first.*

He focused on the concept of concealment. Not invisibility—that was too complex, too demanding. Something subtler. A way to make himself unnoticeable, to blend into the background, to be overlooked.

The qi in his dantian began to stir, responding to his intent. It flowed through his meridians, not in a circulation, but in a pattern—a web of energy that connected his body to the world around him. He could feel the diner's qi, the waitress's life force, the hum of the refrigerator in the back.

And he saw how to weave himself into that tapestry.

The blueprint formed in his mind: a lattice of qi threads, each one tuned to a specific frequency. When activated, they would resonate with the ambient energy, creating a field that made his presence seem ordinary, unremarkable. Not invisible. Just... forgettable.

[BLUEPRINT CREATED: Veil of the Unremarkable]  
[QI COST: 50 (initial activation) + 5/minute (sustained)]  
[DURATION: Until deactivated or qi depleted]  
[NOTE: Effectiveness scales with cultivation level. At current stage, deception is limited to mundane observers.]

Kai opened his eyes, gasping. The blueprint burned in his mind, a permanent addition to his consciousness. He could feel it, like a new limb, waiting to be used.

He focused, and the Veil activated. The qi threads spread from his body, weaving into the ambient energy. The waitress glanced at him, then looked away. The cook didn't even turn around. He was just another customer, drinking cheap coffee in a rundown diner.

It worked.

But the cost was staggering. His already depleted qi reserves plummeted, leaving him lightheaded and weak. He'd need hours to recover, and even then, he couldn't sustain the Veil indefinitely.

*It's a start.*

His phone buzzed. Another message, this time from an unknown number:

*You're harder to find than expected. Good. Meet me at the Old North Bridge at midnight. Come alone. - Li Wei*

Kai stared at the message, his mind racing. Li Wei. The name was familiar—a former classmate, a top-tier gamer who'd gone pro years ago. If he was reaching out now, it meant he'd gotten a class too. And if he knew Kai was being hunted...

*This could be a trap.*

But it could also be an opportunity. The Bureau was after him, the System was investigating him, and he had no allies. Li Wei was powerful, connected, and smart. If anyone could help him navigate this new world, it was him.

Kai drained his coffee, left a crumpled bill on the table, and walked out into the night. The Veil clung to him like a second skin, making him forgettable, invisible, safe.

For now.

---

The Old North Bridge was a relic of a bygone era, a stone arch spanning a narrow river that had long since been diverted. It was dark, overgrown, and utterly abandoned—the perfect place for a clandestine meeting.

Kai arrived at 11:57, his qi reserves barely a quarter full. The Veil had drained him more than he'd anticipated, and he'd had to stop twice to rest. But he'd made it.

He waited in the shadows, his senses stretched to their limit. The night was quiet, save for the rustle of leaves and the distant hum of traffic. No one was here.

*Maybe it was a trap after all.*

A footstep. Soft, deliberate. Kai turned, his hands raised, ready to fight or flee.

A figure emerged from the darkness—tall, lean, with sharp eyes that seemed to cut through the night. Li Wei. He looked the same as he had in high school, except for the faint glow emanating from his skin. A cultivator's glow.

"You're early," Li Wei said, his voice calm, measured. "Good. That means you're smart."

"Or desperate," Kai replied. "Why did you call me here?"

Li Wei smiled, but there was no warmth in it. "Because you're in danger, and I'm the only one who can help you. The Bureau is hunting you. The System is flagging you. And you have no idea what you

title: The Weight of Foundations
content:
Kai stared at the notification, his thumb hovering over the unsent message. The broadcast hung in his vision, pulsing with an urgency that made his newly awakened meridians thrum in response.

[SYSTEM BROADCAST: TUTORIAL PHASE COMPLETE]
[ALL CLASSES HAVE BEEN ASSIGNED]
[WORLD INTEGRATION: 47% COMPLETE]
[ESTIMATED TIME TO FULL INTEGRATION: 72 HOURS]
[WARNING: UNCLASSIFIED ENTITIES DETECTED]
[PROTOCOL: INVESTIGATION INITIATED]

*Unclassified entities.* Him. His class. The words settled in his chest like a stone dropped into still water.

His phone buzzed again. Then again. The group chat exploded.

*47%? What does that mean?*

*72 hours until what?*

*Anyone else get that warning about unclassified entities?*

*Kai, you there?*

He shoved the phone under his pillow. The notifications could wait. He needed to think.

Mentor's words echoed in his mind: *The System does not know what to do with you.* The broadcast confirmed it. He was an anomaly, a glitch in the grand design, and the System had just announced it was coming to investigate.

*Investigation.* The word carried implications he didn't want to consider.

Kai climbed out of bed, his joints protesting after hours of meditation and qi circulation. Every muscle felt like it had been stretched and rewoven. His dantian pulsed with a warmth that was becoming familiar, comforting even.

He walked to the window and pulled back the curtain.

The world outside looked the same—gray apartment buildings, cracked sidewalks, a stray cat picking through an overturned trash can. But his qi sense told a different story. Lines of energy crisscrossed the street, invisible to normal eyes but blazing to his perception. They connected buildings, people, even the stray cat, forming a vast network of spiritual highways.

*The System's infrastructure,* he realized. *It's everywhere.*

A man walked past his building, his qi signature bright and sharp—a Warrior, probably Level 2 or 3 based on the density. He moved with purpose, his steps carrying him toward the main street where Kai could sense half a dozen other signatures clustered together.

*They're grouping up. Forming parties. Grinding.*

The thought made him uneasy. The System had been active for less than a day, and already people were adapting, optimizing, treating it like the game it appeared to be. But Mentor had made it clear: this wasn't a game. The Heavenly Dao didn't play.

His phone buzzed again. And again. And again.

With a sigh, he retrieved it. The group chat had grown to 247 unread messages. He scrolled through, catching fragments:

*I heard someone in District 4 already hit Level 5.*

*My neighbor got a quest! A real quest! Kill 10 rats in the sewers for 50 EXP and a Common-grade item.*

*The sewers? That's insane.*

*It's a game, bro. Gotta grind.*

*A game? My sister almost died in her tutorial. She woke up with third-degree burns. The System healed her, but still.*

Kai's blood ran cold. *Third-degree burns.* He'd been lucky. His tutorial had been dangerous, yes, but Mentor had controlled every variable. What about the people whose tutorials were combat sims or fireball lessons without proper guidance?

He typed: *Has anyone tried NOT following the System's instructions?*

The response was immediate.

*What do you mean?*

*Why would you not follow instructions? Free stats, free skills.*

*Kai, you okay? You sound weird.*

He hesitated. What could he say without sounding insane? *The System is the Heavenly Dao corrupted into code, and my tutorial was taught by a fragment of a dead civilization who warned me not to trust it blindly.*

Instead, he typed: *Just be careful. The System might not have our best interests in mind.*

A pause. Then:

*Bro, it literally gave me the ability to shoot fire from my hands. I'm not complaining.*

*Yeah, I can heal minor wounds now. My entire family is fighting over who gets to be the party healer.*

*Kai, you're overthinking this. It's a game. Enjoy it.*

Kai closed the chat. They wouldn't understand. Not yet. Maybe not ever.

He needed more information. Mentor had vanished after the tutorial, leaving him with nothing but a foundation technique and vague warnings. But she'd mentioned others—other Architects, other fragments of civilizations that had tried and failed to understand the System.

If they existed, they were his only hope.

He reached into his dantian, feeling the qi pool there like water in a reservoir. His foundation was unstable, Mentor had said, but it was forming. If he could circulate the qi properly, maybe he could extend his perception, find others like him.

The technique was still fresh in his mind, etched into his meridians by hours of practice. He sat cross-legged on the floor, closed his eyes, and began.

The first cycle was rough. His meridians, still tender from the tutorial, protested as qi pushed through them. But he persisted, guiding the energy along the path Mentor had taught him, feeling it grow stronger with each rotation.

By the third cycle, his perception expanded.

He could sense the building around him—every tenant, every pet, every plant on every windowsill. The Warrior from earlier was three blocks away now, his signature flaring as he engaged in combat. A Healer on the second floor was tending to a burn victim, her qi flowing in gentle waves of green light.

And there, at the edge of his perception, something *wrong*.

A signature that didn't match any class he'd sensed before. It was faint, barely a whisper, but it pulsed with a frequency that made his qi shudder in recognition.
